COMPLIANCE WITH THIS PUBLICATION IS MANDATORY
ACCESSIBILITY: This publication is available for downloading or ordering on the ePublishing website at www.e-Publishing.af.mil.
RELEASABILITY: There are no releasability restrictions on this publication.

OPR: HQ AFSEC/SEG
Supersedes:

DAFMAN91-203, 25 March 2022

Certified by: AF/SE
(Col Michael A. Thomas)
Pages: 413

This manual implements Department of the Air Force Policy Directive (DAFPD) 91-2, Safety
Programs, and is consistent with parts of Title 29 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), Chapter
XVII, Occupation Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), Department of Labor (DoL). This
manual defines the Air Force’s minimum safety, fire protection and occupational health standards,
including additional requirements not addressed by the OSHA standards. This publication applies
to all civilian employees and uniformed members of the Regular Air Force, Air Force Reserve
(AFR), the Air National Guard (ANG), the United States Space Force (USSF), the Civil Air Patrol
when conducting missions as the official Air Force Auxiliary, and those with a contractually
obligation to abide by the terms of Department of the Air Force (DAF) publications. This
publication requires the collection and/or maintenance of information protected by the Privacy Act
of 1974 authorized by Department of Defense Directive (DoDD) 5400.11, DoD Privacy Program.
The applicable System of Records Notice (SORNs) F024 AF IL C, Motor Vehicle Operator’s
Records, and F031 AF SP L, Traffic Accident and Violation Reports, are available at
https://pclt.defense.gov/DIRECTORATES/Privacy-and-Civil-LibertiesDirectorate/Privacy/SORNs. Ensure all records generated as a result of processes prescribed in
this publication adhere to AFI 33-322, Records Management and Information Governance
Program, and are disposed in accordance with (IAW) Air Force Records Disposition Schedule,
which is located in the Air Force Records Information Management System. Refer recommended
changes and questions about this publication to the Office of Primary Responsibility (OPR) using
the DAF Form 847, Recommendation for Change of Product; route DAF Forms 847 from the field
through the appropriate functional chain of command. Refer to Department of the Air Force

2

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

Instruction (DAFI) 91-202, The Department of the Air Force (DAF) Mishap Prevention Program,
for guidance on submitting requests for exemptions to requirements of this publication. Report
conflicts in guidance between this publication, federal standards or other DAF directives through
Major Command (MAJCOM), Field Command (FLDCOM), Direct Reporting Unit (DRU) or
Field Operating Agency (FOA) occupational safety offices to Headquarters Air Force (HAF)
Safety Center, Occupational Safety Division, Standards and Policy Branch (HQ AFSEC/SEGS),
9700 G Avenue SE, Kirtland AFB NM 87117-5670. This manual may be supplemented at any
level, but to ensure standardization, any organization supplementing this manual must send the
implementing publication to AFSEC, Occupational Safety Division (AFSEC/SEG) for review and
coordination before publishing. The authorities to waive wing/delta/unit-level requirements in this
publication are identified with a Tier (“T-0, T-1, T-2, T-3”) number following the compliance
statement. See Department of the Air Force Manual (DAFMAN) 90-161, Publishing Processes
and Procedures, for a description of the authorities associated with the Tier waiver approval
authority. For non-tiered compliance items, the requestor’s commander is the approval authority.
Submit requests for waivers through the chain of command to the appropriate Tier/Waiver
approval authority, or alternately, to HQ AFSEC/SEG for waivers/exemptions to non-tiered
compliance items. The requested office must send a copy of all approved waivers to the OPR of
the Higher Headquarters publication being waived (see DAFI 90-160, Publications and Forms
Management, paragraph 1.5.3.2) within 30 days of approval. Sending an email to the waived
publication OPR that includes a completed DAF Form 679, Department of the Air Force
Publication Compliance Item Waiver Request/Approval, or equivalent, will suffice. Requirements
in this publication are mandatory, unless otherwise indicated. Not included are safety, fire
prevention and occupational health requirements addressed in specific equipment technical orders
(TOs). Compliance with the attachments in this publication is mandatory. The use of the name or
mark of any specific manufacturer, commercial product, commodity, or service in this publication
does not imply endorsement by the DAF.
SUMMARY OF CHANGES
This document is substantially revised and must be completely reviewed. Major changes include
adding the applicability to the USSF and clarifying and reinforcing requirements for Process Safety
Management, which is now its own standalone chapter, Chapter 33, Process Safety Management
(PSM) of Highly Hazardous Chemicals. Additionally, it includes creating a new Chapter 34,
Additive Manufacturing Safety, for 3D printing, major changes to Chapter 12, Material Handling
Equipment (MHE), regarding inspection criteria for material handling equipment, implementing
required changes to confined space requirements recommended and mandated by the Air Force
Audit Agency.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

15
Chapter 1

INTRODUCTION
1.1. Objective.
1.1.1. OSHA safety guidance published in the DoL 29 CFR series provides employees with a
safe and healthy workplace. OSHA dictates what must be accomplished in the workplace, but
in some cases, not necessarily how it will be accomplished, or by whom. Refer to AFSEC
Occupational Safety SharePoint® site for Introduction to OSHA Standards training
information
located
at
https://usaf.dps.mil/teams/AFSECSEG/SEGS%20Public/Forms/AllItems.aspx?as=json&FolderCTID=0x012000D03CA30
64AB0BB4CA2705F0FFC828818&id=%2Fteams%2FAFSECSEG%2FSEGS%20Public%FOSHA.
1.1.2. Commanders are responsible for the safety of their facilities and personnel, including
the correction of all identified hazards and deficiencies in their workplaces. This publication
compliments DAFI 91-202 and assigns responsibilities to individuals or functions to help
commanders manage their safety and health program, ensuring they comply with OSHA,
Department of Defense (DoD), and DAF guidance. This publication provides uniform
guidance, which safety staffs and commanders may supplement when additional or more
stringent safety, fire prevention and health criteria are required.
1.1.3. MAJCOMs/FLDCOMs may have more stringent or restrictive requirements than those
contained in this publication or other DAF Occupational Safety and Health (OSH)
publications/standards. Therefore, all requests for interpretations or clarifications concerning
these publications must be forwarded through the servicing MAJCOM/FLDCOM/DRU/FOA
safety, health, and/or civil engineering organization, as applicable. Note: With respect to
references in this manual to medical treatment facilities, pursuant to 10 United States Code
(USC) § 1073c, the Defense Health Agency, a combat support agency, will be responsible for
their administration and management; the details of these responsibilities are still being worked
and finalized. (T-0)
1.2. Roles and Responsibilities.
1.2.1. All HAF elements will ensure policies and procedures are consistent with the guidance
of this manual.
1.2.2. Headquarters, Air Force Safety Center (AFSEC) will:
1.2.2.1. Formulate and disseminate policy. Clarify roles, responsibilities, and guidance
applicable to all areas of safety.
1.2.2.2. Act as coordination authority for safety waivers associated with Tier1 and above
wing/delta-level requirements in this manual.
1.2.3. MAJCOMs, FLDCOMs, DRUs and FOAs will provide program oversight and
supplement safety and health guidance, as necessary, ensuring safety training is available to
subordinate organizations.
1.2.4. Commanders and Functional Managers will:

16

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
1.2.4.1. Ensure applicable occupational safety and health (OSH) guidance for workplace
and operations is available to all personnel.
1.2.4.2. Enforce and promote compliance with occupational safety, fire prevention and
health program requirements in their areas of responsibility.
1.2.4.3. Provide a safe and healthy workplace via a monthly spot-inspection program.
1.2.4.4. Provide employees with training in job safety, fire prevention and health, as
required by:
1.2.4.4.1. OSHA directives.
1.2.4.4.2. DAFOSH directives.
1.2.4.4.3. DAFIs, DAFMANs, and associated publications.
1.2.5. Supervisor. Supervisors will require employees to notify them in advance if they have
a medical condition or are taking medications that could interfere with their safe performance
of assigned duties. Supervisors will ensure employees are trained, using manufacturer’s
instructions or lesson plans, prior to the operation of any type of equipment or accomplishing
assigned tasks. Supervisors will routinely evaluate employees on the proper and safe operation
of all equipment and associated personal protective equipment (PPE). Unsafe behaviors will
be corrected on the spot. Supervisors will provide such instruction at the time of initial
assignment and all required refresher training. If guidance is not available, supervisors will
accomplish a job hazard analysis (JHA) to determine safe procedures. Additional supervisor
responsibilities can be found in DAFI 91-202.
1.2.5.1. Conduct pre-task safety briefings where the task(s) present higher mishap risks,
e.g., use of aerial lifts or when conducting new tasks. Refer to DAFPAM 90-803, Risk
Management (RM) Guidelines and Tools, for specific risk management tools.
1.2.5.2. Pre-task safety briefings should include:
1.2.5.2.1. Hazards to be aware of and how to protect employees.
1.2.5.2.2. Review of work assignments and answering employees’ questions
concerning the work, hazards, and controls.
1.2.5.2.3. Provide clarification on why the task must be completed and the work
methods that must be used.
1.2.6. DAF personnel (military and civilian) will:
1.2.6.1. Comply with OSH guidance.
1.2.6.2. Promptly report unsafe working conditions/activities including any safety, fire,
and health hazard deficiencies to the supervisor.
1.2.6.3. Promptly report injuries and illnesses IAW DAFI 91-204, Safety Investigations
and Reports, and DAFMAN 91-224, Ground Safety Investigation and Reports.
1.2.6.4. Comply with PPE requirements, including its use, inspection and care.
1.2.6.5. Only operate machines and equipment with functional safety devices.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

17

1.2.6.6. Notify supervisors in advance if they have a medical condition or are taking
medications that could interfere with their safe performance of assigned duties.
1.3. Waivers, Exemptions and Letters of Interpretation.
1.3.1. The affected work center will process a request for waiver or exemption when it is
impossible to meet OSH guidance due to operational needs, mission impact, or technical
reasons. (T-1) When requesting a waiver or exemption, the work center leadership must
identify and implement interim control measures to reduce the degree of risk associated with
the hazard to protect personnel, equipment and/or property. (T-1) The process for requesting a
waiver IAW DAFMAN 90-161 or exemption is provided in DAFI 91-202.
1.3.2. DAF letters of interpretation (LOIs) are written in response to OSH-related inquiries
submitted by DAF personnel or organizations regarding specific aspects of the terminology in
this manual or with OSHA standards and applicable adopted national consensus safety
guidance. AFSEC/SEG is responsible for responding to and providing clarification to meet the
operational needs of the DAF. LOIs provide this response and are intended to clarify the
application of established standards, policy, or procedure, but they may not, in themselves,
establish or revise DAF policy or procedure. The LOI specifically cites the source policy or
procedure document being interpreted and includes the time duration of the LOI.
1.4. How to Use This Document.
1.4.1. This publication incorporates appropriate parts of the 29 CFR §§ 1910 and 1926
standards that relate to DAF operations. References to other DAF publications and national
consensus standards are provided as appropriate. DAF activities must comply with OSHA
requirements at all times. Use of the military-unique exemption will be applied IAW
Department of Defense Instruction (DoDI) 6055.01, DoD Safety and Occupational Health
(SOH) Program, and DAFI 91-202. (T-0)
1.4.2. This publication is intended for use by all DAF personnel. It provides OSH requirements
that will provide equal or greater protection than applicable federal regulatory standards. The
information in this publication is for the protection of employees, DAF property and
equipment, and to ensure compliance with Federal law. Note: This publication may contain
criteria not fully compatible with host-nation safety criteria. In case of conflict, safety offices
will facilitate coordinated concerns through their chain of command for resolution. (T-1)
1.5. Applying Standards. DAF personnel must comply with all applicable safety guidance
during DAF operations. (T-1) Where there is conflicting guidance, use the guidance that provides
the most protection for the employee until conflict resolution is attained.
1.5.1. Compliance.
1.5.1.1. This standard contains both horizontal and vertical fire, safety, and health
standards/requirements within the various chapters. Horizontal standards/requirements
apply across all industries/activities, whereas, vertical standards/requirements apply to a
specific industry/activity, e.g., construction or telecommunications. Vertical standards will
be followed to protect employees from hazards identified in specific industries/operations
over horizontal standards. (T-0) Where the vertical standard does not cover a hazard, then
the horizontal standard will be applied to protect personnel. (T-0) Example: In 29 CFR
1910.252, Welding, Cutting, Brazing, there are specific confined space requirements

18

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
(vertical standard) that must be addressed prior to using the requirements in 29 CFR
1910.146, Permit-Required Confined Spaces (horizontal standard). However, the welding
standard does not cover certain requirements found in the Permit-Required Confined Space
standard, such as the permit process, training of personnel, etc. that would require
following the Permit-Required Confined Space standard, as applicable. Contact your
occupational safety office if assistance is needed with vertical/horizontal standards.
1.5.1.2. When OSH guidance or other established safety criteria does not cover a situation,
use non-DAF standards to include, but not limited to, national consensus standards,
professional safety and health standards, and other Federal agency standards.
1.5.2. OSHA Requirements. DAF safety officials cannot grant exemptions to OSHA
standards, only the DoL has the authority to grant exemptions to OSHA standards. Refer to
DAFI 91-202 for additional guidance.
1.5.3. National Consensus Standards. National consensus standards referenced in this
document have been adopted, in whole or in part, for DAF use and list the standard,
part/document number, part/document title, and date of standard, as applicable, e.g., American
Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) B30.9, Slings, and National Fire Protection
Association (NFPA) 70, National Electrical Code. Personnel needing access to American
National Standards Institute (ANSI) and/or other national consensus standards can contact the
occupational safety office for guidance and assistance. Consensus standards are identified for
awareness/information unless needed to complete a specific task.
1.5.4. Joint Base Safety Standard Compliance. DAF personnel at Joint Bases will follow
safety requirements outlined in the approved support agreement(s) developed IAW DoDI
4000.19, Support Agreements, and AFI 25-201, Intra-Service, Intra-Agency, and Inter-Agency
Support Agreements Procedures. (T-1) When there is conflicting guidance, use the guidance
that provides the most protection. Further conflicts in safety program requirements will first
be coordinated through senior leadership at the local level for resolution. (T-1) If resolution at
the local level cannot be obtained, the organization will refer conflicts to AFSEC/SEG through
the appropriate MAJCOM/FLDCOM/FOA/DRU safety office for cross-service resolution.
(T-1)

1.6. Job Hazard Analysis (JHA). JHAs are conducted on all work processes, where appropriate,
to identify potential fire, safety, or health hazards, determine appropriate training and PPE, and
include preventative measures in procedures to mitigate the hazards. Refer to DAFI 91-202 for
additional guidance.
1.7. Documentation. This publication specifies the use of Air Force Technical Order (AFTO)
Form 95, Significant Historical Data, AFTO Form 244, Industrial/Support Equipment Record, or
other appropriate inspection, maintenance and general-purpose forms for documenting
maintenance and inspection actions. With expanding the use of automated systems for tracking
these activities, requiring specific paper forms may impede the use of more efficient automated
processes. Therefore, the use of automated systems for documenting inspections, maintenance and
other activities is acceptable in place of specified AFTO Forms 95 and 244 or other appropriate
inspection, maintenance, and general-purpose forms if they contain the same information called
for by the designated form. Electronic signatures or other unique personal identifiers (not initials)
are acceptable. While this publication offers an optional alternative to paper forms, MAJCOMs,
FLDCOMs, Numbered Air Forces (NAFs), local procedures, Technical Orders (TOs), and other

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

19

DAF guidance may continue to require the use of these forms. Check the prescribing directive(s)
before discontinuing use of any forms.
1.8. First Aid and Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) Training. Initial first aid/CPR
training will be accomplished prior to assigning an individual duties where first aid/CPR are
requirements of the position. (T-1) All employees requiring CPR training will receive refresher
training before their current CPR certification expires. (T-1) Organizational/shop CPR instructors
may be trained by the local medical group or trained directly by the American Red Cross,
American Heart Association, or a Military Treatment Facility (MTF) approved source. CPR
instructors will, in turn, train organizational personnel. (T-1) MTF Commanders determine the
MTFs ability to support CPR training and will notify the units when/if they can no longer provide
training. (T-1) Where training is no longer provided by the host medical facility, contact the host
Public Access Defibrillator (PAD) Program Coordinator for assistance in locating training
providers. The CPR instructor will ensure training includes PAD training IAW AFI 44-177, Public
Access Defibrillator Program. (T-1) Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC) IAW DoDI 1322.24,
Medical Readiness Training, may be used to fulfill first aid training requirements; however, it does
not cover CPR. Employees performing duties with risk of severe cuts, burns or electrocution, or
perform duties in remote locations away from emergency responders will have a plan for
immediate medical response pending definitive transfer and care. (T-0) (Note: Remote location is
defined as emergency care that is more than three to four minutes from the workplace.) Refer to
DHA 6000.03, Life Support Training Certification Requirements and Guidance, for additional
requirements.
1.9. Bloodborne Pathogen Training. Any person with routine duties where tasks and
procedures involve reasonably anticipated exposure to blood or other potentially infectious
materials (e.g., individual responsible for rendering medical assistance as part of their duties) must
be trained and enrolled in the Bloodborne Pathogen Program IAW 29 CFR § 1910.1030,
Bloodborne Pathogen, and AFI 44-108, Infection Prevention and Control Program. (T-0) A
person may be required to receive separate first responder training outside of bloodborne pathogen,
e.g., First Aid, CPR, TCCC. For workplaces with employees that meet this job description,
supervisors will establish a written exposure control plan and conduct training IAW 29 CFR §
1910.1030. (T-0)

20

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 2
GENERAL WORK PROCEDURES

2.1. General Information. This chapter provides general work procedures for a safe and healthy
workplace, to include the wearing of jewelry, workplace safety, ergonomics, and housekeeping.
The supervisor will ensure the job safety training outline (JSTO) evaluates and identifies PPE
requirements IAW DAFI 91-202. (T-1) All facilities will comply with DoD standards, UFC 3600-01, Fire Protection Engineering for Facilities, DAF guidance and NFPA 101, Life Safety
Code. (T-0)
2.2. Hazards. Physical hazards are the most common workplace hazards. Hazards can include
working from heights; slips, trips and falls; falling objects; spills; faulty equipment; and noise
hazards. Other examples of workplace hazards include:
2.2.1. Radiation. Ionizing and non-ionizing are the two basic types of radiation. 29 CFR §
1910.1096, Ionizing Radiation, and AFMAN 48-148, Ionizing Radiation Protection, list
requirements for working in an area with ionizing radiation. Non-ionizing radiation includes
most radio frequency energy sources, light sources and lasers. Protection levels and
requirements are outlined in AFI 48-139, Laser and Optical Radiation Protection Program,
and AFI 48-109, Electromagnetic Field Radiation (EMFR) Occupational and Environmental
Health Program.
2.2.2. Noise. Hazardous noise levels can cause hearing loss and/or prevent hearing of warning
signals. Hazardous noise issues are common to industrial and outside work areas such as
flightlines and maintenance areas but should be uncommon in administrative work areas.
Concerns with workplace noise levels should be evaluated. Installation Bioenvironmental
Engineering (BE), or joint base equivalent, conducts occupational environmental health risk
assessments (HRAs), then recommends appropriate controls using the hierarchy of controls
methodology. Refer to AFI 48-127, Occupational Noise and Hearing Conservation Program,
and 29 CFR § 1910.95, Hearing Conservation Program, for additional guidance.
2.2.3. Thermal Exposure. Refer to DAFI 48-151, Thermal Stress Program, for guidance and
information on thermal injuries and their prevention/treatment. Any thermal illness requiring
more than first aid is investigated and recorded in the AFSAS by Public Health Flight
Commander or equivalent IAW DAFI 48-151.
2.2.4. Inhalation. Inhalation of hazardous materials can cause acute (irritation, allergic
reaction and/or damage the lungs and respiratory tract) and/or chronic (permanent damage,
disease, and cancer) health effects. Inhalation hazards need to be evaluated by installation BE
to determine if respiratory protection is required. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.134, Respiratory
Protection, and AFI 48-137, Respiratory Protection Program, for guidance and information.
2.2.5. Chemical. Chemical hazards may exist in everyday products such as cleaning supplies,
pesticides, and paints. It may also include vapors and fumes from welding and brazing,
solvents, flammable, and combustible liquids. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.1200, Hazard
Communication, AFI 90-821, Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) Program, and Chapter 22,
Flammables and Combustibles, of this publication.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

21

2.3. Jewelry. Jewelry, such as rings, watches, bracelets, and necklaces, that presents a potential
for catching, snagging, pulling, or tearing will be evaluated, controlled and restricted from wear if
it presents a hazard to the employee or operation. (T-1)
2.3.1. Finger Rings. Personnel engaged in work in industrial areas, performing maintenance
on aircraft, vehicles, equipment or machinery will not wear rings of any material type. (T-0)
Personnel will comply with established safety precautions to prevent personal injury. (T-0)
Rings will not be worn while performing the following activities (this list is not all-inclusive):
2.3.1.1. Climbing, ascending, or descending, activities where personnel could fall or jump
from elevated surfaces. (T-0) This includes personnel working on elevated surfaces, e.g.,
ladders, scaffolds, platforms, roofs, or high reach vehicles, e.g., refuelers, wreckers,
sweepers, dump trucks and stake bed trucks.
2.3.1.2. Materials handling operations, e.g., warehousing, parts handling, operating
equipment, packing and crating, and attaching/detaching equipment to tow vehicles. (T-0)
2.3.1.3. Any type of work where personnel are exposed to moving machinery, rotating, or
revolving parts, or activities that could result in their hands being caught by a moving part
and injured. (T-0)
2.3.1.4. Performing work or inspection on or near energized electrical parts where contact
could result in injury or damage to equipment. (T-0) Articles such as jewelry, earrings,
rings, hair fasteners, bracelets, key chains, or metallic ID (dog) tags with metal chains will
also be removed and metal framed eyeglasses will be secured by a band or cord to prevent
them from falling into energized electrical circuits or machinery. (T-0) Note: This
prohibition includes conductive jewelry, e.g., necklaces, bracelets, watches, etc. These
items will permit inadvertent electrical current transfer to the employee.
2.3.1.5. Performing maintenance or inspections on aircraft or ground support equipment,
including civil engineering-type or transportation-type maintenance where such activity
could result in injury. (T-0)
2.3.1.6. Performing work on or around high temperature equipment where contact with
these surfaces could result in injury. (T-0)
2.3.2. Eyewear. Metal eyeglasses will be secured by a band or cord to prevent them from
falling into energized electrical circuits or moving industrial machinery. (T-1) Note: It‘s best
practice to wear nonmetallic eyeglasses and neck lanyards (e.g., ID cardholders) with a
breakaway safety band, lanyard or cord to prevent them from falling into energized electrical
circuits or moving industrial machinery.
2.3.3. Medic-Alert. Personnel that require identification for medical issues are permitted to
don non-dangling/non-hazardous medical alert items, e.g., clasps, medical cards in hard hats,
identification badge holders, to warn co-workers of potential medical issues. Supervisors will
ensure such items do not create hazards during employees’ assigned tasks. (T-0)
2.3.4. These restrictions do not apply to administrative and support personnel assigned office
duties or visiting these areas but not performing industrial work.

22

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

2.4. Housekeeping.
2.4.1. Floors and Walking Surfaces. Keep areas, in and around facilities, where personnel
walk and work free of potential tripping and slipping hazards such as tools, electrical cords,
air lines and packaging materials. Clean up spills as soon as possible and post warnings until
cleanup is complete. This includes sidewalks, parking lots etc. Facility managers will promptly
report potholes and uneven surfaces to installation Civil Engineering (CE). (T-0)
2.4.1.1. Facility managers will ensure the removal of snow and ice from all facilities
walkways, sidewalks, handicap ramps and work areas expediently where they may create
a hazard or interfere with the work. (T-0) If ice cannot be readily removed, sand or other
approved materials will be applied. (T-1) Snow and icicles above walkways will be
removed by the unit or a work request will be submitted for areas that cannot be reached.
(T-1) Walkways that are located under rooftops with icicles and accumulation of snow,
should be cordoned off until the hazard can be removed.
2.4.1.2. The supervisor will ensure telephone and electrical cords are placed where they
do not pose tripping hazards. If placement in foot-traffic areas is unavoidable, cords and
cables will be properly protected by molded housing, bridges or other covers approved for
such use to prevent tripping. (T-0)
2.4.1.3. Briefcases, wastebaskets, etc., will be placed under desks, against a wall or
otherwise out of the way. (T-1)
2.4.1.4. Repair or replace stair and floor defects, such as loose tiles, broken steps, torn
carpeting, loose, or curled mats, worn, frayed, or damaged anti-slip material on stairs. (T-1)
2.4.1.5. Do not position a step stool or ladder in front of a door if the door can swing and
contact the stool, ladder, or individual using it. Station a safety observer at the door if a
step stool or ladder must be placed in a doorway. (T-1)
2.4.2. Hazardous Materials and Waste. Refer to DAFMAN 32-7002, Environmental
Compliance and Pollution Prevention, for approved hazardous waste disposal procedures and
guidance. Safety Data Sheets (SDSs) are required for each hazardous chemical, IAW AFI 90821. The SDS can assist with the proper handling of the waste prior to being removed for
disposal, especially for waste generated as a result of unused and/or expired hazardous
chemicals. The supervisor will ensure use or storage of flammable liquids will be kept to a
minimum and not exceed the maximum allowable quantities specified in Chapter 22. (T-1)
2.4.3. Packing Supplies and Recycled Materials. Non-soiled excelsior (fine wood shavings),
straw, shredded paper, recyclable cardboard, recyclable paper, and other packing/recyclable
materials will be stored in an isolated fire-resistant area and/or container. (T-0) Small supplies
of packing materials, which are kept in shops for immediate use, will be stored in metal or
noncombustible covered containers. (T-1) Remove hazardous material markings and/or
placards from empty packaging. Remove protruding nails, brads and staples from crates, cases,
packing boxes, casks, boards and lumber to prevent personnel injury.
2.4.4. Electric Buffing Machines. Supervisors will ensure electric buffing machines are
equipped with under-voltage/over-voltage sensing and cutoff controls. (T-1)
2.4.5. Stacking Materials. Refer to Air Force Joint Manual (AFJMAN) 23-210, Joint Service
Manual (JSM) For Storage and Materials Handling, for guidance on stacking materials. When

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

23

materials are stacked to a height less than 15 feet, maintain an 18-inch clearance below ceiling
sprinkler deflectors. If the stack is over 15 feet in height, increase the clearance to 36 inches
(when only joists, rafters, beams and roof trusses exist, without a ceiling constructed below, a
36 inch clearance will also be required). (T-1) Position all stacks of materials to ensure an 18inch clearance around light or heating fixtures or follow the manufacturer’s instructions,
regardless of height. (T-1)
2.4.6. Storage of materials will not create a hazard. (T-0) Bags, containers, bundles, etc.,
stored in tiers will be stacked, blocked, interlocked, and limited in height so they are stable and
secure against sliding or collapse. (T-1)
2.4.7. Lighting Fixtures. The supervisor will ensure tubular light fixtures (fluorescent/lightemitting diode) will have shields or other protective devices installed to keep the tubes from
falling out of the fixture and from accidental contact. (T-1)
2.4.8. Waste disposal containers in food handling environments will be equipped with
disposable liner material and emptied not less frequently than once each working day, unless
unused. Containers will be maintained in a clean and sanitary condition and sanitized at least
once a month. (T-1) All other waste containers will be cleaned, as needed. (T-1) Exterior
garbage cans will be securely covered when not actually being filled or emptied. (T-1)
2.4.9. General Safe Practices. All areas, shops, yards, buildings, and mobile equipment
facilities/areas will be maintained in a clean and orderly condition. (T-0) Supervisors and
employees are responsible for good housekeeping in or around the work area they are
supervising or working.
2.4.9.1. Do not place equipment or material where it creates a hazard for employees or
visitors, by falling, being a trip hazard or causing the collapse of structures. (T-0)
2.4.9.2. Keep aisles and passageways clear of tripping hazards. (T-0)
2.4.9.3. Ensure moisture accumulation from heating, ventilation and air conditioning
(HVAC) units or other water sources are cleaned up immediately to prevent mold. (T-0)
Submit work orders to installation CE, as appropriate. (T-1)
2.4.9.4. Remove nails from loose lumber or turn the points down. (T-0)
2.4.9.5. Properly store tools and supplies. (T-0)
2.4.9.6. Do not block or obstruct disconnect switches, distribution panels, and alarm
supply boxes preventing ready access. (T-0)
2.4.9.7. Stack wire reels with strips of wood between reels. (T-0)
2.4.9.8. Keep noncombustible materials neat and plainly marked, and in designated
cabinets. (T-1)
2.4.9.9. Storage Racks/Shelving. The load size and rating of storage racks/shelving will
be determined by the manufacturer’s specifications. (T-1) Structural engineers or a
certified testing agency will evaluate load size and rating for storage rack shelving units
where manufacturer’s specifications are not available or fail to specify the load size and
rating. (T-1) The load size and rating will be posted on the storage racks/shelving unit.
(T-1) Exception: Shelving used in administrative areas for lightweight office items
needed for administrative work e.g., examples include books, small stocks of paper and

24

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
paper products, and other lightweight items used in office settings. Employees will report
any damage to racks at the time of incident, specifically damage due to mechanical
equipment. (T-1) Repair defective storage racks in a timely manner. All damage and repairs
will be documented appropriately. (T-1)
2.4.9.9.1. Inspections of storage racks/shelving units will follow the manufacturer’s
instructions. (T-1)
2.4.9.9.2. Inspection will be conducted frequently to ensure preventative maintenance
is completed and damage identified. (T-1) Inspections will include all manufacturer
identified storage racks/shelving components. (T-1)
2.4.9.9.3. Inspections will be documented on AFTO Form 95 or MAJCOM/FLDCOM
or locally devised automated systems. (T-1)
2.4.9.9.4. Racks/shelving identified as damaged will be tagged with AF Form 979,
Danger Tag, or AF Form 981, Out of Order Tag, until repairs are completed. (T-1)
Remove items from racks/shelving and tag or sign “do not use” to prevent damage or
injury until the rack/shelving is repaired.
2.4.9.10. Compressed Air. Compressed air will not be used for cleaning purposes except
where it is reduced to less than 30 psi, and only with effective chip guarding and PPE.
(T-0) Follow TO, manufacturer’s instruction or local procedures as applicable. Ensure the
nozzle discharge tip is a diffuser type. Compressed air will not be used to blow chips or
other debris from an employee’s body or clothing. Screens, barriers, or protective cones
attached to air nozzles will be used to prevent flying chips and particles from striking the
eyes and skin of the operator. (T-0) Compressed air used for tools and equipment will
follow TO, manufacturer’s instruction or local procedures, as applicable. (T-1) Note:
Facility compressed air or air used from an air compressor should not be used on electrical
equipment as it could allow small metal chips or air condensation to accumulate causing
equipment malfunction. Canned compressed air should only be used on electrical
components.
2.4.9.11. Machinery and equipment will be kept clean of excess grease, oil, and excessive
dust. (T-1) Pressure gauges and visual displays will be kept clean, visible, and serviceable.
(T-1) Drip pans and wheeled or stationary containers will be cleaned and emptied at the
end of each shift. (T-2)
2.4.9.12. All work surfaces and floors will be kept free of any visible accumulation of dust,
liquid or any other foreign particles, as practicable. (T-0)

2.5. Office Safety.
2.5.1. General Office Equipment.
2.5.1.1. Chairs.
2.5.1.1.1. The supervisor or designated representative will inspect chairs regularly for
broken casters or other defects. (T-3) New or replacement caster chairs will have at
least five legs. (T-1) Note: Five or more legged caster chairs are more stable and
ergonomically sound than four-legged caster chairs.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

25

2.5.1.1.2. Do not stand on a chair to change a light bulb, retrieve overhead items or
when hanging pictures/decorations, etc. Use stepstools or ladders to access anything
above shoulder level.
2.5.1.1.3. Do not tilt back, lean sideways or reach behind to lift objects while seated in
a chair.
2.5.1.2. File Cabinets.
2.5.1.2.1. Store heavy material in bottom drawers and load cabinets from the bottom
up.
2.5.1.2.2. Close drawers when not in use.
2.5.1.2.3. Do not climb on open file drawers.
2.5.1.3. Office Shelving/Bookcases. Store the heaviest objects/books on lower-level
shelves.
2.5.2. Electrical Equipment.
2.5.2.1. Appliance, electrical equipment and other office machines will comply with 29
CFR 1910.334, Use of Equipment. (T-0)
2.5.2.2. Keep access to fuse or circuit breaker boxes or electrical controls clear and
unobstructed with panel doors closed to prevent electrical flashover if a malfunction
occurs. Refer to NFPA 70, National Electrical Code, for additional guidance on fuse and
circuit breakers in the office area.
2.5.2.3. Modular office furniture electrical systems are normally designed only to provide
power to office equipment, e.g., personal computers, monitors, and printers. Refer to 29
CFR § 1910, Subpart S, Electrical, for additional guidance. Use of appliances for personal
convenience, such as refrigerators, coffee makers, microwave ovens, will be requested
through the facility manager prior to use to ensure the electrical system can safely
accommodate the additional load. (T-2) Facility managers will contact their installation
CE when assistance is needed. (T-2)
2.5.3. Items that could present hazards, such as knives or razors, will not be left unprotected
in desk drawers, storage cabinets, on top of counters or desktops. (T-1)
2.5.4. Lighting. Consult the installation CE facility design section when specific guidance on
lighting is required. Fluorescent light bulbs contain toxic materials and will be managed IAW
local hazardous waste disposal criteria. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure any stroboscopic effect
with moving machinery is eliminated when using fluorescent lighting fixtures. (T-1) Work
area lighting will be installed to eliminate glare and harsh shadows. (T-1) Supplementary or
additional lighting will be provided where general lighting is not sufficient. (T-1)
2.5.5. HVAC systems will be maintained and inspected by a trained HVAC system technician.
Filters will be changed as needed, but minimum of once per year. (T-1) Do not cover air vents
or obstruct air flow from registers. (T-1) Do not place furniture, equipment, or materials where
they interfere with air movement or thermostats. (T-1)
2.5.6. Elevators.

26

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
2.5.6.1. A certified inspector will inspect elevators annually. (T-0) Elevator inspection
certification will be posted in the cab of the elevator in view of all passengers or retained
by the building manager and a permanent sign posted in the cab that identifies the location
of the elevator certification. (T-0)
2.5.6.2. Elevators will stop level with the floor when the doors open to prevent a tripping
hazard. (T-0) Report elevator malfunctions immediately to the unit facility manager.
Employees will not try to stop elevators by placing a hand or foot in closing doors. (T-0)
Employees will not use elevators during emergency evacuation. (T-0)
2.5.6.3. Where an elevator or escalator is operating unsafely or has visible damage, do not
use it until inspected by a certified inspector. (T-1) UFC 3-490-06, Elevators, ASME
A17.1, Safety Code for Elevators and Escalators, and ASME A17.2, Guide for Inspection
of Elevators, Escalators and Moving Walk, provides additional information.
2.5.7. Facility floors, especially raised floors, will be kept smooth, clean and free of
obstructions and slippery materials. (T-0)
2.5.8. Raised floor spaces will be divided in the same manner as the equipment spaces above
the area they serve. (T-0)
2.5.9. Raised Floors of Aircraft Flight Training Devices, Similar High Value Training Devices
and ADPE Facilities. Raised floor spaces will contain a standard or ultra-sensitive smoke
detection system as required by UFC 3-600-01. (T-0)
2.5.10. Raised floor systems will be constructed of noncombustible materials. (T-0)
2.5.11. Floors will not be cleaned with flammable or combustible liquids. (T-0) Follow the
manufacturers instruction and SDS guidelines when using cleaning agents for floors and
facilities.
2.5.12. Sub-floors will be inspected once each operational day to ensure no flooding or leaking
is present unless the sub-floor area is provided with an automatic water leakage detection
system; and clean out trash or dirt accumulation, as needed. (T-1) Note: Any change in facility
or room use requires reassessment of the cleaning schedule.
2.5.13. Utility trenches will be kept clean of foreign objects and inspected at least every 60
days. (T-1) If cleaning history shows a longer period between cleanings is feasible, the Fire
and Emergency Services (F&ES) Flight may allow extending the time between cleanings.

2.6. Ergonomics. Ergonomics attempt to fit the job to the person, rather than forcing the person
to fit the job. Adapting the job to the employee helps reduce stress and eliminates many potential
injuries and disorders from overuse of muscles, poor posture, and repetitive motion. Ergonomic
hazards may include improperly adjusted workstations and/or chairs, poor posture, frequent lifting,
vibration, repetitive motion, or awkward movement. Contact the installation BE office for
questions relating to ergonomics or to have an ergonomic assessment completed. Refer to OSHA
website:
https://www.osha.gov/SLTC/ergonomics/controlhazards.html the USAFSAM
ESOH Service Center: https:/hpws.afrl.af.mil/dhp/OE/ESOHSC/pages/index.cfm?id=646 for
additional guidance.
2.7. Roll-up Doors. Roll-up doors will be maintained in good operating condition with door
tracks free of debris and obstructions to allow smooth operation. (T-1) Operators will conduct a
visual inspection of door path and trackway and correct any discrepancies before operating door.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

27

(T-1) Roll-up doors will also receive an annual inspection per manufacturer instructions. (T-1)
Individuals will remain clear of door path while door is in motion. (T-1)
2.7.1. Operators ensure auxiliary service roll-up doors, to include tow vehicle exit doors, meet
the following requirements:
2.7.1.1. Be able to open a minimum of 8 feet or 70 percent of maximum opening height,
whichever is greater. (T-1)
2.7.1.2. Powered roll-up doors equipped with one touch open or close buttons will have a
crush sensor on the bottom of the door installed and operational, or photoelectric sensors
installed and operational no higher than six inches above the floor. (T-1) Note: Powered
roll-up doors equipped with dead man switches do not require crush sensors.
2.7.1.3. Controls for the door will be located so there is an unobstructed view during door
operation. (T-1)
2.7.1.4. Motion alarm will sound on the closing cycle of powered roll-up doors, unless
equipped with a dead-man switch. (T-1) Note: This requirement does not affect powered
roll-up doors installed prior to January 2016. However, this requirement must be met when
the doors are modified or replaced. (T-1)
2.7.2. Prior to a vehicle entering or exiting a facility, drivers will sound their horn to alert
personnel. (T-0)
2.8. Receiving/Loading Docks. Refer to paragraph 7.1.9.6 information regarding loading
docks.
2.8.1. Refer to paragraph 7.1.9.6.1 for information regarding dock surfaces.
2.8.2. Employees will be instructed in proper handling techniques for the equipment and
materials received. (T-0) Supervisors will periodically evaluate and advise employees on
proper lifting techniques. (T-1) The occupational safety office will be consulted in the planning
stages of a new facility design to minimize material handling hazards. (T-1)
2.8.3. Employees will receive training on the use of any mechanical leveling equipment to
adjust docks to ease loading and unloading of materials. (T-1)
2.9. Walk-in Refrigerators and Freezers. A bypass or other type device will be installed on the
door of a walk-in refrigerator or freezer to permit escape from inside if an employee is locked
inside. (T-0) An internally activated alarm bell will be provided and tested weekly and
documented. (T-1) If the bell is inoperable, it will be replaced immediately. (T-1) When a door
can be locked from the outside, e.g., with a hasp and padlock, a permanent sign stating, “Warning
– Determine No One Is Inside Before Locking Door,” will be mounted on the door’s exterior.
(T-1) The letters will be IAW 29 CFR § 1910.145, Specifications for Accident Prevention Signs
and Tags. (T-0)
2.10. Manual Material Handling and Lifting Techniques. Accomplish a hazard assessment to
identify risks to employees or property and methods to mitigate these hazards, prior to moving
equipment or furniture. (T-1)
2.10.1. Possible Exposures to Hazards. Some possible exposures to hazards include, but are
not limited to:

28

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
2.10.1.1. Movable or detachable parts, such as doors or drawers which must be secured
properly prior to moving. (T-1)
2.10.1.2. Remove doors or drawers to lighten load and to prevent shifting or falling
hazards. (T-1)
2.10.1.3. Secure cords, communication cables or other gear attached to the item to prevent
tripping or snagging hazards. (T-1)
2.10.1.4. For large or difficult to handle items, secure the load to the hand-truck or dolly
with a cargo strap or other fastening device. (T-1)
2.10.1.5. Paths of travel must be evaluated for conditions such as slopes, carpeting or other
irregularities to ensure safe movement of items. (T-1)
2.10.1.6. Ensure pathways are cleared and the object being moved will fit through
doorways or other required openings. (T-1)
2.10.1.7. Use more than one person if moving the load up or down slopes or stairs. (T-1)
Note: Team lifting will be used when items are considered too heavy for one person or
when items are lifted from an awkward position or location. (T-1)
2.10.1.8. Choose the proper sized and designed dolly for the job.
2.10.1.9. If the use of an elevator is required, the size and weight of the item and
manufacturer’s loading and weight limit requirements must be considered. (T-1)
2.10.1.10. Evaluate the need for PPE. (T-0)
2.10.2. Lifting and Carrying. Several variables influence an employee’s ability to manually
handle and lift materials. Proper consideration and knowledge of employee limitations and the
use of correct lifting and carrying techniques will reduce the possibility of injury. Consider the
following issues that affect employee safety while lifting:
2.10.2.1. Physical characteristics include factors such as strength, mobility, fatigue, and
motor functions of the individual.
2.10.2.2. Psychological considerations include the motivation, emotional state, job
satisfaction and attitude of the individual toward work.
2.10.2.3. Pre-existing employee injuries should also be considered, if known.
2.10.2.4. Task variables include the weight, size, shape, distribution, degree of shift (of
the load in the container), and the location of the center of gravity of the load to be handled.
2.10.2.5. Workplace layout and the degree of movement required, obstacles, distances
moved, and direction of movement will be considered.
2.10.2.6. Level of demand to include frequency of lift, duration of lifting task,
accelerations and velocities of lift, shift duration, degree of precision and relative
proportion of muscles involved in the lift will be considered.
2.10.2.7. Environmental variables such as heat and cold stress, noise and vibration,
lighting, toxic agents, traction, stability of the work platform and atmospheric contaminants
will also be considered.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

29

2.10.3. Training. Supervisors will ensure their employees receive thorough training on proper
manual lifting techniques both routine or high risk and any PPE required. (T-0) In addition,
employees will be trained in the use of available manual lifting devices. (T-0) To the new
employee, hand trucks and wheelbarrows look deceptively easy to use. Supervisors will
instruct employees on their use. (T-0) Improvements in work methods, eliminating manual
material handling hazards and understanding the stresses involved during manual handling are
important and need to be addressed during training. This training will include both verbal and
written materials that explain how to do the task correctly with practice and proper motions.
(T-1) Training will be documented IAW DAFI 91-202. (T-1) Additional information on
establishing a program may be found in this chapter and in the National Safety Council (NSC)
Accident Prevention Manual for Industrial Operations, Engineering, and Technology.
Supervisors may contact the Public Health (PH) office for assistance in developing the program
and to review the NSC publication. The supervisor will consider having personnel evaluated
for physical fitness for duty when job tasks require continuous manual lifting. (T-1) Employees
with physical profile changes will be evaluated for possible reassignment to prevent recurring
injuries until cleared by medical authority. (T-1)
2.10.4. Minimizing Manual Material Handling Hazards.
2.10.4.1. Engineering Controls. The preferred method to minimize risks of manual lifting
is the use of engineering controls/mechanical assists to decrease force, repetition, distance
of travel and frequency of manual handling activities. Some examples include the use of
scissor tables, elevators, conveyors, and gravity chutes.
2.10.4.2. Administrative Controls. Job rotation schedules and mandatory work-rest cycles
can be used to reduce mishap potential, but do not eliminate the hazard and are not as
reliable as engineering controls.
2.10.4.3. Work Design Principles. Conduct a JHA to identify potential hazards and, when
practical, arrange tasks and select workstations using the following principles: (Note: A
JHA is not required when existing guidance adequately covers all safety requirements of
an operation or process.)
2.10.4.3.1. Place objects to be lifted at the approximate height of the knuckles when
the arms dangle at the side of the body.
2.10.4.3.2. Place objects as close to the body as possible.
2.10.4.3.3. Do not twist the back while picking up, putting down, carrying, or changing
direction with an object. When rotating, rotate with the hip joint. Crouch down while
keeping the object close in to lower the object.
2.10.4.3.4. Limit stack height to shoulder level. If items must be stacked higher,
provide step-up access to eliminate lifting above shoulder level.
2.10.4.3.5. Use grips, handles and other devices to provide better control of items.
2.10.4.3.6. Slide materials instead of lifting, whenever possible.
2.10.4.3.7. Use gravity assist in moving materials.
2.10.4.3.8. Ensure adequate maneuvering space to eliminate the need to twist the body.

30

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
2.10.4.3.9. Consider team lifting when the person doing the lifting (or their supervisor)
considers the item to be too heavy or awkward to lift safely, or the item or lifter is in
an awkward position or location. Refer to paragraph 2.10.8 for additional guidance
on team lifting.
2.10.5. Proper Lifting Methods. No single technique exists to prevent injury during lifting and
material handling. The best prevention strategy is to utilize effective ergonomic techniques,
i.e., ensure workstations are properly designed, loads are manageable in both size and weight
distribution, the frequency and duration of lifting are not excessively stressful, and employees
can demonstrate a knowledge of proper techniques for material handling. There are three basic
methods of lifting: straight back-bent knees, free style and kinetic. Each has advantages and
limitations. Refer to Figure 2.1 for an illustration of proper lifting technique.

Figure 2.1. How to Lift Properly.

2.10.6. Kinetic Method. The kinetic method is the most widely accepted and taught because
it provides more stability for the employee while reducing load on the back muscles and
intervertebral disks.
2.10.6.1. Inspect the object before lifting to make certain no grease or slippery substance
will cause the object to slip. Also inspect the object for slivers, sharp edges and rough or
slippery surfaces before attempting to lift.
2.10.6.2. Position feet correctly. Place feet far enough apart for balance with one foot to
the rear of the object and the other foot slightly ahead of the other foot and to the side of
the object.
2.10.6.3. Crouch close to the load. Stay close to the load to minimize strain on the muscles.
2.10.6.4. Always keep the back as straight as possible. It may not be possible to keep the
back in the vertical plane but avoid arching the back. Bend from the hips and not from the
middle of the back.
2.10.6.5. Pick up materials with a full palm grip. Do not attempt to pick up items using a
fingertip grip. Gloves (leather or leather-palmed) will be worn when lifting objects which
have sharp or burred edges, or splintered surfaces.
2.10.6.6. With the arms, slide the object towards the body, putting it in motion (kinetic
energy). At the same time, lift the object with the legs and bring the back to a vertical
position. Keep the object close to the body; avoid twisting while lifting.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

31

2.10.7. Setting the Object Down. Use the same motion as when lifting but reverse it to set an
object down. Lower the load by bending the legs and crouching with the back straight. Take
care when releasing the load to prevent injury to fingers, hands, or feet.
2.10.8. Team Lifting. Always obtain assistance when it is necessary to move heavy or
unusually shaped items manually. Assign additional employees to the task when it is not
practical to use mechanical equipment. When two or more people are required to move or carry
an object, adjust the load so each person carries an equal part. If possible, use employees
similar in size and train them in team-lifting. Employees need to understand that if one
employee lifts too soon, shifts the load, or lowers improperly, that person or their partner(s)
may be overloaded and strained. Test lifts should be made before proceeding. The key to lifts
using two or more people is to make every move in unison. Assign one person to give orders
to ensure the necessary coordination for movement. The supervisor and employees are
responsible for assessing all available methods to safely handle materials and for using
mechanical assistance whenever possible.
2.10.9. Carrying Methods. Acceptable carrying methods differ based upon the type of
material, distance, and number of employees. Employees should be instructed during initial
training in each procedure. For example: neck, shoulder, side, tray, two-person, under-arm
carrying methods, etc.
2.10.9.1. Points to remember:
2.10.9.1.1. Use appropriate PPE for each task, such as gloves and protective footwear,
as required by paragraph 14.3.2.
2.10.9.1.2. Keep fingers away from pinch and shear points.
2.10.9.1.3. Make sure the path of travel is clear.
2.10.9.1.4. Do not carry a load that obstructs the view of the direction of travel.
2.10.9.1.5. Do not twist with the back to change direction or put an object down. When
rotating, rotate with the hip joint. Crouch down while keeping the object close in to
lower the object.
2.10.9.2. Neck Carry Method. In this method, the material is carried on the back of the
neck and shoulders with both arms lifted to shoulder height and the hands grasping the
sides or top of the load.
2.10.9.3. Shoulder Carry Method. The load rests on one shoulder with the near arm helping
to support the weight and the other arm brought across the body to steady the load.
2.10.9.4. Side Hand Carry Method. This is simply the suitcase method and can be used
only where the load may be grasped by a handle or equivalent.
2.10.9.5. Tray Carry Method. The material is carried in front of the abdomen with both
hands placed under the bottom surface and the body serving as the balancer.
2.10.9.6. Two-Person Carry Method. A load is carried by two people who use oral signals
to coordinate their movements. They decide on appropriate emergency signals to be given
if the grasp of either person should slip. They walk in step to coordinate their movements.

32

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
2.10.9.7. Underarm Carry Method. The material is carried under one arm with the other
arm brought across in front of the body to steady and balance the load. Also called side
underarm carry method.
2.10.10. Carrying Items Up or Down Stairways.
2.10.10.1. Adhere to the guidance provided by the supervisor.
2.10.10.2. Try to reduce the bulk or size of the object carried to allow for maximum
visibility.
2.10.10.3. Use assistance when required and available.
2.10.10.4. Use mechanical material handling equipment whenever loads are too heavy or
bulky to be lifted or carried efficiently or safely by hand.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

33
Chapter 3

WEATHER SAFETY
3.1. Responsibilities.
3.1.1. The supporting Air Force weather organization is responsible for making initial
notifications of adverse or mission-limiting weather conditions to predetermined support
agencies as defined by local weather support documents and IAW DAFI 15-129, Air and Space
Weather Operations. Note: Adverse or mission-limiting weather conditions include strong and
damaging surface winds, heavy rain, thunderstorms, hail, snow, and freezing precipitation.
3.1.2. Each DAF installation will develop local procedures to ensure key personnel and
agencies involved in weather-impacted high-risk activities and operations are notified
according to the installation weather support documents. (T-1) Examples include, but are not
limited to, agencies with aircraft, petroleum/oil/lubricant (POL) facilities, opened air work,
recreational activities, explosive loading, aircraft maintenance and utilities work. Key
personnel, in turn, will advise all on-duty supervisors and employees to take proper
precautions. (T-1)
3.1.3. Each installation will employ a lightning safety program with a two-tier notification
system to minimize personnel exposure to lightning hazards. (T-0)
3.2. Two-tier Notification System.
3.2.1. A Lightning Watch is in effect 30 minutes prior to thunderstorms being within a 5
nautical mile radius of any predetermined location or activity as forecasted by the supporting
Air Force weather organization. Weather personnel will not cancel a Lightning Watch if the
potential for thunderstorms remain within 5 nautical miles of the installation or activity within
30 minutes. (T-1) However, all personnel must be prepared to implement Lightning Warning
procedures without delay. (T-1) Be alert for any lightning activity, to include thunder, and
advise supervisory personnel of any observations. If lightning does not occur within 5 nautical
miles radius at the valid (forecast) time of the Lightning Watch, the supporting weather
organization will reassess the Lightning Watch and amend or cancel the watch as required
IAW DAFI 15-129. (T-1) Note: During a Lightning Watch, non-explosive operations or
activities may continue.
3.2.2. A Lightning Warning is in effect when lightning occurs within a five nautical miles
radius of the predetermined locations and activities. Personnel in affected locations or engaged
in affected activities will cease all outside activity and seek shelter. (T-1) Recommended
locations that provide safe shelter and locations to avoid are listed in paragraph 3.3. Lightning
warnings will be cancelled by the supporting DAF weather organization that initiated the
warning. (T-1)
3.3. General Lightning Safety for all DAF Activities and Operations. Note: This includes
AF-led joint bases.
3.3.1. When lightning is detected or observed within the immediate vicinity of any activity or
operation, do not go outdoors or remain out unless it is necessary. Seek shelter in:
3.3.1.1. Dwellings or other buildings that are protected against lightning.

34

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
3.3.1.2. Protected underground shelters.
3.3.1.3. Large metal framed buildings.
3.3.1.4. Enclosed automobiles, buses, aircraft and other vehicles with metal tops and
bodies.
3.3.2. Certain locations are extremely hazardous during thunderstorms and the following
locations will be avoided:
3.3.2.1. Hilltops and ridges. (T-1)
3.3.2.2. Areas on top of buildings. (T-1)
3.3.2.3. Open fields, athletic fields, golf courses. (T-1)
3.3.2.4. Parking lots, tennis/outdoor multi-purpose athletic courts. (T-1)
3.3.2.5. Swimming pools (indoor and outdoor), lakes and seashores. (T-1)
3.3.2.6. Near wire fences, power and telephone poles, clotheslines, overhead wires,
towers, and railroad tracks. (T-1)
3.3.2.7. Under isolated trees. (T-1)
3.3.2.8. Near electrical appliances, telephones, plumbing fixtures, and metal or electrically
conductive objects. (T-1)
3.3.2.9. Aircraft dry bays, tanks, and wheel wells. (T-1)
3.3.3. All aircraft fuel service maintenance activities, including liquid oxygen (LOX)
servicing, will cease whenever a Lightning Warning is in effect. (T-0) Note: Refer to DAFI
23-201, Fuels Management, for additional guidance.
3.3.4. Locations with nuclear weapons will request notification of lightning within 10 miles
of their location from their supporting DAF weather organization and follow the cessation and
resumption of operations for lightning environments when necessary IAW DoDM 3150.02,
DoD Nuclear Weapon System Safety Program. (T-0) Local procedures for these advisories are
documented in DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201, Explosives Safety Standards. (T-0)
3.3.5. Controlled termination for explosives operations when lightning is forecasted to occur
in 30 minutes or within 5 nautical miles will follow procedures as outlined in DESR
6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201. (T-0)
3.3.6. When working indoors during lightning storms, keep all doors closed. (T-1) Where
doors must remain open for operations, a formal risk assessment will be conducted. (T-1)

3.4. Snow Safety for all Department of the Air Force (DAF) Activities and Operations.
3.4.1. Refer to Chapters 2, 7, 16, 24 and 30 for specific information concerning hazards and
controls when dealing with snow accumulation on walking-working surfaces and during
vehicle operations.
3.4.2. Snow Loads on Roofs. Some locations may experience significant snowfall. Heavy
snowfall can generate increased snow loads on rooftops that can create hazards requiring
special attention. Snow loading exceeding the facilities’ designed snow load can create

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

35

hazardous conditions in facilities that must be addressed. Facility managers will contact
installation CE to ensure a snow plan addressing snow loads on roofs is in place. (T-1)
3.4.3. Refer to Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Pamphlet 957, FEMA Snow
Load Safety Guidance, for more information.

36

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 4
SERVICES OPERATIONS

4.1. Purpose. This chapter covers the safety requirements for services operations, e.g., laundry,
lodging and food services. Refer to DAFI/AFI 34-series publications for services operations
requirements such as fitness centers, sport fields, rental equipment, bowling centers, auto hobby
shops, etc.
4.2. Laundry. Laundry operations in the DAF applies to installation facilities including billeting
laundries and laundry facilities in shops/workplaces. Medical Treatment Facilities laundries will
meet the requirements of NFPA 99, Health Care Facilities Code, and Joint Commission
Accreditation Manuals. (T-0)
4.2.1. Supervisors will conduct monthly inspections of laundries and dry-cleaning facilities
for hazards or deficiencies using a checklist containing requirements of this chapter and
manufacturer’s instructions, as required. (T-2) Copies of these inspections will be maintained
on file for a rolling year. (T-2)
4.2.2. Facility Requirements.
4.2.2.1. Laundries and dry-cleaning plants will have separate areas designated for
receiving and issue. (T-0)
4.2.2.2. Facilities located more than three to four minutes from a medical facility must
provide first aid kits. (T-0) Note: Remote location is defined as emergency care that is
more than three to four minutes from the workplace.
4.2.3. The F&ES Flight will be immediately notified of any flammable solvent spills. (T-0)
Contact installation CE for guidance on spill procedures and installation BE for health risk
assessment (HRA) and recommendations for personnel protection measures. (T-0)
4.2.4. Machinery producing potentially hazardous noise and/or vibration levels will be
identified, and control measures initiated IAW installation BE’s occupational and
environmental health risk assessment guidance. (T-0)
4.2.5. Hazardous and flammable materials used in laundry and dry-cleaning processes will be
stored IAW Chapter 22. (T-0)
4.2.6. Floors in dry-cleaning rooms will be of fire-resistant construction with a
noncombustible and solvent-resistant surface. (T-0)
4.2.7. Follow manufacturers guidance on cleaning lint from dryers and hoses to prevent fires.
(T-2)
4.3. Lodging.
4.3.1. Some lodging supply operations require forklift trucks. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.178,
Powered Industrial Trucks, DAFI 24-301, Ground Transportation, and Chapter 12 for
additional guidance.
4.3.2. Kitchen Cleaning.
4.3.2.1. As needed, grease will be cleaned from burners and ranges to prevent fires. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

37

4.3.2.2. Unplug heat producing appliances such as irons and coffee makers prior to
cleaning or when not in use. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 6 for additional guidance.
4.3.2.3. Microwave ovens will be cleaned daily. (T-0) Microwave ovens with broken
doors or latches will be removed from service so personnel will not be exposed to
microwave energy. (T-0)
4.4. Food Service Operations.
4.4.1. Walk-in Refrigerators and Freezers. Refer to paragraph 2.9 for additional guidance.
4.4.2. Food Preparation Area.
4.4.2.1. Movable parts of equipment will be properly guarded. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 11
for additional guidance.
4.4.2.2. Knives and cutting attachments will be safeguarded when not in use. (T-0)
4.4.2.3. Hoods and Ducting. Hood filters over grease vapor producing activities, e.g., deep
fat fryers and grills, will be washed at least daily. (T-1) Hood filters in other areas will be
washed at least weekly. (T-1) Ducting will be cleaned as necessary but at least once every
six months. (T-1) Cleaning of filters and ducts will be documented IAW local procedures.
(T-1) Cooking will not be permitted under hoods without properly installed filters or when
the fire extinguishing system is not operating. (T-1) Exhaust hoods will be vented outside
the facility. (T-1) Refer to NFPA 96, Standard for Ventilation Control and Fire Protection
of Commercial Cooking Operations, for additional guidance. Exception: Cleaning of hood
and exhaust ducts used for food service equipment may be extended to every two years if
a dining facility prepares less than 10 meals per month.
4.4.3. Machinery/Equipment. Supervisors will maintain manufacturer’s instructions for all
machinery or equipment under their control or develop local operating instructions (OI), to
include job safety, maintenance, lubrication, inspection and identify operator and maintenance
technician responsibilities. (T-1) Supervisors may use manufacturer’s instructions to develop
OIs or lesson plans to train operators on requirements of specific machinery. Refer to 29 CFR
§ 1910.212, General Requirements for All Machines, and 29 CFR § 1910.263, Bakery
Equipment, for additional guidance on machinery. Equipment connected by flexible cords, e.g.,
extension cords, will be disconnected when not in use for extended periods of time or at the
end of the work shift. (T-1)
4.4.4. Fire Prevention. Refer to Chapter 6 and NFPA 96 for guidance and information on
food service facilities.

38

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 5
NONDESTRUCTIVE INSPECTION (NDI) AND OIL ANALYSIS

5.1. Scope.
5.1.1. Nondestructive Inspection (NDI). The purpose of NDI is to employ inspection methods
such as magnetic particle, eddy current, liquid fluorescent penetrant, ultrasonic and
radiographic to detect cracks or flaws, measure geometric characteristics or determine material
structure or composition of equipment. Refer to TO 33B-1-1, Nondestructive Inspection
Methods, Basic Theory, for additional guidance.
5.1.2. Oil Analysis Program. The purpose of the oil analysis program is to measure and
evaluate metal property results by interpreting oil analysis samples. Most samples are from
engine oil on special or routine aeronautical flights but may also include special and routine
non-aeronautical equipment from other fluid lubricated mechanical systems, such as
equipment from other Services. Refer to TO 33-1-37-1, Joint Oil Analysis Program Manual,
for additional guidance.
5.2. Safety Precautions.
5.2.1. Ultraviolet Radiation. Unfiltered ultraviolet radiation can be harmful to the eyes and
skin. Black light bulbs will not be operated without proper lenses and/or filters. (T-0) Black
lights can severely burn hands, arms, face, or other exposed body areas and ignite fuel vapors.
Black lights will not be operated when flammable vapors are present. (T-0)
5.2.2. Ionizing Radiation and X-ray Equipment. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.1096, Ionizing
Radiation, DAFMAN 48-125, Personnel Ionizing Radiation Dosimetry, AFMAN 48-148, 21
CFR § Parts 1020.30 through 1020.33, TO 33B-1-1, and TO 33B-1-2, Nondestructive
Inspection General Procedures and Process Controls, for additional guidance.
5.2.3. Electrical Safety. NDI equipment to be used in hazardous areas will meet NFPA 70
requirements. (T-0)
5.2.4. Compressed Gases. Compressed gases commonly used in NDI and/or oil analysis
program laboratories are nitrous oxide, acetylene, and sulfur hexafluoride. Care and attention
must be given to handling, storage, marking and disposition of all compressed gas cylinders.
(T-1)
5.2.5. Housekeeping. Good housekeeping is essential to safe operations in the NDI and oil
analysis program laboratories. Soiled rags, paper towels, craft paper and other trash
contaminated with oil or grease will be placed in self-closing metal containers plainly marked
to indicate contents. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 2.4 for additional guidance on housekeeping.
5.2.6. Ventilation Systems. Only basic guidelines for NDI and/or oil analysis program
laboratory ventilation are provided in this publication.
5.2.6.1. Surface area exhaust ventilation evaluations will be conducted at least annually.
(T-0)
5.2.6.2. Ventilation evaluation frequency is determined by installation BE’s HRA. As a
minimum, areas that may require an evaluation for adequate ventilation include cleaning
areas, penetrant tanks, remover tanks, penetrant inspection viewing booths, stationary

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

39

magnetic particle units, film processing rooms and the oil analysis program work area.
Ventilation for oil analysis program spectrometers will comply with TO and
manufacturer’s requirements. (T-1) Refer to installation BE’s occupational health
assessment (OHA) IAW DAFMAN 48-146, Occupational Health Program Management.
Note: Additional reference information can be found in American Conference of
Governmental Industrial Hygienists’ (ACGIH) Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of
Recommended Practice for Operations and Maintenance.
5.3. Supervisor Responsibilities. Supervisors will:
5.3.1. Ensure personnel are properly trained on NDI and oil analysis procedures prior to being
authorized to accomplish NDI and oil analysis work. (T-1)
5.3.2. Ensure used oil and cleaning rags do not accumulate in the oil analysis program lab.
(T-1) Used oil, not to exceed five gallons, may be stored in a metal or plastic container having
a sealed, tight-fitting cover and labeled as “Used Oil.”. Used cleaning rags and towels will be
removed from the lab at the end of each shift. (T-1)
5.4. NDI Equipment and Fire Protection Systems. Contact the F&ES Flight prior to operating
equipment which utilizes black lights, X-rays, ultraviolet and/or infrared rays. Some fire
suppression systems are actuated by ultraviolet or infrared detectors and must be disabled, or other
management actions taken, prior to using this NDI equipment to preclude actuation of the fire
suppression systems. (T-1)

40

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 6
FIRE PREVENTION AND PROTECTION

6.1. Fire Extinguishers.
6.1.1. Hazards. The maintenance and operation of portable fire extinguishers and fire
suppression systems may involve exposure to toxic fire extinguishing agents and by-products
of combustion. Fires produce toxic by-products of combustion, consume oxygen, and produce
high temperatures which create areas immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH). It is
important personnel receive training in the proper use of available portable fire extinguishers
when required in the Emergency Action Plan (EAP) to use extinguishers. Employers will
receive fire education safety on preventing fires, protecting themselves and evacuation plans.
Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.157(a) for additional guidance on the use of portable fire extinguishers.
Coordination and approval from F&ES Flight are required.
6.1.2. Inspections, Testing and Maintenance. Fire detection, suppression, and alarm systems,
including fire hydrants, are subject to failure unless inspection, test, and maintenance programs
are conducted IAW UFC 3-601-02, Fire Protection Systems Inspection, Testing, and
Maintenance. When a fire protection system is out of service follow procedures IAW DAFI
32-2001, Fire and Emergency Services (F&ES) Program. (T-1)
6.1.3. The National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 implemented
requirements for all DoD facilities to have portable fire extinguishers. Portable fire
extinguishers will be mounted as required in Figure 6.1. (T-0)
Figure 6.1. AFCEC Guide to the Provision of Organizational Portable Fire Extinguishers
