6.2. General Requirements.
6.2.1. Facility Managers/Supervisors will:
6.2.1.1. Establish and maintain a fire prevention training program via the JSTO to ensure
employees are trained and understand their fire prevention and protection responsibilities
in their work areas IAW 29 CFR § 1910.157, Portable Fire Extinguishers. (T-0) This
requirement will be fulfilled through job safety training and a documentation process IAW
DAFI 91-202. (T-3)
6.2.1.2. If further guidance is required, consult the F&ES Flight. (T-1) Note: Building
evacuation procedures in Services facilities may be practiced without actual participation
of patrons, with the prior approval of the F&ES Flight Chief.
6.2.1.3. Make sure the facility is in a fire-safe condition at the close of business. (T-1)
6.2.1.4. Conduct and document daily closing inspections. (T-3)
6.2.1.5. Coordinate approval with the F&ES Flight concerning all social events when
temporary decorations or unusual arrangements are anticipated. (T-1)
6.2.1.6. Emergency Lighting. Emergency lighting will be provided IAW NFPA 101. (T-0)
6.2.2. Server rooms, e.g., information technology or data management rooms, located below
floors equipped with water sprinkler systems, are susceptible to water damage if the sprinkler
systems are actuated. Managers will provide a means of protecting data processing equipment
in the event of a water leak or sprinkler activation (time permitting). (T-0) This may be
accomplished by strategically locating pre-cut pieces of plastic sheeting within server rooms.
In the event of a water leak or spill, equipment will be shut down before it is covered with
plastic sheeting. (T-0) This will reduce the possibility of the equipment overheating and
catching fire. The plastic sheeting will be removed before restarting equipment. (T-0)
6.2.3. To determine if a water hazard exists, request a survey of each server room by
installation CE. (T-1) The building custodian will keep the completed survey on file and brief
incoming personnel during initial safety orientation. (T-1)
6.2.4. Commercial Food Service Facilities. Commercial food service facilities will be
maintained in a safe condition to prevent the loss of DAF resources. (T-1) The F&ES Flight
will inspect grease and heat removal hoods, ducts, and exhausts at all food service facilities.
(T-1) The F&ES inspection time and the contractor cleaning frequency will be approved by
the fire chief. (T-1) The commander will appoint in writing a Quality Assurance Evaluator for
cleaning, maintenance and testing conducted under a service contract. (T-3) The Quality
Assurance Evaluator function is usually accomplished by the facility manager. (T-3)
6.2.5. Health Care Facilities. Facility managers and/or safety officers of health care
occupancies will maintain a written fire plan that addresses appropriate staff response to a fire
emergency. (T-0) Appropriate education and training will be provided to employees
concerning all elements of the fire plan. (T-0) The plan will address facility-wide fire response
and evacuation procedures for all staff, employees, and volunteers, and appropriate training

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

43

for facility occupants required to participate in the fire plan. (T-0) The fire chief or designee
will approve the fire plan. (T-3)
6.2.6. Portable Fire Extinguishers.
6.2.6.1. F&ES Flight. The F&ES Flight oversees organizational compliance of the base
fire extinguisher program and is the authority for all matters involving fire extinguishers.
The F&ES Flight will provide facility managers with information on facility fire
extinguisher requirements outlined in NFPA standards and as required by UFC 3-600-01.
(T-1)
6.2.6.2. Extinguishers are not normally provided for military family housing. Should
MAJCOMs, FLDCOMs, DRUs, or FOAs direct installation of extinguishers in military
family housing, procedures will be developed to ensure occupants are trained in their use
and extinguishers are maintained. (T-3)
6.2.6.3. Fire Extinguisher Location. The F&ES Flight will approve the location of all fire
extinguishers. (T-3)
6.2.6.4. Fire Extinguisher Purchase and Maintenance. Facility managers and using
organizations will budget for the purchase and maintenance of fire extinguishers. (T-3)
Note: 150-pound flightline fire extinguisher programs are maintained under the Aircraft
Maintenance Squadrons or Generation Squadrons as required per HAF Mission Directive
(HAFMD) 1-38, Deputy Chief of Staff, Logistics, Engineering and Force Protection.
6.2.6.5. All fire extinguishers will meet the requirements of NFPA 10, Standard for
Portable Fire Extinguisher. (T-1) Note: TO 13F4-4-121, Fire Extinguisher, Wheeled
Liquified Gas, 150 LB Capacity, and NFPA 10 are to be used for maintenance and
inspection of wheeled flightline fire extinguishers.
6.2.6.6. Employee Responsibilities. All employees are responsible for fire extinguishers
in their work areas. Employees will ensure fire extinguishers are readily accessible with
unobstructed access and are used only for the intended purpose. (T-0) F&ES Flights will
be notified of any fire extinguisher discharge, accidental or intentional. (T-3)
6.2.6.7. Supervisor Responsibilities. Supervisors are responsible for fire extinguishers in
areas under their control. Supervisors will:
6.2.6.7.1. Perform visual inspections of fire extinguishers. (T-0)
6.2.6.7.2. Take defective extinguishers to an authorized servicing location, as
determined locally, and coordinated with the F&ES Flight, for services. (T-0) Fire
extinguishers removed from service will be immediately replaced. (T-1)
6.2.6.7.3. Mount fire extinguishers in or on vehicles or equipment in approved
brackets, or otherwise secure them, to prevent damage as directed by TO or other
directive. (T-0) Note: Vehicle fire extinguishers will only be purchased with the
approval of Vehicle Management to ensure the vehicle TOs and AFIs require the
vehicle to carry fire extinguishers. (T-1)
6.2.6.8. Visual Inspection. Managers and supervisors at all levels will ensure a visual
inspection is performed and documented monthly for all fire extinguishers under their

44

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
control. (T-0) Extinguishers with discrepancies will be removed from service and replaced
immediately until repaired. (T-0) This inspection includes:
6.2.6.8.1. Extinguisher located in a designated place.
6.2.6.8.2. No obstruction to access or visibility.
6.2.6.8.3. Instructions on name plates are legible and facing outward.
6.2.6.8.4. Safety seals and/or tamper indicators are not broken or missing.
6.2.6.8.5. No obvious physical damage, corrosion, leakage, or clogged nozzle.
6.2.6.8.6. Pressure gauge reading or indicator in the operable range or position.
6.2.6.8.7. For wheeled units, the condition of the tires, wheels, carriage, hose and
nozzle checked.
6.2.6.8.8. Fullness determined by weighing or “hefting.”
6.2.7. Space Heaters.
6.2.7.1. Use Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory (NRTL)-listed or host nation
equivalent space heaters equipped with automatic shutoff devices that will shut off the
heater if it tips over. Space heaters will also have an overheat protection device, an
automatic thermostat control and will be disconnected when not in use. (T-3) Note: Space
heaters will be approved by the facility manager prior to use. (T-3) For a current list of
OSHA-recognized NRTLs, visit https://www.osha.gov/dts/otpca/nrtl/nrtllist.html.
6.2.7.2. Plug space heaters directly into a wall receptacle, not into extension cords or
multiple outlet strips. (T-0)
6.2.7.3. Maintain a 36-inch minimum distance or IAW manufacturer’s instructions
between any space heater and combustibles. Note: The more restrictive guidance will
apply. (T-3)
6.2.7.4. Do not leave these devices unattended when in use and unplug them at the end of
the day or shift. (T-3)
6.2.7.5. The use of space heaters in any hazardous location as defined by NFPA 70,
National Electric Code, is prohibited. (T-1)
6.2.8. Coffee Makers.
6.2.8.1. Keep coffee makers and the area around them free of paper products and other
loose combustibles. (T-1)
6.2.8.2. Follow manufacturer’s instructions and consult with facility manager prior to
placement of coffee makers.
6.2.8.3. Unplug coffee makers at the end of the day. Note: Coffee makers/machines with
integral timers and single serve coffee makers do not need to be unplugged, e.g., Bunntype coffee brewers and Keurig.
6.2.9. Heating Systems.
6.2.9.1. Heating devices, such as furnaces or heaters, must be kept clear of combustible
and flammable material. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

45

6.2.9.2. All heating, boiler, air conditioning, air handling and mechanical rooms will be
secured. (T-0) Only the F&ES Flight, installation CE or authorized personnel will have
access to these areas. (T-3)
6.2.10. Decorations.
6.2.10.1. Electric string lights and wiring recognized by an NRTL are authorized. (T-0)
6.2.10.2. Unplug or switch off all electrical decorations when the work area is unoccupied.
(T-3)
6.2.10.3. Decorations will be noncombustible or fire retardant. (T-1) Use of an open flame
is prohibited. (T-1)
6.2.11. Fire Alarm Systems. Supervisors will ensure all personnel are familiar with the fire
alarm systems, know how to report an emergency and how to activate the fire alarm system.
(T-0) At no time will a fire detection, suppression or alarm system or device be painted,
blocked, or obstructed in any manner. (T-1)
6.2.12. Emergency Evacuation Procedures.
6.2.12.1. Supervisors will establish an EAP. (T-0) Note: Employees will know primary
and secondary escape routes for every area of the building. (T-1)
6.2.12.2. Consult the F&ES Flight to determine requirements for practicing fire evacuation
drills. Supervisors ensure new personnel are trained in evacuation procedures. (T-3)
6.2.12.3. Personnel will immediately evacuate the building whenever the fire alarm
activates, unless otherwise provided for in an approved building fire evacuation plan or
when non-evacuation testing, or maintenance is being conducted on the fire alarm system.
(T-1) Commanders may approve in writing, with coordination from the occupational safety
office and F&ES Flight, any combat ready positions authorized to remain in their work
center during fire alarm activation. Upon direction of the senior fire official, all personnel
will immediately evacuate to include those combat mission ready positions. (T-3) Note:
Command post and/or job control personnel are not considered combat mission ready.
6.2.12.4. Do not use elevators during evacuation; use the stairs. (T-1)
6.2.12.5. Establish the predetermined areas for personnel to gather after evacuation to
account for personnel. (T-0)
6.2.12.6. Appoint Emergency Action Procedures representatives to account for personnel
at the predetermined meeting area. (T-0) This will help emergency response personnel
determine who may still be in the building. At least two representatives per section will be
appointed; in case the primary leader is not in the building at the time of the emergency or
is unable to evacuate the building, the alternate can fill in. (T-3) Sections with many
personnel (20 or more) in their allocated space may require additional representatives.
6.2.12.7. Assign employees to assist people in wheelchairs or anyone else who would have
difficulty descending stairs on their own. (T-0) Also, ensure people with hearing or sight
problems know they are to evacuate. (T-0) Assign someone to help them evacuate and
report to the designated meeting area. (T-3)

46

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 7
WALKING – WORKING SURFACES

7.1. General Requirements. Slips, trips, and falls at the same level create numerous injuries
each year. Proper design, maintenance, and good lighting are paramount in the prevention of slip,
trip and fall injuries. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart D – Walking-Working Surfaces, for
definition and additional guidance.
7.1.1. Maintenance. All walking-working surfaces will be maintained in clean and dry
condition. (T-0)
7.1.1.1. Drip or oil pans will be used whenever the possibility of spilling or dripping exists.
(T-0)
7.1.1.2. Where wet processes are used, drainage will be maintained and false floors,
platforms, mats, or other dry standing places will be provided. (T-0) If floor drains are
present, floors will be sloped to allow liquids to naturally flow to the drains. (T-0) Drains
will be kept clear to prevent clogging, especially when hazardous or contaminated
materials are present. (T-0)
7.1.1.3. In locations where wet processes do not normally occur, and drains are not
available, wet areas will be clearly marked (e.g., wet floor signage) and immediately
cleaned up and dried before permitting other personnel to enter the area. (T-0)
7.1.1.4. Protective footwear for wet slippery surfaces will be provided where appropriate.
(T-1) 7.1.1.5. Obstacles in walk areas, liquid spills, sawdust accumulations and the use of
slippery surface cleaners can cause slipping hazards and will be cleaned up immediately to
prevent slips, falls and injuries. (T-0)
7.1.2. Condition. Floors, aisles, and passageways will be kept clear and in good repair, with
no obstructions, free of defects that can endanger employees or interfere with the handling of
materials, or hinder people leaving during emergencies. (T-0) Ensure sharp or protruding
objects, loose boards, damaged carpet, damaged concrete are repaired to prevent slips, trips,
and falls. (T-0) Floors will be maintained to ensure that they can support the maximum
intended load for that surface. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.22, General RequirementsWalking Working Surfaces, for additional information.
7.1.3. Illumination. Control of light is important to prevent glare and harsh shadows. Soft
shadows are usually acceptable, but harsh shadows should be avoided since they may obscure
hazards or interfere with visibility. Supplementary lighting or additional lighting will be
provided where general lighting is not sufficient. (T-1) Facility managers will ensure lighting
is sufficient for walking surfaces in their work/facility area and that obstacles are visible. (T-0)
7.1.3.1. Consult the installation CE facility design section whenever specific guidance on
lighting is required. Some work may require increased illumination. Submit a work request
when a lighting survey is required. If the safety of personnel is a factor, the work request
will be coordinated with the occupational safety office, who may assign a Risk Assessment
Code (RAC). (T-1)
7.1.3.2. When fluorescent lighting fixtures are used, supervisors will ensure any
stroboscopic effect with moving machinery is avoided. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

47

7.1.4. Guards for Floor and Wall Openings. Every floor opening, such as a hatchway, chute,
pit, trap door, manhole, and ladderway, will be guarded. (T-0) Note: Manholes will comply
with 29 CFR § 1910.24, Step Bolts and Manhole Steps. (T-0) The type of guard used depends
on the location, reason for the opening and frequency of use. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.28, Duty
to Have Fall Protection and Falling Object Protection, for additional guidance. One of the
following guards will be installed:
7.1.4.1. Standard Railings Systems and Toeboards. (T-0)
7.1.4.1.1. Guardrails used to prevent falls into holes or openings, including skylights,
will be fixed on all exposed sides, except at a stairway entrance. (T-0) If there is danger
of a person walking straight into the opening, a swinging gate or offset passage will be
used. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.28 for specific detail on the use of guardrail
systems.
7.1.4.1.2. All open-sided floors, platforms, and runways four feet or more above the
next lower level will be guarded by a standard railing on all open sides, except at ramp
entrance to a ramp, stairway, or fixed ladder. (T-0) Collapsible or removable railings
may be installed on a runway, but will only be removed when a special task, such as
oiling, shafting, or filling tank cars, is being performed and other protective measures,
approved by the occupational safety office, are used. (T-0) Railings will be reinstalled
when the task is complete. (T-0)
7.1.4.1.3. Every permanent or temporary wall opening, to include windows, less than
39 inches above the floor (where there is a four foot or more drop) will be protected
from falling by use of: guardrail system, safety net system, travel restraint systems or
personal fall arrest systems (PFAS). (T-0) If a guardrail system is used, the guard may
be removable but will be installed when the opening is not in use. (T-0) A toeboard
will be installed when falling materials may present a hazard. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR §
1910.28 for additional information.
7.1.4.1.4. Toeboards are required to prevent objects from falling to a lower level and
prevents struck by hazards to employees below. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.28 for
additional information.
7.1.4.2. Floor Opening Cover. A cover will be used for less frequently used openings
where traffic across the opening prevents the use of fixed railings, such as openings located
in aisle spaces. (T-0) In addition, covers or guards will be used to protect people from the
hazards of open pits, tanks, vats, ditches, etc. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.28 for
additional requirements.
7.1.5. Guardrail Systems, Toeboards and Covers Design.
7.1.5.1. Guard railings are barriers erected along an unprotected or exposed side, edge, or
other area of a walking-working surface to prevent employees from falling to a lower level.
(T-0) Guardrails will be designed and installed to meet the following:
7.1.5.1.1. The vertical height will be 42 inches, plus or minus 3 inches, from the upper
surface of the top rail to the floor, platform, runway, or ramp level. (T-0) Heights
greater than 45 inches are permissible, provided the guard rail system meets all criteria
of 29 CFR § 1910.29(b).

48

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
7.1.5.1.2. Top rails must be capable of withstanding, without failure, a force of at least
200 pounds (890 N) applied in any downward or outward direction at any point along
the top rail. (T-0) When a 200-pound test is applied in a downward direction, the top
rail must not deflect to a height of less than 39 inches above the walking-working
surface. (T-0)
7.1.5.1.3. Midrails, screens, mesh, intermediate vertical members, solid panels, or
equivalent intermediate members will be installed between, at the midpoint of the
walking-working surface and the top edge of the guardrail system when there is not a
wall or parapet that is at least 21 inches high. (T-0) Midrails, screens, mesh,
intermediate vertical members, solid panels, and other equivalent intermediate
members must be capable of withstanding, without failure, a force of at least 150
pounds (667 N) applied in any downward or outward direction at any point along the
intermediate member. (T-0) Additional requirements can be found in 29 CFR §
1910.29.
7.1.5.1.4. Intermediate vertical members or other equivalent intermediate members
will be installed at no more than 19 inches apart. (T-0)
7.1.5.2. Toeboards will be constructed to reach 3.5-inches measured from the walkingworking surface to the top of the top edge of the toeboard and will not have a clearance
greater than one-quarter of an inch (1/4 in) above the walking-working surface. (T-0) Made
of solid material or one with openings not greater than one inch and securely fastened.
(T-0) Toeboards must be capable of withstanding, without failure, a force of at least 50
pounds (222 N) applied in any downward or outward direction at any point along the
toeboard. (T-0)
7.1.5.3. Covers used to protect employees from falling in walking-working surface holes
will be capable of supporting, without failure, at least twice the maximum intended load
that may be imposed on the cover at one time and secured to prevent displacement.
Employees working around skylights will ensure they are protected by guardrails or covers
to prevent falls. (T-0) Personal fall protection may be used when guardrails or covers are
not feasible to use or may create a greater hazard. For personal fall protection requirements
refer to Chapter 13, Fall Protection, for additional information. (T-0)
7.1.6. Coefficient of Friction of Walking and Working Surfaces. Consult installation CE when
coefficient of friction guidance for a specific work surface is required or needs to be
determined. Finished surfaces will have a coefficient of friction of at least 0.5 for level surfaces
and 0.8 for sloped surfaces. ANSI/American Society of Safety Professionals (ASSP) A1264.2,
Provision of Slip Resistance on Walking/Working Surfaces, may provide additional
information. (T-1) This requirement applies to all types of floors and other walking-working
surfaces, including vinyl, tile, wood, carpet, steel, concrete, etc., and any coating or surface
treatment used on them.
7.1.7. Coatings include painted and unpainted walking - working surfaces and will exhibit
anti-skid properties to reduce the risk of employee slips and falls. (T-0) The purchaser will
verify anti-skid properties with coating manufacturer or supplier before purchasing. (T-0)
7.1.8. Interior Walking - Working Surfaces.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

49

7.1.8.1. Layout. All interior walking and working surfaces that are part of the means of
egress will comply with UFC 3-600-01, Fire Protection Engineering for Facilities, and
NFPA 101, Life Safety Code. (T-0)
7.1.8.1.1. Equipment and machinery will be arranged to permit an even flow of
materials. (T-1)
7.1.8.1.1.1. Sufficient space will be provided to handle the material with the least
possible interference from or to employees or other work being performed. (T-1)
7.1.8.1.1.2. Machines will be placed so it will not be necessary for an operator to
stand in a passageway, aisle or exit access. (T-0)
7.1.8.1.1.3. Machine positioning will allow for easy maintenance, cleaning and
removal of scrap. (T-1)
7.1.8.1.1.4. After the initial positioning of equipment and machines is decided,
clear zones (workspaces) of sufficient dimensions to accommodate typical work
will be established. (T-1)
7.1.8.1.1.5. If material exceeds established clear zones, rope and stanchions may
be used to temporarily extend the workspace.
7.1.8.1.1.6. The supervisor will consult with the occupational safety office as
needed to determine the need to mark clear zones based upon hazard potential.
(T-1)
7.1.8.1.1.7. Yellow or yellow-and-black hash-marked lines, two to three inches
wide, will be used when marking is necessary. (T-1)
7.1.8.1.2. Passageways, aisles and exit access will be provided to permit free
movement of employees and material, independent of clear zones and storage spaces
and clearly recognizable. (T-0) Markings will be used unless impractical. (T-0) Floor
markings will provide a contrast to the floor color, such as yellow lines two to three
inches wide on a gray floor. (T-0)
7.1.8.1.3. Where powered materials handling equipment (lift truck, etc.) is used,
facility layout will provide enough clearance in aisles, loading docks and through
doorways to permit safe turns. (T-0) Obstructions that could create hazards will not be
permitted in aisles. (T-0) Aisles will be at least two feet wider than the widest vehicle
used or most common material being transported. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.176,
Handling Materials—General, for additional information.
7.1.8.1.4. Aisles will be at least 36 inches wide to permit free movement of employees,
equipment, and supplies. (T-0) A minimum of 18 inches will be provided for
passageways formed by or between movable obstructions. (T-0) Refer to NFPA 101
and UFC 3-600-01 for additional information.
7.1.8.1.5. Sufficient access and working space of no less than 30 inches or width of
the equipment, whichever is greater, will be provided and maintained around all
electrical equipment and ensure the space permits at least a 90-degree opening of the
equipment doors or hinged panels. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.303, General
(Electrical), for additional information.

50

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
7.1.8.2. Condition.
7.1.8.2.1. Floors will not be cleaned with flammable liquids. (T-0) IAW DAFMAN
32-7002 floor cleaning chemicals not currently loaded in the standardized Air Force
Hazardous Material (HAZMAT) tracking system must be approved by the installation
Hazardous Materials Management Process (HMMP) Team prior to use. (T-1)
Adequate ventilation will be provided and/or respiratory protection may be required.
(T-0) Combustible or explosive dust will be eliminated at the source. (T-0) If this is
not possible, it will be minimized during sweeping by spreading a noncombustible
sweeping compound over the floor. (T-0)
7.1.8.2.2. After floors have been properly cleaned, nonskid finishing compounds may
be used. In some locations, such as electroplating shops, finishing compounds will not
be applied if there is a possibility of a reaction with shop chemicals. (T-0)
7.1.8.2.3. When floors are being cleaned or finishing compounds applied, signs will
be posted to warn employees of a slipping hazard and will remain in place until floors
have dried. (T-0) The placement of these signs will be at the discretion of the supervisor
but will be visible to those entering the hazardous area. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 17 for
additional information on safety, fire prevention and health signs.
7.1.8.2.4. Loading Capacity. Supervisors will ensure equipment weight and
distribution comply with maximum floor loading capacity for all above grade storage
areas, including areas above basements. (T-0) The weights of equipment, such as hoists
suspended under a floor, will also be considered when determining floor loading
capacity. (T-0) The supervisor will have signs that identify the floor load capacity
determined by installation CE permanently installed in plain view of all employees.
(T-0) installation CE will approve the installation of all equipment weighing more than
500 pounds in any multi-story building. (T-1) Supervisors will ensure heavy loads are
evenly distributed according to the floor’s safe load limit. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR §
1910.22. for additional information.
7.1.9. Exterior Walking and Working Surfaces. The proper layout and condition of exterior
surfaces is important to the safe and efficient movement of people and equipment. The
placement of walkways and parking areas is controlled by installation CE.
7.1.9.1. Parking Lots. The parking lots will be smooth, have good drainage and be free of
pedestrian tripping hazards. (T-0) Walkways will be provided. (T-0) Entrances and exits
will not have obstructions that block a driver’s or pedestrian’s view of traffic. (T-0)
Supervisors should contact the occupational safety office and installation CE for more
detailed information. Specifications for signs are contained in UFC 3-120-01, Design: Sign
Standards.
7.1.9.2. Walkways. Lighting will be used to provide sufficient visibility for safe passage
on a walkway and highlight hazards. (T-0) When loose gravel or crushed rock is used for
surfacing, the largest dimensions of material used will not exceed one-half of an inch. (T-0)
Motorized vehicles will not be operated over elevated walkways unless the load bearing
capacity of the walkway will support the vehicle’s weight. (T-0) Broken, uneven, or
cracked surfaces, which might reasonably present a hazard, will be repaired. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

51

7.1.9.3. Grounds. All grounds adjacent to work areas will be kept free of hazardous
materials, trash, weeds, unguarded pits, openings, or obstacles. (T-0)
7.1.9.3.1. Materials will not be stored, left under, or piled against buildings, doors,
exits or stairways. (T-0)
7.1.9.3.2. Poisonous or toxic plants will not be used for landscaping without prior
approval of the medical services. (T-0) Weeds will not be permitted to grow
excessively or to accumulate on walkways. (T-0) Trees and bushes adjacent to
walkways will be trimmed to permit a clear path for pedestrians. (T-0)
7.1.9.3.3. Open drainage ditches present a serious fall hazard and must be clearly
marked or guarded by fences, barriers, or well-lit to identify the ditch location and
prevent accidental falls. (T-0)
7.1.9.4. Construction. All construction work will be clearly identified by signs that can be
read from at least 50 feet, in addition to barriers marked with night reflective materials.
(T-0) Illumination or warning flashers will also be used after dark. (T-0)
7.1.9.5. Vehicle Repair, Service and Assembly Pits. The use of a fall protection system is
not required for those work pits that are less than 10 feet deep provided that:
7.1.9.5.1. Access to pit is marked and limited within six feet of the edge to authorized
and trained employees only IAW 29 CFR § 1910.30, Training Requirements. Note:
Color use for floor marking must contrast with the surrounding area.
7.1.9.5.2. If floor markings are not feasible, physical stanchions (capable of resisting,
without tipping over, a force of at least 16 pounds applied horizontally against the
stanchion and a height of 30 inches) may be used and positioned six feet from the edge
of the pit. Note: The use of stanchions and markings may be combined, where
applicable.
7.1.9.5.3. When two or more pits in a common area are not more than 15 feet apart,
compliance may occur by placing contrasting floor markings at least six feet from the
pit edge around the entire area of the pits. Caution signs will be visible and posted, and
meet the requirements of 29 CFR § 1910.145, Specifications for Accident Prevention
Signs and Tags, and state CAUTION—OPEN PIT. (T-0)
7.1.9.6. Loading Docks. Frequently used loading docks will be located away from
principal streets and intersections. (T-1) Vehicles parked at docks will not block general
vehicular traffic unless control devices, such as cones, barricades or warning signs are used
to redirect traffic. (T-1) Loading docks will adhere to fall protection requirements in
paragraph 7.1.4.1.2. (T-1)
7.1.9.6.1. Dock surfaces will be smooth and even. (T-1) Where necessary, aisleways
will be marked. (T-1) Edges of docks will be marked with four-inch-wide yellow lines
when there is a chance of employees falling. (T-0) Where the potential for serious
injury exists from falls greater than four feet, removable railings will be used. (T-0)
7.1.9.6.2. The dock will be at least two (2) feet wider than the widest vehicle or more
common material being transported. (T-0) Additional clearance will be provided to
permit safe turns. (T-1)

52

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

7.2. Stairs and Ramps Requirements. (Note : Egress components, such as stairs, ramps, guard
rails or handrails, constructed to any 1970 or newer edition of NFPA 101 are acceptable.) The
selection of a particular type of stairway or ramp is dependent upon the location, intended use and
existing environmental conditions, and is normally performed by installation CE with
recommendations from the occupational safety office and F&ES Flight. In addition to the below
criteria, see NFPA 101, UFC 3-600-01, 29 CFR § 1910.28 and 29 CFR § 1910.25, Stairways.
7.2.1. Design Consideration. Stairway and ramp designs may have been based on prior use of
a facility, whereas current use may dictate modifications or replacement of components. This
could include changing the width, angle of rise, length, and vertical clearance, as well as
increasing load bearing capacity, inclusion of railings, or enclosing open risers.
7.2.2. Hazards Associated with Fixed Industrial Stairs and Ramps. Many injuries are the result
of falls when people move from one level to another on stairs or ramps. Proper design and
construction of stairs and ramps will help prevent these falls. (T-0) Other aids, such as
handrails, reduce the threat of slips. Poor lighting or housekeeping, faulty treads and slippery
surfaces are unsafe conditions which often lead to injuries. Others result when people run up
or down stairs, fail to use handrails, wear shoes with slippery soles and/or heels or are careless.
7.2.3. Strength. Stairs and fixed ramps will be designed and built to carry a load of 5 times the
normal live load anticipated but not less than a moving concentrated load of 1,000 pounds.
(T-0)
7.2.4. Width. Stairs, if part of the exit access, will have a minimum width of 36 inches if the
occupant load of the building or structure is less than 50 persons. (T-0) If the occupant load is
greater than 50, the minimum stair width is 44 inches. Fixed ramps, if part of the exit access,
will have a minimum width of 44 inches. (T-0) Ramp requirements will be determined by
NFPA 101 and appropriate building codes. (T-0) New designs will be coordinated through the
Designated Fire Protection Engineer (DFPE) per UFC 3-600-01. (T-0)
7.2.5. Angle of Rise. Stairs will be installed at angles to the horizontal of 30 to 50 degrees.
(T-0) For stairs constructed or installed after 17 January 2017, a maximum riser height of 9.5
inches, minimum tread depth of 9.5 inches and a minimum width of 22 inches between vertical
barriers is required. Stairs constructed/installed prior to 17 January 2017 are compliant if they
meet the requirements of 29 CFR § 1910.25, Table D-1. Tread depth and riser height must be
consistent throughout a new facility. (T-0) Tread depth and riser heights must also be
consistent throughout a staircase. (T-0) Where the slope is less than 30 degrees, a ramp with a
non-slip surface will be installed. (T-0)
7.2.6. Ramps. Ramps not part of the exit access will follow guidance in NFPA 101. (T-1) For
ramps that are part of the exit access, a maximum slope of 1 to 8 inches for existing ramps and
1 to 12 for new ramps is allowed. (T-0) To meet Americans with Disabilities (ADA)
requirements, review the information/guidance on ADA requirements, which can be found at
https://www.access-board.gov/ada/.
7.2.7. Width of Landings. Landings and platforms not part of the exit access will not be less
than the width of the stairs and a minimum of 30 inches in length, measured in the direction of
travel. (T-0) When doors or gates open directly onto a stairway, they will not reduce the
available width of the landing to less than 20 inches for platforms installed before 17 January
2017, and less than 22 inches for platforms installed on or after 17 January 2017. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

53

Landings in stairs or ramps that are part of the exit access will meet the requirements of NFPA
101. (T-0)
7.2.8. Vertical Clearance. Where there is less than six feet, eight inches of headroom over
stairs, obstructions will be padded. (T-0) When they cannot be padded, obstructions will be
color coded yellow or yellow-and-black stripes to highlight the hazards. (T-0) In all cases,
caution signs will be used to warn people of low clearances. (T-0)
7.2.9. Treads. All treads will be slip-resistant and free of projections or lips that could cause a
tripping hazard. (T-0)
7.2.10. Grating. Open grating type treads are desirable for stairs which are not enclosed. The
use of open treads prevents the buildup of rainwater and snow on the tread surface.
7.2.11. Sidewalk Ramps. Sidewalk ramps will be installed where equipment, such as
housekeeping carts or hand trucks, are used. (T-0)
7.2.12. Stair Railings and Guards. Every flight of stairs with four or more risers will have
standard guardrails or standard handrails as called for in 29 CFR § 1910.25. (T-0) The width
of the stair will be measured clear of all obstructions except handrails. (T-0)
7.2.12.1. Stairs less than 44 inches wide with both sides enclosed will have at least one
handrail, preferably on the right side going down. (T-0)
7.2.12.2. Stairs less than 44 inches wide with one side open will have at least one guardrail
on the open side. (T-0)
7.2.12.3. Stairs less than 44 inches wide with both sides open will have one guardrail on
each side. (T-0)
7.2.12.4. Stairs more than 44 inches wide but less than 88 inches wide will have a handrail
on each enclosed side and a guardrail on each open side. (T-0)
7.2.12.5. Stairs 88 or more inches wide will have one handrail on each enclosed side, one
guardrail on each open side and one intermediate guardrail built midway of the width. (T-0)
7.2.12.6. Spiraling and winding stairs will have a handrail offset to stop people from
walking on the parts of the treads that are less than six inches wide. (T-0)
7.2.13. Illumination. Stairs and ramps will be lighted, so all treads and landings are visible.
(T-0) Consult the installation CE facility design section whenever specific guidance on lighting
is required.
7.2.14. Inclined Ladders. Some DAF facilities have permanent stairways installed which
exceed a 50-degree angle. These stairways are commonly referred to as inclined ladders.
Where an inclined ladder has been installed, it will have handrails on both sides and open
risers. (T-0) Construction or installation after 17 January 2017 will have stairs installed within
the range of 30-50 degrees. (T-0)
7.2.15. Maintenance. Stairs and ramps will be kept clean, free of obstructions or slippery
substances, and in good repair. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure:
7.2.15.1. Loose boards, insecure treads, protruding nails and torn or worn stair treads are
repaired or replaced immediately. (T-0)

54

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
7.2.15.2. Posters, bulletin boards and other objects that could distract a person's attention
are not placed in stairwells or rampways. (T-0)
7.2.15.3. Slippery surfaces are replaced or made safe by coating them with nonslip surface
materials. (T-0)
7.2.15.4. Treads and risers are of uniform size. (T-0)
7.2.15.5. Stair nosings (leading edge of the tread) are designed to prevent personnel from
catching their heels on the treads. They must be securely fastened and rounded or beveled.
(T-1)
7.2.15.6. Guardrails and handrails are smooth, free of splinters or burrs and securely
mounted. (T-0)
7.2.15.7. Outside stairways, entrances, sidewalks, loading docks and ramps are cleared of
snow and ice, and that abrasive materials, such as sand, ash, or ice melting chemicals, are
readily available and used during inclement weather. (T-0) This also includes all
emergency exits and walkways leading from them.

7.3. Fixed Ladders.
7.3.1. Requirements.
7.3.1.1. Acquisition. Fixed ladders, and their parts and accessories, and safety devices will
meet requirements of 29 CFR § 1910.23, Ladders. (T-0) Procurement specifications will
include permanent treatment for corrosion control so future treatment will not be needed.
(T-1)
7.3.1.2. Design and Installation. Fixed ladders provide a means of access to roofs, pits,
silos, towers, tanks, and other limited-access areas. Stairs will be provided where access is
daily or during each shift for gauging, inspection, regular maintenance or when carrying
tools or equipment is normally required and sufficient space is available. (T-0) Fixed
ladders may be used when a stairway cannot be used. The following paragraphs provide
general design and installation criteria for fixed ladders:
7.3.1.2.1. Rungs. All fixed ladder rungs, steps and cleats will be parallel, level and
uniformly spaced. (T-0) Spacing between rungs will be consistent at no less than 10
inches and no more than 14 inches apart. (T-0) Rungs will have a minimum width of
16 inches (measured before installation of ladder safety systems). (T-0)
7.3.1.2.2. Length. Ladders 24 feet or less will be of a continuous length. (T-0) When
they are more than 24 feet and of continuous length, the following requirements apply:
7.3.1.2.2.1. When platforms are required, they will not be less than 24 inches wide
by 30 inches long and will be equipped with standard railings. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.2.2. Toeboards are required where the hazard of objects falling is present.
Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.29, Fall Protection Systems and Falling Object
Protection—Criteria and Practices, for additional information.
7.3.1.2.3. Ladder Safety Devices, Cages and Wells. All ladder safety devices such as
those that incorporate positioning belts, friction brakes and sliding attachments will
meet the design requirements of the ladders which they serve. (T-0) Ladder safety

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

55

systems will allow an employee to climb up and down the ladder with both hands and
not require the employee to continuously hold, push or pull any part of the system while
climbing. (T-0) Where a cage is installed, it will extend to a minimum of 42 inches
above the top of landing unless other acceptable protection is provided. (T-0) Cages
will extend down the ladder to a point not less than seven feet or more than eight feet
above the base of the ladder. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR §§ 1910.23 and 1910.28 for specific
design and construction information. Ladder safety devices, cages or wells are required
on all fixed ladders more than 24 feet as follows:
7.3.1.2.3.1. Existing fixed ladders. Each fixed ladder installed before 19 November
2018, is equipped with a PFAS, ladder safety system, cage, or well. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.3.2. New fixed ladders. Each fixed ladder installed on and after 19
November 2018, is equipped with a PFAS or a ladder safety system. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.3.3. Replacement. When a fixed ladder, cage, or well, or any portion of a
section thereof, is replaced, a PFAS or ladder safety system is installed in at least
that section of the fixed ladder, cage, or well where the replacement is located.
(T-0)
7.3.1.2.3.4. Final deadline. IAW 29 CFR § 1910.28(b)(9)(i)(D), on and after 18
November 2036, all fixed ladders are equipped with a PFAS or a ladder safety
system. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.4. When a one-section fixed ladder is equipped with a personal fall protection
or a ladder safety system or a fixed ladder is equipped with a personal fall arrest or
ladder safety system on more than one section, ensure the following:
7.3.1.2.4.1. The PFAS or ladder safety system provides protection throughout the
entire vertical distance of the ladder, including all ladder sections. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.4.2. The ladder has rest platforms at intervals not to exceed 150 feet (45.7
m). (T-0)
7.3.1.2.4.3. Ensure ladder sections having a cage or well are offset from adjacent
sections. (T-0)
7.3.1.2.4.4. Have landing platforms provided at maximum intervals of 50 feet
(15.2). (T-0) Note: A cage or well may be used in combination with a PFAS or
ladder safety system provided that the cage or well does not interfere with the
operation of the system.
7.3.1.3. Ladder Pitch. The preferred pitch of fixed ladders will be 75 to 90 degrees from
the horizontal. (T-1) Ladders with a pitch more than 90 degrees are prohibited. Refer to 29
CFR § 1910.23, Ladders, for additional information.
7.3.1.4. Load Capacity. All ladders, platforms, accessories, and fastenings will not have
their maximum intended loads exceeded. (T-0) Maximum intended loads are defined in 29
CFR § 1910.21(b) as the total load (weight and force) of all employees, equipment,
vehicles, tools, materials and other loads the employer reasonably anticipates being applied
to a walking working surface at any one time.

56

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
7.3.1.5. Lighting. Adequate illumination will be available when using ladders. (T-0)
Lamps will be installed so the light does not reflect in a climber’s eyes. (T-0) Consult the
installation CE when specific guidance on lighting is required.
7.3.1.6. Access. Where unauthorized use of a fixed ladder poses a hazard, the facility
manager will ensure the ladders are secured from unauthorized access. (T-2) Ladders
available to public access require guarding to prevent unauthorized access. (T-0) The
bottom seven feet will be guarded. (T-0) Examples of guarding include the use of a fence
with locked gates and making the bottom portion portable or spring loaded and available
only as needed. Additionally, there must be a warning sign prohibiting access by
unauthorized persons. (T-0)
7.3.1.7. Slipping. When the potential for climbers to get mud, oil or grease on their
footwear creates a slipping hazard, a mat of crushed rock, a raised platform or a boot
scraper will be provided. (T-0)
7.3.1.8. Electric Lines. Electrical conductors will not be installed on or adjacent to fixed
metal ladders unless they are beyond reach and cannot be used as a handrail or grabbed
inadvertently. (T-0) This distance must be at least five feet from the ladder. (T-0) If there
is any potential for contact with parts of electric equipment or circuits, these parts will be
de-energized and locked out IAW Chapter 21. (T-0)
7.3.1.9. Rung Ladders. Rung ladders will be built into the walls of each manhole over 12
feet deep. (T-1) The distance between rungs is the same as other fixed ladders. More
detailed design criteria can be found in 29 CFR § 1910.23. Note: Newly constructed
manholes completed on or after 17 January 2017 will comply with 29 CFR § 1910.24.
(T-0)
7.3.1.10. Grounding and Bonding. Ladders and related equipment will be grounded and/or
bonded, to drain off accumulated static electricity when static electricity is hazardous to
the work operation, such as fuel cell repair or refueling vehicle maintenance. (T-0) Static
discharge plates will also be provided. (T-0)
7.3.2. Inspections.
7.3.2.1. A formal, fully documented inspection of all fixed ladders will be accomplished
upon installation and at least every three years thereafter. (T-0) These inspections will be
performed by installation CE for ladders installed on real property facilities or real property
installed equipment. (T-0) The using organization is responsible for ensuring inspections
are done for facilities and equipment which are not real property. When qualified personnel
are not available in the using organization, the unit will submit a Work Order Request to
installation CE. (T-3) Inspection documentation will include the type of ladder and safety
climb device installed, location, a detailed list of all discrepancies and corrective action
status. (T-2) Note: Real Property Installed Equipment (RPIE) is an installation CE
responsibility. Information regarding RPIE is provided in DAFI 32-9005, Real Property
Accountability, and DAFMAN 65-605, Volume 1, Budget Guidance and Technical
Procedures.
7.3.2.2. Climbers will be alert to potential hazards and perform informal inspections of
fixed ladders prior to every use. (T-1) No attempt to climb will be made until all hazardous
conditions are corrected. (T-1) Specific hazards to look for during inspections are loose,

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

57

worn and damaged rungs or side rails; damaged or corroded bolts and rivet heads; damaged
or corroded handrails and brackets on platforms; and deteriorated masonry where fixed
ladder anchorages are secured to a structure, including loose or damaged carrier rails or
cables.
7.3.3. Maintenance. Repairs made to fixed ladders and related equipment will meet the
following requirements:
7.3.3.1. Materials used will meet or exceed original construction. (T-0)
7.3.3.2. Any modification to ladder components, safety climb device or related equipment
will be coordinated with the property manager and occupational safety office, with final
approval by installation CE. (T-1)
7.3.3.3. All welding will be performed by a qualified welder. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 20
for more information on welding, cutting and brazing.
7.3.4. Selection of Personnel. Some individuals become spatially disoriented when working
on elevated ladders or platforms. This condition is known as height vertigo. Common reactions
to this condition are to “freeze” and hang on to any permanent support or to experience
dizziness, nausea, trembling and/or loss of consciousness. Supervisors will be vigilant in
screening and observing employees required to work at heights. (T-1) If the supervisor
suspects an employee may be subject to vertigo, the employee will be referred to the local
medical facility for evaluation prior to resuming any height work. (T-1) Employees descending
into pits or shafts may also experience vertigo. Individuals known to experience vertigo will
be disqualified as ladder users. (T-1)
7.3.5. Supervisors will ensure climbers are trained to:
7.3.5.1. Check the ladder for defects and slippery substances. (T-0)
7.3.5.2. Ensure the ladder and climber’s footwear are free of slippery substances. (T-0)
7.3.5.3. Raise or lower needed tools and materials by handlines after reaching the work
position. (T-0) Never hand carry while climbing ladder.
7.3.5.4. Face the ladder and use both hands to grip the rungs or side rails firmly. (T-0)
7.3.5.5. Wear slip-resistant shoes with heels. (T-0)
7.3.5.6. Climb carefully, without haste and maintain three-point contact. (T-0)
7.3.5.7. Never run up or down or jump from the ladder. (T-0)
7.3.5.8. Remove hand jewelry (rings) prior to climbing. (T-0)
7.4. Portable Ladders.
7.4.1. Hazards and Human Factors Associated with Portable Ladders. Falls are the primary
hazard associated with portable ladders. Falls result from several unsafe acts and conditions
such as climbing with items in hand, or using a damaged ladder, which are minimized if
employees adhere to proper ladder discipline and if supervisors ensure equipment is inspected,
maintained in good condition and properly used.
7.4.2. The following practices are prohibited:
7.4.2.1. Ladders set on unstable surfaces. (T-0)

58

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
7.4.2.2. Ladders placed in front of doors which open towards the ladder without proper
guarding. (T-0)
7.4.2.3. Ladders used as scaffolds, unless specifically designed for that purpose. (T-0)
7.4.2.4. Personnel reaching too far out to the sides. (T-0)
7.4.2.5. Personnel standing too high to maintain their balance. (T-0)
7.4.2.6. Personnel standing on the cap of the ladder. (T-0)
7.4.2.7. Use of a defective ladder, e.g., broken rail or rung. (T-0)
7.4.2.8. Improvising and using a make-shift ladder. (T-0)
7.4.2.9. Personnel carrying materials in their hands while ascending or descending a
ladder. (T-0)
7.4.2.10. Using a ladder with conductive side rails while working on electrical circuits or
near live electrical lines, etc. (T-0)
7.4.2.11. Descending with their back to the ladder. (T-0)
7.4.2.12. Painting wood ladders with an opaque coating, since possible defects may be
covered up. (T-0)
7.4.3. Requirements.
7.4.3.1. Acquisition and Selection Considerations.
7.4.3.1.1. Portable ladders will meet ANSI design and construction specifications.
(T-0) Exception: Fire department ladders will be maintained and inspected IAW
NFPA 1931, Standard for Manufacturer’s Design of Fire Department Ground
Ladders, and NFPA 1932, Standard on Use, Maintenance, and Service Testing of InService Fire Department Ground Ladders. (T-0)
7.4.3.1.2. There are a variety of ladder styles and selections, including construction
material and proper size. However, the right selection will be based on where and how
the ladder may be used. (T-1) Ladders come in five workload ratings. Supervisors will
order Type IAA, IA or I ladders, whenever possible. (T-1) Type II ladders will only be
considered when local purchase is required, and local manufacturers cannot provide
Type IAA, IA or I. (T-1) Type III ladders will not be procured for industrial use. (T-1)
Refer to Table 7.1 for additional information.
7.4.3.1.3. The supervisor should consider the location as well as durability when
selecting the ladder. Aluminum ladders are normally lighter than wood, while steel
ladders are as heavy or heavier. Metal ladders and wood ladders with metal reinforced
side rails or metal rungs will not be use in areas where an employee or the ladder could
contact exposed energized electrical circuits. (T-0) Carrying steel and wood ladders is
fatiguing if the employee carries them for a considerable distance.
