with design manufacturing requirements and specifications in OSHA standards and applicable
ANSI/ASME requirements and specifications. (T-0) (Note: Refer to manufacturer
certification of compliance for verification that equipment meets requirements.) Local
modifications of centrally procured materials handling equipment will be approved by
manufacturer in writing. (T-0) Where the manufacturer approval cannot be gained a registered
professional engineer will be consulted by equipment item managers. (T-1) Local purchase
acquisition requests for medication will also be coordinated with the Occupational Safety

88

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
office for awareness. (T-1) (Note: Manufacturers may void the warranty of equipment
modified without their approval. Therefore, coordinate with the contracting and occupational
safety offices prior to contracting the equipment item manager.
12.3.2. Lever-operated hoists will have manufacturer’s certification that the hoist meets the
requirements and specifications of recognized industry standards. (T-0) Slings procured for
DAF use will have manufacturer’s certification of compliance with design and manufacturing
requirements found in 29 CFR § 1910.184, Slings, and ASME B30.9, Slings. (T-0) Locally
manufactured slings that do not meet the engineering requirements of ASME B30.9 are
prohibited and will be disassembled and removed from service. (T-0) Note: Design and
manufacturing requirements are specified in OSHA’s Tables and Figures: Guidance on Safe
Sling Use (https://www.osha.gov/dsg/guidance/slings/tables-figures.html).
12.3.3. Capacity of all lever-operated devices will be permanently and conspicuously marked
in a readily visible place on the hoist. (T-1) Only ratchet and pawl, and load brake hoists, which
include a means to prevent a suspended load from self-lowering, will be procured. (T-1)
Lowering under load will be by operation of the hoist lever. (T-1)
12.3.4. Overhead hoists will be certified by the manufacturer that it meets design
specifications, characteristics, and rules of ASME B30.7, Winches, ASME B30.10, Hooks, and
ASME B30.16, Overhead Underhung and Stationary Hoists. (T-0) When applicable, a system
safety program will be established. (T-1) Note 2: Nuclear-certified hoists must also meet
requirements in paragraph 12.5 and DAFMAN 91-118, Safety Design Criteria for Nuclear
Weapon Systems. (T-1)
12.3.5. New or Modified MHE. Prior to initial use, all newly procured or modified (altered or
repaired) MHE will be inspected by a person qualified to ensure compliance with the
provisions of this chapter and manufacturer’s requirements. (T-1)
12.3.6. Hoist, Sling and Hook Certification. Hoist, sling and hook certification documents
received from the manufacturer when hoists, slings or hooks are purchased or repaired will be
maintained for the life of the equipment (or until the hooks are replaced). (T-1) For repaired
items, the previous certification will be replaced with the new documentation. (T-1)

12.4. Inspections. MHE (specifically hoists, cranes, derricks, powered industrial trucks) and
associated components will be inspected for damage or wear at intervals specified, as described
below, and including observations made during operations based on level of usage and/or as
recommended by the manufacturer. (T-0) Deficiencies will be carefully examined, and a
determination made by the operator and the shop or facility supervisor as to whether they constitute
a safety hazard. (T-1) Inspections and preventative maintenance checks on motorized material
handling equipment must be completed IAW TO 36-1-191. (T-0)
12.4.1. Frequent. A visual inspection will be performed by the operator or designated person
at daily (e.g., pre-use) to monthly intervals. (T-0) The record of inspection will be maintained
by the using agency until the next periodic inspection has been performed. (T-1)
12.4.2. Periodic. A complete inspection will be performed by a person qualified to conduct
the inspection at intervals between 1 and 12 months. (T-1) The required intervals of periodic
inspections will be based on the type of use experienced by the equipment: normal service –
annually; heavy service – semiannual; severe service – quarterly. Follow periodic inspection
schedules outlined in applicable technical orders and/or manufacturer’s instructions where

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

89

intervals are established. Note: These intervals will apply unless MAJCOMs/FLDCOMs, or
installations have more stringent directives. (T-1)
12.4.2.1. During the periodic inspection, some systems may require the removal of covers
or parts to gain access. Appropriate hazardous energy control procedures will be used
where removal of covers or parts expose individuals to an uncontrolled energy source (e.g.,
an electrical or mechanical hazard) and result in accidental start-up of equipment. (T-0)
Refer to Chapter 21 for additional guidance.
12.4.2.2. Disassembly of the MHE or major components, not designed to be removed or
opened, is not required for the sole purpose of the inspection.
12.4.2.3. If the individual performing the periodic inspection finds evidence of damage or
wear that constitutes a potential hazard, further inspection to determine cause or to repair
the system may be required and total disassembly or removal of major components may be
necessary. Consult manufacturer or equivalent entity instructions for additional guidance.
12.4.3. Inspections identified as monthly and annual inspections will be considered as such
and not a 30- or 365-day inspection. (T-1) The next inspection must occur by the last day of
the respective month. (T-1)
12.4.4. Mandatory Monthly Inspections. Wire ropes, chains, and hooks associated with
overhead and gantry cranes will be thoroughly inspected monthly. (T-0) Inspections for each
item will be documented. (T-1) Documentation will include date and time of the monthly
inspection, signature of the inspector, an identifier for the equipment inspected, and condition
of the equipment. (T-0) Inspection criteria will be IAW this chapter and manufacturer
instructions. (T-1) Note: Units will review the Air Force Records Disposition Schedule for
disposal guidance for historical records of all inspection, repair, and test documentation. (T-1)
12.4.5. MHE Not in Regular Service. Cranes, hoists, and derricks idle for at least one month,
but less than six months, must be inspected prior to placing back in service. (T-0) Cranes,
hoists, and derricks idle for six months or more will be given a complete inspection as defined
in paragraphs 12.4.1, 12.4.2 and 12.4.4, prior to placing back in service. (T-0) Standby cranes
and derricks will be completely inspected at least semiannually as defined in paragraphs 12.4.1,
12.4.2 and 12.4.4 (T-0) The user will determine the need for more frequent inspections of
cranes and derricks exposed to an adverse environment. (T-1) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.179,
Overhead and Gantry Cranes, 29 CFR § 1910.180, Crawler Locomotive and Truck Cranes,
and 29 CFR § 1910.181, Derricks, for specific equipment requirements.
12.4.6. For cranes and derricks used in construction, consult with the installation CE and refer
to 29 CFR § 1926.1400, Cranes and Derricks in Construction.
12.4.7. Inspections will be documented IAW paragraph 12.2.1. (T-1)
12.5. Nuclear-certified Lifting Equipment. For nuclear-certified MHE, the supervisor or
designated representative will perform periodic inspection semiannually. (T-1) The supervisor or
designated representative will ensure operational and load tests of nuclear-certified hoists, cranes
or slings are conducted annually at not less than 100 percent or more than 125 percent of rated
capacity. (T-0) Perform annual tests in conjunction with one of the two semiannual inspections.
(T-1) Nuclear-certified hoists must also meet requirements in DAFMAN 91-118. (T-1)

90

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.5.1. The annual load test may be performed either as a static or dynamic test as determined
by the person qualified to conduct such tests.
12.5.2. For nuclear-certified slings and wire rope assemblies, supervisors will use the
appropriate 11N series TOs for testing criteria. (T-1)
12.5.3. Supervisors will ensure synthetic rope slings are not used to lift nuclear loads. (T-1)
Critical non-nuclear loads can use slings made of synthetic fibers, such as synthetic round
slings and synthetic web slings, with completion and documentation of a risk assessment
demonstrating the sling can safely handle the load. (T-1)
12.5.4. For nuclear-certified hoist hooks and hooks used in lifting critical loads, perform a dye
penetrant, magnetic particle, or other suitable NDI on the hook annually. (T-1) Perform the
annual NDI in conjunction with one of the two scheduled semiannual hoist and crane
inspections. (T-1) If the hook retaining nut is welded to the hook shank, removal of the hook
for inspection is not required. In this case, a visual inspection of the inside of the block
assembly will be performed. (T-1) If the block is designed such that disassembly is not feasible,
this inspection is not required. If hook is removed from hoist for NDI, verify that the hook is
properly reinstalled before use. Note 1: Hooks repaired by welding or reshaping are not
authorized on nuclear-certified hoists or lifting equipment used to lift critical loads. Note 2:
Painted hooks will not be used on nuclear-certified hoists or lifting equipment or equipment
used to lift critical loads. (T-1)

12.6. Qualification and Training.
12.6.1. MHE operator qualification and training will be provided by the organization
responsible for the operation of equipment and will be IAW applicable DAF guidance, OSHA
requirements and manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0) Organizations requiring MHE training
will designate personnel qualified to instruct trainees. (T-1) Appropriate shop supervisor and
vehicle control officer or noncommissioned officer will keep the list of approved instructors,
with the background and experience on the type(s) of equipment the instructor is providing
training. (T-1)
12.6.2. Lesson plans for each vehicle or equipment type will be accomplished by the unit IAW
DAFI 24-301. (T-1) Lesson plans will include formal instruction, hands-on demonstrations by
the instructor with practical exercises performed by the trainee and an evaluation of the
trainee’s performance to confirm their knowledge of equipment operations and service
instructions. (T-1)
12.6.3. MHE training will be IAW applicable technical data and manufacturer’s instructions,
e.g., cranes, hoists, conveyors, forklifts. (T-1) A qualified instructor will evaluate each forklift
operator at least once every three years and provide refresher training, as applicable, to
maintain currency in relevant topics, where an operator shows unsafe operation of the vehicle,
the operator has been in an accident in the MHE, operation of a different type of MHE, or to
address changes in the workplace that create new hazards. (T-0) MHE will be operated only
by properly trained, and qualified personnel or trainees under direct supervision of a qualified
trainer/instructor. (T-1) The operator will be familiar with all operating controls and be
instructed in the operations to be performed. (T-1) Training should include warnings,
manufacturer’s instructions, requirements from this manual, and OSHA guidelines, as
applicable.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

91

12.6.4. Mobile cranes will only be operated by designated, qualified operators with a valid AF
Form 483, Certificate of Competency, or by persons in training under the direct supervision of
a designated operator. (T-1) (Exception: Operators of DAF special purpose truck crane will
be licensed IAW AFI 24-301 in lieu of AF Form 483.) (T-1) No other personnel will enter a
crane cab except for oilers, supervisors, and qualified inspectors whose duties require cab
entry. (T-1) A list of qualified crane operators will be kept by the using organization and
appropriate entries will be made in the individual’s training records. (T-1)
12.6.5. Training Currency for Deployment. Supervisors will ensure personnel scheduled for
deployment are current/trained in operations on this equipment prior to deploying. (T-0)
Additionally, supervisors at deployed locations will verify personnel are current in operations
before they operate the equipment and provide additional training as needed for personnel to
safely operate any new type of equipment and address local hazards. (T-0)
12.7. Testing.
12.7.1. All new hoists, cranes, derricks, and other similar lifting devices, regardless of type,
required to lift any type of load will be tested prior to initial use and every four years thereafter
by a person qualified to conduct the load test. (T-0) This load test will incorporate a pre-test
and post-test inspection per periodic inspection guidelines and be conducted at not less than
100 percent or more than 125 percent of the rated load, unless otherwise recommended by the
manufacturer or equivalent entity. (T-0) Test reports will be placed on file readily available to
appointed personnel and maintained on file for the life of the equipment. (T-1) Refer to 29
CFR § 1910.179, 29 CFR § 1926.552, Material Hoists, Personnel Hoists and Elevators, 29
CFR § 1926.1433, Design, Construction and Testing, for additional guidance. The Crane
Manufacturer’s Association of America (CMAA) Specification 78, Standards and Guidelines
for Professional Services Performed on Overhead and Traveling Cranes and Associated
Hoisting Equipment, and applicable ASME’s B30 Codes and Standards may also contain
additional information.
12.7.2. DAF materials will be designated as critical loads for purposes of lifting or hoisting
by an appropriate program manager or the functional manager at the facility where the lift will
occur. (T-1) Hoists, cranes, or other similar lifting devices, regardless of type, required to lift
critical loads, as defined in Attachment 1, Terms (Critical Loads), will be tested, as stated in
paragraph 12.7.4 annually per manufacturer requirements. (T-1) Prior to accomplishing load
tests on equipment attached to facility structural members, a qualified person to conduct the
tests such as a civil engineer, will assess the sufficiency of the structure to withstand the loads
being tested. (T-0)
12.7.3. Load Tests.
12.7.3.1. Hoists and Cranes. All new or altered hoists/cranes, with replaced or repaired
load suspension parts will be load tested prior to use at not less than 100 percent or more
than 125 percent of the rated load of the equipment, unless otherwise recommended by the
manufacturer. (T-0) This load test will include all functions outlined in applicable technical
data or manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1) The replacement of load chain or rope is
specifically excluded from this test; however, an operational test under a no-load condition
will be conducted prior to putting the hoist back into service. (T-0) Prior to accomplishing
load tests on equipment attached to facility structural members, a person qualified to
conduct such tests, such as civil engineer, will assess the sufficiency of the structure to

92

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
withstand the loads being tested. (T-0) Using organizations will maintain written
certification that all required load testing has been accomplished. (T-1)
12.7.3.2. The test will be conducted as follows:
12.7.3.2.1. Hoist the test load a distance to assure load is supported by the crane and
held by hoist brake(s). (T-0)
12.7.3.2.2. Transport test load by the trolley or carrier for the full length of the bridge.
(T-0)
12.7.3.2.3. Transport the test load by the bridge for the full length of the runway in one
direction with trolley or carrier as close to the extreme right-hand end of the crane as
practical and in the other direction with trolley or carrier as close to the left-hand end
of the crane as practical. (T-0) When cranes operate on more than two runways
(multiple-truck cranes), the crane will also transport the test load the full length of the
runway with the trolley or carrier positioned at each intermediate end truck. (T-0)
12.7.3.2.4. For monorail systems, follow manufacturer guidance for the hoist test and
transport test load by the carrier for the full length of the monorail system. (T-0) Refer
to ASME B30.16, Overhead Hoists (Underhung), for additional information.
12.7.3.2.5. On hoists incorporating overload devices, a load test will be accomplished
with a test load of least 100 percent of rated capacity, after which the overload device
will be tested. (T-0)
12.7.3.2.6. Test weights for load testing will be tagged or marked indicating total
weight in pounds and owner or agency identification number. (T-0) Rebar steel will
not be used for test weight lift points. (T-0)
12.7.4. Operational Tests. All new hoists, cranes and other similar lifting devices which have
had load-suspension parts altered, replaced, or repaired will be operationally tested before use.
(T-0) Rebar steel will not be used for test weight lift points. (T-0) Conduct tests first by hand,
if practical, and then at the slowest possible speed. Test with increasing speeds up to maximum
speed. Actuated mechanisms will be located so they trip switches or limiting devices in
sufficient time to stop motion without damage to the hoisting arrangement. (T-0) On hoists
having adjustable trip-setting limit devices, care will be exercised to achieve adjustment setting
without the load block striking the hoist frame or without all the slack being taken out of the
unloaded chain or less than one wrap of rope on the drum. (T-0)
12.7.4.1. Hoists, cranes, and other similar lifting devices not used within the preceding 12
months will be operationally tested by a person qualified to do so. (T-0)
12.7.4.2. Operational testing will consist of operating all functions of the lifting device
under a no-load condition to test all functions of the lifting device, including hoisting and
lowering, operation of brakes, and testing of all limits, locking, safety devices, and other
movable mechanisms, such as trolley travel, bridge travel, load lifting and lowering
mechanisms, etc. (T-0)
12.7.5. Derricks. Prior to initial use, all modified and altered derricks will be operationally
tested to ensure compliance with this manual and requirements in 29 CFR § 1926, Subpart CC,
Cranes and Derricks in Construction. (T-0) All new derricks will have the manufacturer’s
certification that all required testing has been completed. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

93

12.8. Powered Industrial Trucks.
12.8.1. Hazards. Injury or death may occur when powered industrial trucks are improperly
operated, inadvertently driven off loading docks, tipping, striking personnel, etc. Most
incidents also involve property damage, including damage to overhead sprinklers, racking,
pipe, walls, and machinery. Most injuries and property damage are caused by unsafe operating
procedures, lack of safety-rule enforcement, insufficient or inadequate training and operator
error. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.178, Powered Industrial Truck, for additional guidance.
12.8.2. New commercially procured powered industrial trucks will meet the design and
construction requirements of ANSI B56.1, Safety Standard for Low, High Lift Trucks, except
those designed primarily for earth moving or over-the-road hauling. (T-0) Supervisors will
review the manufacturer’s certification to validate compliance. (T-1) A label or other
identifying marks showing approval by a testing laboratory must be affixed to the truck. (T-0)
12.8.3. Local modifications and additions of centrally procured MHE will be done IAW
paragraph 12.3.1.
12.8.4. Safe Operations.
12.8.4.1. The maximum load capacity will be posted on each piece of lifting equipment,
in view of the operator. (T-0)
12.8.4.2. All high-lift rider trucks will be equipped and operated with overhead guards to
protect the operator from falling objects. (T-0) Overhead guards will not be covered with
material that could obstruct the operator’s vision. (T-0)
12.8.4.3. When a forklift is used to elevate personnel, an approved work platform with all
designed safety equipment, such as fall protection requirements, will be in place while in
use. (T-0) The operator must always remain at the controls when personnel are on the work
platform and must not move the forklift. (T-0) Instructions prohibiting movement of the
forklift with personnel on the work platform will be posted on the work platform in plain
sight, visible to the operator and all personnel working near the platform. (T-0)
12.8.4.4. Whenever possible, electric or battery powered equipment will be used indoors.
(T-1) Internal combustion engines will not be operated inside buildings unless an exhaust
system or other ventilation approved by installation BE is installed and used. (T-1)
Administrative areas, e.g., offices, must be protected from exhaust buildup either by
distance or positive air flow in relation to the warehouse activities. When internal
combustion engine equipment is used inside warehouses, installation BE personnel will
determine ventilation requirements to preclude exposure of employees to a hazardous
atmosphere. (T-1) Forklifts used in hazardous areas will meet the requirements for
designated locations as found in 29 CFR § 1910.178(c). (T-0) Refer to DESR
6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 for operating in explosives and weapons areas.
12.8.4.5. MHE powered by an internal combustion engine will have an adequate fire
extinguisher available. (T-1) Size, type and location will be determined by the F&ES Flight
and owning organization and will be based on the risk assessment for the operation
conducted. (T-1) Additional requirements may be mandated by DAF publications, TOs or
other directives for specific environments and operations.

94

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.8.4.6. When leaving powered lifts unattended, operators will fully lower the forks,
neutralize controls, shut power off and set brakes. (T-0) Wheels of the vehicle will be
chocked, if parked on an incline. (T-0)
12.8.4.7. Operators will use caution when traveling on docks or loading platforms and
remain clear of the edge. (T-0) Consider the use of spotters or signage where operator
experience or nature of work center could cause confusion.
12.8.4.8. Operators will not use forklifts to push or pull objects, such as freight cars, or to
open and close freight, hangar, or warehouse doors. (T-0) (Exception: DESR
6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 allows munitions containers with permanently installed skids
to be pushed or pulled for positioning. Ensure safety precautions, such as keeping personnel
clear of potentially hazardous areas, have been considered and included in the procedure.)
12.8.4.9. Operators will set brakes and dock locks or put wheel chocks in place to prevent
any movement of trucks, trailers or railroad cars while loading or unloading. (T-0)
12.8.4.10. Personnel will not stand or pass under the elevated portion of a lift when loaded
or empty. (T-1)
12.8.4.11. Operators who load and unload aircraft using materials handling and lifting
equipment will comply with TO 36M-1-141, Operator and Operation Instruction—
Material Handling Equipment System Components of 463L, TO 36M2-3-35-11, Operation
and Operator Maintenance Instructions, Truck, Aircraft Cargo Loading/Unloading 60,000
Pound Capacity, and TO 36M2-3-45-1, Halvorsen 25K Aircraft Cargo Loaders. (T-1)
12.8.4.12. Refer to DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 for operating material handling and
lifting equipment in explosives and weapons areas.
12.8.4.13. Forklifts will not be operated on an incline greater than that allowed by the
manufacturer. (T-0)
12.8.4.14. When traveling, operators will:
12.8.4.14.1. Observe all established driving procedures and will not exceed five miles
per hour inside warehouses. (T-1) Under normal traffic conditions, operators will keep
to the right and maintain a safe distance, approximately three truck lengths, from the
truck ahead. (T-0) Operators will always maintain safe control of trucks, especially
during turns and while traveling over slippery or wet floors and rough surfaces. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.2. Not pass other vehicles or pedestrians at intersections, blind corners or at
other dangerous locations. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.3. Slow down and sound the horn at cross aisles, warehouse entrances and
exits or at other dangerous locations where vision is obstructed. (T-0) Operators will
be prepared to stop and will not proceed until the way is clear. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.4. Travel with the load trailing when loads obstruct the forward view. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.5. Cross railroad tracks diagonally whenever possible to avoid jarring of the
load. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.6. Do not park closer than eight feet from the center of railroad tracks. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.7. Look in the direction of travel and keep a clear view. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

95

12.8.4.14.8. Drive a loaded lift with the load upgrade when traveling up or down ramps
or grades. (T-0) A spotter will be used if the operator’s vision is obscured by the load.
(T-0) Unloaded trucks will be operated on all grades with the forks downgrade. (T-0)
Caution will be used on all grades. (T-0) The load on the forks will be tilted back as far
as necessary to clear the road surface. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.9. Operate the lift at a speed that will permit it to stop in a safe manner. (T-0)
Horseplay or stunt driving will not be permitted. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.10. Not travel with lift forks elevated more than six inches above the ground
or surface or as necessary to clear surface projections. (T-1)
12.8.4.14.11. Tilt the mast back when transporting loads when operating a mast with
tilt capability. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.12. Make smooth starts, turns, and stops to prevent the load from shifting or
the truck from overturning. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.13. Ensure dock boards or bridge plates:
12.8.4.14.13.1. Are anchored or equipped with stops at both ends near the edges of
the platform of the car or truck to prevent them from sliding. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.13.2. Are strong enough to carry the load. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.13.3. Have handholds or other effective means to permit safe handling.
(T-0)
12.8.4.14.13.4. Are of proper width and length. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.13.5. Contain a nonskid surface. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.14. Approach elevators slowly and enter squarely after the elevator car is
properly level. (T-0) Once on the elevator, the operator will neutralize the controls,
shut off the power and set the brakes. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.15. Ensure motorized hand trucks enter elevators or other confined areas
with the load end first. (T-0)
12.8.4.14.16. Not permit passengers to ride on tractors unless adequate seats are
installed. (T-0)
12.8.5. Loading.
12.8.5.1. Only stable or safely arranged loads will be handled. (T-0) Caution will be
exercised when handling loads which cannot be centered. (T-0)
12.8.5.2. Only loads within the rated capacity of the truck will be handled. (T-0)
12.8.5.3. Long or high (including multiple-tiered) loads which may affect capacity will be
adjusted. (T-0)
12.8.6. Training.
12.8.6.1. Each activity requiring training on powered materials handling and lifting
equipment will designate qualified personnel as instructors to train and supervise the
operator trainee. (T-1) The names and grades of instructor candidates will be provided to

96

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
the vehicle operations officer who will thoroughly screen each for background and
experience in the type of equipment for which instruction will be given. (T-1) Refresher
training will be provided when an operator operates the vehicle in an unsafe manner, has a
vehicle mishap or near miss, needs additional training on a new type of truck, or changes
in the workplace that could affect safe operation. (T-1) Operators will be reevaluated every
three years to measure performance. (T-1)
12.8.6.2. Lesson plans for each vehicle will be accomplished IAW DAFI 24-301. (T-1)
Lesson plans (course content) will include formal instruction, hands-on demonstrations by
the instructor with practical exercises performed by the trainee, and an evaluation of the
trainee’s performance to confirm their knowledge of equipment operations and service
instructions. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 12.6 for additional guidance on documenting
training.
12.8.6.3. Powered industrial truck training will include:
12.8.6.3.1. Equipment design, to include restrictions, limitations, and hazards relative
to the environment where used, e.g., steering, maneuvering and visibility. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.2. Operating and maintenance instructions, warnings, and precautions,
including manufacturer’s instructions and those contained in this manual for the types
of trucks the operator will be authorized to operate. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.3. Safe loading and unloading requirements. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.4. Controls and instrumentation, where located, what they do and how they
work. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.5. Engine operation, steering and maneuvering. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.6. Operating techniques inside and outside of warehouses. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.7. Visibility and restrictions caused by loads and other concerns. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.8. Load capacity and stability. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.9. Conducting vehicle inspection and operator maintenance. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.10. Refueling and/or charging of batteries. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.11. Operating limitations. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.12. Flightline rules and regulations, when applicable. (T-1)
12.8.6.3.13. Fire extinguisher training (initial and annual). Refer to Chapter 6, Fire
Prevention and Protection, for additional guidance. (T-1)
12.8.6.3.14. Use of attachments such as extended forks or personnel lift pallets. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.15. Clearances, heights, surface conditions and limitations where vehicles
will be operated. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.16. Safety clothing and equipment requirements. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 14,
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE), for additional guidance.
12.8.6.3.17. Fire protection, fuel spills, maintenance of equipment, smoking, etc. Refer
to Chapter 6 for additional guidance. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

97

12.8.6.3.18. Operating restrictions in potentially hazardous storage areas, which
include flammable, toxic, lumber, coal, etc. Refer to Chapter 22, Flammables and
Combustibles, for additional guidance. (T-1)
12.8.6.3.19. How indoor environments and other areas with insufficient ventilation can
create a build-up of vehicle exhaust that could affect employee safety. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.20. Damage that may be caused by acid or caustic material. (T-1)
12.8.6.3.21. Workplace related topics on stacking, unstacking and load manipulation;
pedestrian traffic; and narrow aisles. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.22. Operation on ramps and other sloped surfaces that could affect vehicle
stability. (T-0)
12.8.6.3.23. Other unique or potentially hazardous environmental conditions in the
workplace that could affect safe operations. (T-0)
12.8.7. Maintenance.
12.8.7.1. Repairs to material handling equipment assets under Vehicle Management’s
control will be accomplished by Vehicle Maintenance (maintenance agreement may be
necessary for locally procured assets). (T-1) Deviations to this requirement will be
approved by host Vehicle Maintenance or vehicle maintenance contractor. (T-1)
12.8.7.2. Equipment will be cleaned in designated locations and only with cleaning agents
approved by the HMMP team and tracked through EESOH-MIS. (T-1)
12.9. Conveyors.
12.9.1. Safe Operations.
12.9.1.1. Employees will not operate conveyors unless all guards are in place. (T-0) Do
not take off guards at end of conveyor or from belts feeding the conveyor. Employees will
report defective or worn shafts, rollers, or bearings that may break and permit rollers to fall
from the frame. (T-0) Refer to ASME B20.1, Safety Standard for Conveyors and Related
Equipment, for further information concerning conveyors and Chapter 21 for additional
guidance on hazardous energy control requirements. Keep the area around them free of
obstructions to permit a clear view of the conveyor and ready access to the devices. (T-0)
12.9.1.2. Employees will not ride, step on or cross over conveyors except at designated
locations. (T-0) Do not operate unless all guards are in place and interlocks are operational.
(T-0) Inspect the conveyor system to keep loose objects and personnel away prior to
operation. (T-1) Ensure employees do not have loose clothing, jewelry, or long hair that
could be caught up in the conveyor. (T-0)
12.9.2. Inspections. Supervisors will ensure the entire conveyor system is inspected daily.
(T-1) Defective parts that affect safe operation of the system will be replaced prior to continued
operation. (T-0) Operators will ensure crossover walkway surfaces are in good condition. (T-0)
12.9.3. Maintenance. No work will be initiated on a power conveyor until hazardous energy
control procedures have been implemented. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 21 for additional guidance
on hazardous energy control procedures.

98

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

12.10. Manual Hoisting and Pulling Devices.
12.10.1. Safe Operations.
12.10.1.1. Lever-operated hoists will only be used in a direct pull. (T-0) Where indirect
pulls are permitted by design of the hoist, a sheave or pulley of adequate size will be used.
(T-0)
12.10.1.2. Hoist cables, ropes and chains will not be wrapped around the load. (T-0) Use
only slings or other approved lifting fixtures. (T-0) Example: Cargo straps will not be
used for lifting, unless stated in approved Air Force technical data. (T-1)
12.10.1.3. Positive action safety latches will be installed on all hooks. (T-0)
12.10.1.4. Hooks will not be point loaded unless designed for this purpose. All loads will
be seated in the saddle of the hook. (T-0)
12.10.1.5. Extensions to levers, such as cheater bars or pipes, unless designed and
permanently installed by the manufacturer, will not be used to increase leverage. (T-0)
12.10.1.6. Manually operated lever hoists will only be operated by personnel trained on
use of the equipment. (T-0) Operator qualifications will be as determined by the facility
manager, shop supervisor or designated representative. (T-2)
12.10.1.7. Capacity of all lever-operated devices will be permanently and conspicuously
marked in a readily visible place on the hoist. (T-0) The rated capacity will not be exceeded
except for authorized load tests. (T-0)
12.10.1.8. Hoists will be attached to well-defined dead-end points capable of withstanding
the intended load. (T-0) Note: Lifeline attach points will not be used for hoists. (T-0)
12.10.1.9. Operators must be careful to avoid pinch points during handling. (T-0)
12.10.2. Inspections.
12.10.2.1. Frequent. The inspection will include checking for:
12.10.2.1.1. Loose or worn parts, nuts, bolts, etc. (T-0)
12.10.2.1.2. Cracked or broken welds or parts. (T-0)
12.10.2.1.3. Damage to hooks (refer to Chapter 12.16.10.1). (T-0)
12.10.2.1.4. Bent or deformed pawls. (T-0)
12.10.2.1.5. Lubrication. (T-0)
12.10.2.1.6. Any additional items required by the manufacturer. (T-1)
12.10.2.2. Periodic.
12.10.2.2.1. Complete inspection of all wire rope, chain and fittings or attachments.
(T-0)
12.10.2.2.2. Inspection of brakes, pawls, or other holding features. (T-0)
12.10.2.2.3. Inspection of chain length. (T-0) Chains elongated more than ¼ inch in
12 inches will be removed from service. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

99

12.10.2.2.4. Any additional items required by the manufacturer. (T-1)
12.10.2.3. Identification tags will be attached to all hoists. Tags will include the following
information:
12.10.2.3.1. Date of annual inspection. (T-0)
12.10.2.3.2. Date of load test. (T-0)
12.10.2.3.3. Capacity of hoist. (T-0)
12.10.2.3.4. Identification number of hoist. (T-0)
12.10.3. Maintenance and testing will be performed IAW the manufacturer’s instructions or
applicable TO. (T-0)
12.11. Hoists. This section outlines basic requirements for power-operated hoists used for
vertical lifting and are not permanently mounted to other equipment, e.g., a crane, monorail or
derrick that serves as a supporting structure. (T-0)
12.11.1. Acquisition. Overhead hoists will be purchased from manufacturers whose products
meet the design specifications, characteristics, and rules of ASME B30.7, Winches, B30.10,
Hooks: Safety Standard for Cableways, Cranes, Derricks, Hoists, Hooks, Jacks, and Slings,
and B30.16, Overhead Underhung and Stationary Hoists. (T-0) When applicable, a system
safety program will be established. (T-1) Note: Nuclear-certified hoists must also meet
requirements in DAFMAN 91-118. (T-1)
12.11.2. Safety Requirements.
12.11.2.1. Hoist platforms will have suitable side and overhead protection to prevent
operator injury from a falling load. (T-0)
12.11.2.2. Standard operating signals, understood by operators and signalers, will be
posted on the equipment or conspicuously posted in the vicinity of hoisting operations.
(T-0)
12.11.2.3. Hoist engines and motors will be guarded to protect personnel. (T-0)
12.11.2.4. Self-locking brakes, also known as holding brakes, will be installed on all
hoists. (T-0)
12.11.2.4.1. 125 percent when used with a control braking means other than
mechanical.
12.11.2.4.2. 100 percent when used in conjunction with a mechanical control braking
means.
12.11.2.4.3. 100 percent each if two holding brakes are provided.
12.11.2.5. The conductors and switches of electric hoists will be guarded against
accidental contact. (T-0)
12.11.2.6. All loads will be balanced on hoist carriages and secured to prevent slipping or
shifting. (T-0)
12.11.2.7. Latch-type safety hooks will be installed on all hoists. (T-0)

100

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.11.2.8. Slack Chain. A chain container will be provided where slack chain hanging
from a hoist may be a hazard. (T-1)
12.11.2.9. Load Rating. The rated capacity will be permanently marked on the hoist or its
load block and must be legible from the operating position. (T-0) The rated capacity for
each member of the suspension device, when used as intended, will be equal to the other
suspension members to which it is attached. (T-0) When this is not feasible, a plan will be
developed to identify special precautions that will be taken to ensure the lowest rated
suspension member’s rated capacity is not exceeded. (T-0)
12.11.2.10. Warnings. Supervisor will ensure information concerning operating
procedures will be posted by all hoists or displayed on a label affixed to the hoist, controls,
or block. (T-1) This will also include cautions regarding:
12.11.2.10.1. Lifting more than the rated capacity. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.2. Operating the hoist when the hook is not centered under the hoist. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.3. Operating a damaged or malfunctioning hoist. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.4. Operating a rope hoist with a rope not properly seated in its groove. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.5. Lifting people or loads over people. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.6. Operating the hoist with twisted, kinked, or damaged chain or rope. (T-0)
12.11.2.10.7. Removing or obscuring the warning label. (T-0)
12.11.2.11. Pendants. The pendant station will be supported by a cable, chain or rope that
will protect the electrical conductors against strain. (T-0) Pendant control stations will be
constructed to prevent electrical shock and will be clearly marked for identification of
functions. (T-0)
12.11.2.12. Support. The supporting structure of the hoist, including tripod, trolley,
monorail, crane, or facility beam will be designed to withstand the load and forces imposed
by the hoist. (T-0)
12.11.2.13. Location. The hoist will be installed to give the operator room to always
remain clear of the load and full view of the load hook in all positions. (T-0)
12.11.2.14. Remote Operated Hoists. Remote operated hoists will function so that if the
central signal for any hoist motion becomes ineffective, hoist motion will stop. (T-0)
12.11.2.15. Upper Limit Switches. Upper limit switches will be installed and operable on
all powered hoists. (T-1)
12.11.3. Safe Operations.
12.11.3.1. Qualifications of Operators. Hoist will only be operated by trained personnel or
trainees under the direct supervision of a qualified trainer/instructor. (T-0) The operator
will be familiar with the equipment and its proper care. (T-0)
12.11.3.2. The rated capacity will not be exceeded except for properly authorized tests.
(T-0) If it is known or suspected that a hoist may have been overloaded (other than a
required and approved test load), the supervisor will ensure that all frequent and periodic
inspections are completed prior to use. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

101

12.11.3.3. When an AF Form 979, AF Form 981, AF Form 982, Do Not Start, or AF Form
983, Danger – Equipment Lockout tag is attached to the starting controls, the operator will
not apply power to the unit or start operations until the condition has been corrected and
the tag and any lock(s) are removed. (T-0)
12.11.3.4. If adjustments or repairs are necessary or any damage is observed or suspected,
the operator will promptly report the problem to the supervisor. (T-0) Equipment identified
as damaged will not be used until repaired. (T-0)
12.11.3.5. Prior to starting hoist, operator will ensure all personnel are clear of the area.
(T-0)
12.11.3.6. Operators who must divert their attention while operating the hoist will stop the
hoist. (T-0)
12.11.3.7. Operators will ensure their hands are clear of all moving parts prior to hoist
operation. (T-0)
12.11.3.8. Handling the load.
12.11.3.8.1. The hoisting rope or chain will not be wrapped around the load. (T-0)
12.11.3.8.2. The load will be attached to the hook, equipped with a safety latch, by
means of slings, or other devices designed specifically for the load lifted. (T-0)
12.11.3.8.3. Slings or other devices will be seated properly in the saddle of the hook
before lift operations start. (T-0)
12.11.3.8.4. The load will not be raised more than a few inches until it is well balanced
in a sling or lifting device and center of gravity is known. (T-0)
12.11.3.8.5. Tag lines will be attached to a lifted load for purposes of controlling load
spinning and pendular motions or used to stabilize a bucket or magnet during material
handling operations. (T-0)
12.11.3.9. Hoisting the load. Ensure that:
12.11.3.9.1. Hoist ropes or chains are not twisted about each other. (T-0)
12.11.3.9.2. The load does not contact any obstruction. (T-0)
12.11.3.9.3. Ropes or chains are protected against sharp edges of the load. (T-0)
12.11.3.10. The rope or chain will be properly seated on the drum sheaves or sprockets
before starting the lift. (T-0) Hoists will not be operated until the hoist unit is centered over
the load. (T-0)
12.11.3.11. A hoist will not be used for hoisting personnel unless specifically designed for
this purpose and only if it is the safest means of accomplishing the work. (T-0) Loads will
not be carried over personnel. (T-0)
12.11.3.12. The operator will test the brakes each time a load is handled by raising the load
just enough to clear the floor or supports and checking for brake action. (T-0) The lift will
be continued only after ensuring the braking system is operating properly. (T-0)
12.11.3.13. The operator will not leave their position at the controls with a load suspended.
(T-0)

102

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.11.3.14. If a load must remain suspended for a considerable time, a pawl, or other
equivalent means, rather than the brake alone, will be used to hold the load. (T-0) An
operator will be at the controls while the load is suspended. (T-0)
12.11.3.15. Excessive jogging or inching (rapid, repeated starts and stops) of hoist controls
will be avoided. (T-0) Premature wear or possible uncontrolled movement could occur.
12.11.4. Maintenance.
12.11.4.1. Preventive Maintenance. The using organization will establish a preventive
maintenance program based on the manufacturer’s recommendations. (T-1) If
manufacturer’s instructions cannot be obtained, the using organization will contact the
DAF equipment item manager for assistance. (T-1) Preventive maintenance will be
documented IAW Chapter 12.2.1, as appropriate. (T-1)
12.11.4.2. Lubrication. All moving parts of the hoist for which lubrication is specified will
be regularly lubricated. (T-0) Manufacturer’s or TO recommendations for points and
frequency of lubrication and quantity and type of lubricant to be used will be followed.
(T-0) Machinery will be stationary, with energy sources locked out, while lubricants are
applied. (T-0)
12.11.4.3. Rope Replacement and Maintenance. Items requiring replacement or
maintenance will be IAW technical data, manufacturer’s instructions. Additional
information can be found in ASME B30.
12.11.4.4. Adjustments and Repairs. Repair, replacements, or adjustments will be made as
necessary to ensure correct performance of all hoist components. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.1. Replace all worn braking components such as friction discs, ratchets,
pawls, and pawl springs. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.2. Replace worn, corroded, or otherwise damaged load chain and rope. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.3. Replace hooks showing defects. Repair or replace damaged hook safety
latches. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.4. Replace load supporting components which are cracked, bent, or
excessively worn. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.5. Replace missing or illegible warning labels. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.6. Replace pitted or burned electrical contacts in sets only. (T-0) Lubricate
controller parts as recommended by the manufacturer. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.7. Keep pendant control stations clean and functions labels legible. (T-0)
12.11.4.4.8. Adjust all functional operating mechanisms, brakes and pawls, limit
switches and other limiting devices to ensure correct functioning. (T-0)
12.11.4.5. Maintenance Procedures. The following precautions will be taken before
adjustments or repairs are started on a hoist:
12.11.4.5.1. Use of lockout device(s) and AF Form 983 will be placed on the hoist and
all energy controls (air, electrical, hydraulic, etc.) IAW Chapter 21 hazardous energy
control requirements when adjustments, modifications, or repairs are scheduled. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

103

The hoist operator will not apply power to the equipment or start operations until the
conditions have been corrected and the tag and lock removed. Refer to Chapter 21 for
additional guidance. (T-0)
12.11.4.5.2. Drum pawls will be engaged, or other means provided to prevent load
ropes from inadvertently rotating the mechanism. (T-0)
12.12. Below-the-Hook Devices.
12.12.1. All below-the-hook devices, defined as the devices used to attach a load to the hook
of a hoist, such as slings and other rigging will be inspected, used and maintained IAW
manufacturer requirements. (T-1) Refer to ANSI/ASME B30.20, Below-the-Hook Lifting
Devices, for additional information.
12.12.2. Slings. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.184 and ASME B30.9 for additional guidance. Note:
Locally manufactured slings that do not meet the engineering requirements of ASME B30.9
are prohibited and will be disassembled and removed from service. (T-1) Note: Written proof
test certification is not required for new synthetic rope slings manufactured IAW ASME B30.9
and not used to lift nuclear or critical loads. (T-1)
12.12.2.1. DAF primarily uses four types of slings: ally steel chain, wire rope, metal mesh
and synthetic web slings. Note: Refer to OSHA’s Guidance on Safe Sling Use located at:
https://www.osha.gov/dsg/guidance/slings/tables-figures.html, for guidance on other
types of slings. Refer to ASME B30.9 for additional information.
12.12.2.2. Ensure manufacturers certify that the required markings and identifications for
each type of sling are provided. Refer to ASME B30.9 for additional information.
12.12.2.3. Prior to use, each new, repaired or reconditioned alloy steel chain sling,
including all welded components in the sling assembly, will be proof tested by the
manufacturer or equivalent entity and certificate of proof test maintained and available for
inspection. (T-0) Periodic load testing will only be accomplished with written permission
and procedural guidance from the sling manufacturer or equivalent, e.g., the specific
equipment TO or the item manager for locally manufactured equipment. (T-1)
12.12.2.4. Specific Requirements.
12.12.2.4.1. Alloy Steel Chain Slings.
12.12.2.4.1.1. Prior to use, all new, repaired, or reconditioned alloy steel chain
slings, including all welded components in the sling assembly will be proof tested
by the sling manufacturer or equivalent entity to certify capacity. The employer will
retain a copy of the manufacturers certificate of the proof test and will make it
available for examination. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.2. For multiple leg slings, each leg will be proof tested by the sling
manufacturer or equivalent entity and certifying compliance with ASTM
Specifications A391-65. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.3. Master links and master coupling links for double or triple leg slings
will be proof tested by the sling manufacturer or equivalent entity and certifying
compliance with ASTM Specification A391-65. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.4. Hooks, rings, oblong or pear-shaped links, welded or mechanical

104

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
coupling links or other attachments will have a rated capacity at least equal to that
of the alloy steel chain with which they are used. (T-0) The sling will not be proof
tested more than the rated capacity of the weakest component. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.5. Unauthorized makeshift links or fasteners, such as those formed
from bolts or rods, will not be used. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.6. Alloy steel chain slings will not be used with loads more than the
rated capacities. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.7. Worn or damaged alloy steel chain slings and attachments will not
be used until repaired (or reconditioned) and proof tested by the manufacturer or
equivalent entity. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.8. Mechanical coupling links or low carbon steel repair links will not
be used to repair broken lengths of chain. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.9. If the chain size at any point of any link is less than that stated in
Table 12.6 the sling will be removed from service. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.10. Alloy steel chain slings with cracked or deformed master links,
coupling links, or other components will be removed from service. (T-0)
12.12.2.4.1.11. Slings will be removed from service if hooks are cracked, have any
visibly apparent bend or twist from the plane of the unbent hook (original
manufacturer or baseline measurements), any distortion causing an increase in
throat opening of 5 percent, not to exceed ¼ inch (or as recommended by the
manufacturer), or any wear exceeding 10 percent (or as recommended by the
manufacturer) or the original manufacturer or baseline section dimension of the
hook. (T-0)
12.12.2.5. Wire Rope Slings.
12.12.2.5.1. Prior to initial use, all new wire rope slings will be proof tested by the
manufacturer or equivalent entity. (T-0) The certificate provided by the manufacturer
will be kept on file for review. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.2. Wire rope slings will not be used with loads exceeding capacities. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.2.1. Cable laid and 6 x 19 and 6 x 37 slings will have a minimum clear
length of wire rope between splices, sleeves, or end fittings 10 times the component
rope diameter. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.3. Braided slings will have a minimum clear length of wire rope between
loops or end fittings 40 times the component rope diameter. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.4. Cable laid grommets, strand laid grommets, and endless slings will have
a minimum circumferential length of 96 times their body diameter. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.5. Fiber core wire rope slings of all grades will be permanently removed
from service if exposed to temperatures more than 200o F. (T-0) When non-fiber core
wire rope slings of any grade are used above 400o F or below -60o F, sling manufacturer
recommendations regarding use at that temperature will be followed. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

105

12.12.2.5.6. Welding of end attachments, except covers to thimbles, will be welded
before the sling is assembled. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.7. Welded end attachments will be proof tested at twice their capacity by the
manufacturer or equivalent entity prior to use. (T-0) The owning agency will maintain
a record or certificate of proof test and make it available for inspection. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8. Wire rope slings will be removed from service if any of the following
conditions are present: (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8.1. Ten randomly distributed broken wires in one rope lay or five
broken wires in one strand in one rope lay. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8.2. Wear or scraping of a third (1/3) of the original diameter of outside
individual wires. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8.3. Kinking, crushing, bird caging or any other damage that distorts the
wire rope structure. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8.4. Evidence of heat damage. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.8.5. Cracked, deformed, or worn end attachments. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.9. Sling hooks that have any visibly apparent bend or twist from the plane
of the unbent hook (using original manufacturer or baseline measurements), any
distortion causing an increase in throat opening of 5 percent, not to exceed 1/4 inch (or
as recommended by the manufacturer), or any wear exceeding 10 percent (or as
recommended by the hook manufacturer) of the original manufacturer or baseline
section dimension of the hook. (T-1)
12.12.2.5.10. Corrosion of rope or end attachments. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.11. Only new ropes will be used to fabricate slings. (T-0) Use of repaired or
reconditioned rope is prohibited. (T-0)
12.12.2.5.12. Slings used in a choker hitch will be long enough, so the choke point is
on the rope and never on a splice. (T-0)
12.12.2.6. Metal Mesh Slings.
12.12.2.6.1. All new and repaired metal mesh slings, including handles, will be proof
tested at a minimum of 150 percent of their rated capacity before use. (T-0) Elastomer
impregnated slings will be proof tested before they are coated. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.2. Handles will have a rated capacity at least equal to the metal fabric and
exhibit no deformation after proof testing. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.3. The fabric and handles will be joined so that:
12.12.2.6.3.1. The rated capacity of the sling is not reduced. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.3.2. The load is evenly distributed across the width of the fabric. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.3.3. Sharp edges will not damage the fabric. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.3.4. Coatings which damage the sling and diminish the rated capacity
will not be applied. (T-0)

106

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.12.2.6.4. Except for required proof tests, metal mesh slings will not be used to lift
loads exceeding their rated capacities. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.5. Metal mesh slings not impregnated with elastomers may be used from
minus 20o F to 550o F without decreasing the working load limit. (T-0) Metal mesh
slings impregnated with polyvinyl chloride or neoprene may be used only from 0o F to
200o F. (T-0) For operations outside these temperature ranges or for metal mesh slings
impregnated with other materials, the sling manufacturer’s recommendations will be
followed. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6. Metal mesh slings will be removed from service if any of the following
conditions are present:
12.12.2.6.6.1. Broken weld or broken brazed joint along the sling edge. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.2. Reduction in wire diameter of 25 percent due to abrasion or 15
percent due to corrosion. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.3. Lack of flexibility due to distortion of the fabric. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.4. Distortion of the female handle so slot depth is increased more than
10 percent. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.5. Distortion of either handle so width is decreased more than 10
percent. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.6. Reduction of 15 percent of the original cross-sectional area of metal
at any point around the handle eye. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.7. Distortion of either handle out of its plane. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.8. Cracked end fitting. (T-0)
12.12.2.6.6.9. A broken wire in any part of the mesh. (T-0)
12.12.2.7. Synthetic Web Slings.
12.12.2.7.1. Prior to use, all new and repaired synthetic web slings will be proof tested
to twice their rated capacity by the manufacturer or equivalent entity. A copy of the
manufacturer certification will be maintained for inspection. Note: Written proof test
certification is not required for new synthetic rope slings certified by the manufacturer
to meet ASME B30.9 and not used to lift nuclear or critical loads. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.2. Synthetic webbing will be of uniform thickness and width, and selvage
edges will not be split from the webbing’s width. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.3. Fittings will be:
12.12.2.7.3.1. Of a minimum breaking strength equal to that of the sling. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.3.2. Free of all sharp edges that could damage the webbing. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.4. Stitching will be the only method used to attach end fittings to webbing
and to form eyes. (T-0) The thread will have an even pattern and contain enough
stitches to develop the full breaking strength of the sling. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

107

12.12.2.7.5. Except for required proof tests, synthetic web slings will not be used with
loads exceeding the specified rated capacities IAW manufacturer’s requirements and
as found in Tables 12.2 – 12.4. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6. When synthetic web slings are used, the following precautions will be
taken:
12.12.2.7.6.1. Nylon web slings will not be used where acid or phenolic fumes,
vapors, sprays, mists, or liquids are present. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.2. Polyester and polypropylene web slings will not be used where
caustic fumes, vapors, sprays, mists, or liquids of acids are present. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.3. Web slings with aluminum fittings will not be used where caustic
fumes, vapors, sprays, mists, or liquids are present. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.4. Synthetic web slings of polyester and nylon will not be used at
temperatures exceeding 180o F. Polypropylene web slings will not be used at
temperatures more than 200o F. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.5. Slings, including webbing and fittings, with temporary or makeshift
repairs will not be used. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.6. Synthetic web slings will be removed from service if any of the
following conditions are present:
12.12.2.7.6.6.1. Damage from acid or caustic materials. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.6.2. Melting or charring of any part of sling surface. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.6.3. Snags, punctures, tears, or cuts. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.6.4. Broken or worn stitches. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.6.5. Distortion of fittings. (T-0)
12.12.2.7.6.7. When not in use, slings will be stored in clean, dry areas that will
protect the sling materials. (T-0)
12.13. Overhead, Gantry and Underhung Cranes and Monorail Systems. This paragraph
outlines requirements for overhead and gantry cranes, including top-running single or multiple
girder bridge with top-running trolley hoists, top-running single-girder bridge with underhung
hoists and monorails/underhung cranes and others having the same fundamental characteristics.
Hoist requirements of paragraph 12.11 are applicable in this chapter. Hoist requirements of this
chapter also apply to hoists used as components of cranes or monorail systems.
12.13.1. Acquisition.
12.13.1.1. All new overhead and gantry cranes constructed and installed on or after 31
August 1971 will be certified by the manufacturer to meet ASME B30.2, Overhead and
Gantry Cranes (Top Running Bridge, Single or Multiple Girder, Top Running Trolley
Hoist), and ASME B30.11, Monorails and Underhung Cranes. Cranes constructed before
31 August 1971 will be modified to conform to these design specifications, unless a
qualified person can show the crane cannot feasibly or economically be altered and
substantially complies with requirements of this standard. (T-0) Contracts for

108

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
procurements of overhead and gantry cranes will mandate compliance with CMAA 70,
Specification for Top Running Bridge and Gantry Type Multiple Girder Electric Overhead
Traveling Cranes, or CMAA 74, Specification for Top Running and Under Running Single
Girder Electric Overhead Cranes Utilizing Under Running Trolley Hoist, as applicable,
and appropriate ANSI/ASME B30 series standards. (T-1) Note: Nuclear-certified hoists
must also meet requirements in DAFMAN 91-118. (T-1)
12.13.1.2. Hazards will be identified by a system safety analysis, MIL-STD-882E, DoD
Standard Practice for System Safety, or commercial equivalent. (T-1) The procuring
activity will specify the required analyses, such as preliminary hazard analysis or operating
hazard analysis. (T-1) The results of these analyses will be included in applicable contract
deliverable documents. (T-1)
12.13.2. General.
12.13.2.1. Cranes may be modified and re-rated, if the modifications and the supporting
structures are checked thoroughly by a qualified engineer or the equipment manufacturer.
The new rated capacity will be displayed on the crane or hoist. (T-0)
12.13.2.2. The rated capacity of the crane will be plainly marked on each side of the crane.
(T-0) If the crane has more than one hoisting unit, each unit will have its rated capacity
marked on it or its load block, and this marking will be clearly visible from the floor or
ground. (T-0)
12.13.2.3. A minimum clearance of three inches overhead and two inches laterally will be
maintained between crane and obstructions. (T-0)
12.13.2.4. Where passageways or walkways are provided, obstructions will not be placed
so the safety of personnel is jeopardized by movements of the crane. (T-0)
12.13.2.5. The general arrangement of the cab and location of control and protective
equipment will be such that all operating handles are within convenient reach of the
operator when facing the area served by the load hook or while facing the direction of travel
of the cab. (T-0) The arrangement will allow the operator a full view of the load hook in
all positions. (T-0)
12.13.2.6. Pendant and cab hoist controls for trolley and bridge movement will use
compass points (north, south, east, and west) as the preferred identification. (T-0)
12.13.2.7. Hoists utilizing synchronous controls for multiple point lifting movements will
be of failsafe design to preclude inadvertent operation caused by malfunctions of selector
switches, power failure, or improper sequencing of controls. (T-0)
12.13.2.8. Access to the cab and bridge walkway will be by a conveniently placed fixed
ladder, stairs, or platform requiring no step over any gap exceeding 12 inches. (T-0) Fixed
ladders will be designed and installed IAW 29 CFR § 1910.27, Fixed Ladders. (T-0) Also,
refer to Chapter 7, Walking – Working Surfaces, for specific ladder requirements.
12.13.2.9. A carbon dioxide or dry chemical (or equivalent) fire extinguisher rated at least
10 BC will be kept in the cab. (T-0)
12.13.2.10. Pendant control boxes and fixed control stations will be constructed to prevent
electrical shock and be clearly and legibly marked for identification of functions. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

109

12.13.2.11. Except for floor-operated cranes, a gong, buzzer, or other effective warning
signal will be provided for each power traveling crane. (T-0)
12.13.2.12. The hoist of all electric traveling cranes will have an over-travel limit switch
in the hoisting direction. (T-0)
12.13.2.13. Emergency descent means will be provided on all crane cabs. (T-0) Rope hand
line is not acceptable. Only approved controlled descent devices will be used. (T-0)
12.13.2.14. Crane operators will not be placed in situations dangerous to life, health or
which may pose safety hazards. (T-0)
12.13.2.15. Lift eyes or lift points used to attach slings, clevis, shackles, or hooks will be
constructed of forged or alloy steel. (T-0) Rebar steel will not be permitted as attach points
for any load. (T-0)
12.13.2.16. Monorails Hoists. Monorail hoists will not be used to move objects by pulling
sideways, unless designed for that purpose. (T-0) Each trolley fame will be safeguarded
against spreading. (T-0) Monorail track and track supports will be installed IAW
manufacturer’s installation specifications and capable of safely carrying intended loads.
(T-0) Rail stops will be provided at ends of the monorail track and will extend at least as
high as the radius of the wheels. (T-0) At switches, turntables and transfer tables, automatic
bumpers will drop into position to prevent the trolley running off open ends of fixed and
movable track if not properly lined up with each other. (T-0) Conversely, the track will be
interlocked with the bumpers so the track cannot move until rail stops are in position. (T-0)
12.13.3. Cab Operated and Pulpit Operated Equipment.
12.13.3.1. Safe Operations.
12.13.3.1.1. Equipment will be operated only by a qualified person, trainees under the
direct supervision of a qualified person, or maintenance and test personnel, when
necessary to perform duties. (T-0)
12.13.3.1.2. Operators will continue to demonstrate the skills and knowledge, as well
as the ability to recognize and avert risk, necessary to operate the equipment safely,
including those specific to the safety devices, operational aids, software, and the size
and configuration of the equipment. (T-0)
12.13.3.1.3. Operators will demonstrate the continued ability to perform the hoisting
activities required for assigned work, including, if applicable, blind lifts, personnel
hoisting, and multi-crane lifts. (T-0)
12.13.3.2. Equipment will only be operated by:
12.13.3.2.1. Persons qualified to do so. (T-0)
12.13.3.2.2. Trainees under the direct supervision of a qualified instructor. (T-0)
12.13.3.2.3. Maintenance and test personnel when it is necessary in performance of
their duties. (T-0)
12.13.3.2.4. Qualified inspectors. (T-0)

110

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.13.3.2.5. No one, other than personnel specified above, will enter a cab or pulpit,
except for maintainers and supervisors whose duties require them to do so, and then
only when performing their duties and with the knowledge of the operator or appointed
person. (T-0)
12.13.4. Floor-Operated Equipment. Equipment will only be operated by:
12.13.4.1. A qualified person. (T-0)
12.13.4.2. Trainees under the direct supervision of a qualified person. (T-0)
12.13.4.3. Maintenance and test personnel when it is necessary in the performance of their
duties. (T-0)
12.13.4.4. Qualified inspectors. (T-0)
12.13.5. Operating Practices.
12.13.5.1. Operators must not divert their attention while engaged in operating the
equipment. (T-0)
12.13.5.2. An operator will not operate equipment when physically or mentally unfit.
(T-0)
12.13.5.3. The operator will respond to signals only from the person directing the lift or a
designated signal person and must obey all stop signals. (T-0)
12.13.5.4. Each operator will be held directly responsible for the safe operation of the
equipment. (T-1) When safety is in doubt, the operator will stop and refuse to handle loads
until safety has been ensured. (T-1)
12.13.5.5. A warning signal will be sounded each time before traveling and intermittently
during travel, particularly when approaching personnel. (T-0)
12.13.5.6. Before leaving the equipment unattended, the operator will lower the load to
the ground, place controls in the “OFF” position, and open the main line switch of the
equipment. (T-0)
12.13.5.7. If the operator finds the main or emergency switch open (off) when starting on
duty, he or she will not close it (turn it on) until making certain no one is on or about the
equipment. (T-0) If there is a warning tag on the main switch or the switch is locked out,
only the supervisor or person placing the tag or lock will remove it as prescribed in
Chapter 21, Hazardous Energy Control (Lockout and Tagout). (T-0)
12.13.5.8. Before closing the main switch, the operator will ensure all controllers are in
the “OFF” position. (T-0)
12.13.5.9. If power goes off during operation, the operator will immediately move all
controllers to the “OFF” position. (T-0)
12.13.5.10. The operator will be familiar with the equipment and its proper care. (T-1) If
adjustments or repairs are necessary (or any damage is observed) the operator will report
it promptly to the appointed person and notify the next operator of the deficiencies when
changing shifts. (T-1) The results of the above will be carefully recorded in the logbook,
in full detail, and will be dated and signed. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

111

12.13.5.11. Contacts with stops or other equipment will be made with extreme caution and
only after all persons on or below equipment are aware of the action. (T-1)
12.13.5.12. Before departing the work area, operators of outdoor cranes will secure them
to prevent inadvertent movement. (T-0)
12.13.5.13. When a wind-indicating alarm is given, the bridge on outside cranes will be
anchored. (T-0)
12.13.5.14. Before performing any maintenance work, the operator or maintainer will lock
the main switch in the open position. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 21 for assistance in
developing hazardous energy control procedures.
12.13.5.15. All controls will be tested by the operator when beginning a new shift except
when an operation is in progress. (T-0) If any controls do not operate properly, they will
be adjusted or repaired before operations begin. (T-0)
12.13.6. Handling the Load.
12.13.6.1. Equipment will not be loaded beyond rated capacity except for tests IAW
paragraph 12.7. (T-1)
12.13.6.2. When attaching the load, ensure:
12.13.6.2.1. Hoist chain or hoist rope is free from kinks or twists and not wrapped
around the load. (T-0)
12.13.6.2.2. Load is attached to load hook by below-the-hook devices. (T-0)
12.13.6.2.3. Care is taken to ensure sling clears all obstacles. (T-0)
12.13.6.2.4. Ensure below-the-hook devices are seated properly in the hook saddle
before operation. (T-0)
12.13.7. When moving the load:
12.13.7.1. Appointed person directing the lift will ensure the load is secured and properly
balanced in the below-the-hook lifting device before lifting more than a few inches. (T-0)
12.13.7.2. Before starting to lift, check that:
12.13.7.2.1. Hoist rope or chain is not kinked or twisted. (T-0)
12.13.7.2.2. Multiple part lines are not twisted around each other. (T-0)
12.13.7.2.3. The hook is brought over the load in a way that prevents swinging. (T-0)
12.13.7.2.4. The rope is seated in drum grooves and sheaves if there is or has been a
slack rope condition. (T-0)
12.13.7.3. Tag lines will be used on all free-swinging loads. (T-0)
12.13.7.4. During hoisting, care will be taken that:
12.13.7.4.1. There is no sudden acceleration or deceleration of the moving load. (T-0)
12.13.7.4.2. Load does not contact any obstructions. (T-0)

112

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.13.7.4.3. Equipment is not used for side pulls except when specifically authorized
by a person qualified to make that decision and who has determined the suitability of
the equipment. (T-0)
12.13.7.5. The operator does not hoist, lower or travel while anyone is on the load or hook
unless specifically recommended by the manufacturer, approved by MAJCOM/FLDCOM,
DRU or FOA, and so indicated on a permanent name plate attached to the hoist. (T-0)
Refer to 29 CFR § 1926.1400, Cranes and Derricks in Construction, for additional
guidance.
12.13.7.6. The operator does not carry loads over personnel. (T-0)
12.13.7.7. The operator tests the brakes each time a load is handled by raising the load two
inches above floor or ground level and applying for brakes. (T-0)
12.13.7.8. The load is not lowered beyond two full wraps of rope remain on the hoisting
drum. (T-0) Exception: One wrap may remain on drum if a lower limit device is provided.
12.13.7.9. When two or more cranes are used to lift one load, one designated person will
be responsible for the operation. (T-0) That designated person will analyze the operation
and instruct all personnel involved in proper positioning, rigging of load and movements
to be made. (T-0)
12.13.7.10. The operator will not leave their position at the controls while the load is
suspended. (T-0)
12.13.7.11. A warning signal will be given when starting the equipment and when the load
or hook approaches near personnel. (T-0)
12.13.7.12. Appropriate clearance will be maintained between electrical power sources
and any part of the crane. (T-0)
12.13.8. Signals and Instruction. Signals to the operator will be IAW Figure 12.1 and Figure
12.2 and be conspicuously posted on or immediately near all cranes (T-0) Voice
communication equipment (telephone, radio or equivalent) will be fully operational when used.
(T-0) Signals and instructions will always be distinct. (T-0)
12.13.9. Miscellaneous Requirements.
12.13.9.1. Ladders and Footwalks.
12.13.9.1.1. Employees will keep hands unencumbered while using ladders. (T-0)
12.13.9.1.2. Articles too large to be carried in pockets or belts will be lifted and
lowered by hand line. (T-0)
12.13.9.1.3. Footwalks will be kept free of loose tools, parts, or other tripping hazards.
(T-0)
12.13.9.2. Cabs.
12.13.9.2.1. Necessary clothing and personal belongings will be stored so they do not
interfere with access or operation. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

113

12.13.9.2.2. Tools will be stored in approved toolboxes. (T-0) Wastes will be disposed
of in appropriate containers. (T-0) No loose articles will be left in or about the cab.
(T-0)
12.13.9.2.3. Fire Extinguishers. Supervisors will ensure operators are familiar with the
operation and care of fire extinguishers provided. (T-0)
12.14. Mobile and Locomotive Cranes. All crawler, locomotive, and truck cranes will be
certified by the manufacturer to meet design specifications, characteristics and rules of ASME
B30.5, Mobile and Locomotive Cranes. (T-1)
12.14.1. Specific Requirements.
12.14.1.1. A substantial and durable rating chart with clearly legible letters and figures
will be securely fixed to the crane cab in a location easily visible to the operator while
seated at the control station. (T-0) A duplicate load rating chart (legible from the ground)
will also be provided on the outside of the crane. (T-0) The load rating chart for truck
cranes will list loads for the crane operating both with and without counterweights. (T-0)
12.14.1.2. Two-Blocking Prevention. A two-blocking damage preventive feature will be
provided on telescoping boom cranes to prevent damage to the hoist rope or other machine
components when hoisting the load, extending the boom, or lowering the boom on a crane
with a stationary hoist mounted to the rear of the boom hinge. (T-0)
12.14.1.3. Boom Angle Indicator. A boom angle indicator will be provided on all cranes.
(T-0)
12.14.1.4. Overload Protection. Devices such as “Load Moment Indicators” are designed
to alarm the operator and de-energize the crane when attempting to lift a load beyond the
crane’s capabilities. The user must evaluate anticipated use of the crane to determine the
need for such a device. (T-0)
12.14.1.5. Color Coding for Strike Hazard. Parts of crane cabs that extend beyond the main
chassis when rotated will be color coded yellow and black on the lower areas to highlight
the strike hazard to ground personnel. (T-0) Colors will be reflective for night operations.
(T-0)
12.14.1.6. Exposed moving parts such as gears, set screws, projecting keys, chains, chain
sprockets and reciprocating parts will be guarded, as they may create a hazard to personnel.
(T-0)
12.14.1.7. Some crane hazards, such as rotating equipment or holes, cannot be
mechanically safeguarded, a risk assessment will be conducted by supervision to evaluate
other means of protecting employees from identified hazards. (T-1) (Note: Contact the
occupational safety office for assistance.) Personnel will follow manufacturers’
requirements and identified controls resulting from risk assessment and exercise care when
exposed to unguarded rotating equipment or holes. (T-1) Such conditions will be clearly
marked with appropriate warning decals. (T-0)
12.14.1.8. Cranes will be designed to protect operators from falling objects, swinging
loads and cable failures. (T-0) Cranes must only be operated under weather conditions that
are determined to ensure safe operation. (T-0)

114

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.14.1.9. An audible warning device will be provided when hoisting operations or
moving equipment might endanger personnel in the work area. (T-0) The device control
will be within easy reach of the operator. (T-0)
12.14.1.9.1. Warning sign. A permanent sign will be posted in the crane cab in full
view of the operator. (T-0) This sign will read, “DANGER HIGH VOLTAGE, Do Not
Operate Within 10 Feet of Electric Lines.” (T-0)
12.14.1.9.2. Night Operations. Cranes operated during darkness will have clearance
lights installed. (T-1) Working areas will be illuminated so the designated signal
person, loads, rigging, obstructions, etc., are readily visible. (T-0)
12.14.2. Safe Operating Practices.
12.14.2.1. The operator will sound or give an audible warning signal each time before
traveling (backing), and when approaching employees or other congested areas. (T-1) The
signal may be mechanical (automatic) or oral.
12.14.2.2. During periods of non-use, high winds, or weather alerts, the operator will lower
the boom to ground level, a resting platform or otherwise ensure the boom is secure against
movement from wind or other forces. (T-1)
12.14.2.3. Operators must be familiar with the equipment and its proper care. (T-0) If
adjustments or repairs are necessary or any damage is known, the operator will report them
promptly to the supervisor and document them IAW paragraph 12.2.1, as appropriate.
(T-1)
12.14.2.4. All controls will be tested by the operator at the start of a new shift. (T-0)
Controls that do not operate properly will be adjusted or repaired before continuing
operation. (T-0) No crane will be loaded beyond its rated capacity, except for test purposes.
(T-0)
12.14.2.5. Weight of all loads will be determined before lifting. (T-1)
12.14.2.6. The crane will not be operated while anyone is on the load or hook. (T-0)
12.14.2.7. The operator will not carry or swing load over personnel. (T-0)
12.14.2.8. On truck mounted cranes, loads will not be lifted over the front area of the truck
unless specifically allowed by manufacturer’s operating instructions. (T-1)
12.14.2.9. When two or more cranes are used to lift one load, one designated person will
be responsible for the operation. (T-0) They will analyze the operation and instruct all
personnel involved in proper positioning, rigging the load, and movements to be made.
(T-0)
12.14.2.10. A designated supervisor will be responsible for determining and controlling
safety before traveling a crane with a load. (T-0) Decisions such as position of load, boom
location, ground support, travel route and speed of movement will be IAW their
determination. (T-0) Specified tire pressures will be maintained. (T-0) The boom will be
carried in line with the direction of motion, sudden starts, and stops will be avoided and
tag or restraint lines will be used to minimize swinging of the load. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

115

12.14.2.11. All crawler, locomotive and truck cranes will operate on surfaces which will
support the weight of the crane and the weight of the loads without supporting surfaces
collapsing or slipping away from the crawlers, wheels, and support stanchions. (T-1)
12.14.2.12. Outriggers will be used when the load at a particular radius exceeds the rated
load without outriggers as provided by the crane manufacturer. (T-0) When floats are used,
they will be securely attached to outriggers. (T-0) Wood blocks used to support outriggers
will be strong enough to prevent crushing, free of defects and of sufficient size to prevent
shifting or toppling of the crane under load. (T-0)
12.14.2.13. Before any crane is moved to a new job site, the route of travel will be checked
to determine that adequate clearances exist along the entire route. (T-0) This survey will
