be conducted by the vehicle heavy equipment supervisor and crane operator. (T-1) The
empty hook will be secured to prohibit swinging, and the boom will be lowered to the boom
rest or travel position. (T-0) A red cloth or warning flag, at least 12 inches square, or a
warning light will be carried at the end of any boom that extends more than 4 feet beyond
the truck platform. (T-0) At night or during periods of reduced visibility, a warning light
will be used. (T-0) The superstructure will be secured to prohibit rotation except when
there is an operator in the cab to ensure proper boom clearances around tight spots and
corners. (T-0) Additional vehicles will be used to warn other motorists if the crane boom
or wide load poses a hazard to the front or rear. (T-0)
12.14.3. Maintenance.
12.14.3.1. The Vehicle Fleet Manager or Vehicle Management Superintendent will
establish a preventive maintenance program based on this publication and crane
manufacturers’ instructions. (T-1)
12.14.3.2. After adjustments and repairs have been made, the crane will not be operated
until all guards have been reinstalled, trapped air removed from hydraulic system, safety
devices reactivated, and maintenance equipment removed. (T-0) Adjustments to operating
mechanisms, safety devices, control systems and power plants will be maintained to ensure
correct functioning. (T-1)
12.14.3.3. All replacement parts or repairs will have at least the original manufacturer’s
design safety factor. (T-0)
12.14.3.4. Lubrication. All moving parts of the crane, where lubrication is specified, will
be regularly lubricated IAW manufacturer’s instructions or TO procedures as to points and
frequency of lubrication, maintenance of lubricant levels and types of lubricant. (T-0)
Cranes not equipped for automatic or remote lubrication will be stationary when lubricated,
with all controls in the “OFF” position, the main power control switch, if equipped, locked
out or “OFF” and pawls applied. (T-0)
12.15. Derricks. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.181, Derricks, and 29 CFR § 1926.1436, Derricks, for
guidance and information.
12.16. Related Hoisting Equipment.
12.16.1. Wire ropes, chains and hooks associated with cranes, hoists and other types of lifting
equipment will be thoroughly inspected IAW paragraph 12.4.4. (T-0) Inspection for each
item will be documented IAW paragraph 12.2.1. (T-1) For routine and preventative

116

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
maintenance on wire ropes, chains, sheaves, equalizers, and other related equipment, refer to
applicable manufacturer documentation and OSHA requirements. Refer to ASME B30-series
standards for additional information.
12.16.2. Wire Ropes.
12.16.2.1. Use and attachment considerations.
12.16.2.2. The rope or crane manufacturer’s recommendation will be followed when
hoisting ropes are used. (T-0)
12.16.2.3. Socketing will be done as specified by the assembly manufacturer. (T-0)
12.16.2.4. Swaged or compressed fittings will be applied as recommended by the rope or
crane manufacturer. (T-0)
12.16.2.5. Rope will be secured to the drum as follows:
12.16.2.5.1. Not less than two wraps of rope will remain on the drum when the hook
is in its extreme lowest position. (T-0)
12.16.2.5.2. The rope end will be anchored by a clamp securely attached to the drum
or by a socket arrangement approved by the crane or rope manufacturer. (T-0)
12.16.2.5.3. If the crane is dual reeved with the rope terminated at the equalizer, the
termination fitting will be rated to develop 100 percent of the rope strength. (T-0)
12.16.3. Rope clips attached with U-bolts will have the U-bolts on the dead or short end of the
rope. (T-0) Spacing and number of clips will follow the clip manufacturer’s recommendation.
(T-0) Clips will be drop-forged steel in all sizes manufactured commercially. (T-0) When a
newly installed rope has been in operation for an hour, all nuts on the clip bolts will be
retightened. (T-0) Refer to Figure 12.4 through 12.13 and Table 12.5 for additional guidance.
12.16.4. Inspection.
12.16.4.1. Frequent. All running ropes in service will be visually inspected daily or prior
to use and have the inspection documented per paragraph 12.2.1, which includes date of
inspection, signature of inspector, an identifier for ropes inspected and rope condition.
(T-0) Inspection will be kept on file by the supervisor for a minimum of one year and made
readily available. (T-0) Sections of rope normally hidden or difficult to see during
inspection or maintenance procedures, such as parts passing over equalizer sheaves, will
be given close inspection, as these are points most likely to fail. (T-0) Any degradation in
conditions described below could result in loss of original strength, will be carefully noted,
and will be considered for rejection:
12.16.4.1.1. Reduction of rope diameter below nominal diameter due to loss of core
support, internal or external corrosion or wear of outside wires. (T-0) Rope calipers
and micrometers are normally used to determine changes in wire rope diameters. Refer
to Figure 12.9 for proper measurement technique.
12.16.4.1.2. Broken outside wires and the degree of distribution or connection of such
broken wires. (T-0)
12.16.4.1.3. Worn outside wires. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

117

12.16.4.1.4. Corroded or broken wires at end connections. (T-0)
12.16.4.1.5. Corroded, cracked, bent, worn, or improperly applied end connections.
(T-0)
12.16.4.1.6. Severe kinking, crushing, cutting, or un-stranding. (T-0)
12.16.4.2. Periodic Inspection. Recommended interval of periodic inspections will be
determined by a person qualified to make that determination, based on such factors as
expected rope life as determined by experience with the installation or similar installations,
severity of environment, percentage of capacity lifts, frequency rates of operation, and
exposure to shock loads. (T-0) Inspections need not be at equal calendar intervals and will
be more frequent as the rope approaches the end of its useful life. (T-0) This inspection
will cover the rope’s entire length and requirements of paragraph 12.16.9.1 (T-1)
Inspections will be documented as outlined in paragraph 12.2.1. (T-1) Refer to
manufacturer’s guidance for additional requirements. Note: When the rope’s periodic
inspection schedule falls within the periodic inspection schedule of the hoist, the rope
inspection will be recorded on the periodic inspection documentation. (T-1)
12.16.4.3. All rope that is idle for a month or more due to shut down or storage of a hoist
on which it is installed will be given a thorough inspection before placed in service. (T-0)
12.16.4.4. Maintenance.
12.16.4.5. Rope will be stored and handled in a manner which prevents damage or
deterioration. (T-0)
12.16.4.6. Unreeling or uncoiling of rope will be done as recommended by rope
manufacturer and with extreme care to avoid kinking or inducing twist. (T-0)
12.16.4.7. Before cutting a rope, seizing will be placed on each side of where the rope is
to be cut to prevent unlaying of the strands. (T-0)
12.16.4.8. During installation, avoid dragging the rope in dirt or around objects which will
scrape, nick, crush, or induce sharp bends in the rope. (T-0)
12.16.4.9. Rope will be maintained in a well-lubricated condition. (T-0) The object of rope
lubrication is to reduce internal friction and to prevent corrosion. Lubricant applied as part
of a maintenance program will be compatible with the original lubricant. (T-0) The rope
manufacturer’s technical guidance/instructions will be consulted. (T-0) Lubricant applied
will not hinder visual inspection. (T-0) Sections of rope located over sheaves or that are
difficult to see during inspection and maintenance procedures require special attention
when lubricating the rope. Exception: When used in clean rooms, factory lubricant may
be removed from stainless steel ropes to prevent contamination.
12.16.4.10. An independent wire-rope or wire-strand core, or other temperature-damage
resistant core, will be used wherever there is exposure to temperatures at which fiber cores
would be damaged. (T-0)
12.16.4.11. Replacement rope will be the same size, grade, and construction as the original
rope furnished by the crane manufacturer, unless otherwise recommended by a wire rope
manufacturer due to actual working conditions. (T-0)
12.16.4.12. Replacement:

118

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.16.4.12.1. Ropes will be removed from service and replaced when any of the
following conditions exist:
12.16.4.12.1.1. In running ropes, six randomly distributed broken wires in one lay
or three broken wires in one strand in one lay. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.1.2. One outer wire broke at the contact point with the core of the rope,
which has worked its way out of the rope structure and protrudes or loops out from
the rope structure. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.1.3. Wear or scraping of 1/3 the original diameter of outside individual
wires. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.1.4. Kinking, crushing, bird caging, or any other damage resulting in
distortion of the rope structure. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.1.5. Evidence of any heat damage or corrosion. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.1.6. Reductions from nominal diameter of more than:
12.16.4.12.1.6.1. One-sixty-fourth (1/64) inch for diameters up to and
including 5/16 inch.
12.16.4.12.1.6.2. One-thirty-second (1/32) inch for diameters 3/8 inch to and
including one-half inch.
12.16.4.12.1.6.3. Three-sixty-fourths (3/64) inch for diameters 9/16 inch to and
including three-fourths inch.
12.16.4.12.1.6.4. One-sixteenth (1/16) inch for diameters 7/8 inch to and
including one and one-eighth inch.
12.16.4.12.1.6.5. Three-thirty-seconds (3/32) inch for diameters one and 1/4
inch to and including one and one-half inch.
12.16.4.12.1.6.6. In standing ropes, more than two broken wires in one lay in
sections beyond end connections or more than one broken wire at an end
connection.
12.16.4.12.2. Special attention will be given to the end fastenings. (T-0) Ropes will be
examined at socketed fittings and when two broken wires are found next to this point,
the rope will be re-socketed. (T-0) Portions of the rope subjected to reverse bends and
operation over small diameter sheaves or drums will be given close attention. (T-0)
12.16.4.12.3. A rope which has been in service, but idle for one month or more, will
be thoroughly examined before being put back into service. (T-0) This examination
will be for all types of deterioration, particularly corrosion, and will be performed by a
designated person, whose approval will be required for further use of the rope. (T-0)
The inspection will be documented per paragraph 12.2.1. (T-1)
12.16.4.12.4. All replacement rope will be of proper size, grade, and construction for
its required function on the machine. (T-0) Note: Discarded or repaired rope will not
be used for slings. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

119

12.16.4.12.5. A continuing inspection record will be maintained to establish a basis for
judging when to replace wire rope. (T-0) This record will cover points of deterioration
listed in paragraphs 12.16.9.12.1 and 12.16.9.12.2.
12.16.4.13. Chains.
12.16.4.13.1. Welded Link Chain Inspection.
12.16.4.13.1.1. Test hoist under load in hoisting and lowering directions and
observe operation of the chain and sprockets. Ensure chain feeds smoothly into and
away from sprockets.
12.16.4.13.1.2. If chain binds, jumps, or is noisy, ensure it is clean and properly
lubricated. If the trouble persists, inspect chain and mating parts for wear,
distortion, or other damage.
12.16.4.13.1.3. Clean the chain for inspection. Examine visually for gouges, nicks,
weld splatter, corrosion, and distorted links. Slacken the chain and move adjacent
links to one side to inspect for wear at the contact points. If wear is observed, or if
stretching is suspected, measure the chain according to the hoist manufacturer’s
instructions. If instructions are not available, proceed as follows:
12.16.4.13.1.3.1. Select an unworn, unstretched length of the chain from the
slack end.
12.16.4.13.1.3.2. Suspend chain vertically under tension and, using a caliper
type gauge, measure outside length of any convenient number of links,
approximately 12 to 14 inches overall.
12.16.4.13.1.3.3. Measure the same number of links in the used sections and
calculate percentage increase in length. If the used chain exceeds the hoist
manufacturer’s recommended length, (or, in the absence of such a
recommendation, if the chain is 1.5 percent longer than unused chain) replace
the chain. Do not repair load chain by welding or any other means; this repair
is only accomplished by the chain manufacturer.
12.16.4.13.1.3.4. Install load chain links which pass over hoist load sprocket
on edge (alternate to those which lie flat in the pockets) with welds away from
the center of the sprocket.
12.16.4.13.1.4. Inspect hoist chains, including end connections, for excessive
wear, twist, and distorted links interfering with proper function or stretched beyond
manufacturer’s recommendations. A thorough inspection of all hoist chains will be
made at least once each month. (T-0) A written report which includes the date of
the monthly inspection, the signature of the inspector, an identifier for the chains
inspected, and chain condition will be kept on file by the user for a minimum of
one year and made readily available. (T-0)
12.16.4.13.1.5. When the chain is replaced, mating parts (chain sprockets, guides,
stripper) will be disassembled and inspected for wear and replaced if necessary.
(T-0)
12.16.4.13.1.6. For non-load bearing drive (slack) chains, inspect for cracks, weld

120

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
splatter, burrs, or other damage. Repair or replace as required.
12.16.4.13.2. Roller Link Chain Inspection.
12.16.4.13.2.1. Test hoist under load in hoisting and lowering directions and
observe operation of chain and sprockets. Ensure chain feeds smoothly into and
away from sprockets.
12.16.4.13.2.2. If the chain binds, jumps or is noisy, ensure it is clean and properly
lubricated. If the trouble persists, inspect chain and mating parts for wear,
distortion, or other damage.
12.16.4.13.2.3. If possible, inspect roller link chain while in the hoist. With hoist
suspended in normal position, apply a light load of approximately 50 pounds.
12.16.4.13.2.3.1. Check chain for elongation following hoist manufacturer’s
instruction. In absence of specific instructions, check the chain by determining
the nominal pitch and measuring a 12-inch section of chain that normally travels
over the chain sprocket. Using a vernier caliper, check the dimension from the
edge of one chain pin to the corresponding edge of another pin for the number
of pitches per foot. If elongation exceeds 1/4 inch in 12 inches, replace the
chain. For example, a 3/4-inch pitch chain should measure 12 inches over 16
pitches. Reject chain if measurement over 16 pitches exceeds 12.25 inches.
12.16.4.13.2.3.2. Check the chain for twists. Replace the chain if the twist in
any 5-foot section exceeds 15o.
12.16.4.13.2.3.3. Check for camber. Replace chain which has a side bow
exceeding 1/4-inch in any 5-foot section.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4. Inspect the chain more thoroughly by removing the chain
from hoist and cleaning it thoroughly in an acid-free solvent. Then check for
the following deficiencies:
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.1. Pins turned from their original position.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.2. Rollers that do not turn freely with light finger pressure.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.3. Joints that cannot be flexed by easy hand pressure.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.4. Link plates that are spread open. A visual check of the pin
extension at the free end of chain can determine the amount of spread and the
condition of the chain.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.5. Corrosion, pitting or discoloration of chain, which is
generally indicative of serious impairment.
12.16.4.13.2.3.4.6. Gouges, nicks, or weld spatter.
12.16.4.13.3. Maintenance. All chains will be kept clean and free from rust, or any
coating deposit build up and any change in dimensions or reduced flexibility. (T-0)
Excessively dirty chains will be soaked in a clean acid-free solvent and agitated to
ensure all joints are free from grit and foreign matter. (T-0) Hand chain normally needs
no lubricant. Roller and load chain will be lubricated according to the hoist
manufacturer’s recommendations. (T-1) In absence of recommendations, the chain

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

121

may be lubricated with a good grade of SAE 20 or SAE 30 automotive motor oil.
Grease will never be applied to a chain.
12.16.4.14. Sheaves and Equalizers.
12.16.4.14.1. Sheaves.
12.16.4.14.1.1. Sheave grooves will be smooth and free from surface defects which
could cause rope damage. (T-0)
12.16.4.14.1.2. Sheave-carrying ropes, which can be momentarily unloaded, will
be provided with close-fitting guards or other suitable devices to guide the rope
back into the groove when the load is applied again. (T-0) Note: Equalizers where
ropes are terminated will be specified to have sufficient adjustment space for block
leveling as the rope stretches. (T-0)
12.16.4.14.1.3. Sheaves in the bottom block will be equipped with close-fitting
guards to prevent ropes from fouling when the block is lying on the ground with
loose ropes. (T-0)
12.16.4.14.1.4. Pockets and flanges of sheaves used with hoist chains will be sized
so the chain does not catch or bind during operation. (T-0)
12.16.4.14.1.5. All running sheaves will be equipped with means for lubrication.
(T-0) Permanently lubricated, sealed bearings meet this requirement.
12.16.4.14.1.6. When the chain is replaced, mating parts (chain sprockets, guides,
stripper) will be disassembled, inspected for wear, and replaced if necessary.
12.16.4.14.2. Equalizers.
12.16.4.14.2.1. If a load is supported by more than one part of rope, the tension in
the parts will be equalized. (T-0)
12.16.4.14.2.2. Equalizers will be readily accessible for maintenance, lubrication,
and inspection. (T-0)
12.16.5. Hooks. Refer to manufacturer documentation for additional guidance and ASME
B30.10, Hooks, for additional information.
12.16.5.1. Inspection.
12.16.5.1.1. Hooks will be examined for the following (See Figure 12.3.):
12.16.5.1.1.1. Distortion such as bending, twisting or increased throat opening.
(T-0)
12.16.5.1.1.2. Any apparent bend or twist from the plane from the original
measurements. (T-0)
12.16.5.1.1.3. Any wear exceeding 10 percent (or as recommended by the
manufacturer) of the original section dimension of the hook or its load pin. (T-0)
12.16.5.1.1.4. Any distortion causing an increase in throat opening of five percent,
not to exceed ¼ inch (or as recommended by the manufacturer). (T-0)
12.16.5.1.1.5. Cracks, severe nicks, or gouges. (T-0)

122

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
12.16.5.1.1.6. Safety latch engagement and damaged or malfunctioning latch, i.e.,
any self-locking latch that does not lock or any latch that does not close the hook’s
throat. (T-0)
12.16.5.1.1.7. Hook attachment and securing means. Hook retaining nuts on collar
and pins, welds or riveting will be used to secure the retaining member. (T-0)
12.16.5.2. Lubrication of swivel point. (T-0)
12.16.5.2.1. Hooks will not be painted. (T-0) Unless specifically permitted by TO or
other technical guidance, DAF-users are not authorized to paint hooks. Hooks
(including hooks on slings) received painted by the manufacturer may be used in
applications not requiring them to be non-destructively inspected. Painted hooks
removed from service will be replaced. (T-0) Note: Painted hooks will not be used on
nuclear-certified hoists or lifting equipment or equipment used to lift critical loads.
(T-1) Note: Hooks with protective coatings applied by the manufacturer that do not
affect or interfere with NDI may be used in applications requiring hook NDI. (T-1)
Note: Existing painted hooks may have the paint removed for NDI but may not be
repainted unless authorized above.
12.16.5.2.2. Repairs by welding or reshaping are not recommended as a standard
practice. If such repairs are attempted, they will only be accomplished by a person
qualified to make such repairs, and the hook will be tested to the load requirements of
the specific equipment in use. (T-0) Note: Hooks repaired by welding or reshaping are
not authorized on nuclear-certified hoists or lifting equipment.
12.16.5.2.3. Rated capacity for a hook, when used as intended, will be equal to other
suspension members to which it is attached. (T-0) When this is not feasible, special
precautions will be taken to ensure the hook’s rated capacity is not exceeded. (T-0)
12.16.5.2.4. Compliance should not preclude the possibility of consulting a person
qualified to evaluate such compliance when the equipment has been altered, repaired,
or modified; the manuals or documents supplied by the manufacturer are no longer
available; or the manufacturer or a successor is no longer in business and the manuals
are no longer available. However, the purpose of consulting such a person will not be
to avoid contacting the manufacturer and obtaining the information supplied by the
manufacturer. (T-0) Use of means to identify chains for tool control must meet
manufacturer requirements and not change the load capacity. (T-1)
12.16.5.2.5. If manufacturer's data is not available and a person qualified to conduct
an inspection cannot determine whether the hook is safe to use, the hook will be
removed from service. (T-0) Note: The manufacturer’s original specifications will be
used as the baseline. (T-1) If the original specifications cannot be obtained through the
manufacturer or item manager, the hook can continue to be used, provided the hook
has been determined safe for use by a qualified person.
12.16.5.3. Intervals of Inspections. All hooks in service will be visually inspected daily or
prior to use. (T-0) The inspection will be documented and maintained in accordance with
paragraph 12.2.1 (T-1) Note: If the hook is part of a hoist, the frequent inspection will
be recorded on the hoist inspection documentation in accordance with paragraph 12.2.1.
(T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

123

12.16.5.4. Where operations require removal or replacement of hooks and/or lifting
fixtures, the lifting capacity of the hooks/lifting fixtures will be marked on the hook/lifting
fixture and readily identifiable to the hoist/crane operator to prevent overloading the crane
or hook/lifting fixture when load capacities are different. (T-0)
12.16.5.5. Hooks, rings, oblong or pear-shaped links, welded or mechanical coupling links
or other attachments will have a rated capacity at least equal to that of the alloy steel chain
with which they are used. (T-0) The sling will not be used more than the rated capacity of
the weakest component. (T-0)
12.17. Portable Automotive Lifting Devices and Related Equipment.
12.17.1. Requirements. All personnel will observe all safety precautions in this chapter and
those recommended by the manufacturers of portable automotive lifting devices. (T-1) Always
read the owner’s manual before operating portable automotive lifting devices. (T-1) The
owner’s manual/instructions spell out the proper operating procedures and basic function of
the components, which includes the recommended replacement fluid, maintenance and
inspection procedures, and intervals, as applicable.
12.17.2. Maintenance and Inspection.
12.17.2.1. Maintenance. The portable automotive lifting devices will be maintained IAW
the manufacturer instructions. (T-0)
12.17.2.2. Inspection.
12.17.2.2.1. Visual inspection will be made before each use of portable automotive
lifting devices by checking for abnormal conditions, such as cracked welds, leaks and
damaged, loose, or missing parts. (T-1)
12.17.2.2.2. Other inspections will be made per product operating instructions. (T-1)
12.17.2.2.3. Each portable automotive lifting device will be inspected immediately if
the lift is believed to have been subjected to abnormal load or shock. (T-1) It is
recommended that this inspection be made by a manufacturer or supplier’s authorized
repair facility.
12.17.2.2.4. Owners and/or operators should be aware that repair of this equipment
may require specialized knowledge and facilities. It is recommended that an annual
inspection of the portable automotive lifting device be made by a manufacturer or
supplier’s repair facility and that any defective parts, decals or safety labels or signs are
replaced with manufacturer or supplier’s specified parts. A list should be maintained
of repair facilities that are available from the manufacturer or supplier to maintain for
reference.

Notes:
1. Apply the initial load and retighten nuts to the recommended torque. The rope will stretch
and shrink in diameter when loads are applied inspected periodically, and retightened.
2. The efficiency rating of a properly prepared termination for clip sizes 1/8 to 7/8 in. is
approximately 80 percent and for sizes 1 to 3 in. is approximately 90 percent. This rating is
based on the catalog breaking strength of wire rope. If a pulley is used in place of a thimble for
turning back the rope, add one additional clip.
3. 1 in. = 2.54 cm. / 1 ft-lb = 1.36 Newton-meter.
4. The number of clips shown is based upon using right regular or Lang lay wire rope, 6 x 19
class or 6 x 37 class, fiber core or Independent Wire Rope Core, Improved Plow Steel or Extra
Improved Plow Steel. If Seale construction or similar large outer wire type construction in the
6 x 19 class is used for sizes 1 in. (2.5 cm) and larger, add one additional clip.
5. The number of clips shown also applies to right regular lay wire rope, 8 x 19 class, fiber
core, Improved Plow Steel, nominal sizes 1-1/2 in. and smaller; and right regular lay wire
rope, 18 x 7 class, fiber core, Improved Plow Steel or Extra Improved Steel, nominal sizes 13/4 in. and smaller.
6. For other classes of wire rope not mentioned above, it may be necessary to add additional
clips to the number shown.
7. Turn back the specified amount of rope from the thimble. Apply the first clip one base
width from the dead end of the wire rope (U-bolt over dead end — live end rests in clip
saddle). Tighten nuts evenly to recommended torque.
8. Apply the next clip as near the loop as possible. Turn on nuts firm but do not tighten.
Space additional clips, if required, equally between the first two. Turn on nuts — take up rope
slack — tighten all nuts evenly on all clips to recommended torque.

Chapter 13

FALL PROTECTION
13.1. Scope. This chapter provides the requirements to implement an effective fall protection
program where employees are exposed to fall hazards while assigned to work at height. Fall
protection program requirements include program policies, duties, and responsibilities, fall hazard
survey, fall protection procedures, rescue procedures, training, equipment selection, inspections
and maintenance, incident investigation, and evaluating program effectiveness. Most DAF
operations fall under the following industries:
13.1.1. General Industry. Fall protection requirements will be followed whenever employees
are exposed to fall hazards greater than four feet or any height where a fall would land an
employee onto or in hazardous machinery/equipment. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart
D – Walking-Working Surfaces, for additional requirements. The following requirements will
be referenced first and areas not prescribed in these chapters will be covered by this chapter.
(T-0)
13.1.1.1. Telecommunications Work. Refer to Chapter 28 of this instruction and 29 CFR
§ 1910.268, Telecommunications, for additional requirements and guidance.
13.1.1.2. Electrical Power Generation, Transmission and Distribution Work. Refer to 29
CFR § 1910.269, Electric Power Generation, Transmission, and Distribution, for
additional requirements and guidance.
13.1.1.3. Aircraft Flightline Operations. Flightline fall protection operations will meet the
requirements of this chapter and Chapter 24. (T-1)
13.1.2. Construction Operations. Fall protection requirements will be followed whenever
employees are exposed to fall hazards greater than six feet or any height where a fall would
land an employee onto or in hazardous machinery/equipment. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1926,
Subpart M – Fall Protection, for additional requirements.
13.1.3. This chapter does not apply to the following:
13.1.3.1. Portable Ladders. Refer to Chapter 7 of this instruction, 29 CFR § 1910.23,
Ladders, and 29 CFR § 1926.1053, Ladders, for additional requirements and guidance.
13.1.3.2. When employers are inspecting, investigating, or assessing workplace conditions
or work to be performed prior to the start of the work or after all work has been performed.
Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.28. Note: This exemption does not apply when fall protection
systems or equipment meeting the requirements of 29 CFR § 1910.29 have been installed
and are available for employees to use for pre-work and post-work inspections,
investigations, or assessments.
13.1.3.3. To fall hazards presented by the exposed perimeters of entertainment stages and
the exposed perimeters of rail-station platforms.
13.1.3.4. Powered Platforms, Aerial Lifts and Scaffolding. Refer to Chapter 16 of this
standard, 29 CFRs § 1910.66(j), Powered Platforms for Building Maintenance,
1910.67(c)(2)(v), Vehicle-Mounted Elevating and Rotating Work Platforms, and 1910.27,
Scaffolds and Rope Descent Systems, for additional requirements and guidance.

136

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
13.1.4. Fire and Emergency Services. Fall protection will be provided for F&ES personnel
when exposed to falls greater than four feet during routine operations as stipulated by General
Industry standards (29 CFR § 1910, Subpart D) when not working as a rescue service. (T-0)
When acting as a rescue service, F&ES personnel will adhere to requirements from NFPA
1006, Standard for Technical Rescue Personnel Professional Qualifications, NFPA 1670,
Standard on Operations and Training for Technical Search and Rescue Incidents, NFPA 101,
Life Safety Code, and other applicable standards. (T-0)

13.2. Fall Protection Program.
13.2.1. Fall Protection Policy. A policy statement will be developed that states the
commander’s commitment to providing a safe workplace for employees working at heights.
(T-1) The policy will also provide general goals, guidance, and responsibilities for the fall
protection program. (T-1)
13.2.2. Duties and Responsibilities.
13.2.2.1. Commander. The commander is ultimately responsible for the development and
implementation of the fall protection program that promotes a safe and healthy workplace.
The commander must ensure that the individuals selected to administer the organization’s
fall protection program, or act as a qualified or competent person have the required
education, training, and experience to safely conduct their duties. (T-1)
13.2.2.2. Fall Protection Program Administrator. The fall protection program will be
coordinated by a member identified as a qualified or competent person as defined in
paragraphs 13.2.2.3 and 13.2.2.4. (T-1)
13.2.2.2.1. This person acts as an administrative liaison between the organization and
the occupational safety office to ensure fall protection program compliance and will be
identified in writing by the commander. (T-1)
13.2.2.2.2. The program administrator advocates for needed fall protection training,
and equipment identified by supervisors, qualified persons, and competent persons to
the commander. Additionally, they maintain the currency of fall protection
requirements and ensure self-evaluation of organizational compliance and coordinate
incident investigations. (T-1)
13.2.2.3. Qualified Person will:
13.2.2.3.1. Be a person, who, by possession of a recognized degree, certificate, or
professional standing, or who by extensive knowledge, training, and experience has
successfully demonstrated the ability to solve or resolve problems relating to the
subject matter, the work, or the project. (T-0)
13.2.2.3.2. Supervise the design, selection, installation, use, and inspection of certified
anchorages and horizontal lifelines. (T-0)
13.2.2.3.3. Participate in incident investigations for falls while working under this
chapter. (T-1)
13.2.2.3.4. Be designated in writing by the commander. (T-1) If the qualified person
comes from outside the organization, documentation of the service agreement will be
kept on file. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

137

13.2.2.4. Competent Person will:
13.2.2.4.1. Be a person who can identify existing and predictable hazards in any
personal fall protection system or any component of it, as well as in their application
and uses with related equipment, and who has authorization to take prompt, corrective
action to eliminate the identified hazards. (T-0)
13.2.2.4.2. Supervise use of fall protection equipment and ensure that the equipment
has been installed, inspected, and used to meet manufacturer’s instructions, OSHA
requirements, and this standard. (T-0)
13.2.2.4.3. Conduct fall hazard survey IAW paragraph 13.3. (T-1)
13.2.2.4.4. Prepare, review, update, and approve fall protection and rescue procedures
IAW paragraph 13.4. (T-1)
13.2.2.4.5. Verify that authorized persons have completed fall protection training and
remain knowledgeable of current procedures. (T-1)
13.2.2.4.6. Inspect fall protection equipment at least quarterly, and ensure defective or
damaged equipment is removed from service. (T-1)
13.2.2.4.7. Participate in incident investigations related to falls from heights. (T-1)
13.2.2.4.8. Be designated in writing by the commander. (T-1)
13.2.2.5. Authorized Person will:
13.2.2.5.1. Know the nature of the fall hazards in their work area and how to recognize
them. (T-0)
13.2.2.5.2. Follow the procedures required in the installation, inspection, operation,
maintenance, and disassembly of personal fall protection systems used. (T-0)
13.2.2.5.3. Correctly use the assigned personal fall protection systems. (T-0)
13.2.2.5.4. Follow the instructions of competent persons and know how to report all
unsafe or hazardous conditions. (T-0)
13.2.2.5.5. Conduct pre-use inspections of personal fall protection equipment for
defects or damage and notify the competent person of any discrepancies. (T-1)
Defective or damaged equipment will be properly disposed of per competent person
direction and not used. (T-0)
13.2.2.6. Occupational Safety. The occupational safety staff (or applicable tenant safety
staff) will provide advice and guidance to the Fall Protection Program Administrator of the
organizational fall protection program elements. (T-1) Note: Occupational safety
professionals do not fully meet the requirements of a qualified or competent person and
will not be used to fulfill those roles in an organization’s program. (T-1) These duties
include:
13.2.2.6.1. Assisting in fall hazard surveys and development of written fall protection
and rescue procedures to ensure rescue procedures, equipment, and training meet fall
protection requirements in paragraph 13.4. (T-1)

138

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
13.2.2.6.2. Identifying unabated fall hazards and providing a RAC and tracked IAW
DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
13.2.2.6.3. Conducting thorough annual review of fall protection program elements
and documenting results annually in safety inspection/assessment reports. (T-1)
13.2.2.7. F&ES Flight. The F&ES Flight will assist in the fall hazard surveys and
development of written fall protection and rescue procedures. The F&ES Flight will ensure
equipment and training meet fall protection requirements in paragraph 13.3 and
paragraph 13.4. (T-1) The F&ES Flight may also act as the competent rescuer when
trained and equipped to meet the needs to perform rescues at height under the
organization’s specific requirements, when included in the organizational rescue plan and
agreed to by the fire chief.
13.2.2.8. Authorized Rescuers will:
13.2.2.8.1. Be trained to evaluate and recognize the hazards that may endanger them
during rescue operations at height. (T-1)
13.2.2.8.2. Inspect the rescue equipment prior to a rescue event, verify that all
equipment is in operable condition, and ensure the equipment is securely stored until
needed. (T-1)
13.2.2.8.3. Verify that a rescue procedure has been developed for the fall arrest
system(s) in use and review the procedure prior to use to properly implement the
procedure. (T-1)
13.2.2.9. Supervisors. Supervisors will: (Note: Refer to DAFI 91-202 for additional
responsibilities.)
13.2.2.9.1. Ensure each authorized, qualified, and competent person using a fall
protection/fall arrest system is trained and evaluated on proper use, application, and
inspection of fall protection/fall arrest systems. (T-1) A supervisor may also be a
qualified, competent and/or authorized person if they meet the requirements of
Chapter 13.6.
13.2.2.9.2. Be familiar with the shop’s typical work assignments and where fall
protection/fall arrest systems are required. (T-1)
13.2.2.9.3. Be responsible for the procurement of fall protection and fall arrest systems
required by the organization or shop as directed by the qualified person. (T-1)
13.2.2.9.4. Be able to provide recommendations for qualified and competent personnel
in the organization to the commander. (T-1)
13.2.2.10. Trainers. Trainers will be designated in writing by the commander. (T-1)
Trainers will have the required experience and knowledge to deliver training at the level
they are expected to instruct and be qualified, as an instructor, to train personnel on fall
protection requirements. (T-1) Note: If the trainer comes from outside the organization,
documentation of the service agreement will be kept on file. (T-1)

13.3. Fall Hazard Survey. A fall hazard survey will be conducted in all work areas where
employees are exposed to fall hazards. (T-1) The survey team will be led by the organization’s
competent/qualified person(s) and be made up of the work center supervisors, authorized persons,

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

139

occupational safety office, and F&ES Flight. (T-1) Team composition allows all personnel to
provide input identifying fall hazards and how to protect against those hazards. Proper
identification of fall hazards and appropriate controls for each work area and/or task will ensure
that the best solutions are used. (T-1)
13.3.1. A fall hazard survey will include:
13.3.1.1. Identification of fall hazards and include details of each hazard such as: (T-1)
13.3.1.1.1. Means of access to the fall hazards.
13.3.1.1.2. Locations of the fall hazards.
13.3.1.1.3. Tasks that create exposure to fall hazards.
13.3.1.1.4. Hidden fall hazards that are not always readily apparent.
13.3.1.2. Pertinent information about the fall hazards to include showing its basic
configuration. (T-1) Note: Use of graphics, drawings and/or photographs are highly
encouraged.
13.3.1.3. Identification of environmental factors that will affect the installation, use,
inspection, maintenance, and dismantling of any fall protection system. (T-1) Note: This
is not an all-inclusive list of fall or environmental hazards:
13.3.1.3.1. Electrical. Wire rope or rope covered wire lanyards, some plastics (such as
nylon), and wet lanyards are conductive and will not be used near electrical hazards.
13.3.1.3.2. Thermal. Heat that can damage equipment or injure employees, such as
welding splatter.
13.3.1.3.3. Chemical. Some chemicals can degrade the PPE material and/or injure an
employee.
13.3.1.3.4. Sharp edges and abrasive surfaces can cut lanyards and/or injure
employees.
13.3.1.4. Conduct a risk assessment to determine the level of risk for each fall hazard
identified. (T-1) Each hazard will be assigned a RAC, as necessary. (T-1) Refer to DAFI
91-202 for additional guidance on RACs.
13.3.1.5. Appropriate abatement actions, as required by DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
13.3.1.6. Selected fall protection method(s) for each identified hazard. Note: If PFAS are
selected, the calculation of all clearances will be included in the written fall protection
procedures. Employees will not use PFAS until adequate clearances are available.
13.3.1.7. Appropriate PPE based on hazards identified will be addressed in the Fall Hazard
Survey. Refer to Chapter 14 for additional information.
13.3.2. Competent or qualified person will prepare the fall hazard survey report and submit
copies to the fall protection program administrator, F&ES Flight, and occupational safety
office. (T-1)
13.3.3. Fall hazard surveys will be updated or re-accomplished when changes occur in the
work center. (T-1) Copies of completed fall hazard surveys will be maintained by the fall

140

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
protection program administrator and be available to all qualified, competent, and authorized
employees for reference. (T-1)
13.3.4. When fall hazards are identified resulting from item or equipment upgrades,
modification or replacements, the DAF item managers (depot level) will perform necessary
functions (e.g., describing planned upgrades, costs, timetables, and prioritization), for DAF
equipment not meeting fall protection standards. (T-1)

13.4. Written Fall Protection and Rescue Procedures. Fall protection and rescue procedures
will be documented specifically for each workplace (location) and/or task they are applied to when
the use of fall protection is required. (T-1) If a procedure can be effectively used in multiple
workplaces/tasks, then the same procedure can be used. Procedures will be prepared and modified
by a qualified or competent person as defined in this chapter. (T-1) Fall protection and rescue
procedures will be reviewed at least annually for currency and accuracy. (T-1) Note: Fall
protection equipment/procedures that are effective in one location or for a specific task, can be
completely unacceptable in another location or for other tasks.
13.4.1. Written procedures will include:
13.4.1.1. Purpose of the fall protection procedure. (T-1)
13.4.1.2. Location and photographs/diagrams of the fall hazard(s) and fall protection
system setup. (T-1)
13.4.1.3. Appropriate standards, regulations or requirements for the task(s) conducted at
height. (T-1)
13.4.1.4. Training requirements for the fall protection procedure based on the fall
protection equipment required. (T-1)
13.4.1.5. Fall protection system design parameters that assure 100% protection from fall
hazards. (T-1) Note: Include fall calculations based on identified equipment and
equipment design parameters, which include a detailed list of PPE, who developed the
procedure, when it was developed, system certifications, and inspection logs.
13.4.1.6. Equipment requirements, to include: (T-1)
13.4.1.6.1. Identification of acceptable anchorages and tie off techniques. (T-0)
13.4.1.6.2. Validation of clearance requirements. (T-0)
13.4.1.6.3. Procedure to safely access and egress from the system. (T-1)
13.4.1.6.4. Identification of limitations in the use of the fall protection system. (T-1)
13.4.1.7. Procedures documenting how to safely erect, use and dismantle the fall
protection equipment. (T-1)
13.4.1.8. Preparatory actions to be conducted by the supervisor to ensure authorized
persons know the fall protection and rescue procedures. (T-1)
13.4.1.9. Steps to take when work is completed, e.g., clean up, storage. (T-1)
13.4.1.10. Fallen employees will be rescued in a prompt manner to prevent physical injury
or death. (T-0) A detailed rescue plan will ensure rescue meets this standard, which
includes:

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

141

13.4.1.10.1. Coordination with outside rescue agencies (F&ES Flight, contracted
rescue, organization, etc.) to determine requirements to ensure prompt rescue of fallen
personnel as part of preplanning. (T-1) Competent person will ensure pre-planning with
the outside rescue agency is completed prior to work start. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.2. Procedures to contact the rescue agency if a fall occurs. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.3. Training on actions an authorized person can take to attempt self-rescue,
when possible. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.4. Actions to be taken by the organization to rescue fallen authorized
personnel who are unable to self-rescue. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.5. Location of rescue anchorage. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.6. Appropriate rescue equipment to use based on pre-identified
requirements. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.7. Location of attachment to fallen employee’s harness. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.8. Specific actions to achieve successful rescue. (T-1) Rescue training will
include specific first aid requirements to prevent further injury/death when employee
is removed from suspension after a fall. (T-1)
13.4.1.10.9. Required training for rescuers. (T-1)
13.4.2. The organization will establish good lines of communication with the F&ES Flight or
a contracted rescue agency for prompt rescue of a fallen employee. (T-1) When rescue by the
F&ES Flight or contracted agencies is unavailable, then the organization will ensure a rescue
team is available or hold operations until available. (T-1) (Warning: Hanging in a harness for
extended time periods can be fatal. Consideration should be given to purchasing and utilizing
suspension trauma prevention devices to extend the time to ensure prompt rescue.)
13.5. Fall Protection Plans. Fall protection plans will be used only in construction, for
residential roofs, leading edge work and precast concrete erection work. (T-0) The written plan
will be coordinated with the occupational safety office (or applicable tenant Safety office) before
the plan and its procedural controls are used. (T-1) When it is determined to not be feasible to use
fall protection systems while on residential roofs as identified in 29 CFR § 1910.28(b)(1)(ii), a
written fall protection plan, meeting the requirements of 29 CFR § 1926.502(k) and coordinated
with the occupational safety office, will be developed by a qualified person. (T-0) Training for the
fall protection plan must meet the requirements of 29 CFR § 1926.503(a) and (c). (T-0) Note: A
corrective action plan will be accomplished describing planned upgrades (equipment, cost, and
timetable, prioritized by the shop) and appropriate RACs, to eliminate need, partially or totally,
for procedural controls. (T-1) Consistent with OSHA guidelines, cost will not be a consideration
for selecting the fall protection plan’s procedural controls versus implementing fall protection.
(T-0)
13.6. Training. Before employees are exposed to a fall hazard, training will be completed IAW
29 CFR § 1910.30, Training Requirements, and for construction use IAW 29 CFR § 1926.503,
Training Requirements. (T-0)

142

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
13.6.1. Authorized personnel will be trained by a competent or qualified person trained to
instruct authorized personnel in their duties under the fall protection program. (T-0) Trainers
conducting fall protection and rescue training must be qualified to train in the following areas:
13.6.1.1. The nature of the fall hazards in the work area and how to recognize them. (T-0)
13.6.1.2. The procedures to be followed to minimize those hazards. (T-0)
13.6.1.3. The correct procedures for installing, inspecting, operating, maintaining, and
disassembling the personal fall protection systems that the employee uses. (T-0)
13.6.1.4. The correct use of personal fall protection systems and equipment, including, but
not limited to, proper hook-up, anchoring, and tie-off techniques, and methods of
equipment inspection and storage, as specified by the manufacturer. (T-0)
13.6.1.5. The employer must train each employee who uses a dock board to properly place
and secure it to prevent unintentional movement. (T-0)
13.6.1.6. The employer must train each employee who uses a rope descent system in
proper rigging and use of the equipment IAW 29 CFR § 1910.27. (T-0)
13.6.1.7. The employer must train each employee who uses a designated area in the proper
set-up and use of the area. (T-0)
13.6.2. Qualified and competent persons will be trained by an instructor who has the level of
knowledge and is qualified to instruct to ensure the employee who completes the training can
perform their duties as a qualified or competent person, as applicable. (T-0) The instructor will
ensure the employee who completes the training can perform their duties as a qualified or
competent person, as applicable. (T-0) Personnel trained as a qualified person must also meet
the training requirements of a competent person before applying fall protection system
principles. (T-1)
13.6.3. Program administrators will be trained on all equipment and practices applicable to the
scope of work in the organization’s program. (T-1)
13.6.4. Retraining. Supervisors will ensure authorized persons are retrained in fall protection
when: (T-0)
13.6.4.1. Changes in the workplace render previous training obsolete or inadequate. (T-0)
13.6.4.2. Changes in the types of fall protection systems or equipment to be used render
previous training obsolete or inadequate. (T-0)
13.6.4.3. Inadequacies in an affected employee’s knowledge or use of fall protection
systems or equipment indicate that the employee no longer has the requisite understanding
or skill necessary to use equipment or perform the job safely. (T-0)
13.6.5. Recurring Training. Recurring training will be performed and documented for
example, use an attendance sheet, at least annually, to maintain employee proficiency. (T-1)
Recurring training will include an in-depth review of current procedures and introduce any
new or revised control methods and procedures, as necessary. (T-1)
13.6.5.1. Competent and authorized persons who are also identified as rescuers must meet
requirements as identified in paragraphs 13.2.2.8 and 13.6.2. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

143

13.6.5.2. Program administrators will also attend annual recurring training to maintain
currency. (T-1)
13.6.6. Training Documentation. All training, e.g., initial, retraining, and recurring, will be
documented with the following information:
13.6.6.1. Name of the employee trained. (T-0)
13.6.6.2. The date(s) of the training. (T-0)
13.6.6.3. The signature of the person who conducted the training or the signature of the
employer. (T-0)
13.7. Fall Protection Equipment and General Information. After conducting fall hazard
surveys and identifying the fall hazards involved, the next step is to evaluate the appropriate
control(s) to implement to protect the employees. The following provides a not all-inclusive
reference to appropriate controls and equipment to use when having employees work at height.
13.7.1. Hierarchy of Controls.
13.7.1.1. Elimination. The preferred method of protecting employees from falls at height
is to remove the hazard completely. Evaluate whether it is necessary to put an employee at
height to do the work or can the operation be restructured to allow an employee to do the
same task at ground level.
13.7.1.2. Prevention. Many fall hazards are controlled using prevention techniques that
include guardrails, walls, covers, or parapets to prevent exposure to the fall. Specific
requirements for these items can be found in Chapter 7, Walking – Working Surfaces.
13.7.1.3. Engineering Controls. The use of engineering controls requires the consideration
of design changes to equipment or different techniques while working at height such as
reaching the work location using an aerial lift, scaffold, or powered
platform/manlift/vehicle mounted work platform. Guidance on requirements to safely use
this equipment is found in Chapter 16, Mobile Elevating Work Platforms and Scaffolding.
13.7.1.4. Personal Protective Systems and Equipment. This control includes the equipment
used for work positioning, travel restraint, and fall arrest. This control sits low on the list
due to variations of compliance in how well an employee dons, doffs, cleans, and maintains
their equipment; how well supervisors enforce the use of PPE; and how well suited the PPE
selected is to the hazard identified.
13.7.1.5. Administrative Controls. This is the least protective of the controls as it does not
prevent exposure to the fall hazard or arrest the fall of an employee. Administrative controls
are only allowed in very limited situations.
13.7.2. Types of Fall Protection. Fall protection systems are designed to protect employees
from fall hazards. When determining specific requirements to address fall hazards refer to 29
CFR § 1910, Subpart D, Walking – Working Surfaces, 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart I, Personal
Protective Equipment, and manufacturer’s documentation.
13.7.2.1. Passive fall protection, such as guardrails, work stands, and platforms (aircraft
maintenance stands, e.g., B-1, B-4, B-5), safety nets, and other devices can prevent an
employee from falling but are not directly connected to the employee. If passive fall
protection is not feasible, active fall protection is next, the preferred option. Note: Nets do

144

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
not prevent an employee from falling but do prevent an employee from hitting the next
level.
13.7.2.2. Active fall protection devices include, but are not limited to, ladder climbing
devices and personal fall arrest, positioning, and restraint systems with each described
below:
13.7.2.2.1. Ladder safety systems ensure personnel can safely climb fixed ladders and
prevent falls to the next level. A typical system contains an anchorage, lifeline, harness,
and connectors.
13.7.2.2.2. Positioning systems allow an employee to be supported while working on
an elevated, vertical working surface with both hands free. The positioning equipment
includes a body harness, connectors, and an anchorage.
13.7.2.2.3. Travel restraint systems restrict an employee’s range of movement to
prevent the possibility of an employee falling off the walking/working surface. The
travel restraint system contains an anchorage, full body harness, and a lanyard (or other
means of connection).
13.7.2.2.4. Personal fall arrest systems (PFAS) arrest an employee’s fall and limits
arresting loads to generally safe levels to prevent the employee from falling to the next
lower level. A PFAS includes an anchorage, body harness, and connectors.
13.7.3. Equipment Inspections and Maintenance.
13.7.3.1. PFAS users will comply with manufacturer’s instructions or use TO 00-25-245,
Operations Instructions - Testing and Inspection Procedures for Personnel Safety and
Rescue Equipment, for inspection, maintenance, cleaning, and storage requirements. (T-1)
When defects or damage to equipment is found due to inadequate maintenance of
equipment, improper storage, improper use, or from wear on the system, the equipment
will be immediately tagged unserviceable and removed from service. (T-1) Unserviceable
PFAS will be destroyed to prevent further use. Inspections will be documented IAW TO
00-25-245 or manufacturer’s instructions, whichever is more restrictive. (T-1)
13.7.3.2. Authorized person(s) will inspect PFAS prior to use. (T-0)
13.7.3.3. Qualified/competent persons will ensure all PFAS components receive a
thorough inspection at least quarterly. (T-1) This inspection will be documented and
maintained. (T-1) Exception: Components of a PFAS that have been professionally
installed and certified will be inspected IAW manufacturer’s instructions, at the interval
recommended by the manufacturer, and by a person qualified to perform the inspection.
Inspection will be documented and maintained. (T-1)
13.7.3.4. Supervisors will maintain manufacturer’s instructions and performance testing
information for PFAS used by their employees. (T-1)
13.7.3.5. For overseas locations, the use of anchorage points is authorized when the host
country provides documentation of applicable testing and inspections.
13.7.3.6. Fall protection anchorage connectors attached to overhead rail systems (i.e., Ibeams and trolley) will be completely inspected by a qualified person per manufacturer

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

145

data. (T-0) The anchorages (i.e., I-beam, trolley) will be inspected by a qualified person at
least every five years or dependent on results of annual qualified person inspections. (T-0)
13.8. Incident Investigation. Falls from heights where the employee utilized active fall
protection equipment as described in paragraph 13.7.2.2, regardless of whether an injury or
damage has occurred, will be investigated by a qualified or competent person. (T-1) The qualified
or competent person can request assistance from occupational safety personnel in completing their
investigation. A copy of the completed investigation will be reported to the servicing occupational
safety office. (T-1) The incident investigation is an organizational level process to determine the
cause of the fall and is used to correct any issues found in procedures, equipment, or rescue, as
applicable. Where the fall results in required mishap investigation, due to injuries and/or property
damage, per DAFI 91-204, then the safety mishap investigation will take priority. (T-1)
13.8.1. The incident report will be provided to the fall protection program administrator to
review the team’s findings and recommendations for corrections to the organization’s program.
(T-1)
13.8.2. Final report will be routed to the commander for review and signature within 10 days
of report completion. (T-1)
13.8.3. The program administrator will then develop a corrective action plan to ensure the
approved corrective actions are acted upon in the timeline established. (T-1)
13.8.4. The fall protection program administrator will maintain a minimum of three years of
completed incident reports. (T-1)
13.8.5. The occupational safety office will annually conduct review of incident reports to
determine any trends. (T-1)
13.9. Fall Protection Assessments.
13.9.1. At least annually, the fall protection program administrator, with assistance from each
affected workplace supervisor, will conduct an internal assessment of the organization’s
program to evaluate.
13.9.1.1. Training curriculum and documentation are current. (T-1)
13.9.1.2. Fall hazard surveys are current. (T-1)
13.9.1.3. Policy statement and documentation of identified personnel are current. (T-1)
13.9.1.4. Review of written fall and rescue procedures to ensure currency. (T-1)
13.9.1.5. Review of all fall protection equipment and anchorages to ensure they are
serviceable, current on required inspections, properly stored and maintained, and reviewed
to determine if still appropriate to address fall hazards. (T-1) Reviewing new options to
currently used equipment to address identified fall hazards provides the opportunity to find
and evaluate new solutions that better address the fall hazards and better protect employees
by using higher level hierarchy of controls such as elimination versus continuing to use
PPE.
13.9.1.6. Review of incident investigations. (T-1)
13.9.1.7. Provide a report, with recommendations where identified, to the commander for
review and approval within 10 days of completion. (T-1)

146

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
13.9.2. The occupational safety office or tenant occupational safety office(s) will complete an
annual assessment of fall protection program requirements for each organization and document
results as required in DAFI 91-202. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

147
Chapter 14

PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE)
14.1. Scope. This chapter establishes DAF specific PPE requirements, to include the use,
selection, care, and maintenance for protective equipment for eyes, face, head and extremities,
protective clothing, respiratory devices, and protective shields/barriers. Additional OSHA
requirements are found in 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart I, Personal Protective Equipment, and 29 CFR
§ 1926, Subpart E, Personal Protection and Life Saving Equipment. For issues involving Radio
Frequency or laser radiation, refer to AFI 48-109, AFI 48-139 and AFMAN 48-148. Note: A
cross-reference of host nation and US standards is not available. On a case-by-case basis, when
procuring PPE outside of the DAF supply system, each wing/delta/installation should conduct a
product risk assessment to determine if the product meets or exceeds the US standards. If the
product does not meet or exceed the US standards, then purchase a product within US standards,
if possible.
14.2. Training.
14.2.1. Training will be provided to each affected employee who is required to use PPE when
accomplishing any work activity. (T-0) Supervisors will train each employee IAW 29 CFR §
1910.132(f), Training, and applicable manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0) Respiratory
protection training will be IAW 29 CFR § 1910.134, Respiratory Protection, and AFI 48-137.
(T-0) Hearing protection will be IAW AFI 48-127.
14.2.2. Retraining. Affected employees will be retrained IAW 29 CFR § 1910.132(f)(3) when:
14.2.2.1. There is a change in their job assignment(s). (T-0)
14.2.2.2. There is a change in the work processes that present a new hazard. (T-0)
14.2.2.3. There is a change in the type of PPE required to be used. (T-0)
14.2.2.4. Other conditions as specified in 29 CFR § 1910.132(f)(3) require retraining.
(T-0)
14.2.3. Supervisor will document training IAW paragraph 1.7 and DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
Respiratory training will be documented IAW AFI 48-137. (T-1)
14.3. Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) and Equipment Selection. The use of PPE is the least
preferred method and not a replacement for other, more effective controls, such as elimination,
substitution, engineering, and administrative controls. If a JHA reveals PPE is the best solution, or
until controls can be implemented, PPE, e.g., hard hats, gloves, hearing protection, will be the
primary means to protect personnel against hazards. Note: If PPE is required for the performance
of a task, the commander and/or functional manager will provide it. (T-1) Subordinates are not
responsible for providing their own PPE. A documented JHA will be conducted to determine if
hazards are present and take actions necessary to protect employees from injury, illness, or death
IAW 29 CFR § 1910.132(d), Hazard the Assessment and Equipment Selection: Personal
Protective Equipment – General Requirements. (T-0) Occupational health PPE will be identified
during the BE HRA process. (T-1) PPE recommendations will be provided on the final HRA
report. (T-1) Organizations will consult with the occupational safety office and installation BE for
questions regarding PPE. (T-1) Note: PPE will be inspected, used, cleaned, and maintained per
manufacturer’s documentation included with the equipment. (T-1)

148

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
14.3.1. Supervisors will:
14.3.1.1. Conduct and document JHAs IAW 29 CFR § 1910.132(d). (T-0) Refer to
paragraph 1.5 and DAFI 91-202 for additional guidance on JHA.
14.3.1.2. Contact installation BE and occupational safety when workplace operations
change to schedule appropriate evaluation when new hazardous materials are introduced,
processed or procedures are changed, or engineering controls are modified or added. (T-1)
14.3.1.3. If PPE is required, the supervisor will ensure that PPE is provided, used, and
maintained in a sanitary and serviceable condition IAW manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0)
14.3.2. DAF personnel will comply with PPE requirements and:
14.3.2.1. Ensure PPE is used when required, serviceable, and properly adjusted or fitted.
(T-0)
14.3.2.2. Notify their supervisors if they wear contact lenses. (T-0) This information is
vital to emergency medical personnel who may need to remove a lens from the individual’s
eye. The use of contact lenses can present additional hazards, e.g., chemical environments,
welding operations. A JHA should be conducted to ensure that the use of contact lenses
does not expose employees to additional risks.
14.3.2.3. Notify their supervisors in advance if they have a medical condition or are taking
medications that could interfere with their safe performance of assigned duties. (T-0)
14.3.2.4. Notify supervisors of any changes in medical status which might impair their
ability to safely wear PPE, e.g., weight changes, facial scarring, dental changes,
disfigurement. (T-0)
14.3.3. Eye and Face Protection. Only protective eye and face devices that are certified by the
manufacturer to meet ANSI/ISEA Z87.1, Occupational and Educational Personal Eye and
Face Protection Devices, will be used. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.133, Eye and Face
Protection, for additional requirements. Note: Eye protection is required to protect against
small particles of debris when performing a task above eye level that could be expected to
create such a hazard. Note: Face shields are considered face protection only and must always
be worn with appropriate eye protection. (T-0)
14.3.4. Respiratory Protection. Installation BE, as the OPR for the installation respiratory
protection program, recommends and approves all respiratory protection devices and will be
consulted on respiratory protection required for specific shop activities and job sites, if
required. (T-1) AFI 48-137 and 29 CFR § 1910.134, Respiratory Protection, provide guidance
on prevention of occupational diseases caused by breathing air contaminated with harmful
dusts, fogs, fumes, mists, gases, smokes, sprays, vapors, or in oxygen-deficient environments.
14.3.5. Head Protection. Personnel working in areas where there is a potential for injury from
falling or flying objects, bumping head against a fixed object, or electrical shock or burns,
proper protective head gear will be provided and used. (T-0) Typical examples of these areas
are construction sites, flightlines and warehouses. Protective helmets must be certified by the
manufacturer to comply with ANSI/ISEA Z89.1, American National Standard for Industrial
Head Protection, and 29 CFR § 1910.135, Head Protection. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

149

14.3.5.1. Hair Protection (Includes Facial Hair). Employees who work around chains,
belts, rotating devices, suction devices, blowers, etc., will secure their hair, especially long
hair, to prevent it from being caught in moving equipment. (T-0) While such equipment is
normally guarded, long hair can fit between the mesh of guards and be drawn into the
moving parts.
14.3.5.2. Safety Helmets (Hard Hats). Commanders, supervisors, and team members will
ensure employees working on or near underground or aerial installation, construction and
certain maintenance-related jobs always wear an approved safety helmet (as determined by
a JHA). (T-1) The chinstrap will be worn during work aloft or anytime the helmet may fall
off the employees’ head while performing a specific task possibly resulting in injury to
Employees or damage to property. (T-1) Refer to ANSI/ISEA Z89.1 for additional
information. (T-0)
14.3.5.3. Bump Caps. Bump caps are constructed of lightweight materials and provide
minimal protection against bumps or lacerations to the head. They do not afford adequate
protection from high impact forces or penetration by flying or falling objects and will not
be used as a substitute for safety helmets, where applicable. Their use will be determined
by the supervisor’s hazard assessment, in conjunction with the occupational safety office.
(T-0)
14.3.6. Hearing Protection. Exposure to high noise levels can cause hearing loss or
impairment, in addition to physical and psychological stress. Specifically designed protection
may be required, depending on the type of noise encountered and the auditory condition of the
employee. AFI 48-127 is the primary guidance on hazardous noise exposure policies,
responsibilities, and procedures, including hearing protective device information. Contact
installation BE for information on the hearing protection program. Refer to AFI 48-127 for
additional guidance and information.
14.3.7. Electrical Workers’ Clothing and Equipment. Electrical clothing will be used IAW
UFC 3-560-01, NFPA 70E, AFMAN 32-1065, and manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0) Rubber
insulating PPE such as blankets, matting, covers, line hose, gloves, and sleeves will be used
IAW 29 CFR § 1910.137, Electrical Protective Equipment, and manufacturer’s instructions.
(T-0)
14.3.8. Hand and Arm Protection. When an employee’s hands or arms are exposed to hazards,
such as skin absorption of harmful substances, severe cuts, lacerations, abrasions, punctures,
chemical burns, or harmful temperature extremes, appropriate hand, or arm protection will be
provided and used. (T-0) Sleeves, padded arm protectors, hand pads, and other items will be
worn to protect the hands and arms when hot or sharp materials are handled. (T-0) There is a
wide assortment of gloves, hand pads, sleeves, and wristlets for protection against various
hazardous situations. Before purchasing any chemical protective equipment, contact
installation BE, who will evaluate the hazard and provide the necessary specifications such as
material type, thickness, and coverage area, then ensure the manufacturer’s recommended use
for the glove matches the particular application and anticipated hazards involved. (T-1)
Supervisors will select appropriate hand protection based on the characteristics required for
the task to be performed, dexterity required, conditions present, duration of use, frequency,
physical stresses, limitations of protective clothing, and degree of exposure to identified
hazards. (T-0)

150

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
14.3.9. Torso Protection. The torso is the largest exposed area of the body. A variety of
protective clothing is available to protect personnel from heat, hot metals, liquids, impacts,
cuts, corrosives, caustics, and radiation. Items such as vests, jackets, aprons, coveralls, and full
body suits have been specifically designed for this purpose. Back support belts are only worn
with supervisory approval and proper training of safe lifting techniques. They will not be relied
upon solely to protect the back from injury. Back support belts should be unfastened when the
individual is not presently involved in a lifting motion, to prevent the possibility of muscle
atrophy from prolonged usage.
14.3.10. Foot and Leg Protection (e.g., Safety Shoes, Toe Caps, Leggings, etc.). Protective
footwear will be provided and worn when there is a reasonable possibility of foot injuries from
heavy or sharp objects and electrical and/or static electricity. (T-0) Use appropriate foot guards,
safety shoes or boots, and leggings for protection of feet and legs from crushing hazards,
falling, rolling, sharp objects, molten metal, hot surfaces, and wet slippery surfaces. Leggings
protect the lower leg and feet from molten metal or welding sparks. Safety snaps permit their
rapid removal.
14.3.10.1. Shop JHA will identify those areas, operations, and occupations which require
protective leg or footwear. (T-1) All individually issued safety footwear used in DAF
operations will be certified by the manufacturer to meet the requirements found in ASTM
F2412, Standard Test Methods for Foot Protection, and ASTM F2413, Standard
Specification for Performance Requirements for Protective (Safety) Toe Cap Footwear.
(T-0)
14.3.10.2. Safety shoes will be sturdy and have an impact-resistant toe. (T-0) Only shoes
certified by the manufacturer, to meet or exceed the requirements found in ASTM F2412
and F2413 international standards, as incorporated by reference in 29 CFR § 1910.6,
Incorporation by Reference, are approved for the DAF. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.136, Foot
Protection, for additional guidance. Metal insoles in shoes protect against puncture
wounds. Additional protection, such as metatarsal guards, may be necessary for some job
tasks. Nonskid shoes will be worn where floors may be wet or slippery. (T-0) Electrical
hazard shoes are not designed to be a replacement for electrically rated matting in high
voltage situations. These shoes are designed to be used when working on low voltage
circuits and as a secondary means of protection. Note: When exposed to cold temperatures
and foot crushing hazards, if cold weather footwear which also provides crushing
protection is not available, personnel will be issued footwear which protects against the
possibility of frostbite. (T-0)
14.3.11. Knee Protection. Knee pads may be used by employees who are required to kneel
while performing most of their work or by employees with known knee problems that could
be compounded by working in a kneeling position.
14.3.12. Chemical Protective Clothing. The purpose of chemical protective clothing and
equipment is to shield or isolate individuals from chemical, physical, and biological hazards
encountered during hazardous materials operations. Many chemicals pose invisible hazards
and offer no warning properties. It is important for employees to realize that no single
protective clothing item can protect against all hazards. Other protective methods, such as
elimination, substitution, engineering, or administrative controls, will be used to limit chemical
contact before considering use of PPE. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

151

14.3.12.1. Chemical Protective Clothing Selection. Workplace supervisors will contact
installation BE to determine the appropriate PPE for all processes involving chemical
exposures. (T-1)
14.3.12.2. Ensemble Selection Factors:
14.3.12.2.1. Hazard Consideration. Chemicals present a variety of hazards such as
toxicity, corrosiveness, flammability, reactivity and oxygen deficiency, or a
combination of hazards may be present.
14.3.12.2.2. Requirements. The proper type of chemical protective clothing or
equipment depends on job requirements, and includes requirements from TOs, SDS
and manufacturer’s instructions. Check with installation BE before purchasing
chemical protective clothing or equipment.
14.3.12.3. Chemical Protective Clothing Limitations. Many chemicals can have adverse
effects on protective clothing and gloves. Permeation, a process by which a chemical can
pass through PPE, and degradation, a reduction in the physical properties of PPE, are two
examples. Chemicals allowed to remain on protective clothing or equipment can be
absorbed into the material. Chemical protective clothing that is reused must be cleaned
before storage. (T-0) In some cases, it may be more cost effective to select chemical
protective clothing with a relatively short breakthrough time (see definitions, Attachment
1) and decontaminate and discard it properly after one use.
14.3.12.3.1. Duration of Exposure. Maximum duration of exposure to a chemical will
be considered when selecting chemical protective clothing. (T-0) Review the SDS and
contact installation BE to determine the appropriate PPE.
14.3.12.3.2. Performance Characteristics.
14.3.12.3.2.1. Resistance to abrasions, cuts, heat, ozone, and punctures or tears will
be considered to maintain the integrity of the material. (T-0)
14.3.12.3.2.2. Flexibility may be an important factor if inflexible equipment
interferes with the employee’s ability to successfully accomplish the task.
14.3.13. Welding, Cutting and Brazing Specific PPE. Welding, cutting and brazing PPE
requirements and usage will be IAW 29 CFR § 1910.252, Welding, Cutting and Brazing –
General Requirements, 29 CFR § 1910.132 and AFI 48-137. (T-0)
14.3.14. Light Reflective Products.
14.3.14.1. Light reflective and luminescent materials should be utilized during hours of
decreased visibility. These items are also available in the primary safety code colors and
should be used for standard signs and markings. Reflective or luminescent materials must
remain reflective when wet.
14.3.14.2. Personnel exposed to traffic environments or flightline operations during hours
of darkness or reduced visibility, or as part of construction, will be provided and use
reflective equipment or will use organizational clothing with sewn-on reflective tape. (T-3)
Refer to ANSI/ISEA 107, American National Standard for High-Visibility Safety Apparel
and Accessories, for additional information. Note: Personnel whose job requires them to

152

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
perform some function in the traffic/flightline environment will be provided reflective
equipment to enhance their visibility, except as previously stated for Security Forces. (T-1)
14.3.15. Heat Reflective and Protective Clothing. Radiant heat is a problem in operations such
as heat treating of metals, foundry operations, and heating plants. Heat reflective qualities of
clothing and other surfaces are considerably affected by color and surface characteristics.
Polished metallic aluminum is an effective surface to reflect radiant heat. Helmets, suits,
gloves, and boots with this type of surface are available and will be used when necessary to
prevent heat stress and physical injury. (T-0) Frequent breaks and drinking plenty of water are
also critical to prevent heat-related injuries. Refer to DAFI 48-151 for additional guidance.
Note: If metallic coating on heat reflective clothing is conductive, it will not be worn where
there is the possibility of electrical shock.
14.3.16. Cold Testing and Clothing. Protective clothing used in cold testing or cold storage
areas must be adequate to protect employees from hazards involved with the task and cold
temperatures. (T-0) Supervisors will coordinate with the Occupational Safety and installation
BE offices to determine proper equipment for the task. The JHAs should identify approved
protection for working in cold temperatures. (T-1) Frostbite can be avoided by wearing
appropriate protective clothing and by taking frequent breaks to warm extremities. Supervisors
will ensure cold weather clothing is provided when needed for work in cold temperatures.
(T-1)
14.3.17. Personal Fall Arrest Systems (Safety Harnesses, Lanyards, Lifelines and Straps.
Refer to Chapter 13 of this standard for guidance on PFAS.

14.4. Proper Care and Maintenance. Employees will maintain all PPE IAW manufacturer’s
instructions to include being kept clean and in good repair. (T-0) Previously used PPE will be
disinfected before reissue to another employee. (T-0) Contaminated PPE that cannot be
decontaminated will be disposed of in a manner that protects employees from exposure to hazards.
(T-0) Contact installation BE and/or installation CE to determine the appropriate disposal
measures of contaminated PPE. Functional managers will ensure disposal of contaminated PPE is
consistent with applicable environmental regulations. (T-1) Reusable PPE that can be
decontaminated can be stored in resealable or drawstring type bags. Refer to the PPE
manufacturer’s instructions, UFC 3-560-01, AFMAN 32-1065, 29 CFR § 1910.137, Table I, and
29 CFR § 1910.141, Sanitation, for additional guidance on cleaning specific equipment.
14.4.1. Shop supervisors will ensure shop personnel use protective clothing and equipment
that will protect them from work hazards. (T-0)
14.4.2. Employees will keep their hands and face clean, change clothes and wash skin
contaminated with solvents, lubricants, or fuels, and keep hands and soiled objects out of their
mouth. (T-0) No food or drink will be brought into or consumed in areas exposed to toxic
materials, chemicals, or industrial shop contaminants nor will drinking water sources such as
water fountains be in those areas. (T-0) Industrial hygiene surveys will be reviewed for shop
hazards. (T-1) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.141 for additional information.
14.4.3. Shop supervisors will provide on-site laundry and shower facilities IAW 29 CFR §
1910.141. (T-0) The shower and laundry facilities will be provided when the occupational
safety office or installation BE determines an occupational hazard warrants them. (T-0) Note:
Contractors who perform laundry services will comply with 29 CFR § 1910.141.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

153
Chapter 15

TRAINING SYSTEMS
15.1. Scope. This chapter provides guidance on fire protection engineering criteria, fire
prevention requirements, and housekeeping procedures for the system safety and risk management
