of all training devices in AFI 10-217, Management of Air Force Operational Training and
Undergraduate Aircrew Training Systems. It also applies to all training systems regardless of the
stage of design, development, or date of installation. Air Force Civil Engineer Center (AFCEC)
will determine the fire prevention requirements for trainers not covered in AFI 10-217.
15.1.1. Hazards. Protective measures, such as grounding, conductive floors and benches,
humidity control, antistatic packaging of spare components, and other controls will be used as
needed to prevent electrostatic discharge. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 2 for guidance on hazards
and human factors.
15.1.2. Fire, Injury and Equipment Damage Hazards. Refer to MIL-STD-1472H, DoD Design
Criteria Standard: Human Engineering, for additional guidance on damage to training
systems equipment.
15.2. General Requirements.
15.2.1. Fire Extinguishers. Listed Class C carbon dioxide, clean agent, or water mist fire
extinguishers with a minimum rating of 10B:C located IAW NFPA10, Standard for Portable
Fire Extinguishers, must be provided within all facility areas containing permanently installed
electronic equipment such as motion bays, model boards, visual display rooms, computer bays,
etc. (T-0) Similar fire extinguishers may be provided in cockpit and/or other crew stations. Dry
and wet chemical agent fire extinguishers are prohibited.
15.2.2. Fire and Emergency Procedures. The training system operator and/or instructor will
provide a fire safety briefing to trainees at the start of each training session. (T-0) Prompt
action during a fire will protect personnel and minimize damage.
15.2.3. Housekeeping.
15.2.3.1. Combustible materials will be disposed of in suitable noncombustible containers,
conspicuously labeled as to contents, and with self-closing lids. (T-0) These containers will
be emptied when full and at least at the end of each working shift. (T-0) Contact installation
CE for disposal procedures for combustible hazardous waste. Refer to Chapter 22 for
additional guidance.
15.2.3.2. Building floors, especially raised floors, will be kept smooth, clean, and free of
obstructions and slippery materials. (T-0)
15.2.3.3. Floors will not be cleaned with flammable or combustible liquids. (T-0) Follow
the manufacturer’s instructions and SDS guidelines when using cleaning agents for floors
and facilities.
15.2.3.4. To prevent buildup of trash and foreign objects, sub-floors will be visually
inspected and cleaned, as needed, but not less than once every 60 days. (T-1) Sub-floors
will be inspected once each operational day to ensure no flooding or leaking is present
unless the sub-floor area is provided with an automatic water leakage detection system or

154

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
where no water intrusion is possible. (T-1) Note: Any change in facility or room use or
configuration will require reassessment of the cleaning schedule and the need to check for
water intrusion. (T-1)
15.2.3.5. Utility trenches will be kept clean of foreign objects and inspected at least every
60 days. (T-1) If cleaning history shows a longer period between cleanings is feasible, the
F&ES Flight may allow extending the time between cleanings.
15.2.3.6. Hot Work. Hot work permits will not be issued until the requirements of Chapter
20 are met. Additional information can be found in NFPA 51B, Fire Prevention During
Welding, Cutting and Other Hot Work. (T-0) Consult with F&ES Flight for additional
guidance.

15.3. Facility Requirements.
15.3.1. Planning. Proper layout, spacing and arrangement of equipment and machinery are
essential and can best be achieved in the planning stages by considering current and future uses
of the facility. The Occupational Safety office, installation CE, F&ES Flight, and installation
BE will coordinate on all plans. (T-1)
15.3.2. Location. Training systems will be in facilities protected by automatic sprinkler
systems. (T-1) Piloted and unpiloted aircraft flight trainers must be in spaces with additional
ultra-sensitive smoke detection as well. (T-1) Electronic procedures training must also be in
spaces with additional ultra-sensitive smoke detection.
15.3.3. Existing facilities that house training devices with internal fire detection and alarm
systems do not require ultra-sensitive systems but will be provided with a standard smoke
detection system. (T-1)
15.3.4. Training system spaces will be separated from other spaces and/or occupancies and
operationally distinct adjacent equipment spaces in the facility by a minimum of 1-hour firerated construction, extending from structural floor to structural ceiling and/or roof. (T-0)
15.3.5. If training system spaces are served by air handling equipment which also serves any
other part of the facility, including an adjacent training system space, automatic smoke, and
fire dampers will be provided to shut off the space from the remainder of the facility upon any
facility fire alarm activation. (T-0) Facility fire alarm activations originating outside the
training system space do not require deactivation of the air handling system that serves an
individual training system.
15.3.6. Communications and interconnecting cable and wiring in training system and raised
floor spaces will comply with NFPA 70, Article 645, Information Technology Equipment.
(T-0) Communications and interconnecting cable and wiring in training system spaces and
other areas of the facility or other facilities will comply with NFPA 70, Article 725, Class 1,
Class 2 and Class 3 Remote-Control, Signaling and Power-Limited Circuits. (T-0)
15.3.7. Electrical.
15.3.7.1. Surge protection will be provided for all incoming power to the training system.
(T-0) New training system facilities will have surge protection for the entire facility. (T-0)
15.3.7.2. All power and distribution circuits will comply with NFPA70 and UFC 3-52001, Interior Electrical Systems. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

155

15.3.8. Drainage. Drainage will be provided in utility trenches and motion bays. (T-0)
15.3.9. Hydraulic Pump Rooms. Hydraulic pump rooms will be of 1-hour fire rated
construction, except rooms which contain more than 250 gallons of hydraulic fluid, which will
be of 2-hour fire rated construction. (T-0)
15.3.10. Emergency Lighting. Emergency lighting will be provided IAW NFPA 101, Life
Safety Code. (T-0)
15.3.11. Fire protection features installed in the facility, or the trainers must be maintained
and comply with the requirements of UFC 3-600-01. (T-0)
15.4. Training Systems Requirements.
15.4.1. General Flammability Requirements. Only fire resistant and non-hazardous (when
exposed to fire) materials will be used in cockpits, seats, shrouds, cable ties, cable coverings
and auxiliary insulation for heat, electrical, sound, etc. (T-0) Manufacturers will certify that
the flame spread rating for these materials will not exceed 75 and smoke development will not
exceed 100 when tested IAW ASTM E84, Standard Test Method for Surface Burning
Characteristics of Building Materials. (T-0) Alternately, materials certified by the
manufacturer will be considered acceptable that meet one of the following: certification under
California State Fire Marshal will Title 19; listing under Underwriters Laboratories (UL) 94,
Standard for Tests for Flammability of Plastic Materials for Parts in Devices and Appliances,
or equivalent OSHA-recognized NRTL certification; or successful testing under NFPA 701,
Standard Methods of Fire Tests for Flame Propagation of Textiles and Films. (T-0) Materials
not meeting one of these requirements may only be used when the training device application
is identical to the aircraft/wheeled vehicle configuration and material.
15.4.2. General Hydraulic System Features.
15.4.2.1. Hydraulic pump controls will be designed to automatically shut down sudden or
excessive flow in the hydraulic lines when the fluid level is low or when excessive
hydraulic fluid temperature is sensed. (T-0)
15.4.2.2. Flexible hoses will be shielded to prevent leaking hydraulic fluid from spraying
on potential ignition sources, trainees, and staff personnel. (T-1) Braided shielding integral
to the flexible hoses will be used whenever the hoses are not located in covered trenches.
(T-1)
15.4.2.3. Upon loss of power or shutdown of the hydraulic pumps, the training device will
automatically return to the rest position within 10 seconds. (T-1)
15.4.2.3.1. For wheeled vehicle egress assistance trainers, it is permitted to provide a
manual hand crank feature, or equivalent method, to enable the instructor/operator to
manually rotate the trainer to a position that allows safe egress by the trainees. (T-1)
15.4.2.3.2. For aircraft fuselage trainers, hydraulically operated doors are not required
to be moveable upon loss of power or shutdown of the hydraulic pumps if exit doors
are provided for trainee egress from the trainer. (T-1) The number of doors and
locations will comply with the requirements in NFPA 101. (T-0)

156

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
15.4.2.3.3. For aircraft flight and similarly high value training systems, access ramps
will automatically deploy after motion is stopped — even when power is removed.
(T-1)
15.4.2.4. Hydraulic pump controls will be designed so the operation of the facility fire
alarm or training system fire alarm system shuts down the pumps. (T-1) This feature is not
required for wheeled vehicle egress assistance trainers.
15.4.3. Emergency Lighting. Emergency lighting will be provided in all enclosed occupied
training spaces IAW NFPA 70, Article 700, Emergency Systems. (T-0)

15.5. Requirements of Wheeled Vehicle Egress Assistance Trainers.
15.5.1. Safety Zone. A safety zone will be provided to keep observers at a safe distance when
the unit is rotating. (T-1)
15.5.2. Head Strike Hazard. Projections inside the unit will be padded and loose objects
secured that could present a head strike hazard to trainees during unit rollover. (T-0) Padding
will comply with flammability requirements in paragraph 15.4.1. (T-0)
15.5.3. Walkways. Walkways will be padded where the trainees egress to minimize the
potential for injury to personnel should a trainee fall from the vehicle doors. (T-1) Padding
will comply with flammability requirements in paragraph 15.4.1. (T-0)
15.5.4. Fire Extinguisher. A portable fire extinguisher with a minimum rating of 10B:C will
be located within immediate vicinity of the instructor/operator. (T-1)
15.5.5. Power Failure. A means will be provided to manually rotate the unit to a safe position
for trainee egress in case of loss of power or failure of the powered rotation motor. (T-1)
15.5.6. Egress Procedures. Procedures will be established to provide safe emergency
occupants egress from the unit, such as when the unit fails to fully rotate to the design rollover
position. (T-1)
15.5.7. Loading and Seating. Approved occupant seating and loading limits will be posted in
the immediate vicinity of the unit. (T-1) Prohibited seating arrangements will also be posted,
such as any unbalanced seating arrangements that could prevent manual rotation of the unit
during an emergency. (T-1)
15.5.8. Retractor Mechanisms. Trainees will not be assigned to any location in the trainer
(such as the gunner’s hatch in the roof of a HMMWV Egress Assistance Trainer unit) that
requires completion of manual actions by the trainee or others to pull/move the trainee to a
safe position during a rollover operation. (T-1) Automatic retractor mechanisms (such as
gunner’s restraint retractors) may be used only after they have been tested to successfully
operate during all possible operational modes of the trainer, including failure to fully rotate to
the design rollover position. (T-1)
15.6. Requirements for Aircraft Flight and Similarly High Value Training Systems.
15.6.1. Fire Detection and Alarm Systems.
15.6.1.1. New or relocated training systems do not require an internal fire detection and
alarm system when located in spaces protected by ultra-sensitive smoke detection IAW
paragraph 15.3.2.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

157

15.6.1.2. Existing training systems in spaces protected by a standard smoke detection
system will have an internal fire detection and alarm system. (T-1) This system will
interconnect all detection and annunciation devices with the various training system
components (e.g., cabinets, instructor operator station, cockpit, motion base, etc.). (T-1)
This system will be either of the dual fixed-temperature type or the ultra-sensitive type.
(T-1)
15.6.1.2.1. Dual fixed-temperature type fire detectors will be installed in electrical,
electronic, hydraulic and computer cabinets. (T-1) Activation of the first detector, e.g.,
the one calibrated to the lower activating temperature will produce a distinctive audible
signal. (T-1) Activation of the second detector, e.g., the one calibrated to the higher
activating temperature, will activate the facility detection system through the training
system fire alarm panel and de-energize the simulator and all peripheral equipment.
(T-1)
15.6.1.2.2. Ultra-sensitive smoke detection systems will be provided to protect
electrical, electronic, and computer cabinets. (T-1) A minimum two-stage warning
capability will be provided. (T-1) The first, more sensitive stage will produce a
distinctive audible signal. (T-1) Activation of the second, less sensitive stage will
activate the facility detection system through the training system fire alarm panel and
de-energize the simulator and all peripheral equipment. (T-1)
15.6.1.2.3. If detection equipment is in an unoccupied area, e.g., computer rooms, a
means will be provided to positively alert operators at the instructor operator station.
(T-1) If multiple unoccupied computer areas are present, then a means will be provided
to inform the instructor operator station which unoccupied area has the activated alarm.
(T-1)
15.6.1.3. A fire alarm graphic annunciator or display panel, to identify training system fire
detection systems signals, will be installed IAW NFPA 72, National Fire Alarm and
Signaling Code. (T-0) Each detection device will be identified by light emitting diodes, or
other readily visible means, in the graphic annunciator or display panel. (T-1)
15.6.1.4. The fire detection system will be powered by a dedicated circuit with backup
battery power and connected to the facility electrical supply ahead of the training system
disconnect switch. (T-1)
15.6.1.5. The fire detection system will be capable of operating from both 50 and 60 hertz.
(T-1)
15.6.1.6. The system fire detection and alarm control panel will be designed to transmit
both coded fire and trouble signals to the fire alarm and receiving center or other
monitoring service (when not located on a DoD installation) IAW NFPA 72. (T-0) This
may be through the facility fire alarm transmitter or an independent fire alarm transmitter.
Contact F&ES Flight for fire alarm and receiving equipment requirements. (T-1)
15.6.1.7. Interface of Facility and Training System Fire Protection Systems.
15.6.1.7.1. Required functions upon activation of the facility fire alarm system
(without activation of any suppression system in the facility):
15.6.1.7.1.1. Occupants in the training spaces will be notified of the alarm

158

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
condition through audible (e.g., siren, horn, bell, etc.) and visual (strobe) cues.
(T-1) Occupants of the facility (including those in the training spaces) will exit the
facility immediately without delay, following the facility’s preexisting emergency
evacuation plan. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.1.2. For training systems meeting the requirements of paragraph 15.4,
the instructor may place the training system in a standby mode, system freeze or
other appropriate conditions prior to evacuation. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.1.3. For existing facilities and training systems awaiting upgrade to
comply with paragraph 15.4, the training system power source will be shut down.
(T-1) This shutdown may be accompanied by an automatic emergency power off
or by an automatic, rapid, sequenced shutdown. (T-1) Also see paragraph 15.7.
15.6.1.7.2. The training system power source will shut down. (T-1) This shutdown
may be accomplished by an automatic emergency power off or by an automatic, rapid,
sequenced shutdown.
15.6.1.7.3. Required functions upon activation of a training system fire detection
system only (without activation of any fire suppression system):
15.6.1.7.3.1. Training staff will be notified upon activation of the first, less
sensitive stage of ultra-sensitive smoke detection systems or upon activation of the
first detector in dual fixed-temperature type systems. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.3.2. The training system will automatically emergency power off upon
activation of the second, less sensitive stage of ultra-sensitive smoke detection
systems or upon activation of the second detector in dual fixed-temperature type
systems for training systems meeting the requirements of paragraph 15.4. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.4. Required functions upon activation of a training system fire suppression
system — the training system power source will automatically turn emergency power
off. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.5. Training staff will be provided with means to manually emergency power
off whenever necessary for the safety of the staff, students, or the equipment, prior to
exiting the facility. (T-1)
15.6.1.7.6. Following an emergency power off, the training system fire alarm system
will notify occupants throughout the facility of the alarm condition through audible
(siren, horn, bell, etc.) and visual (strobe) cues. (T-1)
15.6.2. Fire Stops. All cable trays, utility trenches, etc., that connect to spaces outside training
system spaces will have fire stops installed every 25 feet. (T-1)
15.6.3. Air Handling Equipment. Training systems served by air handling equipment which
also serves any other part of the facility, including any adjacent training system space, will
have automatic smoke and fire dampers to shut off the space from the remainder of the facility
on any facility fire alarm indication. (T-1) Facility fire alarm activations originating outside
the training system space do not require deactivation of the air handling system that serves an
individual training system.
15.6.4. Specifications for Cable and Wiring.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

159

15.6.4.1. Polyvinyl chloride coated wire will not be used in the cockpit areas of noncommercial-off-the-shelf hardware and/or training systems. (T-1) Plenum rated cable and
wiring meeting the requirements of NFPA 70, Article 300.22, Wiring Methods, will be
used. (T-0) Note: Polyvinyl chloride coated wire is allowed in the cockpit areas of
commercial-off-the-shelf hardware and/or training systems per manufacturer’s standard or
commercial design practices provided the cockpit is not enclosed or, if enclosed, that
smoke detectors are installed, and the system design vents any smoke or gases to the
exterior of the cockpit.
15.6.4.2. Communications and interconnecting cable and wiring between components
within the training system space and raised floor space will comply with NFPA 70, Article
670, Industrial Machinery, and those in the training system space and other areas of the
facility or other facilities will comply with NFPA 70, Article 725. (T-0)
15.6.4.3. Power and signal cable groups in which heat cannot quickly dissipate will
contain a heat sensor wire or other heat sensing device. (T-1)
15.6.4.4. Cable and wiring for the training system fire alarm system will comply with
NFPA 70, Article 760, Fire Alarm Systems. (T-0)
15.6.5. Power Disconnect Means. A means to disconnect electrical power to the entire training
system will be provided at all instructor operator stations and at other key locations on the
training system. (T-1)
15.6.6. Computer and/or Electronic Cabinets.
15.6.6.1. Self-contained air conditioning systems will include a product of combustion
detector capable of sounding an audible alarm and activating the facility detection system.
(T-1)
15.6.6.2. Cabinets near a motion base water fog or spray deluge system will be water
resistant or shielded from water discharge. (T-1) Cabinets near a motion base wet pipe
sprinkler system do not require special protection.
15.6.6.3. The acquiring authority may exempt “off-the-shelf” computer and/or electronic
devices with internal thermally activated power disconnects from requirements for dual
fixed-temperature type thermal fire detectors for computer and/or electronic cabinets. (T-1)
15.6.7. Instructor Operator Station.
15.6.7.1. A means to activate the facility fire detection system will be provided in easy
reach of the operators at the instructor operator station. (T-1) Normally, a standard manual
fire alarm station will be provided on the operator’s console with provision to interconnect
to the facility alarm and detection system, and to initiate a training system emergency
power off. (T-1)
15.6.7.2. Smoke detection devices will be provided in the instructor operator station when
located in an enclosed container and/or space not part of the facility. (T-1) Activation of
this system will alert the training system occupants and the operators at the instructor
operator station. (T-1)
15.6.7.3. A facility alarm annunciator will be installed when the instructor operator station
is in an enclosed container and/or space not part of the facility. (T-1)

160

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
15.6.8. Cockpit and/or Other Crew Stations.
15.6.8.1. Single station smoke detection devices will be provided in cockpits and other
enclosed crew stations. (T-1) Battery powered detectors will not be used.
15.6.8.2. The cockpit will be designed to preclude or restrict passage of smoke and gases
through the cockpit floor or sides. (T-1)
15.6.8.3. A means will be provided to alert cockpit and/or other crew station occupants of
a positive activation of the local and facility detection systems. (T-1)
15.6.8.4. Training station ventilation will be shut down if the training system fire detection
is activated. (T-1)
15.6.9. Motion Base.
15.6.9.1. The motion system will return to the rest position within 10 seconds of activation
of either the system fire detection system or the facility fire suppression system. (T-1)
Access ramps will automatically deploy after motion is stopped, even when power is
removed. (T-1)
15.6.9.2. Hydraulically powered motion bases will comply with paragraph 15.4.2 and the
following:
15.6.9.2.1. Rate-compensated type thermal fire detection devices will be provided
under each motion platform. (T-1) These rate-compensated type thermal fire detection
devices are not required when all the following conditions are met:
15.6.9.2.1.1. The training system is in a facility fully protected by a wet pipe
sprinkler system.
15.6.9.2.1.2. Flight training devices and associated controls are in spaces protected
by ultra-sensitive smoke detection systems as defined in UFC 3-600-01.
15.6.9.2.1.3. The cockpit/crew stations located on the motion base fully comply
with the cockpit/crew station requirements listed in paragraph 15.6.8. (T-1)
15.6.9.2.2. A wet pipe sprinkler system (sprinkler heads and piping) will be provided
as part of each motion base. (T-0) This motion base sprinkler system is not required for
training systems when all the following conditions are met: the training system is
located in a facility fully protected by a wet pipe sprinkler system; flight training
devices and associated controls are located in spaces protected by ultra-sensitive smoke
detection systems as defined in UFC 3-600-01; and the cockpit/crew stations located
on the motion base fully comply with the cockpit/crew station requirements listed in
paragraph 15.6.8. The exact position for each sprinkler and number of sprinklers will
be defined for each motion base to ensure complete coverage and prevent interference
with each hydraulic actuator’s range of motion. (T-1) The system will deliver a
minimum of 0.35 gallons per minute per square foot of area under the motion base.
(T-0) Normally, sprinkler piping is interconnected with the facility sprinkler system,
but a separate system riser with separate flow detection is acceptable.
15.6.9.2.3. A pre-action sprinkler system is permitted for the motion base in lieu of the
wet pipe sprinkler system listed in paragraph 15.6.9.2.2 when approved in writing by
the DFPE per UFC 3-600-01. The rate-compensated type thermal fire detection devices

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

161

listed in paragraph 15.6.9.2.1 will be used to activate the pre-action functions of the
system. (T-1) Note: Increased procurement, operational and maintenance expenses are
associated with the use of pre-action sprinkler systems.
15.7. Upgrade of Existing Aircraft Flight and Similarly High Value Training
Systems. Existing facilities and training systems may continue in use while awaiting an upgrade
to comply with paragraph 15.4, if they comply with all the following:
15.7.1. Facilities. No Category I or II fire safety deficiencies exist.
15.7.2. Training Systems. It is assumed existing training system deficiencies have been
upgraded or are programmed to be upgraded to comply with previous (superseded) editions of
OSH standards or previous (superseded) revisions of this DAFMAN. Training systems
currently identified to be upgraded to previous (superseded) editions of this DAFMAN may be
upgraded as programmed provided Halon 1301 or other gaseous agents are not part of the
upgrade.
15.7.3. Maintenance. All installed fire protection features will be regularly serviced and
maintained in operational condition.
15.7.4. Halon. Existing Halon fire suppression systems will be placed on manual activation
and automatic discharge mechanisms disabled. (T-1) Existing Halon fire suppression systems
will not be re-serviced following discharge, and the training device will remain operational.
(T-1) Training systems developed with Halon fire suppression systems will transition to
current standards during any major modification. (T-1)

162

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 16
MOBILE ELEVATING WORK PLATFORMS AND SCAFFOLDING

16.1. Scope. This chapter applies to vehicle-mounted elevating and rotating platforms, including
work platforms and aircraft maintenance equipment used for elevating employees and scaffolding
requirements. It also applies to all manually propelled and self-propelled mobile work platforms
(including ladder stands) and rolling (mobile) scaffolds (towers). In some cases, the DAF changes
the design of commercially purchased mobile work platforms and scaffolds or builds this
equipment; therefore, this chapter includes essential information on loads, rules and requirements
for design, construction, and use of mobile work platforms and scaffolds.
16.1.1. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart D – Walking-Walking Surfaces, 29 CFR § 1910,
Subpart F – Powered Platforms, Manlifts and Vehicle-Mounted Work Platforms, and 29 CFR
§ 1910.27, Scaffolds and Rope Descent Systems, for additional guidance. All fall prevention
and protection options must be evaluated and approved by a qualified person before use.
16.1.2. Modifications to mobile elevating work platforms and scaffolding must be approved
and certified in writing by the manufacturer. (T-0) Authorized modifications include
temporary removal of installed equipment not needed for a particular mission or modification
to provide a new or improved capability. Warning: Any modification which removes or
defeats any safety device or feature on an aerial device is prohibited. (T-0)
16.2. Hazards. Mobile elevating work platforms and scaffolding hazards examples include:
16.2.1. Falls from elevation.
16.2.2. Getting struck by falling objects.
16.2.3. Electrical shock.
16.2.4. Faulty/broken equipment, e.g., loose/bad planking.
16.2.5. Working surface hazards, such as holes that cause the platform/scaffolding to fall over.
16.3. Training.
16.3.1. Training will be provided to each affected employee who is required to perform work
on mobile elevating work platforms and scaffolding. (T-0) Supervisors will train each
employee on the requirements found in Attachment 3 of this standard, 29 CFR § 1910.30,
Training Requirements – Walking-Working Surfaces, and manufacturer’s requirements. (T-0)
Hands-on training will be provided as part of on-the-job training. (T-1)
16.3.2. Retraining. Affected employees will be retrained:
16.3.2.1. When an employee fails to demonstrate the required level of understanding and
skill to safely operate the equipment or has an accident or near miss while operating the
equipment. (T-0)
16.3.2.2. Where changes to the workplace create new hazards that render the previous
training obsolete or inadequate. (T-0)
16.3.2.3. When there are changes to the type of equipment used that make the previous
training obsolete or inadequate.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

163

16.3.2.4. When the operator is required to operate equipment at a new duty location. (T-0)
16.3.3. Supervisors will document training IAW paragraph 1.8 of this standard and DAFI
91-202. (T-1)
16.4. Vehicle-Mounted Elevating and Rotating Work Platforms.
16.4.1. Procurement. All vehicle-mounted elevating and rotating aerial devices will be
maintained to the requirements of the manufacturer. (T-0)
16.4.2. Modifications. Modifications will conform to ANSI/SAIA A92.2 and 29 CFR §
1910.67, Vehicle-Mounted Elevating and Rotating Work Platforms, and will be documented
on the appropriate form. (T-0) Authorized modifications include temporary removal of
installed equipment not needed for a particular mission or modification to provide a new or
improved capability. Warning: Any modification which removes or defeats any safety device
or feature on an aerial device is prohibited. (T-0)
16.4.3. Clearances.
16.4.3.1. Electrical. Maintain the following clearances when operating aerial lifts under,
over, by, or near electric power lines:
16.4.3.1.1. For lines rated 50 kilovolts (kV) or less, the minimum clearance between
lines and any part of the aerial lift will be at least 10 feet. (T-0)
16.4.3.1.2. For lines rated over 50 kV, the minimum clearance between lines and any
part of the aerial lift will be at least 10 feet plus 4 inches for each 10 kV over 50 kV.
(T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.333, Selection and Use of Work Practices, for additional
information.
16.4.3.2. Exceptions: Requirements in paragraph 16.4.3 do not apply if:
16.4.3.2.1. The aerial device is insulated for work and work is performed by trained
telecommunications employees, line clearance tree-trimming employees, or exterior
electric linemen.
16.4.3.2.2. Electric power transmission or distribution lines have been de-energized
and visibly grounded at point of work or where insulating barriers (not part of or
attached to the aerial lift) have been erected to prevent physical contact with the lines.
16.4.3.2.3. Insulating barriers (rated for the voltage of lines being guarded and not part
of or attached to the vehicle or its raised structure) are installed to prevent contact with
lines. In this instance, the clearance may be reduced to a distance within the designed
working dimensions of the insulating barrier. Refer to UFC 3-560-01, Electrical Safety,
Operations and Maintenance (O & M), for additional information.
16.4.3.2.4. Aerial lift is insulated for voltage involved and work is performed by a
qualified person. In this instance, the clearance between uninsulated parts of the aerial
lift and power line may be reduced to the distance in UFC 3-560-01, Table 10.9.
16.4.3.2.5. An aerial lift or equipment contacts an electrical conductor. In this instance,
the vehicle, equipment, and attachments will be considered energized. (T-0) Personnel
standing on the ground will not contact any part of the lift unless using protective

164

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
equipment rated for the voltage. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 28, Communications Cable,
Antenna and Communications Systems, for additional information.
16.4.3.3. Aircraft. When operating aerial lifts under, over, by or near aircraft, ensure
adequate clearance is maintained to allow for slight movement of both the aircraft and
aerial equipment caused by light winds. Refer to the operator’s manual or applicable TO
for specified wind limits.
16.4.4. Proximity Warning Devices. Proximity warning devices may be installed on aerial
platforms but will not release the operator from assuring safe clearance IAW paragraph
16.4.3. (T-0)
16.4.5. Inspection and Test Procedures.
16.4.5.1. Aerial ladders will be inspected and tested annually or immediately following
any activity when it is known, or suspected, to have been loaded beyond its maximum load
capacity, or immediately following any repair of any ladder assembly structural or
mechanical component. (T-0) Refer to manufacturer’s instructions for inspection
requirements. Inspections are the joint responsibility of the principal operator and vehicle
mechanic qualified to perform annual inspections. Document inspections and tests IAW
AFI 24-302, Vehicle Management. (T-1) Note: Aerial devices used for firefighting will be
inspected and tested at least annually IAW NFPA 1914, Standard for Testing Fire
Department Aerial Devices. (T-1) Qualified F&ES personnel may perform many of the
tests and inspection actions. However, it is recommended they be performed by a thirdparty testing company or the aerial device manufacturer. A record of the inspection and
tests will be documented IAW AFI 24-302. (T-1) Refer to ANSI/SAIA A92.2 for
additional information.
16.4.5.2. Articulating and extendible boom platforms will be inspected and tested
annually. (T-0) Special inspections and tests will be conducted immediately following any
use where the work platform may have been loaded beyond the manufacturer’s rated
capacity or immediately following any repair of a boom or work platform assembly
structural or mechanical component. (T-0) Refer to the manufacturer’s manual for
inspection requirements. Inspections will be a joint effort by the principal operator and a
vehicle mechanic qualified to perform annual inspections of the vehicle. (T-1) The
inspection and test will be documented IAW AFI 24-302. (T-1) When the personnel
platform is attached to a telescoping derrick or crane with a rated capacity higher than that
of the work platform, test procedures contained herein will not apply and testing will be
accomplished in accordance with applicable TOs or manufacturer’s maintenance manual.
(T-1)
16.4.5.3. Refer to 29 CFR 1926.1431, Hoisting Personnel, for information on use of cranes
or derricks to hoist personnel work platforms during construction activities.
16.4.6. Operations.
16.4.6.1. ANSI/SAIA A92.2 requires aerial device manufacturers to provide manuals to
dealers and direct sale purchasers. DAF users will ensure manuals are readily available
(stored in weather-resistant compartments provided by manufacturers) to employees. (T-1)
These manuals will contain:

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

165

16.4.6.1.1. Description, specifications, and ratings of the aerial device. (T-1)
16.4.6.1.2. Operating instructions for aerial devices and their auxiliary systems. (T-1)
16.4.6.1.3. Precautions relating to multiple configurations such as performing aerial
work from a moving vehicle. (T-1)
16.4.6.1.4. Instructions regarding routine maintenance and frequency of recommended
maintenance. (T-1)
16.4.6.1.5. Replacement part information. (T-1)
16.4.6.1.6. Instruction markings per paragraph 16.4.6.2. (T-1)
16.4.6.2. Each aerial device will have a clearly visible plate or plates (located in a readily
accessible area) stating:
16.4.6.2.1. Make, model and manufacturer’s serial number. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.2. Rated capacity. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.3. Platform height. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.4. Maximum recommended operating pressures of hydraulic and/or
pneumatic systems. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.5. Hazards that are inherent in operation of an aerial device. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.6. Manufacturer’s marking which describes the function of each control.
(T-1)
16.4.6.2.7. Insulated or non-insulated. (T-1) Note: Insulating aerial devices do not
protect personnel from phase to phase or phase to ground contacts at the platform end.
16.4.6.2.8. Qualification voltage and date of test, if applicable. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.9. Year of manufacture. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.10. Number of platforms. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.11. Category of aerial devices. (T-1)
16.4.6.2.12. Manufacturer’s rated line voltage. (T-1) Where alternative configurations
are possible, the plate will show by chart, schematic or scale the capacities of all
combinations in their operating positions and cautions or restrictions, or both, for
operation of all alternate or combinations of alternate configurations. These plates will
not be removed from the device, painted over or otherwise made unavailable for
reference by the operator. (T-1) Note: If an aerial device does not have a plate attached,
contact the manufacturer, and do not use the aerial lift until the plate is received. The
plate will not be removed or painted over. (T-1)
16.4.6.3. Employees required to use vehicle-mounted elevating and rotating work
platforms will be thoroughly trained in all aspects of the job before using this equipment.
(T-0) Refer to paragraph 16.3., Training, and Attachment 3 for more information.
16.4.6.4. Aerial devices will be secured in the specified lower traveling position (to
prevent rotation) using cradles or locking devices IAW manufacturer’s instructions before
moving the equipment as cargo. (T-0)

166

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.4.6.5. The unit will be visually inspected and lift controls tested prior to each shift or
day’s work to determine the unit and controls are in safe working condition. (T-0) The
override feature explained in paragraph 16.4.6.6 will be tested at this time. (T-0) IAW
ANSI/SAIA A92.2, a record of this test will only be documented if a discrepancy was
found with the unit during the inspection. (T-1)
16.4.6.6. Articulating and extendible boom platforms, primarily designed as personnel
carriers, will have both platform (upper) and lower controls. (T-0) Upper controls will be
in or beside the platform within easy reach of the operator. (T-0) In order to prevent
inadvertent actuation of the platform boom positioning controls, operation of an unlocking
device will precede use of the control itself and will be maintained simultaneously during
use of the controls. (T-0) The unlocking device may be incorporated into each control.
Lower controls will override upper controls. (T-0) Controls will be plainly marked with
their function. (T-0) The override mode will prevent boom movement with the lower
control station unattended. Lower-level controls will not be operated without permission
from the employee in the lift, except in an emergency. (T-0) Whenever an employee is in
or on an elevated work platform, at least one person qualified to operate the equipment will
be stationed near the lower-level control panel. (T-0) Exception: Personnel working in or
on an elevated boom without a qualified person near the lower-level control panel is
permitted if manufacturer’s instructions allow for such operations.
16.4.6.6.1. Emergency Stop. The platform will have an emergency stop control which
will stop all platform movement. (T-0) This control must not require continuous
operator actuation to stop movement. (T-0)
16.4.6.6.2. Outrigger Controls. Aerial device outrigger controls will be guarded to
protect against inadvertent operation and will return to neutral when released by the
operator. (T-0) These controls will be located so the operator can see the outrigger
being operated. (T-0)
16.4.6.7. Boom and basket load limits specified by the manufacturer will not be exceeded.
(T-0)
16.4.6.8. Aerial lift trucks will not be moved with the boom elevated and personnel in the
basket unless the lift truck is specifically designed for such operation. (T-0) In all cases,
the manufacturer’s operating instructions will be followed. (T-1)
16.4.6.9. Vehicle-mounted elevating and rotating work platforms will not be used during
high winds, thunderstorms or any other adverse or mission-limiting weather conditions
which could endanger employees using the platform. (T-0) Refer to the operator’s manual
or applicable TO for specified wind limits.
16.4.6.10. Platforms other than buckets or baskets will include a guardrail system which
will:
16.4.6.10.1. Include a top rail, 42 inches high and at least one rail approximately
midway between top rail and platform surface around its upper periphery. (T-0) Both
rails will withstand 200 pounds of force applied in any direction. (T-0)
16.4.6.10.2. Include toeboards on all sides of platforms. (T-0) Toeboards will be
securely fastened in place and with no more than 1/4-inch clearance above floor level.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

167

(T-0) Exception: A toeboard is not required at the access opening. Where there is
exposure to personnel or electrical wires below the platform, removable toeboards or
the equivalent will be provided IAW 29 CFR § 1910.28, Duty to Have Fall Protection
and Falling Object Protection. (T-0) The minimum toeboard height will be three and
a half (3.5) inches. (T-0)
16.4.6.10.3. Include access for personnel entering the platform in the lowered position.
(T-0) Access steps or rungs will have a slip-resistant surface. (T-0)
16.4.6.11. A full body harness and lanyard, sized to limit free fall distance to six feet or
less, or the free fall distance permitted by the system, whichever is less, will be worn by
each bucket or platform occupant. (T-0) Each lanyard will be attached to a designated
anchor point on the boom or basket. (T-0) Fall restraint will be used when there is
insufficient clearance for a PFAS. (T-0) Note: Fall restraint lanyards/connecting devices,
whether fixed length or adjustable, must be short enough to prevent the employee from
climbing out or being ejected from the basket. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR §§ 1910.67 or
1910.140 for additional information.
16.4.6.12. Belting off to an adjacent pole, structure or equipment while working from an
aerial lift will not be permitted. (T-0)
16.4.6.13. Employees will stand firmly on the basket floor, not sit, or climb on edge of
basket or use planks, ladders or other devices for a work position. (T-0)
16.4.6.14. Climber’s equipment will not be worn while performing work from an aerial
lift. (T-0)
16.4.6.15. Aerial devices, including insulated aerial devices, will not be brought into
contact with energized or potentially energized conductors. (T-0) Employees will not rely
on dielectric capabilities of aerial devices. (T-0)
16.4.6.16. Consult installation BE prior to work near suspected sources of non-ionizing
radiation, e.g., radar and microwave equipment. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 28 for additional
guidance.
16.4.6.17. Before using an aerial device, the worksite will be surveyed for hazards, such
as un-tamped earth fills, ditches, drop-offs and floor obstructions, debris, overhead
obstructions and electrical conductors, weather conditions, and presence of unauthorized
persons. (T-0)
16.4.6.18. Before and during each use, the operator will:
16.4.6.18.1. Check for overhead obstructions and electrical conductors. (T-0)
16.4.6.18.2. Ensure load weight does not exceed manufacturer’s rated capacity. (T-0)
16.4.6.18.3. Ensure outriggers and stabilizers are used if required by manufacturer's
instructions. (T-0)
16.4.6.18.4. Ensure guardrails are properly installed, including bolts, pins or other
locking devices on removable railings and attachments, and that gates are closed. (T-0)
16.4.6.18.5. Use outrigger pads when necessary to provide firm footing. (T-0)
16.4.6.18.6. Set brakes. (T-0)

168

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.4.6.18.7. Chock wheels before using the lift on an incline. (T-0)
16.4.7. Maintenance and Repair of Powered and/or Mobile Units.
16.4.7.1. If deficiencies affecting safe operation are discovered during the daily operator
inspection (or during use), the equipment will immediately be removed from service until
repaired. (T-0) The manufacturer’s recommended maintenance procedures will be
followed, and tests outlined in paragraph 16.4.5 will be conducted prior to returning an
aerial device to service following repairs to the ladder, boom, or work platform. (T-0)
16.4.7.2. All repairs will be accomplished by qualified personnel. (T-0)

16.5. Manually Propelled and Self-Propelled Mobile Work Platform and Scaffolds (Towers).
16.5.1. Work platforms with work levels four feet or higher accessed by a ladder or stairway
at an angle over 60o, will have guardrails, midrails and toeboards IAW paragraph 16.4.6.10
on all four sides of the work platform. (T-0) Exception: A toeboard is not required at the
access opening.
16.5.2. Working Loads.
16.5.2.1. Work platforms and scaffolds will carry their designed load under all conditions
of use. (T-0) Aircraft maintenance work platforms will support design loads listed in their
TOs. (T-1) Parts, additions, or accessories for safe use of platforms and scaffolds will be
part of the design. (T-1)
16.5.2.2. Specific design and construction requirements are not part of this standard due
to the wide variety of materials and design possibilities. However, the design used will
produce a mobile ladder stand or scaffold that will safely hold specified loads. (T-1)
Materials selected will be strong enough to meet test requirements and protected against
corrosion and deterioration. (T-1)
16.5.2.2.1. The design working load of ladder stands will be calculated based on one
or more 250-pound persons with 50 pounds of equipment each. (T-0) Ladder stands
and scaffolds will support at least 4 (four) times the design workload. (T-0)
16.5.2.2.2. Scaffold design workloads will be calculated based on:
16.5.2.2.2.1. Light – Designed and constructed for a workload of 25 pounds per
square foot. (T-0)
16.5.2.2.2.2. Medium – Designed and constructed for a workload of 50 pounds per
square foot. (T-0)
16.5.2.2.2.3. Heavy – Designed and constructed for a workload of 75 pounds per
square foot. (T-0)
16.5.2.3. Mobile ladder stands and scaffold materials will meet strength, dimension and
weight specifications in this standard to safely support the design workload. (T-0)
16.5.2.4. Bolts or other fasteners used to assemble ladders, scaffolds, and towers will be
large enough and in sufficient quantity at each connection to develop designed strength.
(T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

169

16.5.2.5. The operator must ensure there are no sharp edges, burrs, or other hazards to
personnel on exposed surfaces. (T-0)
16.5.3. Work Levels.
16.5.3.1. Maximum work level height will not exceed four times the minimum or least
base dimension (width) of any mobile ladder stand or scaffold. (T-0) Where the basic
mobile unit does not meet this requirement, suitable outrigger frames will be used to obtain
this least base dimension, or the unit will be tied with guy wires or braced to keep the unit
from tipping. (T-0)
16.5.3.2. The minimum platform work level width for mobile scaffolds (towers) will not
be less than 20 inches. Ladder stands will have a minimum step width of 16 inches. (T-0)
16.5.3.3. The supporting structure for the work level will be rigidly braced, using cross
bracing or diagonal bracing with rigid platforms at each work level. (T-0)
16.5.3.4. Ladder stand steps will have slip-resistant treads or a slip-resistant coating. (T-0)
16.5.3.5. The work level platform of scaffolds will be wood, aluminum, plywood
planking, steel or expanded metal, for the scaffold’s full width, except for necessary
openings. (T-0) Work platforms will be secured to the support structure. (T-0) Planking
will be 2-inch (nominal) scaffold grade minimum 1,500 fiber (f) (stress grade) lumber or
equivalent. (T-0)
16.5.3.6. Scaffold work platforms four feet or higher will have guardrails, mid-rails, and
toeboards IAW paragraph 16.4.6.10. (T-0)
16.5.3.7. Scaffolds will have a climbing ladder or stairway (for access and egress) affixed
or built into the scaffold. (T-0) The ladder or stairway will be located so it will not tip the
scaffold. (T-0) A landing platform will be provided at intervals not exceeding 30 feet. (T-0)
16.5.3.8. Self-propelled elevating work platforms and manually propelled mobile work
platforms will not be moved with personnel on the working platform unless allowed by
manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0) Locking pins or other devices, as required, must be in
place IAW equipment-specific TOs or manufacturer’s specifications before anyone is
allowed to ascend the stairs or ladder. (T-0) If allowed by manufacturer’s instructions the
following requirements need to be accomplished prior to any movement with personnel on
the working platform:
16.5.3.8.1. Guardrails, mid-rails, and toeboards will be installed on all exposed sides
IAW paragraph 16.4.6.10. (T-0)
16.5.3.8.2. The floor or surface will be within 3° (0.6288 inches of rise in 12 inches of
run) of level, smooth (the equivalent of broom-finished concrete) and free from pits,
holes, or obstructions. (T-0)
16.5.3.8.3. The minimum dimension of the scaffolding base when ready for rolling
will be at least one-half of the height. (T-0) Outriggers, if used, will be installed on all
four sides of the scaffold, and then can be included as part of the base dimension. (T-0)
Note: Measure at the widest width of the base.
16.5.3.8.4. All tools and materials are secured or removed from the platform before
the mobile scaffold is moved. (T-0)

170

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.5.3.8.5. Employees on the mobile scaffold will be advised by a spotter and be aware
of each movement in advance. (T-0)
16.5.3.8.6. Employees on the work platform of the mobile scaffold may move the
scaffold when the mobile scaffold is equipped with a manual system in which the
propelling force is applied to the wheels only and cannot exceed normal walking speed.
16.5.3.8.7. The force necessary to move the mobile scaffold will be applied as close to
the base as practicable and provision will be made to stabilize the tower during
movement from one location to another. (T-0)
16.5.3.9. Full-body harnesses will be worn with lanyard attached to the manufacturerinstalled structural anchor point when using self-propelled mobile work platforms, with
guardrails removed, and the platform elevated four feet or more above the surface. (T-0)
Self-propelled work platforms manufactured without an approved structural anchor point
will have protective guardrails, mid-rails and toeboards installed on all open sides IAW
paragraph 16.5.1 (T-0) When within 14 inches of the work surface, e.g., aircraft and
buildings, guardrails need not be in place on the side that is within 14 inches of a working
surface. Bolts, pins, or other locking devices will be provided and used on all removable
railings and attachments. (T-0)
16.5.3.10. Personal fall arrest equipment anchorages will be capable of supporting at least
5,000 pounds (22.2 kilonewton [kN]) per worker attached or will be designed, installed,
and used as part of a complete PFAS which maintains a safety factor of two, under the
supervision of a qualified person. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 13, Fall Protection, and 29 CFR
§ 1910.140, Personal Fall Protection Systems, for additional guidance.
16.5.3.11. Lanyards, D-rings, snap-hooks, and all fall arrest system components will
maintain a minimum breaking strength of 5,000-pounds (22.2 kilonewton [kN]) IAW 29
CFR § 1910.140. (T-0)
16.5.4. Operations.
16.5.4.1. Training requirements in paragraph 16.3 also apply to all manually propelled
elevating aerial platforms and self-propelled elevating work platforms.
16.5.4.2. Operators will know the location of operating and safety manuals, including the
manual defining dealer, owner, lessor, lessee, user, and operator responsibilities on the
aerial platform. (T-1) Operators will be familiar with and use these manuals when
questions arise.
16.5.4.3. Aerial platforms will be given a visual inspection and functional test before use
each day or at the beginning of each shift including, but not limited to, the following:
16.5.4.3.1. Operating and emergency controls. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.2. Safety devices. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.3. Personal protective devices, including fall protection. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.4. Air, hydraulic and fuel system leaks. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.5. Cables and wiring harness. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.6. Loose or missing parts. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

171

16.5.4.3.7. Tires and wheels. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.8. Placards, warnings, and control markings. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.9. Outriggers, stabilizers, and other structures.
16.5.4.3.10. Guardrail system, including bolts, pins or other locking devices on all
removable railings and attachments. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.11. Any special items required by the manufacturer. (T-0)
16.5.4.3.12. Inspection deficiencies will be documented IAW applicable TO or local
procedure for stands not covered by a TO. (T-1) A visible indicator, i.e., AF Form 981
or other means will be affixed to stands removed from service until repaired and
returned to service. (T-1)
16.5.4.4. Refer to paragraph 16.4.6.17 for workplace inspection information.
16.5.4.5. The operator will ensure the following before each elevation of the platform:
16.5.4.5.1. The aerial platform is operated on a surface within the manufacturer’s
limits. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.2. Outriggers, stabilizers, extendable axles, or other stability enhancing means
are used as required by the manufacturer. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.3. Guardrails are installed and access gates or openings are closed per
manufacturer’s instructions, including bolts, pins or other locking devices on all
removable railings and attachments. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.4. The load and its distribution on the platform and any platform extensions
do not exceed the manufacturer’s rated capacity for the specific configuration. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.5. There is adequate clearance from overhead obstructions. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.6. Minimum safe approach distances to energized power lines and parts are
maintained. (T-0)
16.5.4.5.7. All personnel on the platform will wear required fall protection devices and
other safety gear when guardrails are removed. (T-0)
16.5.4.6. Users will inspect and maintain aerial platforms to ensure proper operation. (T-0)
The frequency of inspection and maintenance will be based on the manufacturer’s
recommendations and compatible with operating conditions and severity of the operating
environment. (T-0) Aerial platforms not in safe operating condition will be immediately
removed from service until repaired. (T-0) A qualified person will make repairs IAW
manufacturer’s recommendations. (T-0) This periodic inspection will include those
requirements in paragraphs 16.5.4.3 and 16.5.4.5. (T-1)
16.5.4.7. Controls for manually elevated platforms will be clearly marked for direction
and function. (T-0) Controls for platform elevating and lowering mechanisms will be selfholding and readily accessible from the ground. (T-0) Controls at the platform are optional.
16.5.4.8. Upper controls of power elevated platforms will be:
16.5.4.8.1. Clearly marked as to direction and function. (T-0)

172

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.5.4.8.2. A type that automatically returns to the off or neutral position when
released. (T-0)
16.5.4.8.3. Protected against inadvertent operation. (T-0)
16.5.4.8.4. Readily accessible to the operator. (T-0)
16.5.4.8.5. Designed to include a control that must be continuously activated for upper
controls to function and that automatically returns to the off position when released.
(T-0)
16.5.4.9. Aerial platforms with a powered elevating assembly will have a clearly marked
emergency lowering means readily accessible from ground level. (T-0)
16.5.4.10. The aerial platform will have a readily identifiable emergency stop device at
upper control and lower control stations that will stop powered functions and platform
movement. (T-0)
16.5.4.11. The control system will be designed so a single control system malfunction will
not cause unintended platform movement. (T-0)
16.5.5. Wheels and Casters.
16.5.5.1. Wheel and casters will be sized to support four times the design working load.
(T-0)
16.5.5.2. Scaffold casters will have a positive wheel and/or swivel lock to prevent
movement. (T-1) Two of the four casters on ladder stands will swivel and be lockable.
(T-0)
16.5.5.3. Screw jacks or other means for adjusting height will be provided where leveling
of the elevated work platform is required. (T-0)
16.5.5.4. At least two brakes, wheel locks or permanently installed jacks will be operable
on all mobile work platforms. (T-0)
16.5.6. Mobile Work Platforms.
16.5.6.1. Design. Units will be designed IAW paragraphs 16.5.1, 16.5.2 and 16.5.4. (T-0)
16.5.6.2. Base Width. The minimum base width will conform to paragraph 16.5.3.1.
(T-0)
16.5.6.3. Bracing. Rigid diagonal bracing to vertical members will be provided. (T-0)
16.5.7. Mobile Ladder Stands.
16.5.7.1. Design. Units will comply with applicable requirements of paragraphs 16.5.1,
16.5.2 and 16.5.4. (T-0)
16.5.7.2. Base Width. Minimum base width will be IAW paragraph 16.5.3.1 (T-0)
Maximum base section length will be the total length of combined steps and top assembly,
measured horizontally, plus 5/8 inch per step of rise. (T-0)
16.5.7.3. Steps. Steps will be uniformly spaced, with a rise of 9 to 10 inches, and a depth
of at least 7 inches. (T-0) Slope of the steps section will be 55 to 60o, measured from the
horizontal. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.23, Ladders, for more information.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

173

16.5.7.4. Handrails. Units with at least 3 treads and 4 risers, or rising more than 30 inches,
will be equipped with a stair rail and handrails IAW 29 CFR § 1910.28 and 29 CFR §
1926.1052, Stairways. (T-0) Handrails will be at least 29 inches high and the same height
all along the step, measured vertically from the center of the step. (T-0)
16.5.7.5. Load design will meet requirements of paragraph 16.5.2.2.1. (T-0)
16.5.8. Mobile Work Platforms and Ladder Stands will:
16.5.8.1. Meet criteria in paragraphs 16.5.9 and 16.5.10 (T-0) If these mobile work
platforms have steps, they will meet mobile ladder stand criteria for step dimensions and
handrails. (T-0)
16.5.8.2. Be maintained free of grease, oil, ice, snow, mud, or any other slippery substance
or object which could cause slips and falls. (T-0) Step treads and walking surfaces will be
in good repair. (T-0) Repair worn surfaces with suitable materials or make safe using
abrasive or non-slip materials. (T-0)
16.5.8.3. Removable guardrails may be used on all open sides of aircraft maintenance
platforms. Guardrails removed for material handling, will be replaced as soon as material
handling is complete. (T-0) Protective guardrails are not required at the platform entrance
if there is a ramp or stairway (angle less than 60o) IAW paragraph 16.5.1. Protective
guardrails will only be removed from the side(s) of the platform positioned as close as
practical (less than 14 inches) and parallel to aircraft, building, or another working surface.
(T-0) Guardrails will be installed on all open sides if platform levels are four feet or higher
above ground or floor. (T-0) Bolts, pins or other locking devices will be provided and used
on all removable railings and attachments. (T-0) Only guardrails and toeboards meeting
criteria in paragraph 16.4.6.10 will be used. (T-0)
16.5.8.4. Ensure prior to any user ascending a raised mobile platform: all locking devices
(e.g., pins, collars) will be installed, hydraulic pressure will be released, the platform will
be slowly lowered until locking devices are securely engaged, and hydraulic pressure
valve(s) will be closed. (T-0) If the raisable portion of the platform cannot be elevated from
ground level, the user may ascend to that platform portion and adjust platform height.
However, requirements for locking devices, pressure release and closing valves will be met
prior to starting any task or work. (T-0)
16.5.8.4.1. Ascending/descending work platform. The climbing apparatus will be
considered a ladder if it does not meet step dimension criteria in paragraph 16.5.10.3
and/or the angle of the apparatus is greater than 60o from horizontal. (T-0) Personnel
ascending/descending ladders will face the ladder and maintain a firm hold on the
ladder. (T-0) Personnel ascending/descending stairs (less than or equal to 60o) will face
the direction of travel and use handrails as depicted in Figure 16.1. (T-0)

174

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

Figure 16.1. Ascending/Descending Work Platform.

Note: Angle less than or equal to 60o, personnel ascending/descending stairs will face the
direction of travel and use handrails. If the angle is greater than 60o, the climbing device will be
considered a ladder and accessed like a ladder.
16.5.8.4.2. Refer to Chapter 7, Walking – Working Surfaces, for additional guidance
on ladder safety.
16.5.8.5. Mark mobile work platforms with reflective materials to the maximum extent
authorized by appropriate TOs, such as TO 35-1-3, Corrosion Prevention and Control,
Cleaning, Painting, and Marking of USAF Support Equipment (SE). (T-0) When not being
transported, mobile work platforms will be secured to prevent collision with aircraft,
vehicles, or other equipment. (T-1) Designated flightline storage areas for mobile work
platforms will be determined by AGE flight commander and airfield manager and approved
by the Maintenance Group Commander. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 24, Aircraft Flightline –
Ground Operations and Activities, for further guidance.
16.5.8.6. Mobile work platforms will be operationally inspected IAW the manufacturer’s
instructions, equipment TO, and TO 00-20-1, Aerospace Equipment Maintenance
Inspection, Documentation, Policies, and Procedures. (T-0) Maintenance work stands will
have four (in any combination) of the following: operational brakes, wheel locks or
permanently installed jacks (e.g., two operational brakes and two permanently installed
jacks). (T-1) No stand will be used unless all brakes are set and all wheels’ casters locked.
(T-1) Wheel will be locked either 90 or 180o from the caster lock pin if it interferes with
the wheel brake handle. (T-1) Wheel chocks may be used in conjunction with these
requirements. Vehicle chocks may be constructed or locally manufactured IAW TO 00-25172, Ground Servicing of Aircraft and Static Grounding/Bonding. All wheels and casters
will be locked, and all brakes or jacks will be set on mobile work platforms when not in
use to avoid rolling down slanted surfaces or being moved by wind. (T-1) Stands with
defects will be tagged as unserviceable. (T-0) Note 1: Some maintenance work stands may
only have two operational brakes and no permanently installed jacks. Both brakes are
required to be set. Note 2: A daily inspection is not required for days the stand is not used.
16.5.8.7. When mobile work platforms are being moved:
16.5.8.7.1. Personnel and equipment may remain on self-propelled and manually
propelled work platforms when moved if manufacturer’s instructions allow such
activity and the requirements of paragraph 16.5.3.8 and subparagraphs are followed.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

175

16.5.8.7.2. Platforms to be towed will be equipped with hitches designed for such
purpose. (T-0)
16.5.8.7.3. The following rules apply when platforms are towed by vehicles:
16.5.8.7.3.1. Maximum towing speed for 1 maintenance stand is 10 miles per hour
(MPH) and 5 MPH for 2 or more maintenance stands. (T-1) However, when
surfaces and terrain make it necessary, speed will be reduced accordingly. (T-1)
16.5.8.7.3.2. No more than four type B1, B4, B5 and similar small stands may be
towed as follows: two sets of two in tandem on a double hitch or two in tandem on
a single hitch. Type B3, J7 and similar large stands will be towed singly on a center
mounted hitch. (T-1) Four-wheeled units will not be towed behind two-wheeled
units. (T-1) Large pieces of AGE, when towed in tandem, will not block the driver’s
vision of the last item being towed. (T-1)
16.5.8.7.3.3. Safety pins will be used to secure pintle hooks and trailer hitches.
(T-1)
16.5.8.8. Mobile work platforms used where static electricity is hazardous to the work
operation, such as fuel cell repair or refueling vehicle maintenance, will be grounded and
equipped with static discharge plates for personnel to dissipate accumulated static
electricity. (T-0)
16.5.8.9. Maintain mobile work platforms and ladder stands IAW appropriate 35-series
TOs. (T-1)
16.5.9. High Line Docks. High line docks are designed to be loaded by MHE to provide
greater flexibility in loading and unloading operations. Due to the design of a high line dock,
falls from them can create serious injury.
16.5.9.1. Inspection. Prior to use, high line docks will be inspected to ensure structural
soundness of the equipment, that all safety equipment is in place and functioning, e.g.,
guardrails, and all attached equipment is functioning per manufacturer documentation.
(T-1)
16.5.9.1.1. Pre-use inspections will be conducted to ensure serviceability. (T-1)
16.5.9.1.2. Quarterly, high line docks will be thoroughly inspected and documented on
the AFTO Form 95. (T-1)
16.5.9.1.3. High line docks found in unserviceable condition will be marked IAW
paragraph 17.5 and removed from use. (T-1) Supervisors will ensure all affected
personnel are made aware of unserviceable high line docks. (T-1)
16.5.9.2. Manufacturer instructions or training plans developed from instructions will be
used to train personnel on proper use, safeguards, and inspection procedures. (T-1)
16.6. Scaffolding. This section applies to all scaffolding used in the DAF, except rolling
scaffolds (mobile towers), which are covered in paragraph 16.5. With the many different brands,
categories, and varieties of scaffolds, 29 CFR § 1910.27 provides the most practical criteria
regarding the assembly, erection, storage, and safe use of scaffolding. Refer to paragraph 16.5
for information on manually propelled mobile ladder stands, mobile work platforms, or mobile
towers. Refer to Table 16.1 for a list of typical types of scaffolding.

176

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.6.1. General Requirements.
16.6.1.1. Scaffolds will be provided when work cannot be done safely from the ground or
from solid construction and will be erected, moved, dismantled, or altered only under the
supervision and direction of a competent person trained in these activities. (T-0) Such work
will only be performed by experienced, trained personnel under the direction of the
competent person. (T-0) When ladders are used, they will conform to requirements in 29
CFR § 1926.451, General Requirements – Scaffolds. (T-0)
16.6.1.2. The footing or anchorage for scaffolds will be designed to support the maximum
intended load without settling or displacement. (T-0) Unstable objects such as barrels,
boxes, loose bricks, or concrete blocks will not be used to support scaffolds or planks. (T-0)
16.6.2. Guarding requirements. All guarding for scaffolding will meet the requirements
specified in 29 CFR § 1910.27. (T-0)
16.6.3. Scaffolds and their components will be capable of supporting at least four times the
maximum intended load. (T-0)
16.6.4. Scaffolds will not be altered or moved horizontally while in use or occupied. (T-0)
16.6.5. Scaffolds, their accessories and other devices prescribed in this chapter will be
maintained in good condition. (T-0) Scaffold parts will be inspected by a competent person
prior to use. (T-0) If any deficiencies are found, the scaffold must not be used until repaired or
replaced. (T-0)
16.6.6. A ladder or equivalent safe access will be provided. (T-0)
16.6.7. Where swinging loads are being hoisted onto or near a scaffold, they will have a tag
line or equivalent measures to control the loads and prevent contact with the scaffold. (T-0)
16.6.8. Employees will wear appropriate head protection (e.g., hard hats) during erection,
while working on maintenance and teardown of scaffolding. (T-0) Protective gloves are also
recommended.
16.6.9. Clearance Between Scaffolds and Power Lines. Scaffolds will not be erected, used,
dismantled, altered, or moved such that they or any conductive material handled on them might
come close to exposed and energized power lines. (T-0) (Exception: Scaffolds and materials
may be closer to power lines, if the lines are de-energized, the lines are relocated, or a
protective covering is installed to prevent accidental contact with the lines.) Verification of deenergization, relocation or insulation installation must be done prior to beginning work on
erecting scaffolding. (T-0)
16.6.10. Adverse weather conditions such as ice, snow, rain, and wind can create a hazardous
working surface. A plan will be established addressing adverse weather conditions and
measures to be taken to protect employees performing work under adverse weather conditions.
(T-1) The following rules will apply to work under adverse weather.
16.6.10.1. Personnel will not work on scaffolds covered with ice or snow. (T-0) If the
work surface remains slippery after ice or snow removal, it will be sanded or otherwise
treated to prevent employees from slipping. (T-0)
16.6.10.2. Personnel will not work on scaffolds during storms or high winds. (T-0) Due to
the many types and uses of scaffolding, it is impractical to determine specific wind criteria

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

177

for work stoppage. The designated competent persons are responsible for assessing unsafe
environmental and climatic work conditions and will take necessary preventive measures
to protect employees. (T-1)
16.6.11. Tools, materials, and debris will not be allowed to accumulate in quantities that could
cause a hazard. (T-0) When possible, containers will be provided on scaffolds for storage of
loose materials. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure good housekeeping practices are followed to
prevent tripping hazards. (T-1)
16.6.12. Chemically active environments can destroy fiber rope. Rope materials such as
polyester, nylon and manila can be damaged when exposed to caustics and acids. When fiber
rope is used near corrosive substances or chemicals, the manufacturer will be consulted for
assistance in determining the proper type of rope to use. (T-0)
16.6.13. Wire or fiber rope used for scaffold suspension will support at least six times the
intended load. (T-0)
16.6.14. The use of shore scaffolds or lean-to scaffolds is prohibited.
16.6.15. Lumber sizes, when used in this publication, refer to nominal sizes except where
otherwise stated.
16.6.16. Scaffolds will be secured to permanent structures by anchor bolts, reveal bolts or
other equivalent means. (T-0) Window cleaners’ anchor bolts will not be used. (T-0)
16.6.17. Special care will be taken to protect scaffold members, including wire or fiber ropes,
when using heat-producing processes. (T-0)
16.6.18. A covered storage area is recommended to protect scaffolding from damage and
deterioration.
16.6.19. Wooden parts of scaffolding will not be painted as paint can hide defects. (T-0) Clear
varnish, lacquer, shellac, or linseed oil will be used when wood protection is needed. (T-0)
16.6.20. Individual employees will not attempt to manually lift or move scaffold members
which are excessively heavy, or because of their shape or size, cannot be safely handled. (T-0)
In these cases, assistance will be obtained from other employees or mechanical handling
equipment will be used. (T-1)
16.6.21. Hand tools used to assemble scaffolding will be in good condition. (T-0) When
powered hand tools are used, they will be electrically grounded or of double insulated
construction. (T-0)
16.7. Tubular Welded Frame Scaffolds.
16.7.1. Scaffolds will be designed to comply with 29 CFR § 1910.27. (T-0) The installation
CE office is the authorizing agency for scaffold erection. (T-0)
16.7.2. Scaffold vertical members will be properly braced by cross or diagonal braces. (T-0)
Braces will automatically square and align vertical members, so the erected scaffold is always
plumb, square and rigid. (T-0)
16.7.3. Spacing of panels or frames will be consistent with loads imposed. (T-0) Frames will
be placed one on top of the other with coupling or stacking pins to provide vertical alignment
of legs. (T-0)

178

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
16.7.4. Panels will be locked together vertically by pins or equivalent if uplift can occur. (T-0)
16.7.5. Scaffold over 50 feet in height will be erected IAW manufacturer’s instructions and
under the supervision of a qualified professional engineer. (T-0)

16.8. Tubular Welded Sectional Folding Scaffolds.
16.8.1. General. Scaffolds, including sectional stairway and sectional ladder scaffolds, will be
designed and erected IAW paragraph 16.5.6. (T-0)
16.8.2. Stairway. Each section of folding stairway scaffold will include a stairway, detachable
work platform and a sist of pivoting and hinged folding diagonal and horizontal braces. (T-0)
16.8.3. Sectional Folding Stairway Scaffolds. These are medium duty scaffolds (light duty
when equipped with a “high clearance” base section). Note: High clearance bases have a
greater open space so the scaffold may be placed over obstructions like machinery or
shrubbery. When high clearance bases are used to support sectional folding stairway scaffolds,
the entire scaffold’s load capacity will be reduced. (T-0) Sectional folding stairway scaffolds
will not exceed 4 ½ feet in width and 6 feet in length. (T-0)
16.8.4. Sectional Folding Ladder Scaffolds. Sectional folding ladder scaffolds are light duty
scaffolds, including special base (open end) sections designed for high clearance over
obstructions. For special applications, six-foot folding ladder scaffolds, except high clearance
base sections, will be designed as medium duty scaffolds. (T-0) A sectional folding ladder
scaffold will not exceed 6 feet 6 inches in width for a 6-foot-long unit, 8 feet 6 inches for an
8-foot-long unit, 10 feet 6 inches for a 10-foot-long unit. (T-0) End frames of sectional ladder
and stairway scaffolds will be designed so horizontal bearers provide support for multiple
planking levels. (T-0)
16.9. Tube and Coupler Scaffolds.
16.9.1. Scaffolds will be designed and erected IAW 29 CFR § 1910.27. (T-0)
16.9.2. Couplers will be made of structural type material, such as drop-forged steel, malleable
iron, or structural grade aluminum. (T-0) Use of gray cast iron is prohibited.

Chapter 17
MISHAP PREVENTION SAFETY SIGNS, TAGS AND LABELING

17.1. Scope. This chapter outlines DAF requirements for safety, health, fire prevention signs and
tags, piping systems and compressed gas cylinders safety color coding, labeling, and marking.
Table 17.1 lists the regulatory guidance that governs safety signs, tags, labeling and markings.
These requirements apply to all work environments, except for the following:
17.1.1. Signs or tags unique to aircraft, motor vehicles, and railroad or marine operations.
17.1.2. Piping system sections buried underground, in concrete or part of vehicles or field
mobility equipment/systems, etc.

29 CFR § 1910.145, Specifications for Accident Prevention Signs and Tags
29 CFR § 1910.95, Occupational Noise Exposure

17.2. Hazards and General Information.
17.2.1. Work Hazards. Preventive signs, tags, color coding, labeling or markings will be used
wherever a hazard is present in the workplace. (T-0) The following are examples of workplace
hazards (This is not an all-inclusive list):

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

181

17.2.1.1. Physical hazards, e.g., electricity, radiation, high exposures to
sunlight/ultraviolet rays, temperature extremes, noise, flying or falling particles, sharp
edges.
17.2.1.2. Chemical and dust hazards, e.g., cleaning products, paints, acids, solvents,
welding fumes, gases, flammable materials, pesticides, respirable crystalline silica.
17.2.1.3. Biological hazards, e.g., blood/other body fluids, fungus (mold), bacteria,
viruses, plants, insect bites, animal/bird droppings.
17.2.2. Signs, tags, color coding, labeling and markings are an effective means for
communicating hazard information to employees to prevent injuries and illnesses.
17.2.2.1. Signs and tags are not substitutes for abatement action using the hierarchy of
controls, such as elimination, substitution, engineering controls and administrative
controls.
17.2.2.2. Wording on signs and tags will be concise, accurate, easy to read, contain
sufficient information to be easily understood. (T-0)
17.2.2.3. Interior and other installation specific signs will be designed IAW UFC 3-12001, where applicable. (T-1) Where OSHA has specific requirements, they will be followed
in addition to applicable federal, state, or municipal regulations. (T-0)
17.2.3. Availability of Safety Signs and Tags. The functional manager or supervisor will
ensure an adequate supply of safety signs and tags is available to employees. (T-1)
17.2.4. Other signs or tags prescribed by DAF directives for site-specific hazards will be
brought to the attention of HQ AFSEC/SEG, e.g., signs or tags that may be included in this
instruction or clarification of usage. (T-1)
17.2.5. Identification of Hazards.
17.2.5.1. Employees will immediately advise supervisors of unsafe conditions. (T-0)
17.2.5.2. Supervisors will evaluate the situation, ensure an appropriate preventive sign,
tag, color coding, labeling and/or marking is attached/applied. (T-0)
17.2.5.2.1. The supervisor will coordinate placement of tags and assignment of RACs
with the Occupational Safety office, F&ES Flight, and installation BE. (T-1)
17.2.5.2.2. The supervisor, or person in charge at the time, is responsible for issuing
tags. If they have questions, contact the Occupational Safety office, F&ES Flight, or
installation BE. (T-1)
17.3. Training.
17.3.1. All exposed employees will be instructed in the classification and use of safety, health
and fire prevention signs and tags, to include, piping systems safety color coding, labeling and
markings IAW 29 CFR § 1910.144 and 1910.145. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure training is
accomplished and that employees are knowledgeable of the requirements. (T-0)
17.3.2. Retraining. Employees will be retrained when:
17.3.2.1. Changes in their job assignments occur, such as a change in machinery,
equipment or processes that present a new hazard. (T-0)

182

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
17.3.2.2. Changes in the workplace may result in a new hazardous condition. (T-0)
17.3.2.3. Inadequacies in an employee’s knowledge indicate that the employee has not
retained the requisite understanding of the purpose of safety signs, tags, and labels. (T-0)

17.4. Sign Requirements.
17.4.1. Signs will be uniform and properly worded and located. (T-0) The meaning will be
immediately and clearly understood as an automatic warning, caution or notice to all personnel
no matter where they work IAW 29 CFR § 1910.145(c) through (e). (T-0)
17.4.1.1. To prevent confusion, avoid grouping too many signs together in the same
location.
17.4.1.2. Signs will not be placed on moveable objects or adjacent to moveable objects
like wheeled equipment, removable partitions, doors, etc., which, if moved, will obscure
the sign.
17.4.1.3. Signs will have adequate illumination. (T-0) Where illumination is inadequate
under emergency conditions, signs will be equipped with means of illumination, such as
battery operated power source, be luminescent or both. Consult installation CE for lighting
guidance concerning specific locations.
17.4.1.4. Multi-lingual Signs. Use multi-lingual signs in the languages understood by
employees to ensure all employees understand warnings. (T-0) If additional languages are
needed on safety signs, it is strongly encouraged to use symbols, in addition to words, to
better communicate hazard information across language barriers.
