DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

233
Chapter 23

CONFINED SPACES
23.1. Scope. This chapter establishes DAF-specific confined space program requirements based
on OSHA required program elements found in 29 CFR § 1910.146, Permit-Required Confined
Spaces, and 29 CFR § 1926, Subpart AA, Confined Spaces in Construction, as required. Use of
the standards will depend on the industry the work falls under. Units with personnel entering
permit-required confined spaces will comply with 29 CFR § 1910.146, 29 CFR § 1926, Subpart
AA, this chapter, and other applicable standards. (T-0) Industries excepted under 29 CFR §
1910.146(a) and 29 CFR § 1926.1201(b) will use the cited standard as it applies. (T-0) Using the
Construction standard versus 29 CFR § 1910.146 ensures consistent compliance where
construction and general industry activities happen concurrently or intermittently during confined
space entries. OSHA has recognized this as an acceptable practice and considers compliance with
29 CFR § 1926, Subpart AA as compliance with 29 CFR § 1910.146 during these situations.
23.2. Definitions.
23.2.1. Confined space is defined as a space that:
23.2.1.1. Is large enough and so configured that a worker can bodily enter and perform
assigned work.
23.2.1.2. Has limited or restricted means for entry or exit.
23.2.1.3. Is not designed for continuous occupancy. Note 1: Examples of confined spaces
include, but are not limited to, aircraft fuel cells, utility holes, silos, tanks, pits, manholes,
vaults, storage bins, etc. Note 2: All three conditions in paragraphs 23.2.1.1 – 23.2.1.3
must be present to constitute a confined space. (T-0)
23.2.2. There are two classifications of confined spaces:
23.2.2.1. Permit-required confined space is defined as a space that has one or more of the
following characteristics:
23.2.2.1.1. Contains or has the potential to contain a hazardous atmosphere.
23.2.2.1.2. Contains a material that has the potential for engulfing an entrant.
23.2.2.1.3. Has an internal configuration such that an entrant could be trapped or
asphyxiated by inwardly converging walls or by a floor which slopes downward and
tapers to a smaller cross-section.
23.2.2.1.4. Contains any other recognized serious safety or health hazard.
23.2.2.2. Non-permit confined spaces are defined as spaces that do not contain, or with
respect to atmospheric hazards, do not have the potential to contain any hazard capable of
causing death or serious physical harm.
23.2.3. Serious physical harm is defined as an impairment or illness in which a body part is
made functionally useless or is substantially reduced in efficiency. Such impairment or illness
may be permanent or temporary and includes, but is not limited to, loss of consciousness,
disorientation, or other immediate and substantial reduction in mental efficiency. Injuries
involving such impairment would usually require treatment by a physician or other licensed

234

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
healthcare professional. Such physical harm would impair an entrant’s ability to conduct selfrescue.

23.3. Hazards. Personnel entering or working in confined spaces may encounter any number of
serious hazards that can affect their ability to safely work in or evacuate from a confined space.
Refer to Table 23.1 for common atmospheric and serious physical hazards found in permitrequired confined spaces.
Table 23.1. Common Hazards Involving Confined Spaces (Not all-inclusive).
Category
Atmospheric

Serious Physical

Confined Space Hazard
• Oxygen Deficiency (<19.5%)
• Displacement of air by another gas
• Various biological processes or chemical reactions, such as rotting of
organic matter, rusting of metals, burning, etc.
• Oxygen Enrichment (>23.5%)
• An excess of oxygen, in the presence of combustible materials, results in
an increased risk of fire and explosion
• Some materials, which do not burn in air, may burn vigorously or even
spontaneously in an enriched oxygen atmosphere
• Flammable or Explosive Atmospheres
• A flammable atmosphere presents a risk of fire or explosion
• Such an atmosphere can arise from the presence in the confined space of
flammable liquids or gases or of a suspension of combustible dust in the
air
• If a flammable atmosphere inside a confined space ignites, an explosion
may occur, resulting in the expulsion of hot gases and the disintegration
of the structure
• Toxicity – An atmospheric concentration of any chemical substance (e.g.,
hydrogen sulfide (H2S), carbon monoxide (CO), welding fumes) which is
capable of causing death, incapacitation, impairment of ability to self-rescue,
injury or acute illness due to its health effects
• Limited or restricted means for entry or exit
• Cramped working conditions
• Temperature extremes
• Rotating or moving equipment
• Reactive or corrosive residues
• Entrapment hazards
• Electrical hazards
• Wildlife
• Poor light levels
• Engulfment

23.4. Responsibilities.
23.4.1. Major Commands (MAJCOMs), Field Commands (FLDCOMs), Direct Reporting
Units (DRUs) and Field Operating Agencies (FOAs) with safety staff will:
23.4.1.1. Ensure program oversight through various means that includes evaluations, Staff
Assistance Visits, and assistance with resolution of identified deficiencies. (T-1)
23.4.1.2. Formally review Confined Space Programs as part of Safety Program
Evaluations. (T-1) The review will include the number and type of confined spaces found

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

235

on the Master Installation Confined Space Listing, the installation’s best practices and/or
major challenges, cancelled entry permits and approved Master Entry Plans. (T-1)
23.4.1.3. Standardize Master Entry Plan documentation to fit command needs. (T-1)
23.4.1.4. Approve subordinate occupational safety office developed train-the-trainer
materials. (T-1)
23.4.1.5. The MAJCOM/FLDCOM Identified Aircraft Confined Spaces list will be
reviewed annually and updated by MAJCOM/FLDCOM offices when changes occur.
(T-1)
23.4.2. The occupational safety office will:
23.4.2.1. Manage the installation confined space program and lead the Confined Space
Program Team (CSPT). (T-1)
23.4.2.2. Identify a safety representative to the CSPT as assigned by the occupational
safety manager (OSM). (T-1)
23.4.2.2.1. Be competent in confined space program requirements and be trained
according to paragraph 23.10. (T-1) Training and appointment will be in writing, to
show the OSM certifies the representative(s) is/are competent in the confined space
program requirements. (T-1)
23.4.2.2.2. Maintain consolidated confined space inventories provided by each
organization for all confined spaces, whether located on or off the installation, e.g.,
geographically separated units. (T-1) The inventory, at a minimum, will include
organization, classification, data supporting the classification, location of all identified
confined spaces and updates provided to them by each organization. (T-1) A map of
the confined space may be included to support the inventory list but is not mandatory.
23.4.3. Tenant units with assigned safety staff will appoint a representative to CSPT to serve
as an agent for confined spaces program responsibilities within their organization and codified
in the applicable host-tenant support agreement. (T-1) The tenant safety representative will
provide documentation to the OSM, when appointed to represent the tenant unit on the CSPT,
to show competency in the confined space program. (T-1)
23.4.4. The Fire Chief will identify, in writing, to the OSM the appointed F&ES Flight
representative(s) to the CSPT and certify the representative(s) is/are competent in confined
space program requirements. (T-1)
23.4.5. Installation BE will identify, in writing, to the OSM the appointed BE representative(s)
to the CSPT and certify the representative(s) is/are competent in confined space program
requirements. (T-1)
23.4.6. Commanders and/or Functional Managers will:
23.4.6.1. Identify, in writing, to the OSM their designated representative(s) to the CSPT
and certify the representative(s) is/are competent in confined space program requirements
and are trained according to paragraph 23.10. (T-1)
23.4.6.2. Ensure workplaces under their control are evaluated to identify confined spaces
that employees work near or around. (T-0) Identified spaces will be placed in an inventory

236

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
that includes: the number of spaces (quantity), type/number of access points (with every
entry point uniquely identified and listed), type of space (i.e., utility hole, underground
vault, tank), and exact location (e.g., grid coordinates, Global Positioning Satellite
coordinates, if available, highlighted maps, if necessary). (T-1) Provide the inventory to
the CSPT Safety representative to coordinate evaluation for classification of spaces as nonpermit or permit-required confined spaces. (T-1) Newly identified confined spaces or
changes to any spaces on the inventory will be immediately reported to the CSPT to ensure
proper testing, evaluation, and classification of each confined space within the
organization. (T-1)
23.4.6.3. Ensure required equipment needed to conduct safe entry into a permit-required
confined space is purchased and properly maintained. (T-0)
23.4.6.4. Require that all workplaces that contains permit spaces, the supervisor will
inform exposed employees, by posting danger signs or by any other equally effective
means, of the existence and location of and the danger posed by the permit spaces. (T-0)
23.4.6.5. Ensure a written confined space program is developed and approved by the CSPT
to ensure the program meets requirements found in 29 CFR § 1910.146 and this chapter.
(T-0) Master Entry Plan(s) will be annually reviewed by the CSPT for approval. (T-1)
Note: The requesting unit, not the CSPT, will lead the development of the unit’s Confined
Space Program and Master Entry Plan(s). (T-1)
23.4.6.6. Ensure all personnel assigned duties and responsibilities that support confined
space program tasks are properly trained according to paragraph 23.10 (T-0) All
equipment and training will be approved by the CSPT prior to purchase or implementation.
(T-1)
23.4.7. Commander’s designated representative(s) will:
23.4.7.1. Coordinate between each shop supervisor, who has employees who work in
permit-required confined spaces, and the commander on elements of the program needed
to maintain compliance. (T-1)
23.4.7.1.1. Obtain CSPT approval when selecting and purchasing equipment for
confined space entries. (T-1)
23.4.7.1.2. Identify confined spaces program shortfalls, e.g., equipment, manning,
funding, and ensure they are up channeled to the commander and the CSPT. (T-1)
23.4.7.2. Develop the inventory of organizationally controlled permit-required and nonpermit confined spaces with all information required in paragraph 23.4.6.2 (T-1) Submit
the completed confined space inventory to the commander for review prior to submission
to the occupational safety office. (T-1) Initially, annually, and as changes occur, review the
confined spaces inventory, and immediately incorporate any newly identified confined
spaces. (T-1) Where changes to the classification of existing permit and non-permit
confined spaces on the inventory are discovered during (re)evaluation and (re)classification
by the CSPT, update the inventory with the status change and submit to the commander for
review and to the occupational safety office. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

237

23.4.7.3. Be knowledgeable of the general hazards, conditions, and layouts of each
confined space under their program. (T-1) The representative(s) will have experience with
the confined spaces (or similar spaces) they manage. (T-1)
23.4.7.4. Designate the appropriate number of entry supervisors needed to cover all
permit-required confined space entries. (T-1)
23.4.7.5. Ensure personnel entering non-permit confined spaces receive the same initial
confined space training as personnel entering permit-required confined spaces found in
paragraph 23.10.1.1 to confirm employees working in non-permit confined spaces are
aware that tasks conducted in the space can change the nature of the space from non-permit
confined space to a permit-required confined space entry and ensure the protections found
in permit-required confined space entries are followed. (T-1)
23.4.7.6. Assist each shop supervisor with the development of a written permit-required
confined space program, that includes a structured and effective training program that
establishes safe work practices and techniques specific to the confined spaces they enter.
(T-0) Ensure all developed training meets the requirements found in paragraph 23.10
(T-1) Training will be approved by the CSPT prior to implementation. (T-1)
23.4.7.7. Notify the occupational safety member of the CSPT when the organization plans
to contract work in confined spaces. (T-1) It is the organization’s responsibility to provide
required information known about permit-required confined spaces under their
organizational control to contractors working in permit-required confined spaces. (T-1)
Notification to the occupational safety office ensures the CSPT members can be made
aware of all permit-required confined space entries and notify affected organizations of any
cross-tell necessary to ensure all employees are aware of any generated hazards due to
activities by other organizations. (T-0)
23.4.7.8. Be a CSPT member. (T-1)
23.4.7.9. Be trained according to paragraph 23.10. (T-1)
23.4.8. Entry supervisor will:
23.4.8.1. Be knowledgeable of all their confined space duties as identified in 29 CFR §
1910.146(j). (T-0)
23.4.8.2. Issue entry permits consistent with the organizational written confined space
program or the Master Entry Plan, when applicable. (T-0) Entry permits (DAF Form 1024)
will be issued for each permit-required confined space entered and not combined, to
include similar permit-required confined spaces. (T-0)
23.4.8.3. Entry permits will not be issued using a Master Entry Plan when unexpected
conditions exist in the permit space that were not previously anticipated or allowed for in
the entry plan. (T-1) If hazardous conditions develop after entry, the entry will be
terminated, the permit revoked and retained for one year. The Occupational Safety office
will contact the CSPT members to meet and re-evaluate the space and assist in the issuance
of a new entry permit (DAF Form 1024, Confined Space Entry Permit). (T-0)
23.4.8.4. Cancel the entry permit after becoming aware of a prohibited or unexpected
condition. (T-0) Use of suspending an entry permit under 29 CFR § 1926.1205(e)(2) will
not be allowed in DAF confined space entry operation. (T-0)

238

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
23.4.8.5. Ensure employees are aware that work on energized electrical equipment is
prohibited unless approved by the Installation CE IAW AFMAN 32-1065. (T-1)
23.4.8.6. With assistance from Occupational Safety, installation BE, or the F&ES Flight,
as appropriate, determine and evaluate newly identified hazardous condition source(s)
found at the time of entry, e.g., residue from the confined space, leaking valve or pipe in
the confined space, etc. (T-0)
23.4.8.7. Ensure all members of the rescue team are trained and current in CPR for permitrequired confined space entry operations. (T-1)
23.4.8.8. Ensure DAF employees enter a permit-required confined space only after a DAF
Form 1024 or other entry permit, approved by the CSPT that meets the minimum
documentation requirements, has been completed and signed. (T-0)
23.4.8.9. Maintain the entry permit and a copy of the written confined space program and
Master Entry Plan, when applicable, at the permit space entry to provide personnel
fulfilling confined space entry duties with a reference to required procedures applicable to
the confined space entered. (T-0)
23.4.8.10. Request assistance from CSPT, as needed, to ensure all program requirements
are met. (T-1)
23.4.8.11. Be trained according to paragraph 23.10. (T-1)
23.4.8.12. The entry supervisor may use 29 CFR § 1910.146(c)(7) to reclassify a permitrequired confined space as a non-permit confined space. The written program and/or master
entry plan (MEP) will specifically identify the permit-required confined spaces that will be
reclassified and the procedures to be used to make reclassification for CSPT review and
approval. (T-1) All identified hazards in the space must be eliminated to use this temporary
reclassification. (T-0) Control of a hazardous atmosphere, e.g., through forced ventilation,
is not the same as elimination. For example, a chemical tank that contains a hazardous
atmosphere, prior to draining it of its contents, purging any residual chemical content with
water and venting the space after purging is complete, would be eligible for reclassification
if the hazardous atmosphere remains eliminated without continuous ventilation and no
other hazards are present. Entry into confined spaces, where the only hazard is an actual or
potential hazardous atmosphere that can be maintained through continuous forced
ventilation may follow the alternate procedures identified in 29 CFR § 1910.146(c)(5).
Reclassifying a permit-required confined space also applies to those spaces containing
physical hazards, e.g., hazardous energy sources or engulfment hazards. Such
reclassification would allow entry without a permit provided.
23.4.8.12.1. Testing is accomplished prior to entry with the results showing the space
is free of all hazards. (T-0) Note: If entry is required to eliminate the hazards or perform
verification testing in the permit space, the entry must be made with an entry permit
IAW OSHA and this standard. (T-0) Once the hazards have been eliminated, the space
may be reclassified as non-permit confined space as long as the hazards remain
eliminated. (T-0) Elimination is achieved by completely removing all hazards from the
space.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

239

23.4.8.12.2. The actual or potential atmospheric hazards are eliminated, and
continuous monitoring is used to ensure the atmosphere remains free of hazards. (T-0)
That all hazards within the space are eliminated without entering the space at the time
of testing, and any non-atmospheric hazards remain eliminated. (T-0)
23.4.8.12.3. During routine work, the entrant does not take tools, introduce materials,
or perform work in the space that would create a new hazard. (T-0)
23.4.8.12.4. The entry permit is revoked whenever any test, monitoring instrument or
observation shows conditions are developing in the permit-required confined space not
allowed under the permit. (T-0) When this occurs, the entry supervisor will remove
entrants, secure the area, and prevent entry until a revised and approved entry permit is
issued. (T-0)
23.4.8.12.5. The entry supervisor documents the basis for the reclassification on a
separate sheet of paper, attaches it to the entry permit and signs or initials next to the
statement. (T-0) Note: Routine or repetitive entries for daily inspections of lateral fuel
pits are examples of work tasks that may qualify for reclassification.
23.4.9. Confined space attendants will perform the following duties and be trained according
to paragraph 23.10.:
23.4.9.1. Be familiar with and understand the hazards that may be faced during entry,
including information on the mode, signs or symptoms, and consequences of the exposure.
(T-0)
23.4.9.2. Be aware of possible behavioral effects of hazard exposure in authorized
entrants. (T-0)
23.4.9.3. Continuously maintain an accurate count of authorized entrants in the permit
space and ensure a positive means is used to accurately identify authorized entrants who
are in the permit confined space. (T-0)
23.4.9.4. Remain outside the permit space during entry operations until relieved by another
attendant. (T-0) Note: Once an attendant has been relieved by another attendant, the
relieved attendant may enter a permit space to attempt a rescue when the employer's permit
space program allows attendant entry for rescue and the attendant has been trained and
equipped for rescue operations.
23.4.9.5. Communicate with authorized entrants as necessary to assess entrant status and
to alert entrants of the need to evacuate the space. (T-0)
23.4.9.6. Assess activities and conditions inside and outside the space to determine if it is
safe for entrants to remain in the space and order the authorized entrants to evacuate the
permit space immediately under any of the following conditions:
23.4.9.6.1. If there is a prohibited condition. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.2. If the behavioral effects of hazard exposure are apparent in an authorized
entrant. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.3. If there is a situation outside the space that could endanger the authorized
entrants. (T-0)

240

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
23.4.9.6.4. If the attendant cannot effectively and safely perform all the duties required
under this section. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.5. Summons rescue and other emergency services as soon as the attendant
determines that authorized entrants may need assistance to escape from permit space
hazards. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.6. Take the following actions when unauthorized persons approach or enter a
permit space while entry is underway:
23.4.9.6.6.1. Warn the unauthorized persons that they must stay away from the
permit space. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.6.2. Advise the unauthorized persons that they must exit immediately if
they have entered the permit space. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.6.3. Inform the authorized entrants and the entry supervisor if
unauthorized persons have entered the permit space. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.7. Perform non-entry rescues as specified by the organizational rescue
procedure. (T-0)
23.4.9.6.8. Perform no duties that might interfere with the attendant’s primary duty to
assess and protect the authorized entrants. (T-0)
23.4.10. Confined space entrants will perform the following duties and be trained according
to paragraph 23.10.:
23.4.10.1. Be familiar with and understand the hazards that may be faced during entry,
including information on the mode, signs or symptoms, and consequences of the exposure.
(T-0)
23.4.10.2. Properly use equipment provided to safely work in the permit required confined
space. (T-0)
23.4.10.3. Communicate with the attendant as necessary to enable the attendant to assess
entrant status and to enable the attendant to alert entrants of the need to evacuate the space.
(T-0)
23.4.10.4. Alert the attendant whenever:
23.4.10.4.1. There is any warning sign or symptom of exposure to a dangerous
situation. (T-0)
23.4.10.4.2. The entrant detects a prohibited condition. (T-0)
23.4.10.4.3. An exit from the permit space as quickly as possible is warranted via an
order to evacuate is given by the attendant or the entry supervisor, there is any warning
sign or symptom of exposure to a dangerous situation, the entrant detects a prohibited
condition, or an evacuation alarm is activated. (T-0)
23.4.11. Rescue teams will perform duties that can accomplish the following:
23.4.11.1. Reach the victim(s) within a time frame that is appropriate for the permit space
hazard(s) identified. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

241

23.4.11.2. Be equipped for, trained and proficient in performing the needed rescue
services. (T-0)
23.4.11.3. Be informed of the hazards they may confront when called on to perform rescue
at the site. (T-0)
23.4.11.4. Have appropriate access to all permit spaces from which rescue may be
necessary so that the rescue service can develop appropriate rescue plans and practice
rescue operations. (T-0)
23.4.11.5. Practice making permit space rescues at least once every 12 months, by means
of simulated rescue operations in which they remove dummies, manikins, or actual persons
from the actual permit spaces or from representative permit spaces. (T-0) Representative
permit spaces will, with respect to opening size, configuration, and accessibility, simulate
the types of permit spaces from which rescue is to be performed. (T-0)
23.4.12. The Confined Space Program Team (CSPT) will:
23.4.12.1. Assist commanders and/or functional managers in the development and
administration of their organizational confined space program. (T-1) With the participation
of the functional managers, commanders, or their designated representative, assist in the
identification, evaluation, and classification of all confined spaces.
23.4.12.2. When requested, assist the functional manager in identifying ways of obtaining
training for entrants, entry supervisors, attendants, and rescue teams to include basic first
aid and CPR. (T-1) When notified of new confined spaces or changes to existing confined
spaces, the CSPT will test and evaluate identified confined spaces to classify them. (T-0)
Note: Prior to any confined space entries, further testing will be accomplished to identify
all hazards and develop effective controls prior to the start of permit-required confined
space entry operations. (T-0)
23.4.12.3. Validate that organizations who maintain confined spaces have ensured all
exposed personnel are informed of the permit-required confined space hazards. (T-0)
23.4.12.4. Assist the organization in the development of the written confined space
program to ensure the program meets OSHA requirements found in 29 CFR § 1910. 146,
29 CFR § 1926 subpart AA and this chapter. (T-0)
23.4.12.5. Evaluate all equipment for use by entrants to ensure effectiveness against
identified hazards. (T-0)
23.4.12.6. Assist in identification and selection of required equipment, to include PPE and
full-face piece demand self-contained breathing apparatus-certified by National Institute
for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) for a minimum service life of 30 minutes or
a combination full-face piece pressure supplied-air respirator with auxiliary self-contained
air supply, for rescue teams. (T-1)
23.4.12.7. Ensure unit trainers, with assistance from installation BE, certify organizational
personnel, as required, to conduct atmospheric testing on permit-required confined spaces.
(T-1)
23.4.12.8. Ensure installation BE representative assist entry supervisor in the
interpretation of air monitoring results when requested. (T-1)

242

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
23.4.12.9. Ensure installation BE representative serves as the OPR for the respiratory
program IAW AFI 48-137 and is the sole authority for selecting appropriate respiratory
equipment and enrolling all personnel required to wear any type of respirator, to include
rescue teams, who may enter confined spaces into the respiratory protection program. (T-1)
This includes PPE and full-face piece demand self-contained breathing apparatus-certified
by NIOSH for a minimum service life of 30 minutes or a combination full-face piece
pressure supplied-air respirator with auxiliary self-contained air supply, for rescue teams.
23.4.12.10. Ensure installation BE provide appropriate atmospheric testing and
monitoring in isolated cases where trained unit personnel and/or equipment are not
available. (T-1)
23.4.12.11. Ensure installation BE provide appropriate atmospheric testing and
monitoring for IDLH conditions during permit-required confined space operations. (T-1)
23.4.12.12. Ensure installation BE evaluate potential employee exposure related to
confined spaces IAW DAFI 48-145, Occupational and Environmental Health. (T-1)
23.4.12.13. When requested by the unit, assist in the development of the MEP. (T-1)
23.4.12.14. Evaluate and approve MEPs that meet requirements in paragraph 23.7. (T-1)
23.4.12.15. Review and approve all entry permits not issued from a Master Entry Plan.
(T-1)
23.4.12.16. Establish procedures with the appropriate agencies, e.g., contracting office,
United States Army Corp of Engineers, to review all construction projects to identify,
evaluate, and classify new confined spaces, or changes to current confined spaces. (T-1)
23.4.12.17. Allow the occupational safety office to directly coordinate with the contractor
to fulfill requirements in 29 CFR § 1926.1203(h), Permit Space Entry Communication and
Coordination, for government-owned, contractor-operated confined spaces, where no DAF
personnel enter. (T-0)
23.4.12.18. Commanders, through the CSPT, will evaluate prospective rescue teams to
ensure they meet the requirements found in 29 CFR § 1910.146, Appendix F can be used
to help evaluate rescue teams under consideration. (T-0)
23.4.12.19. Review and assess unit and rescue team training program at least annually.
(T-1) The review/assessment will include lesson plans, hands-on training methodology,
exercises, and documentation of training completion. (T-1) The completed annual
assessment will be provided to the assessed unit. (T-1) Incorporate findings from annual
reviews into other reports, such as annual safety reports. (T-1)
23.4.12.20. Where the F&ES Flight has a primary and secondary rescue team, the F&ES
Flight representative will verify the currency of the rescue team members. (T-1)
23.4.12.21. Convene after a confined space mishap investigation to review actions taken
during the entry, evaluate documentation, and re-examine training to determine program
shortfalls, implement improvements, and to collect any quantifiable data for the mishap
investigation, as requested. (T-1)
23.4.12.22. Meet annually to conduct a review of the Confined Space Program. (T-1)
Include all approved written confined space programs, Master Entry Plans, training plans,

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

243

rescue team evaluations and training, changes to OSHA standards, DAF or
MAJCOM/FLDCOM confined spaces guidance, issues discovered during surveillance,
cancelled permits, and any fire, safety, or health inspection findings over the past year.
(T-1)
23.4.12.23. Develop a status report from the results of the annual review to include, as a
minimum, the number and type of confined spaces, the installation’s major
issues/challenges during the past 12-month period, the number of personnel trained on
confined space operations, the number of entry permits issued, and Master Entry Plans
approved. (T-1) Ensure status report also identifies unit confined space program shortfalls,
e.g., equipment, manning, funding, etc., and is then provided to the commander and
appropriate MAJCOM-FLDCOM/SEG for review and/or action. (T-1)
23.5. Written Confined Space Program.
23.5.1. This program will include All required elements outlined in 29 CFR § 1910.146 and
29 CFR § 1926, Subpart AA, as required. (T-0)
23.5.1.1. Testing and classification of confined spaces will be done by the CSPT. (T-1)
23.5.1.2. Only explosion-proof or intrinsically safe equipment may be used where
flammable or explosive atmospheres are present. (T-0) Additionally, NFPA 70, Article
504, Intrinsically Safe Systems, and Article 501, Class I Locations, provide additional
guidance. (T-1)
23.5.1.3. Atmospheric monitoring will be continuously conducted for atmospheric hazards
in permit spaces unless the organization can demonstrate that continuous monitoring for
the identified atmospheric hazard is not commercially available. (T-0) Final determination
will be documented in the written permit confined space program. (T-0) Where continuous
monitoring is proven to be unavailable, procedures for periodic evaluation will be
developed by the supervisor and approved by the CSPT. (T-0) If isolation of a permit space
is not possible because the space is large or is part of a continuous system (e.g., a sewer),
pre-entry testing must be performed to the extent feasible and entry conditions must be
continuously monitored in areas where authorized entrants are working. (T-0) Note: For
aircraft fuel cell/tank confined space monitoring, follow requirements in TO 1-1-3,
Inspection and Repair of Aircraft Integral Tanks and Fuel Cells.
23.5.1.4. Monitoring equipment used to evaluate confined spaces will be calibrated by the
Testing, Measurement, Diagnostic and Evaluation (TMDE) laboratory at an interval
established by manufacturer’s instructions or applicable technical orders. (T-1)
23.5.1.4.1. Some monitoring equipment, e.g., colorimetric tubes, do not require
calibration. Equipment that comes with manufacturer-approved calibration devices and
does not require TMDE calibration may use manufacturer approved methods by
qualified personnel.
23.5.1.4.2. Monitoring equipment that requires calibration, but cannot be calibrated by
TMDE, will be sent to the manufacturer for calibration. (T-1)
23.5.1.5. The user will be required to field check and span gas test equipment IAW the
manufacturer’s instructions immediately before testing the confined space. (T-0)
Employees will not use equipment that cannot be calibrated, or which fails the field check

244

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
or span gas test, until it is repaired and the calibration and/or field check is successfully
accomplished. (T-0)
23.5.2. When employees perform hot riveting, welding, cutting, or burning, or heating
operations within a confined space, they will obtain a DAF Form 592 from the F&ES Flight.
(T-0) Refer to Chapter 20 for additional information. If chemicals are introduced into the
confined space by the hot work, installation BE will be contacted to evaluate the potential
health hazards and recommend ventilation procedures if not previously assessed during the
unit’s OH Assessment. (T-1) Employees will inspect, test, operate and maintain welding and
cutting equipment such as hoses, connections, torches, etc., IAW provisions of Chapter 20,
applicable TOs and manufacturer’ instructions. (T-0)
23.5.3. Entry into telecommunications only controlled spaces is governed by 29 CFR §
1910.268, Telecommunications, and Chapter 28. Note: The requirements of this chapter can
also apply to telecommunications confined spaces. Contact the CSPT for assistance.
23.5.4. Aircraft fuel systems personnel will follow this publication and applicable technical
data for responsibilities, qualifications, training, and rescue procedure requirements for
working in permit-required confined spaces. (T-0) Note: Any conflict between this instruction
and applicable technical data will be addressed to AFSEC/SEG for resolution.

23.6. Entry into Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH) Conditions. Entry
supervisors will not permit entry into and work in known IDLH conditions under normal
operations. (T-0) Entry into known IDLH conditions is only authorized in cases of extreme
emergencies such as rescue efforts or emergency repairs. (T-0) The entry supervisor will
continuously work to reduce the hazard(s) within the confined space. (T-0) The CSPT will
authorize entry only when the following conditions are met:
23.6.1. Continuous monitoring is conducted. (T-0)
23.6.2. An installation BE and occupational safety representative will always be present to
serve as consultants to the entry supervisor. (T-1)
23.6.3. An experienced on-site supervisor will be present. (T-0)
23.6.4. Personnel will be equipped with respirators IAW 29 CFR § 1910.134, Respiratory
Protection. (T-0) Note: Additional guidance can be found in AFI 48-137.
23.6.5. Trained and equipped emergency rescue will be on-site during IDLH entry operations.
(T-1)
23.7. Master Entry Plan. This part of the written confined space program serves as approval for
recurring entries into permit-required confined spaces to conduct work under routine conditions.
The Master Entry Plan is developed by the organization and approved by the CSPT to allow the
commander’s designated representative(s) to identify entry supervisor(s) that will issue entry
permits without the need for the CSPT to come out.
23.7.1. Recurring basis is defined, for the purpose of this manual, as occurring at least once a
quarter or more frequently. Conflict between this standard and a Technical Order (TO) will
comply with requirements in the TO. (T-1) Refer to conflict resolution on precedence
described in TO 00-5-1, AF Technical Order System, paragraph 1.3, TO Precedence, for
additional information.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

245

23.7.2. The Master Entry Plan will:
23.7.2.1. Require the commander’s designated representative(s) or shop supervisor to
brief entry supervisor(s) on their duties prior to performing the entry. (T-1)
23.7.2.2. Describe the acceptable entry conditions, including atmospheric conditions,
under which permits may be issued. (T-0)
23.7.2.3. Designate as many entry supervisors as needed. (T-1)
23.7.2.4. Identify the specific type and location of permit spaces to be entered and specific
types of tasks or operations to be performed. (T-0) Only permit-required confined spaces
that are entered on a recurring routine manner IAW paragraph 23.7.1, but are similar to
those on the MEP, are not included as part of the MEP.
23.7.2.5. Describe the purpose of the entry and list either by reference or direct statement,
the procedures to be used for entry, e.g., shop Operating Instruction (OI) that cover specific
tasks. (T-0)
23.7.2.6. Account for around-the-clock operations, when appropriate. (T-1)
23.7.2.7. List PPE, atmospheric monitoring and rescue equipment, and conditions under
which they will be used. (T-0)
23.7.2.8. Require continuous atmospheric monitoring. (T-0) When continuous monitoring
is not possible, or determined by the CSPT to not be necessary, based on test data verifying
that acceptable entry conditions are being maintained IAW 29 CFR § 1910.146(d)(5)(ii),
documentation must be maintained. Documentation will include why continuous
monitoring is not possible or needed, what procedures will be used to periodically evaluate
for atmospheric hazards, and the evaluation frequency to be used that will ensure exposed
entrants will have adequate time to escape the permit space will be described. (T-0)
23.7.2.9. List other controls required that will eliminate or control identified atmospheric
or other significant hazards for safe entry, e.g., lockout and ventilation. (T-0)
23.7.2.10. List chemicals and quantities authorized for use. (T-1) List expected exposure
levels based on air sampling results. (T-1) Based on exposure levels, perform
reassessments of the confined spaces. (T-1) The installation BE is the subject matter expert
for determining appropriate PPE. Refer to installation BE to identify concerns and
necessary PPE in case of exposure.
23.7.2.11. List conditions under which the space may or will be reclassified IAW 29 CFR
§ 1910.146(c)(7). (T-0)
23.7.2.12. Establish communication procedures and identify communication equipment to
be used during entries between the attendant and entrants. (T-1)
23.7.2.13. Document emergency rescue procedures for each permit-required confined
space covered under the MEP. (T-0) Procedures will include:
23.7.2.13.1. Self-rescue training and equipment. (T-0)
23.7.2.13.2. Retrieval lines must be used to conduct non-entry rescue. (T-0) However,
when a formal risk assessment determines retrieval lines themselves may constitute an
increased risk to the entrant and not contribute to the rescue of the individual, another

246

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
means will be identified and approved by the CSPT. (T-0) Results will be documented
and maintained with the written confined space program. (T-0)
23.7.2.13.3. Rescue Teams. Determine and evaluate the source of rescue services (unit
organized, contracted, F&ES Flight) to be used for permit-required confined space
entries. (T-0)
23.7.2.13.3.1. Identify the method used for reliable summoning of the rescue team,
e.g., telephone, radio, and ensure it is operable, on-hand and easily accessible to
ensure prompt response. (T-0)
23.7.2.13.3.2. Entry will not be made until the rescue team has been notified and
their availability is verified. (T-0)
23.7.2.13.3.3. When the identified rescue service is not available, the operation
will be halted, unless a secondary trained team is available. (T-0) Note: Within
aircraft maintenance, the fuel systems shop/work center may maintain a rescue
team capability IAW TO 1-1-3. (T-1)
23.7.2.14. Ensure the inspection, testing, maintenance and documentation of safety and
rescue equipment is accomplished IAW Chapter 14, Personal Protective Equipment
(PPE), TO 00-25-245, and manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0) If a conflict exists between
these guidance documents, use the most restrictive guidance. (T-1) Contact the CSPT for
additional assistance.
23.7.2.15. Describe the procedure to retain the canceled permit for one year to facilitate
the review of the permit-required confined space program, as required by 29 CFR §
1910.146(e)(6). (T-0)
23.7.2.16. Include procedures to amend the Master Entry Plan, as needed. (T-1)

23.8. Non-permit Confined Space Entry. These spaces are defined as confined spaces because
they meet the definition of a confined space that does not contain or, with respect to atmospheric
hazards, have the potential to contain any hazard capable of causing death or serious physical
harm. (T-0) Permit-required confined spaces can be reclassified as non-permit confined spaces
where the hazards in the confined space can be effectively eliminated from the space following
requirements found in 29 CFR § 1910.146(c)(7). (T-0)
23.8.1. Entries into a permit-required confined space for the purpose of reclassifying the space
will follow permit-required confined space entry requirements while conducting those
activities until the permit-required confined space hazards are eliminated. (T-0)
23.8.2. Confined spaces classified as non-permit confined spaces will have a formal risk
assessment conducted by the shop supervisor to determine if work to be conducted could or
will introduce hazard(s) that will change its classification to a permit-required confined space,
e.g., welding, sanding, use of chemicals. (T-0)
23.8.2.1. Results will be provided to the CSPT for review and concurrence. (T-1)
23.8.2.2. Work differing from that identified in the formal risk assessment will be
identified to the occupational safety representative for evaluation by the CSPT. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

247

23.8.3. Prior to entry into a non-permit confined space, visually inspect in and around the
space to determine if any previously unidentified potential hazards are present or if conditions
have changed. (T-0)
23.8.3.1. Non-permit confined spaces will have atmospheres tested prior to entry and when
conditions or operations introduce new hazards to validate no atmospheric hazards are
present. (T-1)
23.8.3.2. When atmospheric monitoring reveals hazardous condition or results are
uncertain, contact the CSPT for assistance prior to entry. (T-1)
23.8.3.3. Personnel working in non-permit required confined spaces will complete one of
the identified trainings found in paragraph 23.10.1 or a MAJCOM/FLDCOM approved
alternate to be able to identify when a space may need to be reclassified as permit-required
due to the work or changes occurring in the space. (T-0)
23.9. MAJCOM/FLDCOM Identified Aircraft Confined Spaces. Refer to AFSEC
SharePoint®
(https://usaf.dps.mil/teams/AFSECSEG/Lists/ACFT_ConfinedSpaces/Aircraft%20Spaces.aspx?siteid=%7B7514EA6A%2D85
69%2D4015%2D99C5%2DFE30F71D446D%7D&webid=%7BD5E42E4E%2D3E1E%2D
4C97%2DBE47%2D028C8534C821%7D&uniqueid=%7B169EDC5A%2D32FE%2D4D99
%2D8FD2%2D6F81EBFDDB86%7D) for the most current listing.
23.10. Training.
23.10.1. General Information. Each organization will develop a structured training program,
to include a hands-on portion that establishes safe work practices and techniques specific to
the type of permit-required confined space entries made. (T-1) Initial training can be completed
using AFCEC’s CBT, 367 TRSS’s “The Griffin” Confined Space CBT (Course code:
A1AKU00TVT0002), or OSHA Course 2264, Permit-Required Confined Space Entry. Trainthe-Trainer training is provided by the safety office.
23.10.1.1. Commanders and functional managers will take the CBT on myLearning for
general awareness understanding. (T-1)
23.10.1.2. The occupational safety office, installation BE, and F&ES Flight
representatives will receive additional on-the-job training through each career field’s
applicable training program. (T-1)
23.10.1.2.1. On-the-job training should be with an experienced member (Civilian GS09 or Military 7-Level) and include time observing confined space entries to mentor
new members and answer questions.
23.10.1.2.2. Any additional training required by the MAJCOM/FLDCOM/DRU/FOA
safety office, occupational safety office, F&ES Flight and installation BE.
23.10.2. Commander’s designated representative(s) and/or supervisor will ensure initial, trainthe-trainer and unit specific training is provided to employees working under the permitrequired confined space program. (T-1) Training will be comprised of the following:
23.10.2.1. Initial training will be given to all personnel working under the permit-required
confined space program as identified in paragraph 23.10.1. (T-1)

248

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
23.10.2.2. Train-the-trainer will be provided to all supervisors who have employees that
work in permit-required confined spaces IAW paragraph 23.10.4. (T-1)
23.10.2.3. Organization specific training will ensure that personnel receive classroom and
hands-on training to develop proficiency in their expected duties, which will include entry
into simulated or actual confined spaces that represent common hazards and procedures
found in their workplaces. (T-0)
23.10.2.3.1. A formal risk assessment will be conducted as part of lesson planning to
ensure student safety. (T-1) Note: Simulated means using a trainer that can best
simulate the configuration of the represented confined space.
23.10.2.3.2. Students in this training will be required to experience entry into
simulated or actual confined spaces that represent common hazards and procedures
found on the base. (T-1)
23.10.2.4. Personnel required to test the atmospheric conditions in a confined space will
be trained using manufacturer’s instructions or other information to develop effective
training. (T-0) Supervisors will contact installation BE for assistance in developing
training. (T-1) If unable to support this requirement, installation BE will assist in
identifying a training resource. (T-1)
23.10.2.5. Training will ensure that the individual, with atmospheric testing duties, are
proficient in the use, calibration (user level and manufacturer requirements), and care of
atmospheric testing and monitoring equipment. (T-0) This includes testing and calibration
of direct reading portable gas monitors. In addition, if entry is required to conduct tests, the
tester will also meet the training requirements of an entrant. (T-0)
23.10.2.5.1. Annual training on the use, calibration, and care of atmosphere testing and
monitoring equipment will be conducted to maintain proficiency. (T-1)
23.10.2.5.2. Supervisors will maintain a list of personnel trained and knowledgeable
to conduct atmospheric testing and monitoring of confined spaces for their shop by
name, name of trainer, and date completed training; and provide a copy to the
commander’s designated representative. (T-1)
23.10.3. Rescue Training. Supervisors will develop rescue training that covers rescue methods
that the organization will use, e.g., self-rescue, non-entry rescue, entry rescue. (T-0) Contact
the F&ES Flight for assistance in developing the training. (T-1) The F&ES Flight will validate
that all organizationally developed rescue training, equipment and procedures meet
requirements. (T-1)
23.10.4. Train-the-Trainer. The CSPT will develop a train-the-trainer program to provide
entry supervisors with the initial program knowledge needed to effectively manage their
program. (T-1)
23.10.4.1. This training will not replace the organization specific training required to be
developed specifically to address the procedures required to enter organizational permitrequired confined spaces but will supplement initial training. Training objectives must
focus on providing a clear understanding of how a confined space program will be
developed and implemented. (T-1) Training will include:
23.10.4.1.1. General confined space, hazard identification and evaluation. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

249

23.10.4.1.2. Discussion on fulfilling training requirements for employees working in
permit-required confined spaces. (T-1)
23.10.4.1.3. Discussion on written program requirements. (T-1)
23.10.4.1.4. Discussion on proper completion of entry permits with exercise. (T-1)
23.10.4.1.5. Discussion of MEP requirements. (T-1)
23.10.4.1.6. Discussion of MAJCOM/FLDCOM requirements, as applicable. (T-1)
23.10.4.2. The MAJCOM-FLDCOM/SEG or FOA/DRU SEG (when the organization has
their own safety staff) will approve the train-the-trainer course materials and provide
MAJCOM/FLDCOM or FOA/DRU specific requirements, as appropriate. (T-1)
23.10.5. Refresher training will be provided as prescribed by the CSPT, at least annually, or
when an employee shows lack of knowledge or demonstrates unsafe behavior while working
in a permit-required confined space. (T-1)
23.10.6. Documentation of training will have name of student, name of trainer and dates of
training. (T-0)
23.11. Instructions for Completing the DAF Form 1024, Confined Space Entry Permit. The
following guidance will be used to complete the DAF Form 1024, or equivalent DoD or
commercial form:
23.11.1. Section 1 – Master Entry Plan. Mark the appropriate block indicating whether or not
the entry permit was issued under a Confined Space Program Team-approved Master Entry
Plan. (T-1) If not, occupational safety office, installation BE and F&ES Flight will be
coordinated with using Section 9 prior to entering a confined space.
23.11.2. Section 2 – General Information. Enter the location (Global Positioning Satellite
coordinates, if known) of the confined space and indicate if the space is located on or off the
installation. (T-0) Include a description of the space to be entered, and the purpose for entry.
(T-0) Identify any TO or OI that covers the entry. (T-1) Enter the date and duration (time used
and time expires) of the permit. (T-0)
23.11.3. Section 3 – Hazards. Specify all probable hazards associated with the entry, e.g.,
oxygen enrichment, engulfment, mechanical. (T-0)
23.11.4. Section 4 – Hazard Controls. Specify exact items required, to include manufacturer
and part number, and add any equipment not listed in the form. (T-0)
23.11.5. Section 5 – Preparation for Entry. Identify required preparations prior to entering the
space. (T-0) Refer to Master Entry Plan, governing TO or OI, as appropriate.
23.11.6. Section 6 – Atmospheric Testing and Monitoring Record. Enter the make, model, and
serial number for all testing equipment along with its calibration and bump test date. (T-0)
Indicate if continuous monitoring is required; if yes, document the frequency. (T-0) Consider
the potential for stratified atmospheres that would facilitate additional testing/monitoring.
Indicate if there is additional testing/monitoring or entrant paperwork; if yes, ensure it is
attached. (T-0)
23.11.7. Section 7 – Authorized Personnel. List the names of all entry supervisors,
atmospheric monitors, attendants, and entrants for entry. (T-0) If the entry supervisor(s) will

250

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
enter the permit-required confined space, list them as entrants. (T-0) Attach an additional
separate sheet to list additional entrants, if needed. Note: Attendants and entrants are not
required to sign or initial next to their names.
23.11.8. Section 8 – F&ES Flight or equivalent. Identify and notify emergency response
personnel. (T-0) Include POC name, contact information (e.g., phone number, radio call sign,
etc.) and indicate the date/time when availability of emergency response personnel was
confirmed. (T-0)
23.11.9. Section 9 – Coordination. Occupational safety office, installation BE, and F&ES
Flight will be coordinated with prior to entry. (T-1) Note: Coordination is not required if entry
is covered by a Confined Space Program Team-approved Master Entry Plan.
23.11.10. Section 10 – Entry Time Log. Enter the name of each entrant and their time of entry
and exit. (T-0)
23.11.11. Section 11 – Close-out/Cancellation. The entry supervisor will sign each entry
permit to validate the form. (T-0) (For Construction Only—Personnel responsible for the
confined space will brief the owner that all occupants for the space have exited and the permit
has been closed). (T-0)

23.12. Contractor Requirements.
23.12.1. Contractors performing confined space work for the DAF will comply with OSHA,
DoDI 6055.01 and DAFI 91-202 per contract requirements. (T-0) Required communication of
known hazards in permit-required confined spaces will be conducted IAW 29 CFR §
1926.1203(h). (T-0)
23.12.1.1. The contractor is required to conduct its own exposure monitoring during
permit-required confined space operations. (T-1) Installation BE will not test or monitor a
confined space for a contracted confined space operation. (T-1)
23.12.1.2. Rescue services for contractors will not be provided by the F&ES Flight without
specific written approval from the fire chief. (T-1) Rescue support must also be a part of
the agreed upon contract requirements by the unit, the installation contract office, the fire
chief and the contractor. (T-1)
23.12.2. The contracting office will notify the safety office of contracts where permit-required
confined space entries are written in the contract. (T-1) The contracting office will set up a
meeting between the safety office and the contractor to fulfill the requirements of 29 CFR §
1926.1203(h). (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

251
Chapter 24

AIRCRAFT FLIGHTLINE – GROUND OPERATIONS AND ACTIVITIES
24.1. Scope. This chapter addresses hazards and safe operating procedures on the flightline, to
include activities in maintenance hangars and ground operations conducted on the flightline ramp.
Refer to DAFI 91-202 for additional guidance on job hazard analysis (JHA). Note: A JHA is not
required when existing guidance adequately covers all safety requirements of an operation or
process.
24.2. Specific Requirements.
24.2.1. Guidance. Supervisors and managers will follow requirements applicable to their areas
of responsibility. (T-1)
24.2.2. Fire Prevention. Refer to Chapter 6 for guidance on fire prevention guidance and
paragraph 24.17 for additional guidance on contractor operations regarding fire prevention
and protection.
24.2.3. CPR/TCCC. Aircraft maintenance, support shop employees, and personnel acting as
safety observers exposed to electrical hazards from energized aircraft electrical systems,
aircraft components or aircraft equipment will be provided CPR and TCCC training. (T-1)
Maintenance commanders and supervisors may identify other job tasks that require
CPR/TCCC training based on hazards associated with those tasks. Unit CPR instructors will
meet training requirements described in paragraph 1.9. All personnel requiring CPR/TCCC
training will remain current. (T-0)
24.2.4. Lifting Devices. Refer to Chapter 12 for guidance on material handling guidance for
hoists and below-the-hook equipment.
24.2.5. Compressed Air.
24.2.5.1. Compressed air will not be used for cleaning purposes, except where reduced to
30 psi and only when used with effective chip guarding and PPE. (T-0) OSHA prohibits
the use of compressed air for cleaning when cadmium, lead, chromium dusts or any other
regulated air contaminant may be present. (T-0) Refer to paragraph 25.3.1 for additional
guidance.
24.2.5.2. Before servicing or checking pressure for any aircraft system or component, e.g.,
aircraft tires, air bottles or landing gear struts, mechanics will:
24.2.5.2.1. Inspect all chucks and valve stems for serviceability to ensure safe
servicing. (T-1)
24.2.5.2.2. Not over-inflate these components. (T-1)
24.2.5.2.3. Install pressure regulators to prevent over-pressurization of components.
(T-1)
24.2.5.2.4. Wear proper PPE and eye protection IAW TO and local directives. (T-0)
24.2.5.2.5. Ensure appropriate equipment has been calibrated, as required. (T-1)
24.2.5.3. Air Lines and Fittings. Employees will:

252

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.2.5.3.1. Avoid bending or kinking air hose lines. (T-1)
24.2.5.3.2. Place air hose lines where they minimize a tripping hazard and are
protected against damage caused by equipment or vehicles rolling over them. (T-0)
24.2.5.3.3. Inspect airlines and fittings for defects before each use. (T-1)
24.2.5.3.4. Ensure non-interchangeable chucks and connections are used on high- and
low- pressure equipment to preclude servicing low-pressure systems with highpressure equipment. (T-1)
24.2.5.4. A drainpipe and valve will be installed at the lowest point of the air receiver to
remove accumulated oil and water. (T-1) The air receiver drain valve, if not automatic, will
be opened and receiver completely drained daily to prevent accumulation of excess fluids.
(T-1) Refer to manufacturer instructions for additional guidance.
24.2.6. Oxygen and Nitrogen (Liquid and Gaseous). Oxygen is not flammable but supports
combustion. Fire or explosion may result if oxygen contacts petroleum products, alcohol or
other flammable or combustible materials. Oxygen systems subject to excessive pressures may
result in rupture and explosion. Liquid oxygen (LOX) and liquid nitrogen (LN2) are extremely
cold, can cause serious burns and can freeze skin upon contact. Nitrogen also depletes oxygen
in unventilated, confined areas. Refer to Chapter 26, Cryogenic Liquids, for additional
guidance.
24.2.6.1. Personnel Training and Safety. Only fully trained and qualified personnel will
operate oxygen and nitrogen equipment or service aircraft systems. (T-0) A trainee may
service only if directly supervised by a qualified supervisor or operator. (T-1)
24.2.6.2. Mobile Servicing Units or Bottles. Mobile servicing units or bottles used to
service aircraft or components will be positioned and not left unattended after hook-up or
during servicing operations. (T-1)
24.2.6.3. Aircraft oxygen systems (both gaseous and liquid) will be emptied/drained prior
to entering a major maintenance cycle. (T-1) Safeguards will be taken while performing
aircraft servicing or maintenance operations to prevent inherently or accidentally
introducing ignition or combustible sources. (T-1) Refer to TO 00-25-172 for additional
guidance.
24.2.7. Aircraft arresting systems may pose hazards to aircraft and vehicle operations.
24.2.8. Confined Spaces. Supervisors will comply with requirements in Chapter 23. (T-0)
24.2.9. Red Ball Maintenance Events. Maintenance personnel will not deviate from authorized
maintenance procedures or violate any flightline requirements, e.g., flightline speed limits or
safety requirements, during Red Ball maintenance events. (T-1)
24.2.9.1. Emphasis will be placed on foreign object damage (FOD) awareness/prevention
during this critical maintenance operation. (T-1) TOs, tools, rags, parts, unused supplies
and checklists will be accounted for before aircraft engine startup. (T-1)
24.2.9.2. If aircraft engines are operating, a safety observer (maintenance or aircrew
member) will maintain interphone communications or remain in full view of the flight crew
and be positioned to maintain overall surveillance of the aircraft and personnel performing
maintenance. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

253

24.2.9.3. Weapons loaded aircraft will be made safe IAW applicable Mission Design
Series (MDS) and/or weapons specific TOs. (T-1)
24.2.10. Aircraft Lavatory Servicing. Aircraft lavatory servicing will be conducted IAW AFI
24-605V2, Air Transportation Operations. (T-1)
24.3. Flightline Potentially Hazardous Areas and Operations.
24.3.1. Flammable Fuel Vapor Areas. Supervisors will ensure all personnel are aware of
potentially flammable fuel vapor areas and the restriction against ignition sources in these
areas. (T-0)
24.3.1.1. Fuel vapors are heavier than air, settle to ground level, and will enter belowground areas. These areas include, but are not limited to, fuel pits below ground level, areas
within 10 feet of aircraft fuel vent systems and fuel spills, hydrant pump houses, and fuels
laboratories.
24.3.1.2. Spark arresters are not required on general or special purpose vehicles driving on
the flightline and around aircraft. However, these vehicles are not designed, certified, or
authorized to operate in flammable fuel vapor areas. Employees in flammable fuel vapor
areas will receive specialized training emphasizing hazards associated with these
operations. (T-0) Refer to DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 for requirements for spark
arresters on general or special purpose vehicles within or near structures containing
exposed explosives.
24.3.1.3. Not rated and/or approved equipment will be turned off or removed from the area
during refueling operations and fuel cell/tank work, unless permitted by TO 1-1-3. (T-1)
Only explosion-proof, intrinsically safe radios/equipment, or equipment incapable of
having sufficient energy to be an ignition source (approved by competent authority, e.g.,
HQ AFSEC, FLDCOM, HQ AFMC, AFCEC) will be used in and around flammable fuel
vapor areas. (T-0) Refer to NFPA 70, Article 501, Class I Locations, for additional
information.
24.3.2. Electrical Receptacles. Exterior electrical receptacles on the flightline are protected
from weather conditions but are not authorized for use in hazardous areas as defined in NFPA
70, National Electrical Code. If separation is not possible, receptacles in the immediate area
of the fueling or defueling operations will be de-energized using administrative devices IAW
Chapter 17. (T-1)
24.3.3. Aircraft Maintenance Operations. Aircraft maintenance operations in hangars,
shelters, or docks (closed or semi-enclosed) on the flightline or outside maintenance areas,
such as wash racks, will be accomplished IAW Air Force guidance or recognized national
consensus standards. (T-1) MAJCOMs, FLDCOMs and units operating in overseas or remote
areas may supplement this publication to meet international or host country safety
requirements or unique working conditions.
24.3.3.1. Electrical Devices and Power Equipment. Portable electrical equipment in Class
I, Division 1 and Class I, Division 2 locations will be explosion-proof or intrinsically safe
and will comply with NFPA 70 or will be approved by competent authority, e.g., HQ
AFMC, AFCEC/COS, to show that the equipment is incapable of having sufficient energy

254

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
to be an ignition source. (T-0) Items listed in TO 00-25-172 can be used in Class 1, Division
2 locations.
24.3.3.1.1. Equipment not meeting this requirement may be temporarily approved by
the F&ES Flight prior to the use of the equipment. Approval will be documented in
writing, stating the area, nature, duration, and the reason for the exception. (T-1)
24.3.3.1.2. If the working environment has been tested and contains flammable fuel
vapors, all maintenance activities will be stopped until the hazardous condition is
eliminated, and a safe work environment restored. (T-0)
24.3.3.1.3. Equipment in an area with a high potential for flammable fuel vapor release
and that can be automatically energized when unattended will be considered for
explosion- proof or intrinsically safe design. (T-0)
24.3.3.2. Tobacco. IAW AFI 48-104, Tobacco Free Living, tobacco use is prohibited in
aircraft maintenance facilities, flightline areas, and weapons storage and maintenance
areas, unless designated by the F&ES Flight, in coordination with the Maintenance Group
Commander (MXG/CC) or equivalent, Airfield Manager and/or the functional manager.
(T-1)
24.3.3.3. Aircraft Fuels Management and Servicing. Fuel system maintenance and
servicing operations will be IAW Chapter 18, Hydrocarbon Fuels, TO 00-25-172 and
other applicable TOs. (T-1). Aircraft fuel cell and integral tank inspections and repairs will
be IAW TO 1-1-3. (T-1)
24.3.3.4. Welding on Aircraft. Welding on aircraft will be done outside of hangars,
whenever possible. (T-1) A DAF Form 592 will be issued prior to aircraft welding
operations. (T-1) Welding will only be performed on aircraft system components IAW
applicable aircraft technical data and Chapter 20, Welding, Cutting and Brazing. (T-1)
Welding operations in hangars will meet the requirements of NFPA 410, Standard on
Aircraft Hangars. . (T-1) Contact installation BE for ventilation concerns regarding
welding on aircraft.
24.3.3.5. Fire Extinguishers. Suitable fire extinguishers will be placed throughout
flightline and maintenance areas, within easy reach of the operator and close to ground
power equipment. (T-1) Refer to TO 00-25-172, Table 3-1, for flightline operations fire
extinguisher requirements and Chapter 6, Fire Protection and Prevention, for additional
information. Note: 150-pound flightline fire extinguisher programs are maintained under
the Aircraft Maintenance Squadrons or Generation Squadrons as required per HAFMD 138, Deputy Chief of Staff, Logistics, Engineering and Force Protection.
24.3.3.6. Nondestructive Inspection (NDI). NDI operations involving ionizing radiation
(normally X-ray operations) will be conducted IAW TO 33B-1-1. (T-1) The IRSO will
approve flightline X-ray procedures and locations. (T-1)
24.3.4. Hazardous Noise. Personnel assigned to the flightline may be exposed to hazardous
noise levels. Supervisors will ensure assigned personnel adhere to engineering controls,
administrative controls and properly wear PPE. (T-0) Refer to AFI 48-127 for additional
guidance.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

255

24.4. Additional PPE Requirements. The information below is specific to flightline operations
and activities and is in addition to the PPE requirements of Chapter 14. Maintenance employees
will wear heat-resistant gloves and long-sleeved shirts to cover exposed skin to prevent burns when
working around hot exhaust or tail pipes. (T-1)
24.4.1. Head Apparel. Hard hats or bump caps without chinstraps will not be worn in an engine
intake danger zone, as defined by the specific aircraft TO, while engines are operating. (T-1)
Refer to DAFI 21-101, Aircraft and Equipment Maintenance Management, for additional
guidance.
24.4.1.1. Bump Caps. If bump caps are the chosen PPE for use after conducting a hazard
assessment IAW paragraphs 14.3.1 and 14.3.2, follow the guidance in paragraph 14.3.5.
24.4.1.2. Metal, plastic or leather hair fasteners and wigs will not be worn during aircraft
maintenance. Hair ties (with no metal parts) that secure the hair at the back of the head may
be worn. Refer to paragraph 14.3.5.1 for additional guidance. Umbrellas are not permitted
on the operational flightline except when assisting passengers on or off aircraft during
inclement weather. Criteria in Chapter 14, Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) will be
used when aircraft or equipment TOs do not provide specific PPE guidance. (T-1). When
in doubt, consult the occupational safety office and installation BE. Note: Helmets to
protect employees from impact and flying objects must meet requirements in Chapter 14.
(T-1)
24.4.2. Footwear. Protective footwear will be provided and worn when there is a reasonable
possibility of foot injuries. (T-0) Removable shoe cleats may be worn on the flightline in areas
where severe snow/ice conditions are common. Organizations may permit use of removable
shoe cleats to improve traction on ice/snow covered surfaces and prevent employee slips and
falls. Organizations will obtain coordinated approval from their installation management, to
include the F&ES Flight and occupational safety office. (T-1) Organizations should consider
using non-metallic cleats to reduce the potential for spark- initiated fire. Organizations will
define the type(s) of footwear to be worn, the conditions and locations where such footwear
may be worn, and procedures/controls to prevent cleats/spikes/studs becoming a FOD hazard.
(T-1) Cleats will not be worn while working on or inside aircraft. (T-1) Metal cleats will not
be worn within 50 feet of fueling, defueling or fuel cell repair operations, or operations that
involve flammable solvents, paints, etc. (T-1)
24.4.3. Jet Intakes. Mechanics entering jet intake sections will wear appropriate PPE that
consists of a pocket-less, zipper-less, button-less bunny-suit labeled, “Intakes Only,” and cloth
over-booties or stocking feet. (T-0)
24.4.3.1. Boots, if worn, must be inspected and FOD removed from boots prior to
installing cloth over-booties. (T-2) Note: Boots may be worn if not restricted for use by
TO or MAJCOM/FLDCOM and are authorized by MXG/CC.
24.4.3.2. To minimize heat stress during high heat/humidity conditions, e.g., air
temperatures above 85° F, employees in engine intakes may wear half-bunny suits and
garments designed for use over trousers. When worn with a pocket-less T-shirt, these items
provide the same level of FOD protection as a full bunny suit. Units using half-bunny suits
will have procedures in place to control use. (T-1)

256

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.4.4. LOX/LN2 Transfers. When transferring LOX/LN2, personnel will wear PPE (all items
will be clean and free of grease, oil and fuel) as identified in the task specific JHA, OI and/or
JSTO. (T-1) Protective footwear, which fit closely around the top, with rubber soles and heels.
(T-0) Note: A leather boot approximately eight inches in height with close fitting top and
rubber soles and heel is recommended since LOX spills normally subject one’s foot area to a
freeze burn exposure hazard. This type of footwear, when equipped with a hard protective toe
area, is generally classified as a safety shoe or boot.
24.4.5. Face Shield. Face shields are secondary eye protection and must be worn in
conjunction with primary eye protection, e.g., safety glasses, goggles. (T-0)

24.5. Aircraft Parking Requirements.
24.5.1. Specific parking locations will be designated for each aircraft IAW DAFMAN 321084, Standard Facility Requirements. (T-1) Generally, distances between parked aircraft,
wingtip to wingtip, will allow immediate access of emergency vehicles and permit free
movement of equipment and materials. (T-1) Refer to DAFMAN 32-1084 for specific aircraft
wingtip distances. Refer to DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 and TO 11A-1-33, Handling
and Maintenance of Explosives Loaded Aircraft, for parking explosives loaded aircraft.
24.5.2. Temporary Parking. A qualified ground observer will be used to warn of oncoming
traffic when temporarily parked aircraft extend into an active taxiway. (T-1) A suitable flag
will suffice in daytime; however, when dark, a high-visibility reflective equipment/gear and
red warning light will be used. (T-1) A ground observer will remain with the aircraft until
moved to a safe location. (T-1).
24.5.3. Wheel Chocking. Aircraft wheel chocks will be placed fore and aft of the main landing
gear or as specified in applicable aircraft TOs. (T-1)
24.5.4. Clean Parking Areas. Maintenance equipment, work stands, loose aircraft parts, and
materials not actually required for work in progress or planned, will be moved from the aircraft
parking area to designated storage locations. (T-1) Equipment stored outside buildings will be
tied down and/or chocked or brakes applied to prevent movement by wind or engine blast.
(T-1) Mobile work platforms and stands will be moored/tied down when in designated storage
locations to prevent collision with aircraft, vehicles, or other equipment. (T-1) Designated
flightline support equipment storage areas will be determined by the Aerospace Ground
Equipment (AGE) flight commander and airfield manager and approved by the MXG/CC or
equivalent in coordination with the occupational safety office. (T-1)
24.5.5. Taxi/Tow Lines. Refer to AFMAN 11-218, Aircraft Operations and Movement on the
Ground, for guidance.
24.5.6. Mooring or Tie-Down of Aircraft. Follow applicable aircraft TOs and use designated
fittings on aircraft. (T-1) Square or bowline knots will be used to provide secure rope
fastenings. (T-1) Allow sufficient slack to prevent excessive stress on wings, fittings and rope
from tire or strut expansion or deflation and contraction of tie-down ropes when wet. (T-1)
Aircraft mooring points will be placed directly over ground mooring points, or as close as
possible. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

257

24.5.7. Aircraft Emergency Removal. Emergency procedures will be developed to remove
endangered aircraft in the event of fire, adverse weather, or other emergency situations on the
flightline, or in hangars. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 24.14.10 or additional guidance.
24.5.8. Propeller Driven Aircraft and Helicopters. Do not walk through the propeller or tail
rotor arc(s). (T-1) Walk around the outside of the propeller or tail rotor arc(s). (T-1)
24.6. Adverse Weather Conditions.
24.6.1. Specific weather thresholds and notification procedures will be documented in the
installation weather support document. (T-1) Aircraft will be moved/evacuated to safe weather
areas when tornadoes, hurricanes, or winds of 50 knots or greater are forecast. (T-1) Local
damaging wind and severe weather safety plans will be developed to include an evacuation
plan and hangaring of installation and transient aircraft. (T-1) The option to evacuate is the
commander’s decision in coordination with the appropriate MAJCOM/FLDCOM. Refer to
Chapter 3, Weather Safety, for additional guidance.
24.6.2. Safeguarding Aircraft.
24.6.2.1. Taxiing Aircraft. Avoid taxiing aircraft during strong or damaging wind
conditions defined by local weather support document. Taxi only at the discretion of the
wing/delta commander when the action will lessen the possibility of aircraft damage.
24.6.2.2. Outside Parking. If possible, park aircraft inside hangars during periods of strong
or damaging winds. Light aircraft will be given priority over heavy aircraft for parking
inside hangars. (T-1) Aircraft parked outside will be faced into the wind and tied down.
(T-1) Wing spoilers will be used when necessary to reduce airfoil lift. (T-1) Transient
aircraft will be parked inside hangars, if space permits, or tied down as directed by the
MXG/CC or equivalent. (T-1)
24.6.2.3. When to Tow Aircraft. Light aircraft in docks or extending outside hangars will
be towed clear and parked IAW applicable aircraft TOs when winds exceed 30 knots
(sustained or gusts). (T-1) Medium or heavy aircraft in docks or extending outside hangars
will be towed clear and parked IAW applicable aircraft TOs when winds meet or exceed
50 knots (sustained or gusts). (T-1) Refer to Table 24.1 for additional information.
24.6.2.4. Working Materials. All maintenance equipment, work stands, loose aircraft
parts, flightline fire extinguishers and materials not in use will be removed from aircraft
parking areas and stored in a safe area. (T-1) Equipment remaining outside will be secured
against wind movement. (T-1)
24.6.2.5. Personnel. Activities other than those required to safeguard aircraft will be
suspended and avoided when winds reach velocities of 40 knots or greater. (T-1)
24.6.3. High Profile Vehicles (e.g., C-5 Wide-body Staircases, Raised K-Loader, etc.).
Caution is required when using high profile vehicles during high wind conditions. Refer to the
vehicle technical order and lesson plan for specifics regarding specific vehicle types.
