24.7. Towing and Taxiing Aircraft.
24.7.1. Tow Operations and Procedures. In addition to this manual, DAFI 21-101, AFMAN
11-218 and aircraft specific TOs outline additional requirements for aircraft towing. Note:
Technical data takes precedence over this publication when differences exist in towing
procedures.
24.7.1.1. Avoid backing aircraft, when possible.
24.7.1.2. Tow Operations with Engines Running. MAJCOMs/FLDCOMs may authorize
movement of aircraft into hardened aircraft shelters while the aircraft engines are operating,
provided a System Safety Engineering Analysis (SSEA) has been conducted. A SSEA will
be conducted for each type of generation shelter and type of aircraft to be moved into the
shelter. (T-1)
24.7.1.3. Commercial aircraft may be towed or pushed short distances, e.g., pushing away
from hangar or terminal gate, with engines operating IAW Federal Aviation
Administration-approved procedures in operations and maintenance handbooks. Copies of
these procedures will be carried on the aircraft. (T-1) DAF aircraft may only be pushed

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

259

back with engines running during non-routine or abnormal operational requirements using
push-back procedures supported by engineering analysis and the aircraft TOs.
24.7.1.4. Place propellers in an “X” position or position suitable for the aircraft and dock
combination. (T-1)
24.7.1.5. Station two team members to observe the top clearance of propeller blades. (T-1)
24.7.1.6. Towing Speed. Towing speed must not exceed the walking speed of team
members, with a maximum of five miles per hour. (T-1)
24.7.1.7. Brakes. Aircraft brake systems will be charged before each towing operation.
(T-1) Towing will be stopped immediately if brake pressure drops below safe operating
limits. (T-1) Aircraft with inoperative brakes will follow MDS-specific procedures. (T-1)
Procedures for towing aircraft without MDS-specific guidance for inoperative brakes as
follows:
24.7.1.7.1. Obtain squadron commander or designated representative approval prior to
each tow. (T-2)
24.7.1.7.2. Maintain radio communication throughout the entire towing operation.
(T-1)
24.7.1.7.3. Additional personnel will chock walk the aircraft, positioned outboard of
each landing gear strut requiring chocks. (T-1)
24.7.1.7.4. Local procedures for aircraft without MDS-specific guidance will be
developed and published in a supplement. (T-2) Procedures will include main landing
gear chock walk requirements. (T-2)
24.7.1.8. Steerable Gear. Steerable landing gear, including outriggers, will be set in tow
position before the aircraft is moved and returned to original position after tow bar removal.
(T-1) The tow team supervisor will instruct cockpit personnel to allow the nose gear
steering wheel to turn freely and not attempt to steer or turn the nose wheel when the tow
bar is connected to the aircraft. (T-1)
24.7.1.9. Starts and Stops. Tow vehicle operators will not stop and start suddenly when
moving aircraft. (T-1) Aircraft brakes will only be applied upon direction from tow
supervisor. (T-1) Chocks will be properly placed, and the aircraft’s brakes set before the
towing vehicle is unhooked from the aircraft. (T-1)
24.7.1.10. Equipment, Stands and Similar Materials. The tow team supervisor will ensure
all equipment, work stands, loose aircraft parts, fire extinguishers and all other materials
are removed from the vicinity of an aircraft and properly stored. (T-1) Equipment or
materials left outside will be secured to prevent accidental movement by wind or jet or
propeller blast. (T-1)
24.7.1.11. Chocks. Chocks will be maintained near the tow vehicle or aircraft for
emergency use throughout the tow operation. (T-1) Chocks will be properly placed before
the tow vehicle is unhooked. (T-1) Chocks or other support equipment will not be placed
on or hung from any part of the aircraft exterior during tow operations. (T-1) Exception:
Lights attached to tow during reduced visibility are excluded.

260

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.7.1.12. Tow Equipment. Only authorized equipment in good condition will be used in
tow operations. (T-1) Tow vehicles, tow bars, and any other equipment required to
facilitate the tow operation will be inspected for defects by the tow team supervisor. (T-1)
The tow team supervisor is responsible for the entire tow operation and will ensure the tow
vehicle is free of defects or extraneous material that may interfere with safe operation
before the tow bar is hooked to the aircraft. (T-1) Tow vehicles with safety defects will be
removed from service until repaired. (T-1)
24.7.1.12.1. Tow Bars. Tow bars may be designed with load limiting devices to
prevent secondary damage to the aircraft when a mechanical overload occurs due to
sudden braking, deceleration, acceleration, excessive steering force, exceeding turn
limits, etc.
24.7.1.12.1.1. Only authorized equipment in good condition will be used in towing
operations. (T-1) Before moving any aircraft, the tow vehicle, tow bar and
connections, and other associated equipment will be inspected for defects by the
tow team supervisor. (T-1)
24.7.1.12.1.2. Tow Bar Failure. In the event of a tow bar failure, separation
between the towing vehicle and aircraft must be maintained. (T-1) Many tow bars
incorporate a load limiting device (e.g., shear joints, axial load limiters, etc.) by
design with the purpose of preventing secondary damage to the aircraft in the event
of a mechanical overload that could occur with sudden braking or deceleration,
acceleration, excessive steering force, exceeding turn limits, etc. The tow team
supervisor will brief tow bar failure contingencies to the entire tow team prior to
commencing towing operations. (T-1)
24.7.1.12.2. Tow Vehicles. Tow vehicle operators will inspect the tow vehicle to
ensure it is serviceable prior to performing any tow operation. (T-1) The inspection
will be documented on the AF Form 1800, Operator’s Inspection Guide and Trouble
Report. (T-1) Towing connections will be inspected before each use. (T-1) Pintle
assemblies and towing connections will be secured with a pintle hook safety or cotter
pin that will positively lock towing connections. (T-1) Tow vehicles will stop at least
50 feet from the aircraft to be moved and will only proceed when directed by the tow
team supervisor. (T-1) The tow team supervisor is responsible for the entire towing
operation and will ensure the tow vehicle is free of defects or extraneous material that
may interfere with safe operation before the tow bar is hooked to the aircraft. (T-1)
Tow vehicles with safety defects will be removed from service until repaired. (T-1)
24.7.1.12.2.1. During inclement weather conditions, when towing or parking
aircraft with snow, ice, or frost present, use the heaviest tow vehicle authorized per
aircraft technical data to prevent loss of aircraft or tow vehicle control during tow
operations. Also, equip the tow vehicle with chains to maintain traction, as needed,
to ensure safe towing operations in ice or snow conditions.
24.7.1.12.2.2. Conduct a risk assessment of proposed tow operation based on
current runway and taxiway condition readings from local Airfield Management.
(T-1) The decision to proceed will be approved by appropriate squadron command
levels. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

261

24.7.2. Qualified Personnel. Newly assigned aircraft maintenance personnel will pass a
proficiency test on the types of aircraft towed after completing supervised on-the-job training
(OJT). (T-1) All tow team members will be familiar with all published towing procedures for
the aircraft being towed. Tow vehicle operators, brake-riders, wing and tail walkers, and tow
supervisors require annual proficiency training. (T-1) Tow team supervisors will clearly define
duties and responsibilities of each tow team member at the time of the pre-tow briefing. (T-1)
24.7.2.1. Tow Team Supervisor. The tow team supervisors will be in complete command
of the movement. (T-1) Tow team supervisors will take a position to effectively supervise
the tow operation and performance of the tow team. (T-1) The tow team supervisors will
use applicable technical data and instructions/manuals to ensure personnel safety and safe
movement of the aircraft. (T-1) The tow team supervisor is the only position authorized to
give the “all clear to move” order. (T-2) The tow team supervisors will determine the safe
distance, based on aircraft type and amount of allowable space to maneuver the aircraft.
(T-1) Prior to towing an aircraft, the tow team supervisor will conduct a pre-tow briefing
to ensure all members of the tow team are qualified and understand the duties and
responsibilities to be performed. (T-1)
24.7.2.2. Brake Operator. A qualified person, authorized by the tow team supervisor, will
be in the pilot’s seat to operate aircraft brakes, and follow the tow team supervisor’s
instructions. (T-1) If the brake operator is unable to maintain hydraulic pressure, another
qualified person will be stationed to watch and maintain the pressure. (T-1) Tow operations
will be terminated if brake pressure drops below safe operating limits. (T-1)
24.7.2.3. Tow Vehicle Operator. A qualified and authorized tow vehicle operator will
operate the vehicle in a safe manner, follow the tow team supervisor’s instructions, and
always maintain control of the tow vehicle. (T-1) The tow vehicle operator will stop the
tow vehicle if line of sight or communication with the tow team supervisor is lost. (T-1)
The tow vehicle operator will face the direction of travel while seated and not look over
the shoulder to face the direction of travel except while making minor changes during final
positioning. (T-1)
24.7.2.4. Wing Walker. A wing walker will be positioned at each wing tip to ensure
adequate clearance of any obstruction. (T-1) Wing walkers will signal the supervisor
whenever the aircraft is in danger of colliding with an obstruction. (T-1) In such cases,
towing will be stopped until clearance is personally checked by the tow team supervisor.
(T-1) Wing walkers will be used when towing through obstructed areas and during final
positioning in hangars/docks. (T-1)
24.7.2.5. Tail Walker. A tail walker will be used when the aircraft is turned sharply or
backed into position. (T-1) A tail walker will signal the supervisor whenever the aircraft is
in danger of colliding with an obstruction. (T-1) In such cases, towing will be stopped until
clearance is personally checked by the tow team supervisor. (T-1)
24.7.2.6. Nose Walker. The tow team supervisor serves as the nose walker and will
maintain a position in front of the nose of the aircraft with a clear view of wing walkers,
the vehicle operator, and the brake operator in the cockpit. (T-1) When tail and wing
walkers are not required, the tow team supervisor may ride in the tow vehicle if the
traversed path can be observed.

262

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.7.3. Taxiing Operations. Refer to AFMAN 11-218 for additional guidance. A marshaller
will be used. (T-1) Personnel will remain clear of taxiing aircraft until the aircraft has come to
a complete stop. (T-1) Aircraft will not be taxied without clearance. (T-1) Radio contact will
be maintained with the control tower throughout taxi operations. (T-1) Taxi speeds in
uncongested areas will be reasonable, with aircraft and personnel safety the determining
factors. (T-1) Follow guidance in appropriate TOs for taxi operations under varying wind
conditions. Personnel, vehicles, and towed aircraft will remain clear of taxiing traffic and will
not pass within 200 feet behind aircraft with engines running. (T-1) Note: Exercise care not
to blind ground crewmembers when taxiing with landing lights on. Caution: The wingtip path
on swept-back wing aircraft extends beyond the straight-line wingtip path during turns, based
on the degree of turn and the degree of sweep on the wings. Equipment, vehicles, and other
aircraft may appear to be clear of the intended path while impeding it.
24.7.4. Employees Riding or Walking. Personnel will not cross a tow bar when connected to
the aircraft and the tow vehicle, nor ride on the outside of a moving aircraft, tow bar or on the
outside of tow vehicles unless an authorized seat is provided. (T-1) No one will try to board or
leave a moving aircraft or tow vehicle. (T-1) The tow supervisor will brief this information
prior to towing. (T-1)
24.7.5. Night Crew Signals. Refer to AFMAN 11-218 for night crew signaling practices.
Many units have established local policy on night crew signaling procedures. Local policy will
be written IAW the tenets of AFMAN 11-218. (T-1)
24.7.6. Control Tower Clearance. The tow team supervisor will obtain control tower clearance
before towing aircraft on or across a taxiway or runway, maintain radio contact with tower and
inform the tower when tow is complete. (T-1) The primary means of communication will be
the aircraft radio, but, if not feasible, a radio-equipped escort vehicle, in direct radio contact
with the control tower, will accompany the aircraft throughout the towing operation. (T-1)
24.7.7. Engine Operation. As a rule, aircraft will not be towed with engines operating. (T-1)
However, civilian contract air carrier jet engine transport aircraft may be towed or pushed short
distances, e.g., pushing away from hangar or terminal gate, with engines operating IAW
Federal Aviation Administration-approved procedures in operations and maintenance
handbooks. Copies of these procedures will be carried on the aircraft. (T-0) DAF aircraft may
only be pushed back with engines running during non-routine or abnormal operational
requirements using push-back procedures supported by engineering analysis and the aircraft
TO.

24.8. Aircraft Jacking Operations.
24.8.1. Jacks will be:
24.8.1.1. Operated, maintained, inspected, and tested IAW applicable TOs, handbooks or
manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1)
24.8.1.2. Inspected before use to verify lifting capacity, proper functioning of safety locks,
conditions of pins, and general serviceability. (T-1)
24.8.1.3. Inspected with documentation maintained by work section supervisor. (T-1)
24.8.1.4. Removed from service if integrity of the jack is questionable, and the supervisor
will be notified. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

263

24.8.2. Employees will follow specific aircraft TO jacking procedures. (T-1) Non-essential
work stands and equipment will be removed from under the aircraft before jacking. (T-1) If
the aircraft weight-and-balance condition has been affected by removing heavy items, weight
will be added to reestablish the proper center of gravity before jacking. (T-1) Employees will
not remain in aircraft being raised or lowered, unless directed by TO, such as to observe
leveling instruments. TO-prescribed jack pads, jack pad fittings and correct jack screw adapters
and extensions will be installed prior to jacking. (T-1) Chocks will be removed, and brakes
released before the aircraft is raised or lowered. (T-1)
24.8.3. Safety jack stands of suitable capacity or as specified by the manufacturer will be
installed under aircraft wings and tail after the aircraft is jacked and leveled if equipment failure
or an unbalanced condition could occur. (T-1)
24.8.4. Personnel will not pass under, climb, or walk on any portion of the aircraft when the
entire aircraft is supported by jacks, except to support jacking activities or when operationally
necessary to perform maintenance. The area around jacked aircraft will be secured and warning
signs posted. (T-1) Personnel not directly involved in jacking operations or performing
maintenance on jacked aircraft will remain outside the posted area. (T-1) Depot maintenance
facilities may post signs at work area, hangar and flightline entrances in lieu of securing and
signing individual aircraft.
24.8.5. Engines and other major components, such as wing panels and stabilizers, that affect
aircraft center of gravity or are identified as stress areas, will not be changed or removed on
jacked aircraft with landing gear clear of the ramp or floor except IAW aircraft TOs. (T-1)
24.8.6. Outside Jacking. Aircraft may be jacked outside hangars when wind conditions permit.
When aircraft wind velocity specifications are not available, 13 knots (15 miles per hour) will
be used as a safe maximum velocity for outside jacking. (T-1) Jacking will be done on a level
surface. (T-1) High tail surfaces of some aircraft require special consideration when exposed
to even moderate wind. Maintenance employees will be familiar with appropriate aircraft TOs
before jacking. (T-1)
24.8.7. Hangar-dock Jacking. Hydraulic pumping units for aircraft jacks will not be operated
in aircraft hangars unless authorized by the MXG/CC or equivalent, occupational safety
representative and the F&ES Flight or equivalent. (T-1) When authorized, hydraulic pumping
unit design will ensure all electrical equipment, sparking contacts, hot surfaces and other
possible ignition sources ae at least 18 inches above floor level. (T-1) Refer to NFPA 410 and
NFPA 70 for additional guidance. Hydraulic pumping units will be placed at least 25 feet from
the aircraft in an area free of combustible material and the pumping unit engine properly
grounded. (T-1)
24.8.8. Releasing Jacks. Maintenance employees will ensure the area beneath the aircraft is
clear of equipment, cribbing and personnel, and verify the aircraft is ready for lowering before
releasing jack pressure and lowering the aircraft. (T-1) Employees will ensure the landing gear
is completely down and locked in position with all ground locking devices installed. (T-1)
Only employees required to operate jacks and free the struts will remain near the aircraft while
lowering. (T-1) The aircraft will be lowered slowly and evenly to the surface. (T-1) Employees
should exercise care when removing jacks to prevent skin or structure damage. Jacks will be
removed by first turning down the jack screw extension, then depressing the ram into the

264

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
cylinder. (T-1) Personnel will not place any part of their hands on top of the ram to avoid injury
while depressing the ram. (T-1)
24.8.9. Landing Gear Retraction Tests. Landing gear retraction tests will be directed orally,
using an interphone system for communication between a ground observer outside the aircraft
and the landing gear operator. (T-1) Use of hand signals is acceptable if the interphone system
is inoperable, and the ground observer always maintains direct visual contact with both the
wheel well/gear swing areas and the landing gear operator. The ground observer will ensure
the wheel well and gear swing areas are clear of personnel and equipment prior to and during
all testing. (T-1)
24.8.10. Fire Fighting Equipment. Maintenance personnel will ensure suitable portable
firefighting equipment is readily available and properly positioned. (T-1) After aircraft
departure, fire extinguishers used to support that aircraft will be removed from the ramp. (T-1)
Refer to Chapter 6 for additional guidance or contact the F&ES Flight.

24.9. Aircraft Engine Operations.
24.9.1. Follow aircraft TOs and procedures. Refer to AFMAN 11-218 for engine start, run-up
and testing.
24.9.1.1. Personnel near an engine start or run-up will wear, at a minimum, hearing
protection as prescribed by installation BE. (T-1) Eye protection will be required if
conditions exist where flying debris is present, e.g., unimproved/austere runways or aircraft
backing maneuvers. (T-1)
24.9.1.2. The fire hazard is high because fuel vapors may be ignited by exhaust flames.
Rotating propellers are dangerous to personnel and can cause extensive equipment damage.
Except for turboprop aircraft, personnel will not hand-pull the propeller of a recently
operated engine. (T-1) The ignition switch for reciprocating or electric start engines will
be in the OFF position when engines are not operating, and the master battery switch will
be turned off when no longer required. (T-1) Warning: For C-130 aircraft, personnel will
not enter the plane of rotation of the propellers except by walking around the outside
(number 1 or number 4) propeller arcs. Exception: If bleed air is not supplied to the
aircraft, personnel may enter the plane of rotation for preflight inspections and maintenance
actions.
24.9.1.3. Personnel will not stand in line with the stripe that designates the plane of
propeller operation. (T-1) Radio contact with the control tower will be maintained during
engine operation or taxiing or with job control during engine runs when the control tower
is not operating. (T-1) Maintenance Operations Center will be prepared to request F&ES
Flight or medical services assistance, as needed. (T-1)
24.9.1.4. MXG/CCs or equivalent will develop and implement aircraft maintenance
engine pre-run safety checklist that include, at a minimum, review of aircraft and
subsystem serviceability (i.e., inoperable systems with warning tags), maintenance crew
qualifications, FOD check, tool accountability, and actions to be taken by supporting
maintenance personnel in the event of an emergency.
24.9.2. Aircraft Chocks. When aircraft engines are in operation, chocks will be removed with
the utmost caution after the proper signal has been given. (T-1) Employees will approach the

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

265

aircraft from the safest direction, considering location of propellers, jet intakes and exhausts.
(T-1)
24.9.3. Fire Guard. A qualified fire guard (need not be a certified firefighter) will be positioned
by the fire bottle(s) as required by the aircraft TO prior to starting aircraft engines. (T-1) This
individual must remain in standby readiness until all engines are operating, and no fire danger
exists, e.g., crew chief or team leader can release fire guard once engines are started and no
fire danger exists. (T-1) If no fire guard is required by aircraft TO:
24.9.3.1. Aircraft with internal firefighting capability will have a ground observer in
position on interphone to monitor and report any problems, e.g., fire, fuel leaks, aircraft
movement, and will remain in position until released by the individual operating the engine.
(T-1)
24.9.3.2. Aircraft without interphone capability will have a fire guard stationed to observe
the engine start process. (T-1) After starting, the fire guard will remain in clear view of
cockpit personnel and observe aircraft and area during engine operation. (T-1) Warning:
Use extreme caution to ensure all personnel remain clear of propeller arc and/or jet intakes
or exhaust during ground operation. Exception: Aero Club or similar aircraft used by Civil
Air Patrol may be started/operated without a fire guard if fire extinguishers are readily
accessible during engine starts, aircraft maintenance and refueling, and if it does not violate
manufacturer’s operating handbook procedures.
24.9.4. Qualified Personnel. Only personnel trained and certified IAW AFMAN 11-218 will
be authorized to start and run-up aircraft engines. (T-1) The engine run supervisor is
responsible for inspecting the surrounding area to ensure it is clear of equipment, personnel
and materials that might cause or be subject to FOD. Equipment or materials subject to engine
blasts or being blown into other equipment will be repositioned or secured. (T-1) Refer to
DAFI 21-101 for additional information.
24.9.5. Jet Engine Start Observers. A ground crewmember will maintain constant contact by
interphone with the person operating the engine control and will be stationed in front and to
one side of the engine being started. (T-1) On aircraft without interphone capability, a ground
crewmember will be stationed to observe the start and give necessary signals to the person in
the cockpit. (T-1)
24.9.6. Cockpit Checklist. Employees starting, operating, and testing aircraft engines will use
the appropriate cockpit checklist before, during, and after the operation. (T-1)
24.9.7. Jet Engine FOD. Damage caused to jet engines by FOD sucked through inlet ducts can
be reduced by use of inlet duct run-up screens on certain aircraft types. Operational needs may
require installation and removal of screens while engines are running. Ensure engine inlet runup screens and anti-personnel guards are used IAW applicable system TOs. Refer to DAFI 21101 for additional information on FOD prevention and FOD prevention programs.
24.9.8. Hearing Protection Devices. Crew and maintenance personnel will wear approved
hearing protection in noise hazard areas to prevent permanent hearing loss. (T-1) Installation
BE will determine noise hazard areas, protection requirements, and adequacy of hearing
protection. (T-1)
24.9.9. Rotary Wing Aircraft.

266

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.9.9.1. The collective pitch stick will be locked in the full low position during engine
run-up by ground employees. (T-1) Personnel and equipment will be kept clear of rotary
wing aircraft at least a distance equal to its length, to prevent injury and damage if a quick
excessive throttle start swings the tail. (T-1)
24.9.9.2. Main rotor blades will be tracked only with an experienced pilot at the controls.
(T-1) Engine run-up will not be attempted by fewer employees than specified in specific
aircraft TOs. (T-1) Personnel will exercise caution when approaching the plane of rotation
of main rotor blades because they droop at decreased speeds. (T-1)
24.9.9.3. All personnel will remain in full view of the pilot or helicopter operator and keep
a crouched position approaching or leaving a helicopter with rotating blades. (T-1)
Personnel will avoid the area from cockpit or cabin rearward unless authorized by
helicopter operator to work in this location. (T-1) Keep unauthorized personnel at least 50
feet from helicopters with turning rotor blades.
24.9.9.4. Personnel will use caution to stay clear of main and stabilizing rotors when
visibility is reduced by dust or other conditions. (T-1) Protective goggles will be worn by
all personnel and other precautions taken as needed to minimize problems from reduced
visibility. (T-0)
24.9.9.5. Adequate precautions will be taken to protect personnel from flying objects
caused by rotor downwash. (T-1) All loose gear within 100 feet of the area susceptible to
rotor downwash will be secured or removed. (T-1)

24.10. Jet Aircraft Requirements.
24.10.1. General safety standards for conventional powered aircraft also apply to jet aircraft,
and ground handling of jet aircraft involves hazards not common to conventional types. Engine
inlet suction and exhaust blast pose hazards to maintenance, support, and flight crew personnel.
24.10.2. Suction Effect. Jet engine suction can ingest personnel, causing loss of life.
Additionally, ingestion of tools, personal articles, or any form of FOD causes extensive engine
damage. Unless required by TO, crewmembers, maintainers, and other personnel will not
approach closer than 5 feet from the side or rear or 25 feet from the front of engine intakes of
running engines. Personnel may approach multi-engine aircraft, e.g., F-15, from the opposite
direction of the running engine, but will use extreme caution to prevent ingestion. (T-1) All
objects will be removed from in front of or within intakes prior to starting engines. (T-1)
Visiting personnel or observers will be briefed on removing hats and carrying objects near jet
engines. (T-1) Employees will not place hands or fingers on auxiliary air inlet doors as they
operate automatically and may close. (T-1) If open, access doors will be secured or removed
as they may be forced closed by engine suction. (T-1)
24.10.3. Exhaust Blast. Jet engine exhaust blast is hazardous to personnel and property. Local
procedures will be established to prevent personnel, vehicles, and aircraft passing behind
engines operating above idle speed or through hazardous jet blast areas IAW applicable aircraft
TOs. (T-1) A ground or flight crewmember will be stationed alongside the operating engine to
warn personnel and traffic to remain clear at least 25-feet in front and 200-feet to the rear of
the aircraft, depending on the aircraft involved or as prescribed IAW applicable aircraft TOs
and handbooks. (T-1) A blast fence will be installed where needed to protect personnel and

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

267

equipment from exhaust blast. (T-1) Tools, spare parts, and other objects will be removed from
blast areas before any jet engine is started. (T-1)
24.10.4. Ejection Seats and Canopies. Accidental discharge of ejection seats, cockpit
canopies, and jettisonable hatches (e.g., A-10, B-2, and F-16) is hazardous to employees.
Extreme heat or unintentional movement of actuating mechanisms can fire the ejection seat
catapult or canopy remover. The following precautions will reduce accidental seat or canopy
ejections for aircraft on the ground. Note: Anyone entering a cockpit or flight deck equipped
with escape system components will have aircraft cockpit and flight deck familiarization
training prior to entry IAW DAFI 21-101. (T-1) Exception: C-17 Aircraft, Flotation
Equipment Deployment Systems – Electro-Environmental employees with task qualification
training and annual explosive safety training for time change compliance related to Flotation
Equipment Deployment Systems meets the intent of this paragraph, and paragraphs 24.10.4.1
and 24.10.4.2.
24.10.4.1. Personnel will avoid accidental arming and firing when working near ejection
seat catapults and ejection seat or canopy remover controls. (T-1) Exercised caution to
avoid snagging arming, and firing mechanisms when removing crewmembers from
crashed aircraft. (T-1) Cook-off, inadvertent actuation, or residual pressure stored in lines
and tubes of explosive egress components may pose hazards during rescue attempts.
24.10.4.2. Salvage Operations. Explosive Ordnance Disposal personnel will ensure
explosive devices are removed before any aircraft salvage operations begin. (T-1) Only
qualified and certified egress personnel will remove escape and/or egress system explosive
components. (T-1)
24.10.4.3. Transient Alert Checklists. Transient alert organizations will develop local
checklists to ask pilots about explosive egress systems for aircraft not normally processed
through their installations and will follow specific TO procedures for aircraft being
serviced. (T-1)
24.10.5. Sharp Edges. Some jet aircraft control surfaces have sharp edges, personnel will use
caution when working near these edges. (T-1) Suitable protective covers will be installed
during prolonged maintenance periods to protect personnel from these hazards and include
their location. (T-1)
24.10.6. Entering Tail Pipe. Employee entry to evaluate condition of tailpipe, e.g., to inspect
engine turbine wheel assembly, will be coordinated with CSPT IAW Chapter 23. (T-1) The
CSPT will evaluate the tailpipe to include atmospheric testing and determine classification of
the confined space prior to employee entry. (T-0) CSPT determination of confined space
classification will direct employee entry procedures IAW Chapter 23. (T-0)
24.10.7. Handling Hydrazine. Firefighting and transient maintenance personnel may be
exposed to hydrazine. Employees will be trained IAW Chapter 14 of this manual, and AFI
90-821, Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) Program, and TO 00-25-172. (T-0) Training will
be documented IAW DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
24.11. Aircraft Cleaning.
24.11.1. General Requirements. Aircraft will be cleaned IAW MDS-specific TOs. (T-1) To
prevent falls, employees will not climb or walk on wet aircraft surfaces. (T-1) Separate

268

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
elevated work platforms and long-handle brushes will be used to the maximum extent possible.
(T-1) Exercise caution when walking on aircraft TO-designated areas during washing. Locally
designed fall protection systems, such as skylines (horizontal lifelines) will be used. (T-1)
Refer to paragraph 24.16 for additional guidance. Employees will wear PPE identified by the
installation BE OH Assessment. (T-1) Only authorized cleaning agents will be used. (T-0)
Appropriate footwear will be worn to prevent falls from wet surfaces. (T-0) Refer to Chapter
14 for additional PPE guidance and AFI 90-821 for hazard communication requirements.
24.11.2. Aircraft Cleaning Operations.
24.11.2.1. Cleaning Parts. Parts will be cleaned with approved high flashpoint or
nonflammable solvents or compounds whenever possible. (T-1) Do not clean aircraft parts,
hangar floors, equipment, or clothing with gasoline or unauthorized solvents. Operations
requiring low flashpoint solvents will be considered hazardous and performed in a facility
or area compatible with the solvents. (T-1)
24.11.2.2. Flammable liquids will be kept in approved containers, marked to identify
contents, and stored in buildings/areas specifically approved by the F&ES Flight. (T-1)
Refer to Chapter 22 for additional guidance.
24.11.2.3. Wiping cloths, oily waste, and other combustible materials will be disposed of
IAW paragraph 24.11.2.2. (T-1)
24.11.2.4. Maintain proper ventilation and use protective clothing when working with
solvents per installation BE recommendations. (T-1) Refer to AFI 90-821 when handling
and using hazardous materials.
24.11.3. Fire Control. Use Class B extinguishers for flammable solvents. In no case will water
be used. Employees using flammable solvents will be trained and proficient in extinguisher
use. (T-0) Extinguishers will be sized for possible fires; for example, a gallon of spilled solvent
can cover 25 to 50 square feet, but a small extinguisher may only extinguish 2 square feet.
(T-1) The F&ES Flight will be consulted to ensure extinguishers with proper capacity are
obtained. (T-1) Employees will remain vigilant after a fire is extinguished because flammable
solvent may still be present beneath the extinguishing material. (T-1) If an ignition source is
present, the vapors can re-ignite. Safety cans and solvent storage cabinets will have flash
arresters to prevent flame from entering the container. (T-1)

24.12. Aircraft Tire Mounting and Servicing Operations. Refer to TO 4T-1-3, Inspection,
Maintenance Instructions, Storage, and Disposition of Aircraft Tires and Inner Tubes, and 29 CFR
§ 1910.177, Servicing Multi-piece and Single Piece Rim Wheels, for additional guidance on tire
mounting and servicing operations.
24.12.1. Maintenance employees will use tire dollies or mechanical devices when mounting,
servicing, or removing heavy aircraft tires. (T-0) Sufficient personnel are also necessary, in
addition to mechanical aids, to safely handle heavy tires and wheels. Employees will use tire
cage guards or tire inflation equipment to prevent the risk of personal injury and equipment
damage, i.e. over-inflate a tire, use uncalibrated equipment, etc. (T-1) Tire cages will be used
when inflating tires in the shop. (T-0) Caution will be used to avoid over-inflating tires. (T-1)
Only qualified PMEL personnel will adjust and/or calibrate tire inflation equipment. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

269

24.12.2. The supervisor will ensure each employee demonstrates and maintains the ability to
service aircraft wheels safely. (T-0) An employee is considered qualified after completing the
installation or unit’s training program. The supervisor will evaluate each employee’s ability to
perform tasks safely and provide additional training as necessary to ensure each employee
maintains proficiency. (T-0)
24.13. Flightline Vehicle Operations.
24.13.1. General Requirements. Refer to DAFI 24-301, DAFI 13-213, Airfield Driving, and
AFI 31-218, Motor Vehicle Traffic Supervision, for additional guidance and information on
vehicles operated on the flightline, including motorcycles, mopeds, bicycles and tricycles.
24.13.2. Vehicle and Equipment Marking. Vehicles, ground servicing and ground powered
equipment will be marked with reflective materials authorized by appropriate TOs, such as TO
36-1-191 and TO 35-1-3. (T-1) Fire extinguishers and all hazardous obstacles on or adjacent
to the flightline will be marked with reflective materials. (T-1)
24.13.3. Speed Limits. No vehicle, including motorcycles, mopeds, bicycle, or tricycles will
be operated at a speed deemed reasonable and prudent for existing traffic, road, weather
conditions, and posted flightline speed limits. (T-2) Emergency vehicles will not automatically
assume the right of way. (T-2) Commanders and aircraft maintenance commanders are
responsible for ensuring personnel authorized to drive on the flightline are informed of
flightline speed limits. Note: Vehicles responding to Red Balls (emergency flightline
scenarios), exercises and precautionary landings are not authorized to exceed posted flightline
speed limits.
24.13.4. Entering or Leaving Flightline Driving Areas. Entering or Leaving Flightline Driving
Areas. All vehicles, except emergency and alert vehicles responding to an alert or emergency,
shall stop prior to entering the flightline and accomplish a FOD check. (T-2) Refer to DAFI
13-213, Chapter 4, for additional information. FOD check signs shall be positioned at every
entrance to ramp areas and flightline. (T-3) Traffic lanes on aircraft parking ramps are normally
to the right of the aircraft. Unless it is prevented by local procedures, traffic flow on aircraft
parking ramps will be parallel to the noses of parked aircraft with the driver side toward the
aircraft. (T-1) Vehicles will not be driven diagonally across the parking ramp, but at 90-degree
angles to the driving lanes. (T-1)
24.13.5. Vehicle Parking. Vehicles will not be backed or parked within 25 feet of any aircraft,
unless authorized for operations such as loading or unloading, servicing, or towing. (T-1) A
spotter will be posted when backing a vehicle towards an aircraft. (T-1) Prepositioned wheel
chocks will be used to prevent vehicles backing into aircraft. (T-1) Refer to paragraph
30.3.3.2 for additional backing/spotter guidance.
24.13.5.1. Unattended vehicles will be parked with the driver’s side facing the aircraft and
so it will not interfere with aircraft being towed or taxied. (T-1) Ignition will be turned off;
keys left in the ignition; and the gear lever put in reverse gear for manual transmissions,
and in ‘park’ for automatic transmissions. (T-1) All vehicles parked and left unattended
will have brakes set or chocks placed in front of and behind a rear wheel, or one chock
placed between the tandem wheels of dual (tandem) axle vehicles. (T-1)
24.13.5.1.1. Only alert and emergency vehicles responding to an alert or emergency
are exempt from these requirements. IAW AFI 24-302, the installation/delta

270

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
commander may authorize operators to leave vehicles idling subject to weather
conditions. With approval from the MAJCOM/FLDCOM, the commander may permit
leaving the vehicle running while unattended. Note: AGE towing vehicles may be
placed in “park” (neutral for manual transmissions) with parking brake set and engine
left running during equipment hitching and unhitching operations, and during FOD
checks.
24.13.5.1.2. To conduct a rolling FOD check, the vehicle operator must enter the
vehicle and “close ALL doors” prior to placing the vehicle in drive to rotate tires the
minimum distance necessary to finish the FOD check. (T-1) AGE towing vehicles must
be turned off when the driver seat is vacated for any other purpose. (T-1)
24.13.5.2. Vehicles will not be parked or driven less than 25-feet in front or 200-feet
behind aircraft with running engines, unless prescribed in applicable aircraft handbooks.
Vehicles parked next to aircraft will be clear of wingtips and clearly visible to personnel in
cockpit. (T-1)
24.13.6. Passengers in Vehicle. Passengers will be limited to the designed seating capacity of
the vehicle during routine operations. (T-1) This includes pickup trucks and cargo vans with
passenger seats as well as sedans, station-wagons, and buses. Passengers will use available seat
belts when the vehicle is in motion. (T-1) If deemed operationally necessary by the unit
commander to ride in the bed of pickup trucks, occupants will sit only on the floor, not on
wheel wells, with their backs against the cab and remain seated while vehicle is moving. (T-1)
Passengers in vans will remain seated, keep their arms and legs within the vehicle body, and
keep side doors closed while the van is moving. (T-1) Passengers will not ride in doorways or
sit on engine covers in metro vans/step vans. Note: Personnel jumping or descending from the
bed of pickup and stake bed trucks have caused many injuries and the practice of riding in the
bed of these type vehicles is highly discouraged.
24.13.7. Restricted Visibility or Night Operations.
24.13.7.1. Flashing or parking lights will be used at night when vehicles are temporarily
parked on any part of the aircraft ramp. (T-1) This does not apply to vehicles parked in a
designated parking area. Note: Due to possible battery and alternator concerns,
MAJCOMs and FLDCOMs may substitute this requirement by adding portable flashing
lights.
24.13.7.2. Refueling and explosive loaded (laden) vehicles will not be operated when
visibility is less than 300 feet unless directed by the base commander.
24.13.7.3. PMVs and GMVs, except emergency and alert vehicles, will not be operated on
the flightline with visibility less than 100 feet. A walking guide with a flashing or
luminescent wand will be used during emergency movement of alert vehicles when
visibility is under 50 feet. (T-1) Vehicle operators will ensure headlights do not point
toward taxiing aircraft or towing operations to prevent blinding aircraft or tow vehicle
operators. (T-1)
24.13.8. Control Tower Signs. Refer to DAFI 13-213 for information.
24.13.9. Follow Me Vehicles. Follow Me vehicles for guiding aircraft will be equipped with
signs, easily visible at night, reading “Stop” and “Follow Me”. (T-1) They will have two-way

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

271

radio communication on control tower frequencies. (T-1) When approaching the parking spot,
the Follow Me vehicle operator should illuminate the “Stop” signal, move the vehicle from
the intended path of aircraft travel, and position it laterally – clear of the aircraft wingtip. The
marshaller, who may be the vehicle operator, will guide the aircraft to the parking spot using
approved marshaling signals. (T-1) Follow Me vehicles may exceed the 15 MPH flightline
speed limit when necessary to accommodate the safe taxiing speed of aircraft. Tugs will not
be used as Follow Me vehicles. (T-1)
24.13.10. Forklift and Hi-Lift Truck Operations Around Aircraft. Forklifts and hi-lift trucks
are used to move cargo to/from aircraft and for raising/lowering cargo/equipment between
ground and aircraft. Only licensed drives will operate forklifts and hi-lift trucks. (T-1) Training
and licensing will be IAW DAFI 24-301. (T-1) Forklift or hi-lift truck operators will not exceed
10 MPH on ramps or 5 MPH within 50 feet of any aircraft. The responsibility to provide hilift truck operators special training falls on the owning organization. (T-1) Refer to Chapter
12, Material Handling Equipment, and DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201 for additional
guidance.
24.13.10.1. A spotter will assist operators in determining safe clearances when
maneuvering close to aircraft. (T-1) Bumper blocks or vehicle chocks placed on ramps will
be used to prevent backing into aircraft. (T-1) Spotters will assist operators when necessary
to back the vehicle. (T-1) The driver and spotter will be able to always communicate. (T-1)
The spotter will use luminous wands or flashlights to relay signals to the hi-lift operator
during the night unless supplemental lighting (light carts) is used. (T-1)
24.13.10.2. Spotters will be used any time the cargo bed is raised or lowered. (T-1) Forklift
carriage operation (e.g., raise/lower, tilt, side shift) is prohibited while forklift is in motion.
Forklifts will never be driven under an aircraft except when required by the aircraft
involved. (T-1)
24.13.10.3. When travelling long distances and/or when carrying bulky loads, the forklift
will be driven in reverse as the operator’s field of vision is less obstructed. (T-1) Forks of
parked forklifts will be lowered flat on the ground to prevent injury to personnel working
or walking in the area. (T-1) Parked and unattended forklifts will have operating levers
placed in neutral, ignition switched off and handbrake set. (T-1)
24.13.11. Hand Pallet Trucks Inside Aircraft. Hand pallet trucks are hydraulically operated
material handling devices used for moving heavy objects inside aircraft. Since hand pallet
trucks do not have brakes, at least two persons will operate this piece of equipment. (T-1) 1inch by 12- inch shoring or equivalent protection will be used to prevent floor damage when
moving extremely heavy loads inside aircraft. (T-1) Care should be taken to avoid damaging
aircraft ceilings and sidewalls.
24.13.12. Tractor Operations. All tractor-trailer combinations will be equipped with lights
when operating at night or during low visibility. (T-1) Signs reading “Slow Moving Vehicle”
or the triangular slow moving vehicle emblem will be displayed on these vehicles. (T-1)
Passengers will not be allowed to ride on tractors unless adequate seats are installed. (T-1) No
more than four trailers, loaded or empty, will be pulled by any tractor. (T-1) To avoid
jackknifing, trailer trains will be arranged with the most heavily loaded trailer next to the
towing vehicle, the next heaviest second in line, etc. (T-1) Exception: Six A/M- 3H-6
palletized cargo trailers may be moved behind one prime mover.

272

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.13.13. Deicing Operations. Conduct deicing operations IAW TO 42C-1-2, Anti-icing,
Deicing and Defrosting of Parked Aircraft, and aircraft-specific deicing TO guidance. Only
licensed drivers will operate deicing trucks. (T-1)

24.14. Aircraft Hangar Operations.
24.14.1. Hangaring Aircraft. The MXG/CC or equivalent will ensure detailed local operating
procedures for hangaring aircraft, including safety precautions, are prepared, and coordinated
with the Occupational Safety office and F&ES Flight. (T-1) Include Weapons Safety Office
coordination if hangaring weapons-loaded aircraft.
24.14.2. Aircraft Hangar, Shelter, and Dock Facility Guidance. The Occupational Safety
office, F&ES Flight, and installation BE will evaluate facilities, equipment, processes, etc.,
and provide recommendations to ensure safety, fire, and health hazards are properly mitigated
and/or controlled, and complied with required standards, codes, etc. (T-1) Refer to NFPA 70,
Article 513, Standard on Aircraft Hangars, NFPA 409, Standard on Aircraft Hangars, NFPA
410, UFC 3-600-01, and associated engineering technical letters (ETLs) for detailed design
guidance. Note 1: ETLs supplement UFC 3-600-01 and compliance with ETLs is required per
AFI 32-1023, Designing and Constructing Military Construction Projects. Note 2: ETLs take
precedence in conflicts with national fire codes, building codes or UFC 3-600-01.
24.14.2.1. Fixed electrical equipment approved for Class I, Division 1 locations may be
used in Class I, Division 2 locations of the same class and compatibility group. Equipment
not meeting these guidelines will be de-energized or disabled and locked out until special
operations are complete and hazardous conditions permanently eliminated. (T-1) Refer to
paragraph 24.3.3.1 for additional guidance.
24.14.2.2. Keep portable electrical equipment out of hangars during special hazardous
procedures unless it meets requirements in paragraph 24.14.2.1 and is marked
accordingly.
24.14.2.3. Install barriers such as chains and fencing to restrict personnel from hazardous
areas. Ensure mechanical safeguards for hangar, nose dock, and shelter doors are in place
such as installing limit switches and doorstops where necessary to limit unnecessary door
travel. (T-1) Doors not in compliance will be assigned a RAC by the occupational safety
office. (T-1) A JHA will be performed, and appropriate controls put in place until doors
are compliant. (T-1) Establish a clear zone, e.g., five feet if space allows, around all
hazardous areas. (T-1)
24.14.2.3.1. Hazardous areas include crush and pinch points between structural beams
and hangar doors.
24.14.2.3.2. Highlight hazardous areas such as crush and pinch points that cannot be
eliminated by engineering controls or mechanical safeguards with colored paint and
signs. Clear zones will be marked with solid yellow or yellow and black stripes. (T-2)
24.14.2.4. Safety signs will be printed in English and the predominant language of nonEnglish reading employees. (T-0) Established symbols should also be considered.
Employees unable to read posted signs will be informed of hazardous areas and instructions
printed on the signs. (T-0) Note: For overseas locations with host-nation employees, the
maintenance group or squadron commander should consider a training class on safety signs

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

273

and symbols for host-nation employees. All signage will meet the design requirements
found in Chapter 17, Mishap Prevention Safety Signs, Tags and Labeling. (T-1)
24.14.3. Training. Employees who routinely work in hangars or require access through hangar
doors will receive hangar door awareness Training, both an initial training and refresher
training for when personnel who fail to demonstrate appropriate level of knowledge. (T-1)
Training courses will include, at a minimum, door hazards, emergency procedures, including
manual door operation, and safe operation. (T-1)
24.14.4. Operating Condition. Hangar doors will be maintained in safe working condition.
(T-1) Door paths will be free of obstructions with door tracks and trackways in good condition
and free of debris. (T-1) A visual inspection will be made prior to operation and discrepancies
corrected before operation. (T-1) Hangar doors will be on a preventive maintenance schedule
and only qualified employees will perform hangar door maintenance. (T-1)
24.14.4.1. Hangar doors will be fully opened, doors not completely closed must have an
opening of at least 10 feet to prevent injury to personnel. (T-1)
24.14.4.2. Personnel will not cross the hangar door threshold while hangar doors are in
operation. (T-1)
24.14.5. Door Pockets. Hangar door pockets and recessed areas will not be used for storage
and will be adequately illuminated so the door operator can see any obstructions or personnel
in the door path. (T-1) Door pockets will be checked prior to movement of hangar doors to
ensure the pockets are free of personnel or equipment. (T-1)
24.14.6. Alarm/Warning Lights. To prevent confusion, powered hangar doors will have an
alarm device with a sound distinguishable from the facility fire alarm and audible above normal
noise levels. (T-1) The warning device will automatically signal at least five seconds before
any door section movement, continuously while the door is operated and will reset immediately
after movement stops. (T-1) Additionally, warning lights are required in high noise areas if the
audible alarm is difficult to hear but use caution to avoid stroboscopic effects. (T-1)
24.14.7. Operational Checkout of Doors and Safety Features. Facility managers will conduct
monthly inspections of hangar door operational and safety features. (T-1) Installation CE and
Airfield Management will be notified immediately of any discrepancies. (T-1)
24.14.8. Roll-up Doors. Refer to paragraph 2.7 for roll up door requirements.
24.14.9. Clear Space Requirements Around Hangars. Maintain a clear zone of at least 50 feet
around all sides of a hangar, shelter, or nose dock. Do not store or park aircraft, position
concentrations of combustible materials or erect buildings of any kind within the clear zone.
Refer to NFPA 409 for additional guidance.
24.14.10. Heaters. Combustion heaters installed in aircraft will not be operated in a hangar.
Portable engines and cabin heaters will be removed and stored immediately after each use or
when no longer required. (T-1) Engine and shelter heaters will be placed as far from the aircraft
as ducting permits. (T-1) No portable heater will be left operating unattended. (T-1) Only
heaters approved for operation inside hangars and that do not give off unacceptable levels of
hazardous gases will be used. (T-1) Heaters and the processes for which they are used will be
evaluated by installation BE to ensure adequate ventilation is provided and that health threats

274

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
(e.g., CO) are effectively controlled (e.g., CO alarms are installed) to prevent harmful
exposures. (T-1)
24.14.11. Aircraft Emergency Removal. The aircraft maintenance officer, designated by the
MXG/CC or equivalent, will establish emergency procedures to remove aircraft from hangars
in case of fire, severe weather, or other hazards. (T-1) Employees will be trained on emergency
procedures to eliminate confusion and ensure timely evacuation of aircraft. (T-1) If normal
towing method (nose gear) is not available, snatch blocks and/or harnesses laid out on hangar
floors will be attached to the aircraft when in-progress work permits. (T-1)
24.14.12. Aircraft will only be fueled or defueled in facilities approved through a SSEA to the
current weapons system and facility configuration IAW instructions in TO 00-25-172. (T-1)
Documentation of SSEA, other than Tactical Air Base Hardened Aircraft shelters, modified
Tactical Air Base Hardened Aircraft, 2nd and 3rd generation aircraft shelters, flow-through
revetments and facilities listed in TO 00-25-172 will be maintained by the appropriate
maintenance unit. (T-1) Aircraft fueling and fueling operations in aircraft fuel system
maintenance (fuel cell repair) facilities will not be allowed unless the facility is in full
compliance with requirements of TO 1-1-3 and/or UFC 4-211-01, Aircraft Maintenance
Hangars. (T-1)

24.15. Aircraft Shop and Flightline Maintenance Operations.
24.15.1. General Requirements. Note: Refer to Chapter 7, Walking-Working Surfaces, and
Chapter 11, Hand Tools, Portable Power Tools, and Machinery, for additional guidance on
machine safeguarding and shop layout.
24.15.1.1. Tool Safety. Use of personally owned hand and power tools is prohibited. (T-1)
Refer to TO 32-1-101 and TO 32-1-2, Use of Hand Tools (International Business
Machines), and paragraphs 14.15.1.2.1 and 24.15.1.2.2 for battery-powered tool use.
24.15.1.2. Battery-Powered Screwdrivers and Drills. Cordless drills and screwdrivers are
authorized for use outdoors and indoors during all periods of aircraft maintenance on JP-8
or other high flashpoint fueled aircraft, with the following restrictions:
24.15.1.2.1. Battery-powered screwdrivers and drills that are intrinsically safe or listed
for use in Class 1, Division 1 or 2 areas (may be used in listed areas).
24.15.1.2.2. Tool battery exchanges and recharging will not be conducted in Class 1,
Division 1 or 2 hazard areas.
24.15.1.3. Compressed Gases. Refer to TO 42B-5-1-2, Gas Cylinders (Storage Type) –
Use, Handling, and Maintenance, for additional guidance on compressed gases.
24.15.1.4. Occupational Health. Aircraft maintenance operations on the flightline, in
shops, aircraft shelters, hangars and maintenance facilities pose chemical and physical
hazards. These hazards will be evaluated, the level of risk established, and controls
incorporated where necessary. (T-1) Installation BE performs a unit OH Assessment
routinely at least every three years and HRAs upon request when process or
chemical/physical hazard changes occur.
24.15.2. Aircraft Electrical System Maintenance. Electrical systems will be de-energized
whenever possible and approved procedures strictly followed. (T-1) If the approved procedure
requires work on an energized circuit, approved technical data or manufacturer’s instructions

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

275

and 29 CFR § 1910.333, Selection and Use of Work Practices, will be followed and other
maintenance personnel informed of the actions. (T-0) If work is done on energized circuits of
50 volts or more, arc flash protection must be worn. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 8.17.2.2 for
additional guidance.
24.15.2.1. For on-equipment aircraft maintenance, the AFTO Form 492, MX Warning
Tag, will be used to flag a condition that could cause damage or injury if ignored. (T-1)
Warning tags will be used during maintenance actions as required by applicable technical
data and/or local procedures. (T-1) Refer to DAFI 21-101 for additional guidance. Do not
use the AF Form 979, for on-equipment aircraft maintenance. The AF Form 979 or
commercial equivalent will only be used when an immediate hazard exists and specific
precautions are required to protect personnel or property or as required by TOs, instructions
or other-directed requirements. (T-1) Tags will be placed on damaged equipment to be
taken out of service and sent to the repair shop. To reduce the probability of internal
electrical shorts, aircraft wiring, including wire bundles, will be secured to prevent scraping
and chafing. (T-1) Electrical components will be bench-tested in a properly designed shop.
(T-1) Testing of electrical systems or equipment while installed on the aircraft will be held
to a minimum.
24.15.2.2. If a circuit breaker opens (pops) during maintenance on an aircraft, the popped
circuit breaker will not be reset without following specific guidance in the applicable
aircraft system TO and the cause is determined. (T-1) An unexpected, popped circuit
breaker may result from an overload or ground fault.
24.15.2.3. Electrical components and systems will be de-energized and isolated from other
energized circuits or power sources during cleaning operations. (T-1)
24.15.3. Aircraft Painting and Paint Removal Operations. There are many safety and health
hazards associated with painting and paint removal operations. Spray painting an entire aircraft
is permitted only in hangars designed for the purpose. Refer to Chapter 27, NFPA 410 and
NFPA 33, Standard for Spray Application Using Flammable or Combustible Materials, for
additional information.
24.15.4. Aerospace Ground Equipment (AGE). Operators will be familiar with OIs for
equipment they use, including emergency shutdown, other precautionary measures, and use of
fire extinguishers. (T-1) Operators will review maintenance records and inspect equipment for
leaks, damage, or malfunction before operation. (T-1) AGE equipment will not be left
unattended while running, unless specifically designed for unattended operation. (T-1) Contact
the AGE Maintenance Shop for further guidance. Warning: Personnel will not climb inside
combustion powered AGE (e.g., light carts) as the enclosure may contain fatal levels of
hazardous gases. (T-1)
24.15.5. Communication and Navigation Equipment Repair. Repair communication and
electronic equipment on an approved shop bench IAW TO or manufacturer’s instructions. Do
not operate, test, or check radar or high frequency radio transmitting equipment with poweron during aircraft fueling/defueling, fuel cell/integral tank repair activities or when hazardous
vapors may be present. No radar emissions are allowed in hangars due to personnel hazards or
inadvertent activation of facility warning and fire protection systems. See AFI 48-109 for
additional information.

276

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
24.15.6. Flight Control Systems. Unexpected movement of flight controls may cause severe
injuries. Maintenance personnel will be trained and aware of dangers with flight control system
operation. (T-1) Flight control operation will be directed orally by interphone or after visual
assurance from a person outside the aircraft that flight control surfaces and movement areas
are clear. (T-1) All maintenance activities will be coordinated, and TO procedures applied to
the flight controls to prevent movement when employees are exposed to potential injury. (T-1)
Local procedures will be developed to address work shift changes. (T-1)
24.15.7. Aircraft Hydraulic Systems. Employees will ensure pressures are relieved prior to
disassembling any hydraulic lines or system components. (T-1) Control(s) that could injure
personnel or damage equipment will be tagged out IAW TO procedures and/or local
procedures while maintenance activities are in progress. (T-1) Proper PPE, such as goggles,
will be worn. (T-0) Hydraulic spills will be cleaned up immediately to prevent slips and falls.
(T-1)
24.15.8. Explosives. Storing, handling, and using explosives will be IAW specific TOs and
DESR 6055.09_DAFMAN 91-201. (T-1)

24.16. Fall Protection Requirements. MXG/CCs or designated representative must ensure safe
DAF maintenance operations IAW this chapter and Chapter 13. (T-0) Flightline and hangar
operations must include an appropriate level of fall protection commensurate with DAF mission
requirements. (T-0) To ensure consistency across DAF operations, AFMC and the lead
MAJCOM/FLDCOM for the MDS will work together to ensure development of standardized fall
protection requirements and procedures are published in the applicable weapon system TOs.
Operations are termed as military unique and nonmilitary unique, these terms are discussed below.
24.16.1. Military-Unique. Military-unique operations are specifically defined in DAFI 91202, paragraph 1.8.1. The determination of military-unique does not allow for safety
requirements to be ignored, but to allow for deviations that protect employees while
accomplishing the mission. For example, during deployed operations where compliance with
all fall protection requirements is not possible, a Fall Hazard Survey (refer to Chapter 13 for
additional information) will be used to evaluate the hazards and plan the appropriate
procedures to best protect employees while addressing the limitations due to location and
mission requirements to best mitigate the risks for employees falling while working at height.
24.16.2. Nonmilitary-Unique. Nonmilitary-unique operations refer to most day-to-day
operations as defined in DAFI 91-202, paragraph 1.8.2.
24.16.3. Given the diversity and complexity of DAF operations, it is impossible to address
every situation in this manual. However, the intent is reasonably clear that commanders must
employ a balance between safety and mission objectives that ensure mission success when
regarding military uniqueness. Decisions that are based upon sound RM principles, and the
DoD guidance provided, are very likely to be defendable and correct. Working with the local
safety staff is the best way to receive recommendations that will assist commanders to balance
safety and mission accomplishment objectives. (T-1) Organizations should discuss fall hazard
surveys with the applicable TO writer or Aircraft Item Manager for their airframe, then involve
organizational/appropriate safety office. Note: For Air Logistics Center (ALC) Depots, a
standardized Process Order integrated into the task may be used in lieu of a fall hazard survey.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

277

24.16.4. Types of Fall Protection. Fall hazards from aircraft walking and working surfaces are
controlled through application of RM at command and local levels to determine which fall
protection method offers optimal employee safety for the tasks performed. The maximum use
of maintenance stands and work platforms, whenever possible, will reduce exposure and risk.
Whenever it becomes necessary to perform required tasks where an employee can fall four feet
or more, fall protection will be used. (T-0)
24.16.4.1. Passive. Passive fall protection is the most preferred means of protecting
employees from falls. Passive systems need not be directly connected to the employee to
prevent an employee from falling. These systems include guardrails, work stands and
platforms (including aircraft maintenance stands, e.g., B-1, B-4, B-5), nets and other
devices. If passive fall protection is not feasible, active fall protection will be the next
option considered. (T-0)
24.16.4.2. Active. Active fall protection, such as positioning devices, fall restraint
systems, ladder climbing devices and PFAS, require the employee to wear a full body
harness and components to attach to an anchorage or lifeline. Fall restraint systems prevent
an employee from falling off unprotected sides and edges by restricting the employee’s
range of movement while a PFAS stops an employee’s fall off unprotected sides and edges,
before striking the next level. Positioning systems allow employees to work from vertical
surfaces with both hands free.
24.16.4.2.1. PFAS allow an employee to fall, but limits arresting loads to generally
safe levels and prevents the employee from hitting the next level. Refer to Chapter 13
and 29 CFR § 1910.140, Personal Fall Arrest Systems, for additional guidance on
PFAS. (Caution: Shock absorbing lanyards will not be used in conjunction with selfretracting lifelines, fall limiters, retractable web lanyards or decelerator packs (inertial
reel type lifelines) devices. Multi-use or combinations of use can result in two falls and
could hinder and/or prevent self-rescue or external rescue.) (Warning: Hanging in a
harness for an extended period can be fatal.) Supervisors will ensure employees using
a personal fall arrest system can be promptly rescued or be able to self-rescue should a
fall occur. (T-0) Rescue procedures will be developed prior to using a fall arrest system.
(T-0)
24.16.4.2.2. Positioning and Restraint Systems. Fall restraint systems restrict an
employee’s range of movement so the individual cannot get into a position that would
allow a fall. Positioning systems allow an employee to be supported on an elevated
vertical/inclined surface and body support is hands free. Anchorage connecting
point(s), work positioning and restraint lanyards for each employee will have minimal
support strength of 5,000 pounds (22.2 kN). (T-0) Secure the employee to an anchor
using a lanyard short enough to prevent the employee’s center of gravity from reaching
the fall hazard.
24.16.5. Mobile Work Platforms. Refer to Chapter 16 for additional guidance.
24.17. Safety in Flightline Contractor Operations.
24.17.1. Contractor operations may create hazardous working conditions for DAF personnel.
To ensure contracts and activities of contractors performing a task or service for the DAF does
not subject DAF personnel or resources to hazards, the Occupational Safety office, F&ES

278

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Flight, Airfield Management, and installation BE will review requirement documents prior to
submission to Contracting office. (T-1) Appropriate safety, fire, and health requirements will
be included in contract specifications. (T-1) This is normally accomplished by including
appropriate Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) provisions or clauses in the contract.
Occupational Safety office, F&ES Flight, Airfield Management, and installation BE will assist
the contracting officer and using organization establish appropriate requirements. (T-1) If a
contractor procedure or condition requires correction, action will be initiated through the
Contracting Officer. (T-1) Note: Contractors are responsible for compliance with all OSHA
requirements, as applicable, while performing work on military installations.
24.17.2. Construction Contracts. The contracting officer will call a pre-construction
conference before the contractor begins construction on a DAF installation. (T-1) Occupational
Safety office, Flight Safety Office, F&ES Flight, Airfield Management, installation BE, the
using organization, installation CE, contractor representatives, and other affected agencies, as
appropriate, will be present. (T-1) The Contracting Officer will ensure the contractor knows
all necessary DAF safety requirements associated with the contract. (T-1) Note: This guidance
does not apply to US Army Corps of Engineers contracts.
24.17.3. Non-Construction Contracts. Most non-construction contracts on DAF installations
involve persons or companies providing goods and services to base personnel and activities.
During requirement development, requiring activity coordination with the Occupational Safety
office, F&ES Flight, Airfield Management, installation BE, contract representatives, using
organization, and other affected organizations, as appropriate, is necessary to assure
appropriate safety, fire prevention, and health requirements are included in the contract. Prior
to contract renewal, Occupational Safety office, F&ES Flight, Airfield Management,
installation BE, contract representatives and the using organization will review the contract to
ensure its continued adequacy. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

279
Chapter 25

CIVIL ENGINEERING
25.1. Scope. Installation CE is typically involved in numerous planning, maintenance and fire
prevention activities that have a high risk for mishaps. Safe management of the workforce,
inclusion of safety features (most economical during the identification, planning, or design phases)
and the correction of facility and grounds hazards are instrumental in preventing mishaps.
Training, safe work practices, correct PPE, proper tools, human factors, and risk management
should all be addressed and accomplished prior to starting tasks.
25.1.1. Interruption of services, injury to employees, and equipment damage can occur when
electrical, gas, and steam lines are broken during digging operations. Ensure DAF Form 103,
Base Civil Engineering Work Clearance Request, is obtained prior to digging. (T-1)
25.1.2. The use of propane, gasoline, or diesel-powered equipment, e.g., forklifts and
materials handling carts, can produce hazardous gases. Employee exposure to hazardous gases
may be excessive if such operations are conducted in low ceiling, enclosed, or poorly ventilated
areas. Proper ventilation will always be used. (T-0)
25.1.3. Asbestos fibers can cause lung diseases such as asbestosis and cancer of the lung. If
asbestos is used or already in place, safe handling procedures will be IAW 29 CFR §
1910.1001, Asbestos. (T-0) Employees will not be exposed to unsafe levels of airborne
asbestos. (T-0) Consult installation BE prior to all planned asbestos projects. Immediately
notify installation BE and installation CE if asbestos is suspected or confirmed during a project.
Employees will not resume work until receiving proper guidance from installation BE. (T-1)
Refer to DAFMAN 48-146, Occupational Health Program Management, AFI 32-1001, Civil
Engineer Operations, 15 U.S.C. Chapter 53, Toxic Substances Control Act, 42 U.S.C.
Chapter 85, Air Pollution Prevention and Control, and 29 U.S.C. Chapter 15, Occupational
Safety and Health Act, for additional guidance.
