the occupational safety office for additional guidance.
27.9.6. Flame-Producing Devices. Using or carrying matches, lighters, or other spark or
flame-producing devices in or adjacent to paint spray booths is prohibited.
27.9.7. Containers. Containers supplying spray nozzles will be a closed type or provided with
metal covers, which will be kept closed. (T-0) Original shipping containers will not be
subjected to air pressure above atmospheric for supplying spray nozzles. (T-0) Containers
under pressure exceeding atmospheric pressure will be designed and approved for such use,
will be provided with a visible pressure gauge, and will be provided with both a relief valve
and a shutoff valve. (T-0)
27.9.8. Hoses and Couplings. Pressure hoses and couplings will be regularly inspected for
condition and will be replaced, as needed. (T-0) When positive displacement pumps are used,
a relief valve will be installed in the discharge line to prevent overpressure. (T-0)
27.10. Compressed Air Paint Spraying.
27.10.1. On compressed air spraying equipment, a pressure regulator valve will be installed
in the air line between the compressor and painting equipment. (T-0) Refer to the compressor
manufacturer’s operating instructions for proper configuration, operation, and pressure
adjustments for the type of paint being used. Warning: Compressed air will not be directed
toward a fellow employee. (T-0)
27.10.2. Air Hoses. Inspect hoses prior to each shift to ensure proper connection at both ends
and condition of hose. (T-0) Air hoses will not be connected to a pipe outlet unless the loose
end is secured, since a loose hose connected to a compressed air outlet will whip. (T-0) Hose
couplings must be incompatible with airline respiratory protection hoses and/or respirators.
(T-0) Note: Quick release fittings or similar devices that prevent flow or air when hose is not
attached to a tool or other equipment, may be used in place of securing the loose end of an air
hose.
27.10.3. Any hose showing signs of wear, deterioration, leakage, or weakness will be removed
from service and repaired or replaced. (T-0)
27.10.4. Portable Paint Spray Equipment.
27.10.4.1. Description. Equipment usually consists of an air compressor, paint spray gun,
and hose. The paint reservoir on most portable spray guns holds one quart of fluid or less.
When a considerable amount of paint is to be applied, a 2-1/2 or 5-gallon pressure tank is
usually employed.
27.10.4.2. Compressor. The air compressor will be equipped with an ASME-rated air tank,
a visible pressure gauge on the tank, a pressure reducer with its own gauge, a guard fully
enclosing the drive belt and pulleys, and a pressure-limiting switch to shut down the
compressor when the system’s working pressure has been reached. (T-0) The equipment
will be securely mounted on a wheeled carriage for portability. (T-1) For interior painting,
where the compressor will be located inside the facility, only electric motor-driven
equipment will be used. (T-0)

308

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
27.10.4.3. Overpressure Protection. When separate paint pressure tanks are used, they will
be equipped with a gauge and a relief valve to prevent overpressure. (T-0) Hoses will be
rated for the maximum working pressure of the system. (T-0)
27.10.5. Maintenance.
27.10.5.1. A preventive maintenance program covering periodic inspection and testing of
all components will be implemented by all organizations using this equipment. (T-1)
27.10.5.2. Compressors, hoses, paint pressure tanks, and spray guns will be stored in areas
designated and approved by F&ES Flight. (T-1)
27.10.5.3. All paint pressure tanks and spray guns will be cleaned after use and before the
equipment is stored. (T-1) If solvents are used in the cleaning process, provisions of
Chapter 22 will be followed. (T-1)
27.10.5.4. Respiratory Protection. If the process is not already evaluated, contact
installation BE for an HRA. Respiratory protective devices will meet the requirements of
AFI 48-137. (T-1)

27.11. Drying Ovens.
27.11.1. Overview. Most drying ovens use gas or electricity to heat air, which is blown across
the components being dried, although infrared and microwave drying ovens are also used.
Refer to the SDS for the specific equipment to use.
27.11.2. Fire Prevention. When used to dry flammable finishes, the ovens must meet or exceed
NFPA 86, Standard for Ovens and Furnaces. (T-0) Avoid using spray booths or rooms for
drying purposes. Raising the temperature of a spray booth increases the chance of spontaneous
combustion of paint residues. Do not install drying ovens in a spraying area if the oven contains
an open flame or a spark-producing element. Never conduct spraying operations in proximity
to an operating drying oven.
27.11.3. Ventilation. Adequately vent the exhaust air from the oven. The oven will have its
own ventilation system. (T-1) If spray booth ventilation systems are utilized, the oven vent
will enter the system downstream from filters and baffles. (T-1) In addition, an interlock will
be installed which will automatically start the blower when the oven is turned on and
automatically shut down the oven if the blower stops operating, either by accident or
intentionally. (T-1)
27.11.4. Electrical Wiring. Make sure electrical wiring for drying ovens and associated
equipment conforms to NFPA 70. (T-0)
27.11.5. Grounding. Ensure metal parts of drying ovens are bonded and grounded. (T-0)
27.11.6. Warning Signs. Post signs in the vicinity of operating drying ovens to warn of heat
and to caution against conducting spraying operations. (T-0)
27.12. Spray Painting of Aircraft.
27.12.1. Aircraft Painting. Spray painting of an entire aircraft is permitted only in hangars
specifically designed for this purpose. (T-1)
27.12.2. Fire Prevention. When painting aircraft, the aircraft will be isolated and adequate fire
prevention measures will be taken. (T-1) Solvents used for cleaning operations will have

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

309

flashpoints at or above 100° F. (T-1) The aircraft will be properly grounded, and all sources of
ignition removed from the area. (T-0) All electrical devices and equipment will be of an
approved explosion-proof type. (T-0) Except when essential to perform concurrent operations,
all aircraft electrical systems will be de-energized during cleaning, painting, and paint removal.
(T-0) Smoking is prohibited. (T-0) To prevent the accumulation of static charges, all materials,
or objects will be effectively grounded. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 6 for additional guidance.
27.12.3. Ventilation. Ventilation will be provided, and installation BE will periodically check
the adequacy of ventilation IAW paragraph 27.5.7. (T-1)
27.12.4. Housekeeping. All spraying areas will be kept free from the accumulation of deposits
of combustible residues. (T-0) Cleaning will be conducted daily. (T-1) Scraping tools will be
non- sparking. (T-0) Roof trusses, light fixtures and other overhead equipment will be checked
for accumulation of over-spray at least annually. (T-1) Rags or waste soiled with sprayed
materials will be deposited in approved self-closing metal waste containers immediately after
use. (T-0) IAW DAFMAN 32-7002 and 29 CFR § 1910.107(g)(3), the contents of waste
containers will be disposed of at the end of each shift or at least once daily. (T-0)
27.12.5. Limited Painting. Refer to TO 1-1-8, Application and Removal of Organic Coatings,
Aerospace and Non-Aerospace Equipment, for additional guidance and information.
27.12.6. Grounding. Aircraft surfaces will be grounded to preclude the build-up of static
electricity whenever operations involving sanding, rubbing, painting, or wiping surfaces are
performed. (T-0)
27.13. Painting Radio and Radar Equipment.
27.13.1. Fire Prevention. The paints used to spray coat radio and radar equipment are very
flammable and will not be used near any open flames. (T-0) IAW Chapters 6 and 22, smoking
is prohibited within 50 feet of painting operations. (T-1)
27.13.2. These paints are toxic and will only be used in ventilated spray booths. (T-1)
Installation BE uses guidance provided in ACGIH’s Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of
Recommended Practice for Operations and Maintenance, to ensure ventilation meets
requirements. (T-1)
27.13.3. Personal Protective Equipment. Paint shop personnel will wear PPE as identified in
installation BE’s OH Assessment. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 14 for additional guidance.
27.13.4. Drying. Radio and radar equipment that has been spray painted will be placed in the
open air for several hours or, if possible, for a full day. (T-1) This will prevent vapors from off
gassing during the drying process being ignited by sparks generated when the equipment is put
into operation. Emery paper or steel wool will not be used on radio or radar equipment until
the vapors have totally dissipated. (T-1)
27.14. Electrostatic Paint Spraying.
27.14.1. Overview. In electrostatic paint spraying, low velocity particles of paint are passed
through a negatively charged electrostatic field. The particles of paint assume the negative
polarity of the electrodes and are then attracted to the work, which is positively charged,
usually by attachment to a grounded conveyor.

310

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
27.14.2. Fire Prevention. Fire hazards involved in electrostatic paint spraying include solvents
used with the paint, possible sparking of the unit and the use of oil-filled transformers. Fireprotective equipment will be checked regularly with particular attention given to automatic
sprinkler heads. (T-0) Sprinklers protecting spraying areas will be kept free from deposits by
frequent or daily cleaning. (T-0) Portable fire extinguishers will be available for use. Refer to
Chapter 6 for additional guidance.
27.14.3. Daily Inspection. Spraying equipment, including spray gun tips, will be checked
daily. (T-0) The entire electrostatic unit will also be checked daily, including the electrical
terminals, electrodes, and insulators. (T-0) On humid days, condensation will be removed from
insulators. (T-0) The conveyor system will be checked for jerky operation, loose hooks, or
other defects that could permit swinging of the units being painted. (T-0) Moisture will be
removed from the compressed air storage tanks. (T-0) Refer to the manufacturer’s operating
instructions or Chapter 25 for drainage requirements.
27.14.4. Insulators. The insulators on electrodes and conductors will be kept dry and cleaned
frequently to prevent flashover. (T-0)
27.14.5. Maintenance. Before cleaning a nozzle or attempting any work on a spray gun, the
power unit will be turned off and the nozzle will be grounded to discharge any static charge
remaining in the system. (T-0) Maintenance will not be completed until enough time has
elapsed to permit the solvent vapors to be drawn out of the area. (T-0) In the absence of
automatic grounding equipment, sufficient time will be allowed to permit any remaining
electrical charge to drain off. (T-0) Only approved spark-resistant tools will be used during
cleaning operations unless other types of tools are permitted by the equipment manufacturer
or TO. (T-0)
27.14.6. Grounding. All metal parts, including guard rails, shields, barriers, and enclosures in
the vicinity of a unit, will be grounded to prevent static build-up. (T-0) Manual grounding
wires will be available, and employees will be familiar with their use. (T-0) Grounding will be
IAW AFMAN 32-1065. (T-1) Items painted in an interior spray booth will be bonded or
grounded to lessen the possibility of static discharge and explosion. (T-0)
27.14.7. Manual Spraying. To prevent build-up of a static charge on the operator’s body, the
spray gun handle will be electrically connected to ground so the operator, in normal operating
position, is in contact with the handle by a resistance of not more than 1 megohm to ground.
(T-0)
27.14.8. Personnel. Only properly trained, qualified and physically fit personnel will be
permitted to work around electrostatic apparatus. (T-0) Medical examinations will be
scheduled with base medical services, as needed. (T-0)
27.14.9. Warning Signs. Signs will be conspicuously posted, designating the process zone as
dangerous, and indicating the necessity for grounding personnel entering the spray area. A red
ON light will be installed to the interlocked power transformer circuit. (T-0)

27.15. Airless Paint Spraying.
27.15.1. Principle. The principle of the airless spray method is to force paint through a small
orifice at pressures from 1,800 to 4,000 pounds per square inch (psi). An electric or air-powered
pump increases the hydraulic pressure of the paint in the hose and pressure vessel, if so

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

311

equipped, and forces the paint, which may be heated to decrease its viscosity, from the spray
gun. When the paint is forced through the orifice under high pressure, it expands and breaks
into small particles. The atomized particles have enough velocity to carry them to the surface
to be painted, but not enough to rebound. A spray pattern of wide coverage is created rapidly
and with very little mist. Since the pattern has unusually sharp edges, masking and shielding
are not necessary for most jobs.
27.15.2. Operation.
27.15.2.1. Paint can be hypodermically injected into the body by high operating pressures.
Injected paint may be life threatening and requires immediate medical treatment.
Warning: Never point an airless spray gun at any part of the body.
27.15.2.2. Do not disconnect the gun from the fluid hose or the hose from the pump until
the pressure has been released from the hose and other components. This is accomplished
by first closing off the electric power or air pressure to the pump and then bleeding off
pressure in the fluid hose, by triggering the gun, before disconnecting it.
27.15.2.3. When handling the gun, but not actually spraying (e.g., when changing parts or
work position), hold the gun with the grip and remove the fingers from the trigger. This
will prevent the gun from being activated if the operator’s hold will inadvertently tighten
due to slipping or stumbling. Note: Guns will be equipped with trigger guards and a safety
lock. (T-0) The lock will be positioned to prevent operation, except when the gun is in use.
(T-0)
27.15.2.4. Ensure all hose connections and fittings are tight and not leaking, and are
compatible with the pressure the system uses. (T-1) Where equipped, the hose, gun, and
pressure vessel will have special fittings that are not interchangeable with low pressure
fittings or any other fittings.
27.15.2.5. Employees will inspect hoses for weak or worn spots prior to use. (T-0)
Damaged hoses will be tagged and removed from service. (T-1) Make certain the hoses do
not contact moving parts of machinery, lie over or around sharp edges and corners, or
contact other objects that would damage them. (T-0) Also, check for deterioration caused
by exposure to chemicals for damage. Warning: High-pressure leaks from hoses or
connections may also cause hypodermic injection of paint.
27.15.2.6. Cleaning. Consult the manufacturer’s operating instructions for cleaning
procedures. (T-1)
27.15.2.7. Ground the object sprayed and the spray gun, to prevent accumulation of static
electricity; a static electric discharge may ignite vapors. Fluid hoses with a ground wire
prevent the accumulation of a static charge where the airless unit and the object being
sprayed are grounded. Periodic continuity checks will be performed to ensure hose
grounding is intact. (T-0)
27.15.2.8. Operators will wear eye protection and gloves to guard against accidental
contact with the spray. (T-0) Installation BE-approved respiratory protective equipment
will be worn where exhaust ventilation is inadequate or not available. (T-0)

312

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

27.16. Aerosol Cans of Spray Paint.
27.16.1. Precautions. The same general safety and health precautions apply to spray painting
from pressurized cans as to spray painting by other means.
27.16.2. Storage. Spray paint cans will be considered flammable materials and stored IAW
criteria in Chapter 22. (T-1) Office desks will not be used to store cans of spray paint. (T-1)
Office storerooms will not be used to store cans of spray paint unless the storage area has been
approved by F&ES Flight. (T-1)
27.16.3. Disposal.
27.16.3.1. Dispose of empty and malfunctioning cans IAW DAFMAN 32-7002, 29 CFR
§ 1910.107(g)(3), other approved hazardous waste disposal procedures and applicable
environmental regulations. (T-0) Contact installation CE for local guidance.
27.16.3.2. Rags and other hazardous waste materials will be disposed of in self-closing
metal containers labeled to indicate the contents and IAW the procedures established by
the installation CE, DAFMAN 32-7002, 29 CFR § 1910.107(g)(3), other approved
hazardous waste disposal procedures, and applicable environmental regulations. (T-0) Do
not dispose of cans of spray paint, rags, and other hazardous waste materials in office waste
receptacles. (T-0)
27.16.3.3. PPE. Refer to other sections of this chapter and Chapter 14 for additional
guidance.
27.17. Powder Coating.
27.17.1. Overview. These operations deposit air suspended powders on components in
specially constructed containers called fluidized beds. Some beds are designed to place an
electrostatic charge on the powder and the opposite charge on the component being coated.
These aerated solid powders must be trapped and not allowed to escape to the atmosphere.
(T-0) Refer to manufacturer’s specifications and NFPA 33 for fluidized bed use.
27.17.2. Fire Prevention. Electrostatic fluidized beds and associated equipment will be
approved types. (T-0) The maximum surface temperature of equipment in the coating area will
not exceed 150° F. (T-0) The high voltage circuits will be designed to not produce a spark of
sufficient intensity to ignite any powder-air mixtures or result in an appreciable shock hazard
upon contact with a grounded object, under normal operating conditions. (T-0) Transformers,
power packs, control apparatus, and all other electrical portions of the equipment, except for
the charging electrodes and their connections to the power supply, will be located outside the
powder coating area. (T-0)
27.17.3. Ventilation. Forced exhaust ventilation is mandatory to maintain a safe atmosphere.
(T-0) Dust collectors will be incorporated within the ventilation system to trap all excess
powder. (T-0) Where applicable, exhaust ventilation will be sufficient to maintain the
atmosphere below the lowest explosive limits for the materials being applied. (T-0) The
electrical equipment will be interlocked with the ventilation system so the equipment cannot
be operated unless the ventilation fans are in operation. (T-0)
27.17.4. Housekeeping. All areas will be kept free of the accumulation of powder coating
dusts, including horizontal surfaces such as ledges, beams, pipes, hoods, booths, and floors.
(T-0) Surfaces will be cleaned in a manner that does not scatter powder or create dust clouds.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

313

(T-0) Vacuum sweeping equipment, where used, will be of a type approved for use in
hazardous locations. (T-0) Water-saturated cleaning cloths will be used to remove powder dust
and to preclude scattering dust deposits. (T-0)
27.17.5. Grounding. Objects being coated will be maintained in contact with the conveyor or
other support to ensure proper grounding. (T-0) Hangers will be regularly cleaned to ensure
effective electrical contact and areas of contact will be sharp points or knife edges where
possible. (T-0) The powder coating equipment will carry a prominent, permanently installed
warning regarding the necessity for bonding and grounding all metal parts of the fluidized bed
and the objects being coated. (T-0)
27.18. Organic Peroxide Coating.
27.18.1. Principle. This type coating is applied using a special purpose spray gun. The
peroxide acts as a catalyst and is introduced through one nozzle of the gun. A second material,
a polyester, is introduced through a second nozzle. When the two mix at the application point,
a chemical reaction occurs producing a plastic polymer coating. Refer to the manufacturer’s
label for the specific material in use and NFPA 33 for additional guidance.
27.18.2. Training. Only designated personnel, trained to use and handle organic peroxide
formulations, will be permitted to use these materials. (T-0)
27.18.3. Fire Prevention. Do not exchange equipment used for organic peroxide coating with
that used for any other application. (T-0) Peroxides are oxidizers and will not be mixed with
other materials. (T-0) Promptly clean up any overspray or spills. (T-0) Use noncombustible
absorbent material for cleanup purposes. (T-0) Do not store or place organic peroxide materials
near any source of heat. Avoid rough handling, since these materials are sensitive to shock.
(T-0)
27.18.3.1. Smoking is prohibited and NO SMOKING signs will be posted in any area
where organic peroxides are stored, mixed, or applied. (T-0) Use non-sparking tools in
areas where these materials are stored, mixed, or applied. (T-0) Dispose of this material
IAW AFMAN 32-7002, 29 CFR § 1910.107(g)(3), other approved hazardous waste
disposal procedures and applicable environmental regulations. (T-0)
27.18.3.2. Contact installation CE for local guidance. Copper beryllium tools must not be
purchased, kept, or dressed. (T-0) If there is no copper beryllium non-sparking tool
substitute, consult with installation CE to determine how to apply for an exception to this
requirement. Note: If a copper beryllium tool is found that has lain undisturbed for more
than three years, coordinate with installation CE and BE for assistance in removal. (T-0)
27.18.4. Protective Equipment. Respirators, gloves, and protective clothing will be used IAW
installation BE’s OH Assessment. (T-0)
27.18.5. Ventilation. Ensure all spraying operations involving the use of organic peroxides are
conducted in spray booths meeting the requirements of the installation BE OH Assessment
determined IAW ACGIH’s Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice for
Operations and Maintenance and NFPA 13. (T-0)
27.18.6. Storage of Organic Peroxides. Personnel will maintain a minimum quantity of
peroxides needed for daily operations in the processing area. (T-0) Organic peroxide
formulations will be stored IAW NFPA 400 and the manufacturers’ recommendations. (T-0)

314

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
27.18.7. Mixing of Organic Peroxides with Promoters. Organic peroxide formulations will not
be mixed directly with any cobalt compounds or other promoters or accelerations, as violent
decomposition or explosion can result. (T-0) To minimize the possibility of such accidental
mixing, these materials will not be stored adjacent to each other. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

315
Chapter 28

COMMUNICATION CABLE, ANTENNA AND COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS
28.1. Scope. This chapter addresses safety requirements for communications systems
professionals when installing and maintaining radars, cable and wireless systems, antennas, and
airfield and weather systems. Communications systems personnel are inherently susceptible to
electrical, hazardous energy, climbing towers and utility poles, radiation, chemicals, falling from
heights, soldering, and confined space hazards. Personnel will be thoroughly familiar with safetyrelated work practices that prevent injuries resulting from falling from heights, direct or indirect
contact with electrical contacts, or work performed near or on equipment or circuits, which are or
may be energized. (T-0)
28.2. General Safety Practices.
28.2.1. Electrical Hazards. Specific safety-related work practices will be consistent with the
nature and extent of the associated electrical hazards or any other potential release of hazardous
energy. Refer to Chapter 8 or Chapter 21 for specific and additional requirements.
28.2.1.1. Shock. The severity of electric shock is determined by the amount of current
flowing through the body, the time of exposure, and the body’s physical condition.
Normally, any voltage capable of producing a current flow of 50 milliamperes or more
through the body can cause a shock that may result in cardiac arrest. Any individual
receiving an electric shock will need immediate medical attention. Some effects of
electrical exposure to the body are:
28.2.1.1.1. Contraction of the chest muscles, which may interfere with breathing to
such an extent that death will result from asphyxiation with prolonged exposure.
28.2.1.1.2. Temporary paralysis of the nerve center, which may result in failure of
respiration, a condition which often continues long after the victim is freed from the
circuit.
28.2.1.1.3. Ventricular fibrillation, an irregular and erratic heartbeat which may result
in cardiac arrest.
28.2.1.1.4. Serious internal injuries to nerves and bones can occur with the passage of
electrical current through limbs. Externally, apparent damage does not always appear
severe. If pain, loss of sensation or function occur, seek medical attention immediately.
28.2.1.2. Arcing. When a metal object, such as a tool, contacts an electric current, it will
become a conductor. The electric current may result in an arc, which can cause serious
burns to the body and temporary or permanent blindness.
28.2.2. Other. A natural reaction after contacting an electric current or seeing an arc is to push
oneself away from the source. This can result in falls or abrupt contact with fixed objects
causing serious or fatal injuries.
28.2.3. Climbing Hazards. The primary hazards associated with climbing are falls and contact
with electrical systems. A record of climbing certification will be maintained on DAF Form
1098, Special Task Certification and Recurring Training, or an electronic-approved product,
for military and government civilian personnel. (T-1) Personnel who have not climbed within

316

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
the last 12 months as a part of their normal job will require refresher training under the
supervision of a qualified instructor. (T-1) The amount of training required will be determined
by the climbing instructor. (T-1) Records will be updated whenever recertification is
accomplished. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 28.7 for additional guidance. (Warning: Pole Top
rescue will only be attempted by personnel who are certified or in a training status under the
supervision of an instructor.) (T-0) Examples of climbing hazards include:
28.2.3.1. Pole conditions such as knots, knotholes, cracks, excessive gaff marks, crooked
or raked poles, splinters, ice on poles, and heavily creosoted or arsenic-treated poles.
28.2.3.2. Pole attachments such as conduits, molding, cable and ground wire, strain plates,
signboards, nails, and metal pole numbers.
28.2.3.3. Tower conditions such as cracks, rust, corrosion, loose/missing hardware, ice on
climbing surfaces, bent/broken steps, improper grounding, dirt, and excessive grease and
oil.
28.2.3.4. Improper clothing such as badly worn shoes (e.g., loose heels, thin soles), lowcut shoes, trousers not worn correctly or securely inside of climbing irons.
28.2.3.5. Equipment items that do not fit properly, such as loose, short, or dull gaffs;
climber straps too tight, too loose, too long or broken; or the wrong sized climbers.
28.2.3.6. Failure to follow approved methods for climbing and working on poles and tower
structures.
28.2.3.7. Wearing climbers while working on wooden poles and tower structures.
28.2.3.8. Failure to maintain good physical condition.
28.2.4. Radiation Hazards.
28.2.4.1. Non-ionizing Radiation. Refer to AFI 48-109 and 29 CFR § 1910.97,
Nonionizing Radiation, for additional guidance on management of hazards associated with
non-ionizing radiation. Employees with implanted medical devices must notify their
supervisors before work and seek medical assessment before performing assigned duties.
(T-0) Caution: Work in areas where exposure to non-ionizing radiation or electromagnetic
fields occur could affect employees with pacemakers and other electrically active
implanted medical devices by interrupting their proper operation.
28.2.4.2. Ionizing Radiation. Radioactive material contained in electron tubes presents no
significant hazard if the tube is intact. The level of radiation from a small number of
electron tubes at maintenance shops does not approach a dangerous level; however, at
major supply points, the storage of large quantities of radioactive tubes in a relatively small
area may create hazards. Further, a broken radioactive tube is potentially hazardous since
the radioactive material may be inhaled or ingested. If breakage does occur, do not touch
the tube or the immediate area. Seek immediate medical attention if personnel are exposed
to broken electron tube material. Notify your immediate supervisor and the IRSO of the
exposure. Carefully handle tubes and ensure they are packaged correctly to prevent
breakage. The use of cushioning material is necessary, leave tubes in the packing, shipping,
or storage container and remove them just prior to installation. Control of the disposition
operations, i.e., packaging, marking, identifying temporary storage, and shipping, is the
responsibility of assigned radiological monitors with guidance from the IRSO. Radio

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

317

frequency generators such as certain klystrons, thyratrons, magnetrons, transmit-receive
tubes, and similar high voltage devices emit ionizing radiation. Refer to 29 CFR §
1910.1096, Ionizing Radiation, and AFMAN 48-148 for guidance on hazards associated
with ionizing radiation.
28.2.5. Chemical Hazards. Chemicals used in conjunction with communications equipment
maintenance may present health hazards due to liquid-to-skin contact and/or inhalation of toxic
vapors. All employees who may be potentially exposed to chemical hazards during work will
require HAZCOM training IAW AFI 90-821, Hazard Communication (HAZCOM) Program,
upon initial assignment and thereafter when a new hazard or chemical is introduced into the
work area, a new employee is assigned, or existent tasks are reevaluated with possible new
hazards. (T-0) Disposal of hazardous waste will be coordinated with installation CE. (T-1)
Supervisors will ensure all personnel have access to SDSs for chemicals used in work
processes. (T-0) Refer to AFI 90-821 and 29 CFR § 1910.1200, Hazard Communication, for
additional guidance.
28.2.6. Compressed Gases. When using or transporting compressed gas cylinders (such as dry
nitrogen cylinders) in a horizontal position, special compartments, racks, or adequate blocking
will be provided to prevent cylinder movement and the cylinders will be secured with safety
straps or chains. (T-0) Regulators will be removed, and safety caps installed before a cylinder
is transported or when not in use. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 19, Compressed Gases, for additional
guidance.
28.2.6.1. Compressed gas cylinders will be kept away from excessive heat and protected
from direct rays of the sun and will not be stored where they might be damaged or knocked
over by passing or falling objects. (T-1) Cylinders will be secured and stored at least 50
feet away from combustible materials. (T-0) Refer to TO 42B5-1-2, Gas Cylinders Use,
Handling, and Maintenance, for additional information.
28.2.6.2. Cylinders will be stored and used in a vertical position only, valve-end up. (T-0)
28.2.6.3. Dispose of compressed gas cylinders in a manner consistent with approved
hazardous waste disposal procedures and applicable environmental regulations. (T-0)
Contact installation CE for guidance.
28.2.7. Falling from Heights. The potential of falling from heights is the main hazard
encountered during climbing activities. Any work that requires employees to work at heights
above four feet will require a fall protection program and the appropriate safety gear. (T-0)
Refer to Chapter 13 and paragraph 28.7.6 for additional guidance on fall protection program
requirements and personal fall arrest system.
28.2.8. Soldering Precautions. Ensure fire extinguishers are accessible in the soldering area.
(T-1) Remove combustible materials from the work area to prevent fires. (T-1) Never sling
excess solder from a soldering iron, use a damp cloth or sponge to wipe away the excess. (T-1)
Always place the soldering iron in the proper holder when not in use, and never leave the iron
plugged in and unattended. (T-1)
28.2.8.1. Wear a face shield or safety goggles when soldering. (T-1) Normal prescription
glasses or plain safety glasses may be used in place of safety goggles for light electronic
equipment soldering, such as electronic circuit boards.

318

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
28.2.8.2. Ensure the work area where soldering is performed is well ventilated IAW the
requirements in the installation BE OH Assessment derived from ACGIH’s Industrial
Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice for Operations and Maintenance. (T-0)
Ensure all power unit exhausts are vented to the building exterior. (T-0) Use approved
respiratory protection when working in permit-required confined spaces where ventilation
does not remove the hazard. (T-0) Refer to AFI 48-137, for additional guidance and
information.
28.2.9. Confined Spaces. Employees required to enter confined spaces, such as manholes or
underground vaults, will be trained to safely accomplish their duties and rescue procedures
specifically to the permit-required confined space entered. (T-0) Employees who enter nonpermit-required confined spaces will be trained and equipped to enter those spaces. (T-1) Refer
to Chapter 23, applicable TOs and 29 CFR § 1910.146, Permit-Required Confined Spaces,
for additional requirements.
28.2.10. Grounding and Bonding. Most electricians and electronics employees consider the
ground to be a stake or other direct connection into the ground (earth) to which the electrical
system of the facility is connected. In a two-wire electrical cord, there is a neutral or ground
wire and a hot wire. The neutral wire for the system will be white. (T-1) The other wire is the
hot wire, and it may be any color other than white or green. In a three-wire system, the third
wire is the ground wire, and it is always green or green with one or more yellow stripes.
Bonding, on the other hand, consists of interconnecting two or more pieces of conductive
equipment with a suitable wire or strap to equalize the resistance and, in effect, make them as
one piece of equipment as far as electrical potential is concerned. For specific guidance, refer
to AFMAN 32-1065.
28.2.10.1. Electrical and electronics equipment will be grounded so a potential cannot
exist between the unit and the ground. (T-0) Fixed electrical equipment will be electrically
bonded to a grounding connector. (T-0) Special protection will be provided to safeguard
grounding wires from mechanical damage. (T-0) Metal workbenches and metal framed or
legged workbenches used for requiring and testing of electronic equipment will be
grounded. (T-0) The size and type of material for grounding conductors will be selected
according to NFPA 70, National Electric Code, Article 250, Grounding. (T-0) All
workbenches used for “power on” maintenance of any electronic equipment will be
provided with a means for grounding the equipment. (T-0) This will be interconnected to
the facility ground system. (T-0)
28.2.10.2. Grounding circuits, as a minimum, will have a separate circuit for each chassis,
cabinet, and frame. (T-0) Each chassis ground may terminate at its cabinet or frame ground.
Additionally, ground circuits will have a neutral circuit and connections that are
mechanically secured by:
28.2.10.2.1. A spot-welded terminal lug. (T-0)
28.2.10.2.2. A portion of the chassis or frame that has been identified as a grounding
point. (T-0)
28.2.10.2.3. The use of a terminal on the ground wire by a lock washer and screw or a
lock washer and nut. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

319

28.2.10.3. Grounding braids installed on panels and cabinets that are removable or hinged
so bonding is not accomplished through hinges, slides, or mounting hardware.
Nonconductive panels and cabinets that removable or hinged need not be equipped with
grounding braids. (T-0)
28.2.10.4. System grounding within mobile equipment will be by means of a grounding
conductor. (T-0) As these grounding conductors are replaced, they will be of the
appropriate gauge and will have a green insulated cover. (T-0)
28.2.10.5. Bus bars will be clean and free of corrosion, splices will be tight, and paint will
be removed from all vertical angles at the point of attachment to the ground bus bar. (T-0)
28.2.11. Respiratory Precautions. Respiratory protection is required if prolonged breathing of
chemical aerosols, mists and vapors is expected or if working in permit-required confined
spaces when the atmosphere is oxygen deficient or hazardous. A supplied breathing air system
with quick access emergency egress air supply may be needed. The installation BE’s OH
Assessment will identify recommended controls. Refer to DAFMAN 48-146, Occupational
Health Program Management, for additional guidance.
28.2.12. Miscellaneous.
28.2.12.1. Fluid/Chemical Leakage. Employees will be watchful for any sign of oil or fluid
leakage from transformers or similar devices. (T-1) Where the possibility the leaked liquid
contains high concentrations of polychlorinated biphenyl, which is extremely toxic, report
leakage to the installation CE. (T-1) Where leakage is found, do not touch or disturb the
leaking fluid until approval to clean and dispose of the material is provided. (T-1)
Employees will use required PPE and dispose as required by installation CE. (T-1)
28.2.12.2. Heaters and Torches Used in Ground and Aerial Tents. Flame-type heaters will
not be used within ground tents or on platforms within aerial tents unless the tent covers
are constructed of fire-resistant materials and adequate ventilation is maintained. (T-0)
Torches may be used on aerial splicing platforms or in buckets enclosed by tents, provided
the material is constructed of fire-resistant material and the torch is turned off when not in
use. The tent will be ventilated while the torch is in use. (T-0)
28.2.12.3. Notifications. Provide F&ES Flight and the emergency response units with
maps indicating routes to locations of remote sites. (T-1) Where practical, establish a
memorandum of understanding with F&ES Flight and emergency rescue units to conduct
actual tests to evaluate the route suitability and response times. Ensure all areas containing
flammable materials have appropriate fire prevention signs posted and fire extinguishers
available. (T-0) Off-base facilities/sites will coordinate and plan with the nearest local area
medical facility for emergency medical services if the medical facility is too far to provide
immediate emergency medical attention. (T-1)
28.2.12.4. Safety Observers. When installing or repairing commercial and industrial
systems, communication cables and antenna systems that may expose employees to
energized equipment, high voltage circuits, 600 volts or above, or low voltage power if it
has a high current flow, work will not begin until a qualified safety observer is present.
(T-0) An individual will not be assigned other duties while serving as safety observer.
(T-1) Normally, the supervisor is also the safety observer.

320

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
28.2.12.4.1. When repairing or troubleshooting energized high voltage
communications equipment, the safety observer does not have to be proficient in the
task being observed.
28.2.12.4.2. Safety observers will be trained IAW paragraph 1.9 and be familiar with
local procedures to obtain medical assistance. (T-1)
28.2.12.4.3. While the task is being performed, the safety observer will stand where
they can plainly see all personnel who are working on the equipment, have access to
the main power switch and give a positive warning of potential danger to anyone
approaching the equipment. (T-0) If unable to maintain visual contact with the main
power switch, the hazardous energy control procedure will be used. (T-0) Additionally,
the safety observer will have ready access to safety equipment when high voltage is
involved. (T-0)

28.3. Training. All training will be documented on the DAF Form 55, Employee Safety and
Health Record, DAF Form 623, Individual Training Record Folder, or an approved computer
automated system IAW DAFI 91-202. (T-1) A record of climbing certification will be maintained
on DAF Form 1098 or approved computer automated system. (T-0)
28.3.1. Climbing. Pole top rescue will only be attempted by personnel who are certified or in
a training status under the supervision of an instructor. (T-1) A record of climbing certification
will be maintained on DAF Form 1098 for military and civilian personnel, or an approved
automated computer system. Personnel who have not climbed within the last 12 months as a
part of their normal job require refresher training under the supervision of a qualified instructor.
(T-1) The amount of training required will be determined by the climbing instructor. (T-1)
Training records will be updated whenever the recertification is accomplished. (T-1) Refer to
paragraph 28.7 for additional guidance.
28.3.2. Confined Spaces. Employees required to enter confined spaces, such as utility holes
or underground vaults, will be trained in rescue requirements as found in Chapter 23 and 29
CFR § 1910.146 to include how to safely self-rescue. (T-0) Safety observers or attendants will
be trained rescue procedures for each type of permit-required confined space entered. (T-0)
28.3.3. Hazard Communication Training. All employees who may be potentially exposed to
chemical hazards during the course of work will complete hazard communication training IAW
AFI 90-821 and 29 CFR § 1910.1200 upon initial assignment, when a new hazard or chemical
is introduced into the work area/shop or an employee is assigned new or different tasks with
new hazards. (T-0) Safety precautions for materials will be covered in the pre-task safety
briefing prior to any task requiring the use of or exposure to hazardous materials. (T-0)
28.4. Safety Equipment and Devices.
28.4.1. Manholes, Aerial Cables, and Pole Lines. Motor vehicle traffic is a hazard to personnel
working in and around manholes, aerial cable installation, and pole line construction along
streets and highways. Warning devices, barriers, and guarding will be used to protect
personnel. (T-0) Ventilation equipment will be positioned so the air intake is located away
from vehicular exhaust. (T-0) Refer to TO 31W3-10-12, Air Force Communications Command
(D-1 Standard) – Outside Plant Cable Placement, for additional guidance on guarding
requirements and warning devices.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

321

28.4.2. Safety Straps, Harnesses, and Lanyards. Nylon straps, not leather safety straps, will be
used on steel structures. (T-0) Safety harnesses, straps, and lanyards will be provided, and
supervisors will ensure their use when work is performed at positions more than four feet above
the ground. (T-0) Safety harnesses, straps, and lanyards are not required for portable ladders.
Refer to Chapter 13 and 29 CFR § 1910.268(g), Telecommunications, Personal Climbing
Equipment, for additional guidance. Note: If personnel can sustain a fall of more than two feet
while wearing climbing equipment, fall arrest procedures and equipment guidance in Chapter
13, UFC 3-560-01, 29 CFR § 1910.140 and 29 CFR § 1926, Subpart M, Appendix C,
Personnel Fall Arrest Systems – Non-Mandatory Guidelines for Complying with 1926.502(d),
will be followed. (T-0) Safety straps and harnesses will also be worn when working at elevated
positions on poles, towers, or similar structures, which do not have adequately guarded work
areas IAW 29 CFR § 1910.268. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure all safety climbing equipment
is inspected daily by authorized employees to determine if it is in safe working condition. (T-0)
Each person using safety harnesses, straps, and lanyards will inspect the equipment prior to
each use. (T-0)
28.4.3. Climbing Safety Devices. Climbing safety devices will be installed on all fixed ladders
24 feet or more in length that are an integral part of the antenna support. (T-0) Where climbing
safety devices are not installed, installation or maintenance will be performed only by a
certified climber using prescribed fall protection. (T-0) Personnel climbing metal antenna
support poles equipped with metal steps will use climbing safety devices. (T-0) Note: All
Cyber Support field technicians required to climb un-stepped communication poles will use
the squeeze pole fall protector (pole choker) arrest system. (T-0) Users of the pole chokers will
comply with TO 00-25-245, Operations Instructions - Testing and Inspection Procedures for
Personnel Safety and Rescue Equipment, and manufacturer’s instructions regarding inspection,
maintenance, cleaning, and storage of personal fall arrest ystem equipment. (T-0)
28.4.4. Markings. All antenna support, power, telephone, and transmission line poles will be
marked 12-feet from the butt of the pole to determine the depth of the pole. (T-0) The 12-foot
mark will be indicated with aluminum pole tags or marker nails. (T-0) If these are not available,
1-inch galvanized steel roofing nails will be driven into the pole to form the numerals “12.”
(T-0)
28.4.5. Proximity Warning Devices. These devices may be used on cranes or aerial lifts. Refer
to Chapters 12 and 16 of this manual, 29 CFR § 1910.67, Vehicle-Mounted Elevating and
Rotating Work Platforms, and 29 CFR § 1910.180, Crawler Locomotive and Truck Cranes,
for additional guidance.
28.4.6. Electrical Safety Boards. Refer to paragraph 8.12 for required items in an emergency
equipment kit or board.
28.4.7. Head Protection. Electrical workers will wear Class G safety helmets/hard hats. (T-0)
The preferred color for hard hats is yellow due to its high visibility during inclement weather
and darkness. Hard hats will not be painted. (T-1) Markings on hard hats will be for the stickon type and will be limited to the name of the owner (letters no more than 1/2 -inch high by
½-inch wide) and a unit emblem (no more than 3-inches in diameter). (T-1) All markings will
be conservative and will not degrade the effectiveness or non-conductivity of the hard hat.
(T-1) Refer to paragraph 14.3.5.2 for additional guidance.

322

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
28.4.8. Rubber Insulating Floor Matting. Refer to paragraph 8.11.5 for insulating matting
specific guidance.

28.5. Tools and Equipment. Refer to Chapter 11 for additional information.
28.5.1. Nonconductive tools will be used while performing work on energized
communications systems equipment. (T-0) Taping or plastic coating is not an acceptable
means of insulation. Wooden handle tools will not be used on energized communications
systems equipment. (T-0)
28.5.2. All tools will be kept clean and free of grease, oil, paint, or other foreign material IAW
TO 32-1-101. (T-0) Exception: This does not prevent the use of a light film of oil on tools for
rust protection.
28.5.3. Ladders. Refer to Chapter 7 for additional guidance.
28.5.4. Equipment and/or Component Cleaning. Vacuuming is the preferred method of
cleaning electronic equipment. If compressed air is used, employees will limit the air pressure
to less than 30 psi, use effective chip guarding, and wear required PPE. (T-0) When using
canned compressed air, wear eye protection. (T-1)
28.5.5. Test equipment will be designed, constructed, and installed to provide safe work
procedures and to minimize personnel exposure to hazardous work situations. (T-0) When tests
involve live circuits, the area will be closed. (T-0) Only authorized personnel who have been
trained about the potential hazards involved will be in this area when tests are performed. (T-0)
At least one safety observer will be present when high voltage is involved. (T-0)
28.5.5.1. Set Up. Test operators and technicians will follow the procedures for adjustment,
operation and repair of test equipment provided in TOs, manufacturer’s instructions and
manuals or applicable diagrams. (T-0) Employees will be trained on the characteristics and
safe operation of the various instruments before authorized use. (T-0) Before voltage is
applied, cable conductors will be isolated. (T-0)
28.5.5.2. Approved warning signs and/or signals will be used to indicate when power is
on. (T-0) A means of emergency power shutdown will be provided outside the test area in
addition to the main power switch within the test area. (T-0)
28.5.5.3. Connections to test tables, bus bars, plug racks, terminal cabinets, and
distribution boards will be secure. (T-0)
28.5.6. PPE. All employees involved in test operations will be provided with appropriate PPE.
(T-0) Refer to Chapter 14, 29 CFR § 1910.268(e) and 29 CFR § 1910.268(i) for additional
requirements.
28.6. High Voltage.
28.6.1. High voltage is defined as greater than 600 volts (root-mean-square) nominal or
greater. However, much lower voltage can be lethal. The design and development of all
military electronic equipment will provide fail-safe features for safety of employees during the
installation, operation, maintenance, or interchanging of a complete equipment assembly or
component part. (T-1) Operators and technicians will not attempt to adjust any electronic
equipment when there is a possibility of injury from unprotected high voltage. (T-0)
Adjustments on operating high voltage equipment, other than those specified by TO or

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

323

manufacturer’s instructions, will only be authorized by the commander. (T-1) The commander
will consider all operational requirements, TOs, manufacturer’s instructions, safety
precautions and emergency procedures before authorizing work to proceed. (T-1) Employees
using high voltage to troubleshoot and/or test cables will be instructed in the precautions
necessary for their safety and the safety of others. (T-0) Employees will be warned to stay clear
while voltage is applied. (T-0)
28.6.2. Only qualified personnel will perform work near energized overhead power lines.
(T-0) Approach distances for qualified employees will be IAW UFC 3-560-01, Table 3-1.
(T-0) Before starting any communications work near overhead power lines, coordinate the
work with installation CE’s electrical shop. (T-1) Safety precautions IAW 29 CFR §§ 1910.268
and 1910.333, Selection and Use of Work Practices, will be considered when working near
overhead power lines. (T-0)
28.6.3. If an aerial lift or equipment contacts an electrical conductor, the vehicle, equipment,
and attachments will be considered energized. (T-0)
28.6.4. Tree branches hanging on an energized conductor will be removed only with
appropriate electrically insulation equipment, and only by authorized personnel. (T-0)
28.6.5. Line employees’ electrical safety boots will be worn to provide additional protection
against electrical hazards. (T-0) Rubber footwear or line employees’ overshoes will not be
worn. (T-0)
28.6.6. Warning signs will be prominently posted in all areas housing high voltage equipment;
the highest expected voltage will also be posted. (T-0)
28.7. Aerial Work.
28.7.1. Only properly trained and certified personnel, or individuals in training status and
under the direct observation of a qualified instructor, are authorized to climb poles and towers.
Appropriate safety equipment will be worn while performing aerial work. (T-0)
28.7.2. Commanders will designate, in writing, all personnel required to maintain climbing
proficiency. (T-2) Restrict climbing authorizations to structures that must be climbed to
accomplish mission requirements. (T-1) Organizations will coordinate with installation CE to
ensure real property structures have current inspection and maintenance completed. (T-1)
Ensure employees who must climb structures are provided access to inspections/maintenance
records. (T-1)
28.7.3. Installation CE will maintain records on the inspection and maintenance of poles and
towers considered real property IAW DAFI 32-9005, Real Property Accountability and
Reporting, and the Real Property Handbook. (T-1)
28.7.4. The Occupational Safety office will review these records during annual inspections to
ensure they are current. (T-1)
28.7.5. Initial Certification Procedures. Upon assignment to a unit, everyone whose future
duties will require climbing poles and towers where they may be subject to a fall of four feet
or more in height will be trained and certified. (T-1) For Air Force Specialty Codes other than
3D1X7, commanders will restrict climbing authorizations to those structures that must be
climbed to accomplish mission requirements. (T-1)

324

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
28.7.6. Climbing Certifier Requirements. Climbing certifier must:
28.7.6.1. Complete a climbing certification training course or commercial equivalent.
(T-1)
28.7.6.2. Be designated, in writing, by the commander to conduct climbing certification.
(T-1)
28.7.6.3. Be current in CPR and first aid training. (T-1)
28.7.6.4. Demonstrate ability to perform and teach complex tasks aloft. (T-1)
28.7.6.5. Be certified to train individuals on rescue on the following structures when
present on the installation. (T-1)
28.7.6.5.1. Pole Top.
28.7.6.5.2. Tower.
28.7.6.6. Use a training plan to conduct rescue training and climbing certification on the
following structures when present on the installation: (T-1)
28.7.6.6.1. Pole Top.
28.7.6.6.2. Tower.
28.7.6.7. Maintain climbing proficiency and knowledge of current OSHA requirements
found in 29 CFR § 1910.268, Telecommunications, and 29 CFR § 1910 Subpart D,
Walking-Working Surfaces, and Subpart I, Personal Protective Equipment. (T-0)
28.7.6.8. Determine and arrange for the specific climbing safety equipment needed for
performance evaluations. (T-1)
28.7.6.9. Evaluate students using a written knowledge test and hands-on performance
review. (T-1)
28.7.7. Climber’s Responsibilities.
28.7.7.1. Each team member has the responsibility to ensure all personnel are prepared for
the climbing task.
28.7.7.2. Personnel will conduct a pre-inspection of the climbing equipment to evaluate
serviceability using manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0)
28.7.7.3. Climbers must maintain three points of contact while climbing. For example,
either one hand and two feet, or two hands and one foot must always be in contact with the
tower or pole while ascending or descending.
28.7.8. Safety Observers. When performing aerial work on communication cable and antenna
systems, a safety observer will be present. (T-1) Safety observers must:
28.7.8.1. Be qualified to operate the equipment in use. (T-1)
28.7.8.2. Have a current climbing certification. (T-1)
28.7.8.3. Be proficient in CPR and first aid. (T-1)
28.7.8.4. Know the procedures for obtaining medical assistance. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

325

28.7.9. Tower and Pole Climbing Precautions. Refer to TO 31-10-19, Antenna Systems—
Anchors and Supports, and TO 31R-10-5, Air Force Comm Commands (E-I Standards) –
Antenna Systems, Maintenance, Repair and Testing, for requirements on tower and pole
climbing.
28.7.10. Steps and Ladders. When installed, steps and ladders will be equipped with
appropriate climbing safety devices. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 7, 29 CFR § 1910.29, Fall
Protection Systems and Falling Object Protection – Criteria and Practices, 29 CFR § 1910.25,
Stairways, and 29 CFR § 1910.23, Ladders, for additional guidance on steps and ladders.
28.7.11. Radome Installation, Maintenance, or Removal. Employees engaged in radome work
will be experienced riggers and work under the direction of a qualified supervisor. (T-0)
28.7.11.1. Those who work on or in the immediate vicinity of the radome will wear hard
hats and safety-toed shoes. (T-0)
28.7.11.2. Employees will make frequent checks with the supporting DAF weather
organization to allow time to lash down equipment for impending inclement weather. (T-1)
Employees will never try to replace panels when the wind is blowing more than 30 miles
per hour and will never remove more than one panel at a time under normal conditions.
(T-0)
28.7.11.3. Employees will use the maintenance rope to lift the maintenance ladder to the
top of the radome. (T-0) Prior to each use, the rope will be inspected for frayed or worn
spots and replaced, if required. (T-0)
28.7.11.4. When mixing resin for repairs, employees will follow the manufacturer’s
instructions. (T-0)
28.8. Ground-Controlled Approach Radars. The supervisor will inform all nearby personnel
that work is being performed on the roof before ascending to the roof of the ground-controlled
approach trailer or other rotating antenna location. The following will be accomplished: (T-1)
28.8.1. The surveillance antenna control switch will be turned off, locked out, and a warning
sign or tag is placed on the high voltage power supply switch IAW hazardous energy control
procedures. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 21 for additional guidance on hazardous energy control
requirements.
28.8.2. TO 35-1-3 requires rooftops of mobile ground-controlled approach vans be painted to
warn personnel of rotating antenna hazards. Exception: In combat areas, the sweep area
covered by antenna rotation may be indicated by a broken line of ¾ inch-wide red dashes.
28.9. Single Phase Portable and Vehicle-Mounted Generators.
28.9.1. Portable Generators. Under the following conditions, the frame of a portable generator
is not required to be grounded and may serve as the grounding electrode for a system supplied
by the generator when:
28.9.1.1. The generator supplies only equipment mounted on the generator and/or cord
and plug-connected equipment through receptacles mounted on the generator.
28.9.1.2. The non-current-carrying metal parts of equipment and the equipment grounding
conductor terminals of the receptacles are bonded to the generator frame.

326

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
28.9.2. Vehicle-Mounted Generators. Under the following conditions, the frame of a vehicle
may be permitted to serve as the grounding electrode for a system supplied by a generator
located on the vehicle:
28.9.2.1. The frame of the generator is bonded to the vehicle frame.
28.9.2.2. The generator supplies only equipment located on the vehicle and/or cord- and
plug-connected equipment through receptacles mounted on the vehicle or on the generator.
28.9.2.3. The non-current-carrying metal parts of equipment and the equipment grounding
conductor terminals of the receptacles are bonded to the generator frame.
28.9.3. Neutral Conductor Bonding. A neutral conductor will be bonded to the generator frame
when the generator is a component of a separately derived system. (T-0) The bonding of any
conductor other than a neutral within the generator to its frame will not be required.
28.9.4. Power Tools. The tools and equipment being powered by generators will contain threewire cords with ground plugs. (T-0)
28.9.5. Fixed Wiring Systems. Portable and vehicle-mounted generators that supply fixed
wiring systems will be grounded IAW NFPA 70, Article 250, Grounding. (T-0)

28.10. Repairs and Adjustments.
28.10.1. Repairs and adjustments will be made to energized circuits only when a power-on
condition is essential. (T-1) Refer to applicable TO for additional guidance.
28.10.2. Repairs and adjustments of components, when the equipment slides are extended
from their cabinets and voltages are applied, will be permitted only when specified by
applicable TOs, manufacturer’s instructions or approved isolation and troubleshooting
methods. (T-1) Personnel not essential to the operation will be removed from the area while
work is in progress. (T-1) When high voltages are present or transmitters of 1-kW power rating
or higher are involved, the commander will approve procedures and adjustments not specified
by TO or manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1) Use identified PPE directed by TOs or
manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1) Insulated matting will be used and a qualified safety
observer will be present. (T-1)
28.11. Power Distribution Panels.
28.11.1. Power Distribution Panels and Interlocks. Power distribution panels and interlocks
will be secured to prevent personnel contacting energized circuits. (T-0) When equipment must
be removed from service for inspection or repair, the appropriate circuit breaker or interlock
will be locked open (off) and posted with a danger tag, warning sign, visual aid, or other
suitable identification until the equipment is again ready for use. (T-0) Tape will not be used
to “lock” open circuit breakers. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 21 for additional guidance on
hazardous energy control procedures.
28.11.2. Fuses and Circuit Breakers. The inside cover of fuse(s) and circuit breaker panels (or
area adjacent if not equipped with a cover) will indicate in writing the equipment controlled
by each fuse or circuit breaker. (T-0) Over-current devices will be installed in electrical circuits
of a size and type to interrupt the current flow when the current exceeds the current rating of
the equipment or exceeds the capacity of the conductor, whichever is smaller. (T-0) Replace
blown fuses by the type required by the manufacturer or manufacturer’s instructions. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

327

Use insulated fuse pullers when removing or replacing clip type or flat-mount cartridge fuses.
Wire, foil, solder, and similar materials will not be used as substitutes for fuses. (T-0)
28.11.3. Carbon Blocks and Heat Coils. Prior to removal of operating carbon blocks or heat
coils, a measurement of line potential will be made using a voltmeter having a minimum input
impedance of 20,000 ohms per volt to ensure the foreign electromotive force which energized
these protective devices is no longer present. (T-1) Do not remove carbon blocks and/or heat
coils if the foreign electromotive force is still present. (T-1)
28.12. Radar and Microwave Equipment.
28.12.1. Radar and microwave equipment are sources of non-ionizing radiation. Instruct
personnel on the hazards of non-ionizing radiation and do not expose them to levels above the
permissible exposure limits outlined in AFI 48-109. (T-0)
28.12.2. Exercise caution when working on or adjacent to transmitter antennas. A transmitter
connected to an antenna under inspection or repair will be de-energized IAW hazardous energy
control procedures in Chapter 21. (T-1) Secure adjacent antennas creating hazardous levels
of RF radiation at the work location to prevent exposure. (T-1) Employees will remove all
jewelry prior to performing any task on equipment. (T-0) Review the site standard operating
procedures for radiation hazard control and site RF hazards reports and drawings. This will
ensure locations where RF hazards exist are known and appropriate measures (shutdown or
blanking of antennas) are taken to prevent exposure of personnel working in those areas. A
danger tag, warning sign, or other suitable will be posted on the console of the transmitter and
in all other critical locations. (T-1)
28.12.3. Employees will not look into an open waveguide that is connected to an energized
source of microwave radiation. (T-1)
28.12.4. A non-ionizing radiation warning sign will be posted in areas where a hazard may
exist. (T-1) Refer to AFI 48-109 for additional guidance.
28.13. Entering Manholes, Handholes, and Unvented Vaults. All requirements in Chapter 23
will be met before, during and after any entry operation.
28.13.1. Manholes, vaults, and handholes will be positively identified as to utility type (e.g.,
communication, electrical power distribution, sewer, etc.) and classification prior to entry.
(T-0) Where confined space evaluation has not occurred, shop supervisors will coordinate with
the occupational safety office to schedule a confined space evaluation. (T-1)
28.13.2. Unidentified cables will be evaluated to identify their utility type. (T-1) Cables will
be evaluated using a nonintrusive device, such as a clamp-on voltmeter, to determine the
presence of electrical voltage prior to beginning any work. (T-1) Although discouraged, a small
percentage of manholes, vaults, or handholes may contain more than one utility type. In these
few instances, caution will be used, and the cable will be positively identified before work is
initiated. (T-1) When requested, installation CE will provide an electrician to determine safe
procedures. (T-1)
28.14. Cables.
28.14.1. Aerial Cables. Maintain minimum distances (42 inches up to 87,000 volts and 48
inches over 87,000 volts) between power and communications cables on joint-use poles. (T-0)
If minimum distances cannot be maintained, de-energize the power line before performing

328

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
installation or maintenance work. Employees will comply with the minimum approach
distance requirements in UFC 3-560- 01, Table 3-1. (T-0)
28.14.2. Observe caution when installing messenger strand so the loose ends do not contact
power lines. (T-0)
28.14.3. When crossing over roadways, railroads, walkways, etc., ensure proper overhead
clearances are maintained. (T-0)
28.14.4. Before riding or placing a ladder against an aerial strand, test the strand by suspending
approximately 300 pounds in the middle of the span. (T-0) An easy test method is to place a
rope over the strand and have two employees suspend their weight on it.
28.14.5. Cable cars will not be ridden over power lines (primary or secondary). (T-0)
28.14.6. Check handlines for serviceability prior to use. (T-0) The line employee will remove
the handline from the safety harness when they reach the work position and secure it to the
pole. (T-0)
28.14.7. Use safety straps and safety harnesses while working on elevated work platforms
such as aerial splicing platforms, pole platforms, ladder platforms, and terminal balconies.
(T-0) Wear insulating rubber gloves when handling suspension strand that is being installed
on joint-use poles. (T-0)
28.14.8. Underground and Buried Cables. Refer to TO 31W3-10-12 for guidance and
requirements.
28.14.9. Pressurized Cables. Refer to TO 31W3-10-16, Outside Plant Cable Pressurization,
for information and guidance.
28.14.10. Trenching and Excavations. Refer to TO 3lW3-10-12, Outside Plant Cable
Placement, US Army Corps of Engineers Manual (EM) 385-1-1, Safety and Health
Requirements Manual, and 29 CFR § 1926.652, Excavations — Requirement for Protective
Systems, for information and guidance.

28.15. Special Purpose Vehicles.
28.15.1. Inspection. Prior to operation, visually inspect the vehicle and check vehicle
documentation, e.g., AF Form 1800, Operator’s Inspection Guide and Trouble Report, to
determine if it is serviceable. (T-1) Inspect for correct assembly, storage of equipment, and
worn equipment. (T-1) After operation, document and correct any operating deficiencies,
report defects that require repair to the unit vehicle control officer and/or organizational
maintenance section and replace unserviceable equipment. (T-1) Refer to AFI 24-302 for
additional guidance.
28.15.2. Truck-Mounted Winches. Telephone line and maintenance trucks are equipped with
front and/or back-mounted drum-type winches. A safety observer will stand to the front and
clear of the winch when used. (T-0) Winches are extremely powerful and caution will be
exercised when used. (T-0) The winch operator will be trained on both the operation of a winch
gear train and power takeoff lever movement. (T-0) Winch operators will wear leather gloves
when handling a winch line and never hand feed the line onto the drum. (T-1) Inspect the winch
line before use for defects such as broken or worn strands, kinks, flat spots, and worn eye loops.
(T-1) Remove damaged or badly kinked winch lines from service. (T-1) Winch lines will not

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

329

be rigged to pull against the flange of the winch drum. (T-1) Some winches are designed for
pulling while others are designed for raising or lowering. Winches will be used for their design
purpose. (T-1)
28.15.3. Cable Reels. The safe handling, moving, and positioning of cable reels require trained
operators in good physical condition. Pre-planning the move and final positioning will
determine the best methods to use and identify any problems with the surface condition. (T-1)
Use a spotter when backing cable reel trailers. (T-1) Caution will be used when handling cable
reels and cable reel trailer as a full reel of large cable may weigh as much as 10 tons. Carefully
control the movement of the reel to prevent the reel from tilting and provide a substantial
runway of heavy planks when or uneven or soft ground. (T-0) Level the reel by blocking it in
a manner that prevents tilting. After positioning in the desired storage location, block the reel
to prevent rolling. (T-0)

330

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 29

BATTERIES – MAINTENANCE, HANDLING, AND STORAGE REQUIREMENTS
29.1. Scope. DAF battery shops, rooms, and areas contain space and equipment for shipping,
receiving, cleaning, charging, discharging, storing, and repairing storage batteries for aircraft,
vehicles, and ground support equipment. Personnel engaged in these activities will use approved
TO and manufacturer’s instruction. (T-1) Commercial manufacturers’ manuals are not authorized
for DAF use on aircraft batteries and must be converted to technical data by the appropriate DAF
Engineering Support Office or Systems Program Office. (T-1) The battery shop, room, or area will
be designed to eliminate certain hazards associated with battery maintenance. (T-1)
29.1.1. Refer to UFC 3-520-05, Stationary and Mission Batteries, 29 CFR § 1926.403,
General Requirements (Electrical), 29 CFR § 1926.441, Batteries and Battery Charging, and
NFPA 70, including Article 480, Storage Batteries, and Chapter 5, Special Occupancies, for
requirements regarding battery maintenance and operations. (T-0)
29.1.2. Refer to DAFMAN 32-1084 for guidance on battery facility requirements. Whenever
a facility deficiency is identified, appropriate controls and corrective actions are required. If
the deficiency involves a local requirement and cannot be corrected, a waiver letter will be
obtained from the applicable base agency. (T-1)
29.2. Safety.
29.2.1. Installation BE will accomplish an HRA on battery maintenance and operations. (T-1)
The assessment will include engineering controls, PPE requirements, and required battery
maintenance and operations procedures. (T-1)
29.2.2. Electrical components and systems will be de-energized and isolated from other
energized circuits and/or power sources before working on, servicing, removing, or replacing
a battery, unless directed by a TO or other documented requirement. (T-1) Refer to Chapter
21 for additional guidance on hazardous energy control.
29.2.3. Battery Equipment and Charging Operations. Only trained and qualified personnel will
be permitted to change, maintain, or charge batteries. (T-1) Qualified personnel or those
accompanied by qualified personnel will be allowed access to battery shops, rooms, or areas.
(T-1)
29.2.4. When working with corrosives, an emergency shower or eyewash unit will be provided
for emergency use. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 9 for additional information on emergency showers
and eyewash units.
29.3. General Facility and Equipment Requirements for Battery Rooms and Areas. This
paragraph addresses general requirements for lead acid and lithium type batteries utilized in
aerospace, ground transportation, electrical ground infrastructure, office equipment, etc.
29.3.1. There are three common lead-acid battery technologies: flooded, gel and absorbed
glass mat. A valve-regulated lead-acid battery, e.g., the absorbed glass mat and the gel battery
(gel cell), is a type of low- maintenance lead-acid rechargeable battery. Because of their
construction, valve-regulated lead-acid batteries do not require regular addition of water to the
cells. The charging, maintaining, or storage of each battery type will follow the manufacturer’s
instructions and recommendations. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

331

29.3.2. For the architectural requirements for the storage, charging, ventilation, exhaust, and
thermal management of Lithium Ion (Li-Ion) batteries for mobile applications and equipment,
refer to UFC 3-520-05. (T-0) The installation of stationary battery applications using Li-Ion
technologies are prohibited in occupied facilities per UFC 3-520-01. Installation BE will use
ACGIH’s Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice for Operations and
Maintenance, to determine ventilation requirements. (T-0)
29.3.3. Doors. Battery shop exit doors will swing outwards. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 2.7 for
roll up door requirements.
29.3.4. Floors, walls, ceilings, doors, and other painted surfaces in battery rooms and areas
will be painted with an acid-alkali-resistant epoxy-poly-urethane paint. (T-1) These paints
provide the most protection against corrosion.
29.3.5. All mechanical equipment and fixtures will be designed and specified to withstand the
corrosive acid-alkali atmosphere from battery operations. (T-1)
29.3.6. Racks and Tools. Racks used to support batteries during charging will be made of or
coated with non-sparking materials and designed to permit free access for servicing batteries.
(T-1) Metal racks will be protected with an acid-alkali resistive coating, and the actual supports
on which a battery rests will be made of non-conductive materials or be suitably insulated.
(T-1) Tools used during battery charging or servicing will be non-conductive or coated with
non-conductive materials. (T-1)
29.3.7. Work Surfaces. Work surfaces will be non-conductive, and resistant to both acid and
alkali. (T-1) Whenever possible, battery servicing and maintenance will be performed on
elevated, non-conductive racks. (T-1) These racks will allow air to flow under the battery to
provide additional cooling to dissipate heat generated during charging and discharging. (T-1)
29.3.8. Charging Bench Floor Matting. Provide three-foot wide non-slip matting in front of
all charging benches. (T-1) Matting protects personnel from electric shock and slipping
hazards. Matting will conform to TO 00-25-232, Control and Use of Insulating Matting for
High-Voltage Application. (T-1)
29.3.9. Battery Charging of Powered Industrial Trucks. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.178(g) for
additional guidance.
29.3.9.1. Chargers will be turned off before a battery is connected or disconnected. (T-1)
Failure to follow this practice can cause sparks, arcing, and pitting of contact surfaces of
mating plugs or connectors. Connections between a battery and charger will be through a
mating plug or connector assembly. (T-1) Before performing work on charger plug
contacts, employees will ensure chargers are turned off and disconnected from the input
power source. (T-1)
29.3.9.2. Work will not be conducted on batteries while they are being charged or
discharged unless otherwise specified by the applicable TO. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 21 for
additional guidance.
29.3.9.3. Charger leads will be kept off the floor and out of aisles to avoid damage and
potential tripping hazards. (T-1) Overhead leads will be plainly marked and be high enough
to keep personnel from walking into them. (T-1)

332

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

29.4. Fire Prevention.
29.4.1. Explosive Hazards. Always assume explosive gases exist in and around batteries
unless positive steps have been taken to eliminate them. For this reason, smoking, open flames,
sparks, arcs, and other sources of ignition will be prohibited in the immediate vicinity of
batteries that are being charged or discharged. (T-1)
29.4.2. Protection from Sparks. The following spark ignition sources will not be permitted in
the vicinity of batteries:
29.4.2.1. Shorting out a battery with metal tools or objects. (T-1)
29.4.2.2. Connecting or disconnecting batteries while they are in use. (T-1) Also,
connecting or disconnecting a battery from a charger before turning the charger off, or
while removing or installing a battery in a vehicle when current is flowing in the battery
circuit.
29.4.2.3. Vent cap removal. (T-1) If the battery is equipped with removable vent caps and
removal is required for service by the battery manufacturer, combustible gases will be
flushed out of each cell with a gentle stream of air (the use of proper mechanical
ventilation), or manually (e.g., by fanning each vent opening with a stiff piece of cardboard
or other suitable non-conductor). (T-1) PPE, to include face and hand protection, will be
worn. (T-0)
29.4.2.4. Installing or replacing electrical components in the presence of flammable or
combustible liquids. (T-1) The employee will keep battery ground cable disconnected until
liquids are no longer present. (T-1)
29.4.2.5. Cleaning batteries or terminals. (T-1) Never clean batteries with metal brushes
or other metal devices, which may generate sparks or contact both terminals and short
circuit the cells.
29.5. Ventilation Systems.
29.5.1. The ventilation system will ensure the maximum gas-air mixture generated during
charging is maintained below 25 percent of the lower explosive limit. (T-1) The installation
BE’s HRA will identify ventilation needs for battery shops, rooms, and areas IAW ACGIH’s
Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice for Operations and Maintenance.
(T-1) Note: NFPA 70 requires adequate ventilation to prevent classification of a battery
location as a hazardous (classified) location. Without adequate ventilation, NFPA 70 requires
installation of explosion proof lighting fixtures and receptacles.
29.5.2. Ventilation in battery charging rooms will be sufficient to keep rooms or areas
maintained between 68°F and 85°F, unless permitted by a TO or other appropriate guidance.
(T-1)
29.5.3. Batteries should not be operated, charged, or discharged at temperatures above 120° F
to ensure normal service life. Floor fans with explosion-proof motors can help keep battery
temperature from rising above 120oF.
29.6. Storage and Handling.
29.6.1. Chemical Spills and Environmental Issues.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

333

29.6.1.1. Precautions will be taken to prevent the electrolyte from spilling. (T-1) Batteries
will not be laid on their side unless permitted by TO. (T-1) Batteries will not be transported
without being properly secured with straps or other restraint devices. (T-1)
29.6.1.2. Spill control procedures will be developed and coordinated with the
Occupational Safety office, installation BE, and CE. (T- 1)
29.6.1.3. Dispose of waste batteries and fluids in a manner consistent with approved
hazardous waste disposal procedures and applicable environmental regulations. Contact
installation CE for waste disposal guidance.
29.6.2. Vented lead-acid and vented nickel-cadmium batteries will be maintained and stored
in separate rooms or areas. (T-0) The battery shop supervisor will ensure all visitors entering
the battery shop receive a safety briefing on the hazards located in the battery room or area
before entry is authorized. (T-1) If maintenance is performed while visitors are in the battery
shop, the visitors will also wear required PPE and prescribed in paragraph 29.2.1. (T-1)
29.6.3. Sealed lead-acid batteries and sealed nickel-cadmium batteries do not require a
dedicated battery room or area and can be charged in the same battery room or area with a fivefoot minimum clearance between the different batteries.
29.6.4. The electrolyte in lead-acid batteries contains water. These batteries are subject to
damage in freezing temperatures and will be stored in temperature-controlled areas. (T-1)
