Lower battery charges will allow for freezing at higher temperatures. (T-1)
29.6.5. Batteries of different chemistries will be stored as directed by the occupational safety
office, installation BE, and CE. (T-1) Ensure nickel-cadmium and silver-zinc batteries are
serviced in areas isolated from lead-acid batteries. Batteries of different chemistries must be
kept at least five feet apart when charging, discharging, or being serviced. (T-1)
29.6.6. Batteries received during cold weather, or which cannot be disposed of during warm
weather, will be stored in heated or protected storage areas (temperature-controlled) to avoid
broken cases and/or contamination of storage areas from electrolyte spill caused by freezing
or overheating. (T-1)
29.6.7. Industrial motive power batteries vary widely in the number of cells, size, weight, and
configuration. Handling these batteries is different from handling automobile batteries and will
not be attempted without proper equipment. (T-1) The battery weight is usually stamped on
the steel tray near one of the lifting ears. The battery manufacturer will be consulted if the
weight of a battery is not known and cannot be weighed. (T-1)
29.7. Electrical.
29.7.1. Electrical test equipment will be de-energized prior to being disconnected from circuits
or power sources unless specifically directed by the appropriate TO. (T-1)
29.7.2. Receptacles and lighting switches will be located outside the battery maintenance or
storage area. (T-1) Lighting fixtures will be protected from physical damage by guards or
isolation. (T-1)
29.7.3. Flexible cords used for charging will be suitable for the type of service used and
approved for extra-hard usage. (T-1) Connectors will have a rating not less than the currentcarrying capacity of the cord. (T-1)

334

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
29.7.4. Refer to paragraph 2.3 on the wear of jewelry and eyeglasses/safety goggles.
29.7.5. Refer to Chapter 8 for additional guidance on electrical safety.

29.8. Installation and Care of Vehicle and Support Equipment Batteries.
29.8.1. Batteries will be installed in a level position to prevent leakage of electrolyte. (T-1)
Excessive tightening of hold-down brackets can distort or crack the battery case. Cable clamps
will not be pounded on battery terminals but will be spread until they can be seated properly.
(T-1)
29.8.2. When removing a battery from a vehicle, the battery ground cable will be disconnected
first to prevent the possibility of a spark. (T-1) Upon reinstallation, the battery ground cable
will be installed and tightened last to prevent a short or spark and possible explosion. (T-1)
29.8.3. Some vehicles have two or more batteries, hooked in parallel, with one or more of the
batteries remotely located. When the positive and negative cables are removed from one
battery, the spark potential can still exist until the ground cable has been removed from the
other batteries. Personnel will consult the appropriate TO for proper maintenance and
procedures working on vehicles with two or more batteries. (T-1)
29.8.4. When removing or replacing a battery, if there is a possibility of shorting out the
battery, the battery terminals will be covered with a non-conductive material, such as rubber
or a piece of plywood. (T-1)
29.8.5. Vehicle Battery Jump-Starting. Battery jump-starting procedures will only be
accomplished by trained personnel. (T-1) Follow TO guidance for proper jump-start
procedures. If not available, follow the manufacturer’s instructions.
29.8.6. The battery in most industrial trucks also serves as a counterweight for the carried load.
A replacement battery will never be installed when its service weight is not within the range
of battery weights marked on the truck nameplate. (T-1) A lighter weight battery could change
the truck’s center of gravity and upset a loaded truck.
29.8.7. Visual inspections are usually sufficient to determine the condition of contacts, which
will be cleaned or replaced if they show evidence of oxidation or pitting. (T-1) Excessive
heating of current-carrying parts indicates poor electrical contact which will be investigated
and repaired. (T-1)
29.9. Aircraft Battery and Electrical System Specific Requirements.
29.9.1. Aircraft Electrical System Maintenance. Only trained personnel on aircraft battery and
electrical systems will perform maintenance on aircraft battery and electrical systems. (T-1)
Specialists will follow TO guidance whenever troubleshooting aircraft electrical systems.
(T-1) When the TO does not cover a specific troubleshooting procedure, reference the aircraft
wiring schematics.
29.9.2. Electrical systems will be de-energized TO requirements with a provision to
effectively lockout and tagout the power source. (T-1) When the approved TO procedure
requires work on an energized circuit, other maintenance personnel will be informed. (T-1)
Use an AFTO Form 492, MX Warning Tag, as prescribed in technical data, lock out
procedures, or both. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 21 for additional guidance on hazardous energy
control.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

335

29.9.3. Aircraft Battery Shop Requirements. Batteries will not be charged while in the aircraft,
unless permitted by the aircraft TO. (T-1) Battery chargers will carry at least one permanently
affixed warning sign, which reads: Warning—Keep 25 Feet Clear of Aircraft Engines and
Fuel Tank Areas. (T-1) Refer to NFPA 70 for additional guidance on Class I locations and
aircraft hangars.

336

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 30
MOTOR VEHICLE – OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE

30.1. Scope. This chapter provides information on general safety guidelines for vehicle
maintenance facilities and operations, including refueler maintenance facility operations,
equipment usage, and ventilation and exhaust requirements. Additionally, it covers the use,
handling, and storage of solvents, such as MIL-PRF-680C, Performance Specification:
Degreasing Solvent, welding operations, mobile maintenance, and vehicle repair pit requirements.
Refer to Table 30.1 for a list of additional references for motor vehicle operations and maintenance
guidance.
Table 30.1. Motor Vehicle Guidance—Operation and Maintenance (O&M).
AFI 24-302, Vehicle Management
DAFI 24-301, Ground Transportation
DAFMAN 48-146, Occupational Health Program Management
TO 36-1-191, Technical and Managerial Reference for Vehicle Maintenance
TO 42B5-1-2, Gas Cylinders (Storage Type) Use, Handling and Maintenance

30.2. Specific Hazards.
30.2.1. Vehicle maintenance hazards include hazardous noise, compressed gases, pressurized
air, toxic materials, flammable or explosive mists, aerosols, and vapors. During servicing of
single piece rim wheels, pressurized air in the tire may release suddenly either by bead breakin or by the bead slipping over the rim flange. Brake and clutch assemblies on military vehicles
may contain asbestos, which poses an inhalation hazard. Inhalation of aerosols and vapors from
nearly all fuels, paints, solvents, thinners, cleaning chemicals, strippers, and epoxies can be
hazardous depending upon the material’s toxic characteristics and amount and method of
exposure.
30.2.2. Many chemicals including solvents can cause skin irritations, burns, and/or defatting
of the skin. These chemicals, including solvents, can also be absorbed through the skin and
cause internal damage. Paints containing epoxy or polyurethanes can cause employee
sensitization, which is an allergic reaction to a given substance. Sensitivity usually occurs after
exposure of a few days to a few months, and after sensitivity is established, even small
exposures could produce a severe reaction. Refer to AFI 90-821 for additional guidance and
information on chemical exposures.
30.3. General Safety.
30.3.1. Keep street clothing and protective clothing in separate lockers. (T-0) Work clothing
will be stored in metal lockers, vented at top and bottom. (T-1)
30.3.2. Approved self-closing metal waste containers will be provided wherever rags or waste
are impregnated with flammable or combustible materials and all such rags or waste deposited
therein immediately after use. (T-1) IAW 29 CFR § 1910.107(g)(3), the contents of waste
containers will be disposed of at least once daily or at the end of each shift. (T-0) Contact
installation CE for waste disposal guidance. (T-1)
30.3.3. Backing of government-owned motor vehicles.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

337

30.3.3.1. Government-owned motor vehicles equipped with back-up warning alert
systems will be kept operational and not modified. (T-0)
30.3.3.2. Backing of government-owned motor vehicles and the usage of spotters will be
IAW DAFI 24-301.
30.3.3.2.1. Government-owned motor vehicle operators will immediately stop if they
lose visual contact with the spotter or notice the spotter is dangerously positioned
between the vehicle and another object. (T-1) In such cases, vehicle operators will
secure the vehicle, exit, and make an on-the-spot correction before continuing
operations. (T-1)
30.3.3.2.2. Spotters will position themselves away from the vehicle’s path of travel
where they maintain visual contact with the vehicle operator. (T-1)
30.3.4. Respiratory protection is required where breathing of chemical vapors, mists or fumes
is expected or if working in permit-required confined spaces when the atmosphere is oxygen
deficient or hazardous. A supplied breathing air system with quick access emergency egress
air supply may be needed. Installation BE’s OH Assessment identifies recommended controls
to the unit.
30.4. Occupational Health.
30.4.1. Refer to paragraph 2.2.2 for additional guidance on noise level surveys.
30.4.2. Stationary internal combustion engine-driven equipment will not be operated inside
buildings unless an exhaust system, approved by installation BE, is installed, and used. When
equipment is operated adjacent to buildings, the exhaust will be directed away from the
building. (T-1) Ventilation will be adequate to provide fresh air and prevent accumulation of
fuel vapors, carbon monoxide and other contaminants. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 30.6 for
additional guidance on ventilation.
30.4.3. Jet engine fuels containing toxic aromatics will be handled with the same precaution(s)
as gasoline. (T-1)
30.4.4. First Aid. First aid kits will be approved by medical services IAW paragraph 1.9.
(T-1)
30.5. Facility and Equipment Requirements for Vehicle Maintenance Operations.
30.5.1. Proper layout, spacing and arrangement of equipment and machinery are essential. The
occupational safety office, F&ES Flight, installation BE, and installation CE will coordinate
on all layout plans. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 7 for additional guidance.
30.5.2. Only authorized shop personnel will be allowed on the shop floor, including escorted
personnel. (T-1)
30.5.3. Vehicle operators and mechanics will sound vehicle horns before backing and
intermittently during the entire backing operation unless vehicles are equipped with backup
alarms. (T-1) Vehicle operators will stop and sound the horn prior to entering and leaving the
maintenance shop. (T-1) A spotter will be posted when moving large equipment and vehicles
backwards or in close quarters. (T-1) Refer to paragraph 12.2.7 for additional backing/spotter
guidance.

338

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.5.4. Shop entrances and exits will be clearly marked and lighted to prevent mishaps. (T-0)
Appropriate traffic signs will be posted at entrances and exits. (T-1) To minimize mishaps, a
maximum speed limit of five miles per hour will be enforced in and around shops. (T-1)
30.5.5. Floor slopes, drains and oil/water separators in shops will be constructed and
maintained using guidance from installation CE. (T-1) Oil separators will be cleaned at a
frequency determined by installation CE. (T-1) The shop supervisor will maintain the letter
identifying the cleaning frequency and document compliance. (T-1) Drains will not be
connected to sanitary sewers, except for the effluent from oil separators. (T-0)
30.5.6. When jacking a vehicle, consult the vehicle manuals for proper jacking procedures.
30.5.7. Do not perform work with high risk of injury, unless you are within quick
communication with another person to assist in an emergency.
30.5.8. Machinery and Machine Guarding. Supervisors will maintain technical data, as well
as manufacturer’s operator instructions for all machinery and shop equipment in their area of
responsibility. Machinery and shop equipment will be maintained and operated by trained
personnel. Machine and power transmission guarding not covered in this manual, will be IAW
equipment manufacturer’s instructions and 29 CFR § 1910, Subpart O, Machinery and
Machine Guarding. Refer to Chapter 11 for additional guidance.
30.5.9. Portable Powered and Non-powered Hand Tools. Specific requirements unique to each
tool will be contained in the technical data of that tool. (T-1) To determine required tools and
equipment, consult TO 32-1-2. Allowance Standards 403, General Purpose Tools, Allowance
Standards 457, Vehicle Maintenance, Locomotive Maintenance, and Vehicle Operations, and
General Services Administration catalogs for requirements necessary for a particular
maintenance mission. (T-1) Note: Refer to Chapter 11 for specific requirements on hand
tools.
30.5.10. Roll-up Doors. Refer to paragraph 2.7 for roll up door requirements.

30.6. Fire Prevention.
30.6.1. The F&ES Flight will be consulted for specific shop requirements. (T-1) Refer to
Chapter 6 for additional guidance.
30.6.2. Open Flames. Flame-producing equipment will not be used in refueler maintenance
shops. (T-1) Other vehicle maintenance shops may use flame-producing equipment if all safety
procedures are followed and requirements in Chapter 20 are complied with.
30.7. Ventilation.
30.7.1. Local Engine Exhaust Ventilation.
30.7.1.1. Vehicle maintenance shops will have a suitable exhaust ventilation system either
under the floor with readily available adapter hoses or an overhead system arranged so
connecting hoses can be raised when not in use. (T-1) In shops with under floor systems,
adapters will be used on equipment having vertical exhaust stacks to move the exhaust to
the floor connections. (T-1) Hazardous gas concentrations will be monitored by installation
BE and levels kept under exposure limits listed in DAFMAN 48-146. (T-1) The facility
owner/operator will ensure a proper calibration schedule for the airborne gas analyzer.
(T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

339

30.7.1.2. Exhaust ducts will be protected against mechanical damage. (T-1) In-floor ducts
will be flushed with the floor, level when closed and capable of withstanding vehicle and
equipment traffic or the maintenance operation being performed. (T-1) In-floor ducts will
be equipped with a fluid collection system that prevents drainage into the sanitary sewage
system. (T-1) This can be accomplished by sump pumps, a fuel and/or oil separator, a
combination thereof, or by routing collected fluids to the shop’s main drainage system.
Overhead ducting will be properly supported and located to avoid damage from moving
vehicles and equipment. (T-1)
30.7.1.3. When not in use, ducting will be stowed in a way that prevents damage to the
equipment and does not cause a hazard to personnel. (T-1)
30.7.2. General Engine Exhaust Ventilation. Supervisors will contact installation BE for a
HRA IAW DAFMAN 48-146, if not previously assessed during the unit’s OH Assessment, to
determine the appropriate ventilation required to remove vehicle exhaust gases and fuel vapors.
(T-1)
30.7.3. The following operations or processes may produce airborne contaminants that exceed
exposure limits. The vehicle maintenance supervisor will request an evaluation by installation
BE, who will document the results:
30.7.3.1. Spray painting, spray cleaning, solvent cleaning or stripping operations. (T-1)
30.7.3.2. Open surface tanks (e.g., dip tanks, etc.). (T-1)
30.7.3.3. Welding, soldering, torch cutting, and metalizing. (T-1)
30.7.3.4. Abrasive blasting operations. (T-1)
30.7.3.5. Metal cutting, grinding, buffing, and polishing operations. (T-1)
30.7.3.6. Machine shop operations (e.g., lathes, etc.). (T-1)
30.7.3.7. Battery shops. (T-1)
30.7.3.8. Open tanks on refueler vehicle. (T-1)
30.7.3.9. Brake disservice. (T-1)
30.7.3.10. Clutch linings.
30.7.4. If ventilation system deficiencies exist, contact installation BE to determine interim
control measures. (T-1)
30.7.5. Periodic Testing. IAW DAFMAN 48-146, installation BE will perform periodic
evaluations. (T-1) Installation BE will provide evaluation results to the vehicle maintenance
supervisor. (T-1) If a shop has a manometer or similar piece of equipment that measures the
ventilation system’s air flow, then only the monitoring data needs to be reviewed annually by
installation BE and shop supervisor.
30.8. Cleaning With Solvents.
30.8.1. General Requirements.
30.8.1.1. A metal tank or container approved for flammable or combustible liquid will be
used to immerse articles or materials for cleaning, finishing, treating or similar processes
whenever flammable or combustible cleaning materials are used. (T-1) The metal tank or

340

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
container will be labeled with the contents. (T-1) Care will be taken to avoid solvent
spillage.
30.8.1.2. Care will be exercised in the correct selection of solvents. The lower the
flashpoint, the higher the risk of ignition at low temperatures. The flashpoints of various
solvents differ widely due to their chemical properties. These properties determine
flammability, thus the need to understand the difference between solvents.
30.8.1.3. The following items will not be used as cleaning solvents: acetone, benzene,
carbon tetrachloride, gasoline, isopropyl alcohol, methanol, methyl ethyl ketone, naphtha
(solvent), perchloroethylene, toluene, trichloroethylene, or turpentine. (T-1) Commercial
cleaning solvents or other non-flammable emulsion type cleaning liquids should be
considered for use whenever possible.
30.8.1.4. Flammable cleaning solvents, jet fuel or AVGAS will not be used to clean floors
or clothing. JP-8 may be used for parts cleaning provided the requirements of paragraph
30.9.1.1 and any TO or manufacturer’s restrictions are met.
30.8.1.5. Cleaning rooms will be equipped with adequate mechanical ventilation system
blowers, and exhaust systems that conform to NFPA 91. (T-1) Installation BE will consult
ACGIH’s Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice for Operations and
Maintenance, and DAFMAN 48-146 for guidance. (T-1)
30.8.1.6. There will be no open flames, spark-producing devices, or heated surfaces having
a temperature sufficient to ignite vapors in any vapor area. (T-1)
30.8.1.7. There will be no electrical equipment in the immediate vicinity of cleaning tanks
subject to splashing or dripping of cleaning tank liquids, unless approved for use in that
environment. (T-1)
30.8.1.8. There will be no open flames or spark-producing devices in any floor space
outside a vapor area or within 20 feet (not separated by tight partitions), except as
specifically permitted in NFPA 86. Areas in the vicinity of dip tanks will be kept as clear
of combustible stock and will be kept free of combustible debris. (T-1)
30.8.1.9. Periodic inspection or tests of all dip tank facilities, including associated
ventilation and fire protection equipment, will be made. (T-1) Any defects will be promptly
corrected. (T-1)
30.8.1.10. DANGER - NO SMOKING signs will be conspicuously posted in the vicinity
of dip tanks. (T-1)
30.8.1.11. Areas in the vicinity of dip tanks will have manual fire extinguishers suitable
for combustible liquids. (T-1) Protection systems will be provided for tanks of 150-gallon
capacity or more, or 10 square feet or more of liquid surface area. (T-1) Refer to NFPA 34,
Standard for Dipping and Coating Processes Using Flammable or Combustible Liquids,
for additional guidance.
30.8.2. Specific Requirements.
30.8.2.1. Personal Protective Equipment.
30.8.2.1.1. All personnel will wear protective gloves and keep the inside of gloves
clean and dry while handling parts that are cleaned in degreasing solvent. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

341

Protective gloves and an apron will be worn when acids are poured or dumped. (T-1)
Eye protection with side shields, certified by the manufacturer to be compliant with
ANSI/ISEA Z87.1, will also be worn. (T-1) Refer to installation BE OH Assessment
for specific PPE requirements. Refer to Chapter 14 for additional guidance.
30.8.2.1.2. Employees will advise supervisors if they wear contact lenses. (T-1)
Contact lenses may trap toxic or dangerous substances that are harmful to the eye.
Contact lenses decrease the effectiveness of emergency eyewash fountains and can
compound the severity of an injury. Soft contact lenses may absorb and be
contaminated by chemicals, their vapors and/or fumes. Supervisors will contact
installation BE for advice on contact lens wear while performing work in the shop.
(T-1)
30.8.2.2. Vehicle Engine Cleaning. Follow TO 36-1-191 when cleaning installed engines.
Employees will wear PPE IAW the installation BE’s OH Assessment. (T-1) Refer to
Chapter 14 for additional guidance.
30.8.2.3. Solvent Storage, Handling and Usage. Bulk solvents will be stored in wellventilated rooms, physically separated from other storage and operations. (T-1) Caution
and warning labels will not be removed.
30.8.2.3.1. Stored leaking drums will be removed outdoors immediately so vapors or
liquid will not collect and become a hazard. (T-1) Contact installation CE for proper
disposal guidelines for leaking drums.
30.8.2.3.2. Drip pans will not be used to catch spilled liquids unless these pans drain
into a closed container. Approved containers with the solvent name clearly marked on
the container will be used. (T-1)
30.8.2.3.3. The supervisor will be notified prior to using cleaning tanks located in an
isolated room or facility. (T-1) Supervision should consider a buddy system to ensure
immediate assistance is available if a mishap or emergency occurs.
30.8.2.3.4. Installation BE will determine the adequacy of ventilation in areas where
solvents will be used, prior to their use. (T-1)
30.8.2.4. Oil and Hydraulic Fluid Spills. The owning organization will contain and clean
the spill using the applicable method, such as compound, oil and water absorbent. (T-1)
Contact installation CE for additional guidance.
30.9. Paint Shop.
30.9.1. General Requirements. To ensure safe and efficient operation, all painting activities
will be performed IAW accepted safety standards and Chapter 27.
30.9.2. Specific Requirements.
30.9.2.1. Personal Protective Equipment. The installation BE’s OH Assessment identifies
recommended PPE. Refer to Chapter 14 for additional guidance.
30.9.2.2. Housekeeping. Good housekeeping is essential to safe paint shop operations.
Paint rooms will be kept clean, and their equipment stored in an orderly manner.

342

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.9.2.3. Spray Booths. All high-volume, low-pressure spraying will be accomplished in
a paint booth to minimize health, fire, and explosion hazards. (T-1) The walls of these
booths will be made of fire-resistant material that can be easily and frequently cleaned.
(T-1) Refer to Chapter 27 for additional guidance.
30.9.2.3.1. Walls. Walls and floors of spray booths may be covered with thin paper
bags or a cellophane bag having a thickness of .003 inches or less to protect them from
paint deposits. Replace coverings frequently to prevent the accumulation of deposits.
(T-1) This paper will be removed and disposed of IAW state and federal hazardous
waste regulations. (T-1) Contact installation CE for disposal guidelines. Protective
paper wall coatings will not be used for dry or dusty paint substances that can be
removed from the booths by adequate ventilation. Refer to NFPA 33, Spray Application
Using Flammable and Combustible Materials, for additional guidance. Paint facilities
with overhead sprinklers will have sprinkler heads covered with a paper bag to prevent
paint overspray from clogging the heads. (T-1)
30.9.2.3.2. Direction of Spray. Personnel will always spray paint toward the exhaust
portal to minimize accumulation of harmful mists and vapors in the booth and to
minimize any potential health hazards from airborne contaminants. (T-1) Warning:
Spray guns will never be pointed toward other personnel.
30.9.2.3.3. Hand Work. Ensure all recommendations and warnings listed on the
container label are followed. (T-0) Adequate NIOSH-approved respiratory protection
will be worn during the entire painting process. (T-1) Adequate exhaust ventilation will
be provided in booths when hand-spray painting. (T-1) Refer to installation BE‘s OH
Assessment for recommended controls. Caution: Some paints and additives contain
toxic isocyanates that carry specific respiratory protection requirements. When
applicable, the installation BE’s OH Assessment will outline specific respiratory
protection required for isocyanate exposure. Refer to Chapter 14 and 29 CFR §
1910.134 for additional guidance.
30.9.2.4. Ventilation. Mechanical ventilation will be provided in all paint spray booths to
prevent accumulation of flammable and injurious vapors. (T-0) Refer to the installation
BE’s OH Assessment and 29 CFR § 1910.107, Spray Finishing Using Flammable and
Combustible Materials, for additional guidance.
30.9.2.5. Interlocks. Electrical equipment for electrostatic hand spraying will be
interlocked with spray booth ventilation controls so spraying cannot be done unless the
ventilation fans are operating. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.107 for additional guidance.
30.9.2.6. Ensure hoses do not present a tripping hazard for personnel or are cut by passing
vehicles. (T-0)

30.10. Air Compressors.
30.10.1. General Requirements.
30.10.1.1. Only trained personnel will repair or adjust pressure-regulating equipment.
(T-0)
30.10.1.2. All personnel operating air compressors will be familiar with air compressor
operating instructions. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

343

30.10.2. Specific Requirements.
30.10.2.1. All new air tanks and safety valves will be constructed, installed, and
maintained IAW manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1) The ASME’s code seal is permanently
stamped on tanks meeting this criterion. Refer to ASME’s Boiler and Pressure Vessel
Code, Section VIII, for additional information.
30.10.2.2. Air receivers will be installed so all drains are easily accessible. (T-1) Air
receivers will be installed with sufficient clearance to permit a complete external inspection
and to avoid corrosion of external surfaces. (T-1)
30.10.2.3. A drainpipe and valve will be installed at the lowest point of the air receiver to
remove accumulated oil and water. (T-1) The drain valve will be opened, and the receiver
completely drained daily to prevent accumulation of excessive amounts of liquid in the
receiver. (T-1) Air receivers will be stenciled to reflect this requirement (exclude air
receivers with automatic drains). (T-1)
30.10.2.4. Every air receiver will be equipped with a readily visible pressure indicating
gauge and with one or more spring-loaded safety valves. (T-1) The total relieving capacity
of such valves will prevent receiver pressure exceeding the maximum allowable working
pressure by more than 10 percent. (T-1)
30.10.2.5. No valve of any type will be placed between the air receiver and its safety valve
or valves. (T-1)
30.10.2.6. All safety valves will be tested at regular intervals by installation CE or
designated representative to ensure the valves are operational, and will repair and replace
the safety valves, if faulty/non-operational. (T-1)
30.10.2.7. Installed compressed airline outlets will have the delivered air pressure tagged
or marked showing maximum working pressure. (T-1)
30.10.2.8. Air hoses will not be placed where they may create tripping hazards. All hoses
will be inspected to ensure they are properly connected to pipe outlets before use. (T-1)
Hoses will not be kinked to stop air flow. The control valve will be used to turn off the air.
(T-1) Water hose type clamps or wire connections will not be used to replace or repair
broken fittings.
30.10.2.9. When used for approved cleaning, compressed air will be regulated to less than
30 psi and only when effective chip guarding and eye protection is used. (T-0) Compressed
air will not be used to blow dry parts since this process can increase the inhalation hazards
to employees. (T-1)
30.10.2.10. Vehicles will not be parked or left running near air intakes of compressors
used to supply breathing air. (T-1)
30.10.3. Compressed Air. Compressed air is used in many shop operations: spray painting,
tire inflation, fuel line cleaning, and others. Compressed air can be used safely when handled
with care and according to accepted safety standards. Eye protection will be used when
cleaning with compressed air. (T-0)
30.10.3.1. A vacuum system or water hose will be used to remove dust, etc., from large
surfaces. (T-1)

344

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.10.3.2. Compressed air will not be used to clean clothing or body parts.
30.10.3.3. Compressed air will not be used to “spin-dry” or “air-dry” bearings, unless
permitted by the applicable TO.
30.10.3.4. Never point compressed air toward other personnel.
30.10.3.5. Assume all brakes contain asbestos, unless proven otherwise. Personnel will
utilize the OSHA Wet Method, IAW 29 CFR § 1910.1001, Appendix F, Work Practices
and Engineering Controls for Automotive Brake and Clutch Inspection, Disassembly,
Repair and Assembly. (T-0) Cleaning materials will be disposed of IAW local guidance.
(T-1) Consult installation CE for further disposal guidelines. Note: Never use compressed
air to clean clutch and brake assemblies.

30.11. Compressed Gas Cylinders. Vehicle maintenance shops use acetylene and oxygen in
welding, R-12 and Freon as refrigerants, and liquid petroleum gas (butane-propane) as a motor
vehicle fuel. These cylinders may contain pressures up to 3,600 psi. Cylinders are designed and
constructed IAW DOT regulations and bear the DOT identification. This stamping is generally
found near the neck of the cylinder. Subsequent re-inspections will be stamped in the same area.
(T-0) The cylinder contents will be legibly marked on each cylinder in large letters. (T-0) Nongovernment owned cylinders will be tagged or otherwise marked to indicate cylinder contents.
(T-0) Refer to Chapter 19, MIL-STD-101, 29 CFR §§ 1910.101, Compressed Gases, 1910.102,
Acetylene, 1910.103, Hydrogen, 1910.104, Oxygen, 1910.105, Nitrous Oxide, and applicable TOs
for additional guidance.
30.12. Lifting Devices.
30.12.1. Inspection. Many lifting device problems can be detected prior to use if properly
inspected. Periodic inspections will be performed by qualified inspection or maintenance
personnel. (T-1) Test and certification of cranes and hoists will be performed by qualified
inspectors. Certification, inspection, and test reports will be available on the premises where
the crane or hoist is located, in the supervisor’s office or in a central maintenance shop. (T-1)
Refer to Chapter 12 for additional inspection guidance.
30.12.2. Responsibilities. Responsibilities for periodic inspection, records, maintenance, and
test of lifting devices and separate lifting aids will be as follows:
30.12.2.1. Permanently Installed Equipment (Real Property Installed Equipment).
Installation CE will be responsible for Real Property Installed Equipment inspection
requirements. (T-1) Real property installed-equipment includes installed equipment
attached to and made part of buildings and structures, e.g., air conditioning/heating
systems, but not movable equipment such as plant equipment.
30.12.2.2. Mobile Equipment. The vehicle maintenance officer and/or superintendent will
be responsible for mobile equipment inspection requirements. (T-1)
30.12.2.3. Fixed Shop and Portable Equipment (Equipment Authorized Inventory Data).
The owning organization will be responsible for fixed shop and portable equipment
inspection requirements. (T-1)
30.12.2.4. Hoists and Cranes. Refer to specific technical data. If none is available, consult
installation CE or occupational safety representatives. Refer to Chapter 12 for additional
guidance.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

345

30.12.3. Specific Requirements.
30.12.3.1. Hydraulic Vehicle Lifts.
30.12.3.1.1. When operating hydraulic lifts, employees will:
30.12.3.1.1.1. Stand to one side of the vehicle as it is driven on or off the lift. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.1.2. Ensure the load rests squarely on the lift. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.1.3. Close the vehicle doors, hood, and trunk and make sure there is no
one inside the vehicle prior to raising it, except to facilitate repairs. (T-1) This
applies to vehicle lifts equipped with automatic locking devices that will not allow
the vehicle to descend with loss of power.
30.12.3.1.1.4. Know the load limits of the lift and adapter and ensure it is not
overloaded. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.1.5. Not lock the hoist controls in the open or shut position but operate
them manually. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.1.6. Ensure vehicle lifts are equipped with a safety device to prevent
accidental lowering. (T-1) The device can be a simple mechanism such as a safety
leg, which locks in a vertical position as the lift is raised, or a restricted orifice
device, which permits controlled lowering during hydraulic failure. As an added
safety feature, air-oil operated hydraulic lifts will be equipped with a lock which
prevents raising by air if the oil supply is low. (T-1) This is a removable device
which prevents compressed air entering the lift cylinder assembly. The arrangement
prevents the plungers being raised above the oil supply in the air-oil reservoir.
30.12.3.1.2. Roll-on lifts will be equipped with stop chocks to prevent the vehicle
moving while the lift is hoisting, lowering, or in the elevated position. (T-1) Stop
chocks will be automatic (springing into position when the vehicle is on the lift). (T-1)
30.12.3.1.3. All vehicle lifts will be marked with the manufacturer’s name, lift capacity
and date of installation. (T-1) These markings will be stamped or etched on a metal
plate permanently attached to the lift in a position where it can be inspected. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.4. All vehicle lifts will be equipped with “dead man” controls, which
automatically return to NEUTRAL or OFF when released by an operator. (T-1)
Controls will be located near the lift. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5. A lift will not be used, and a hazard warning tag will be attached when it:
30.12.3.1.5.1. Jerks or jumps when raised. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5.2. Slowly settles downward after being raised. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5.3. Slowly rises, either when in use or when not in use. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5.4. Comes down very slowly. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5.5. Blows oil out of the exhaust line. (T-1)
30.12.3.1.5.6. Leaks oil at the packing gland. (T-1)
30.12.3.2. Electric Vehicle Lifts.

346

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.12.3.2.1. Inspect the lift daily; do not operate it if it has damaged parts which render
it unsafe. (T-1) Repairs with original equipment manufacturer parts, or suitable
substitute. (T-3)
30.12.3.2.2. Operating controls are designed to close when released. Do not block open
or override them.
30.12.3.2.3. Do not exceed the manufacturer’s rated capacity as shown on the lift
nameplate. (T-1)
30.12.3.2.4. Do not operate the lift if any safety device is inoperative. (T-1)
30.12.3.2.5. Allow only trained and authorized personnel to position the vehicle and
operate the lift. (T-1)
30.12.3.2.6. Never raise the vehicle with anyone inside, except to facilitate repairs.
(T-0) Do not allow anyone under the vehicle when raising or lowering the lift. (T-1)
When operating the lift, stand outside of it and keep hands clear of moving parts. (T-0)
Note: This applies to vehicle lifts with automatic locking devices that do not allow the
vehicle to descend with loss of power.
30.12.3.2.7. Keep the lift area free of obstructions, grease, oil, trash, and other debris.
(T-1)
30.12.3.2.8. Before driving the vehicle over the lift, position arms and supports to
provide unobstructed clearance. Do not hit or run over lift arms, adapters, or axle
supports. This could damage the lift or vehicle.
30.12.3.2.9. Position the vehicle on the lift to ensure contact with the manufacturers
recommended lifting points. (T-1) Raise the lift until supports contact the vehicle.
Check supports for secure contact with vehicle. Raise the lift to desired working height.
Raise the lift high enough for the locking device to engage whenever someone is
working under the vehicle.
30.12.3.2.10. Do not attempt to move the vehicle when it is lifted off the ground.
30.12.3.2.11. With some vehicles, removal or installation of components may cause a
critical shift in the center of gravity and result in raised vehicle instability. Refer to the
vehicle manufacturer’s service manual for recommended procedures when vehicle
components are removed.
30.12.3.2.12. When performing maintenance on electrical lifts, ensure electrical power
is disconnected unless required for checking unit operation.
30.12.3.2.13. Before lowering lift, ensure tool trays, stands, etc., are removed from
under the vehicle and vicinity of the lift. (T-1) Release locking devices before
attempting to lower the lift.
30.12.3.2.14. Lower lift slowly.
30.12.3.2.15. Before removing vehicle from the lift area, position lift arms and
supports to provide an unobstructed exit.
30.12.3.3. Jacks. Vehicle maintenance personnel will not use hydraulic floor jacks, post
jacks, or mechanical jacks to support vehicles while repair is accomplished. The vehicle

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

347

will be blocked and placed on approved axle or frame stands before a repair operation is
started. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.1. The rated load of the jack will be legibly and permanently marked in a
prominent location by casting, stamping, or other suitable means. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.2. Shop personnel will not use a leaking or faulty jack. (T-1) Defective jacks
will be taken out of service immediately, tagged, and not used until repaired. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.3. Shop personnel will position jacks under vehicles to ensure the cap is
properly located to preclude the jack slipping after the load is applied. (T-1) A flat piece
of wood, thick enough to withstand the load, may be placed between the jack cap and
vehicle lift point to help prevent the jack cap from slipping.
30.12.3.3.4. All jacks will be lubricated at regular intervals using lubricants
recommended by the manufacturer. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5. Improper jacking can cause serious injury or property damage. The
following jacking procedures will be used:
30.12.3.3.5.1. Equipment will be chocked prior to jacking. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.2. Jack stands will be used any time equipment is under extended
maintenance. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.3. Equipment will be removed from jacks as soon as possible. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.4. Equipment will be jacked to the minimum height required for the
task. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.5. Personnel will only remain under a jacked unit for the time required
to place axle or frame stands for support. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.6. Personnel will not position any portion of themselves under the tire
or wheel of jacked equipment. (T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.7. Supervisors are responsible for ensuring jacks and jack stands of
sufficient capacity are available and utilized.
30.12.3.3.5.8. Employees will ensure placement of jack does not cause damage.
(T-1)
30.12.3.3.5.9. Jacks and jack stands will be inspected and maintained IAW
equipment TOs or manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910,
Subpart P, Hand and Portable Powered Tools and Other Hand-Held Equipment,
for additional guidance.
30.12.3.3.5.10. Defective jack stands will be taken out of service immediately and
tagged for disposition. (T-1)
30.13. Wheel and Tire Maintenance.
30.13.1. Employee Training.
30.13.1.1. Extensive damage to equipment and serious injury to personnel may result from
careless or improper demounting, mounting, and inflation of tires on both single piece and
multi-piece rim wheels. To ensure safety, all personnel involved in wheel dismantling or

348

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
buildup operations will use applicable TOs, rim manuals, OSHA 3421, Servicing MultiPiece and Single-Piece Rim Wheels, and this publication for the wheel involved. (T-1)
Review procedures prior to starting tire maintenance operations.
30.13.1.2. Supervisors will ensure all employees who service multi-piece or single piece
rims are trained in the proper servicing techniques and practices. (T-0) Employee’s
proficiency will be established by demonstrating their knowledge and use of the
information contained in charts, rim manuals, TOs and this publication. (T-0)
30.13.1.3. Supervisors will ensure each employee demonstrates and maintains the ability
to service wheels safely, including the following tasks:
30.13.1.3.1. Inspection of wheel components. (T-0)
30.13.1.3.2. Mounting of tires, including inflation within a restraining device or other
safeguard required by this chapter. (T-0)
30.13.1.3.3. Use of the restraining device, barrier, and other equipment required by
this chapter. (T-0)
30.13.1.3.4. Handling of rim wheels. (T-0)
30.13.1.3.5. Inflation of tire when a rim wheel is mounted on the vehicle. (T-1)
30.13.1.4. The supervisor will evaluate each employee’s ability to perform these tasks and
to service rim wheels safely and will provide additional training as necessary to ensure
each employee maintains proficiency. (T-1)
30.13.2. Tire Servicing Equipment.
30.13.2.1. Supervisors will ensure servicing multi-piece rim wheels will be completed in
a cage or rack to contain components during explosive separation. (T-0)
30.13.2.2. When servicing single piece rim wheels, the employee will use a restraining
device or barrier, except where the rim wheel is bolted to the vehicle during inflation. (T-0)
30.13.2.3. Supervisors will ensure cages or racks meet the following requirements:
30.13.2.3.1. Each cage or rack will withstand the maximum force transferred to it
during an explosive rim and tire separation occurring at 150 percent of maximum tire
specification pressure for the type of wheel being serviced. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.2. Cages and racks will prevent rim wheel components being thrown outside
or beyond the cage or rack for any rim wheel positioned within the device. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.3. A cage or rack will not contain a solid flat surface against which the rim
wheel can lie or lean during inflation, such as the bed or table of a tire changing
machine. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4. Cages and racks will be visually inspected prior to each day’s use and
after any explosion or explosive separation of the rim wheel components. (T-1) Any
Cage or rack exhibiting damage such as the following defects will be immediately
removed from service:
30.13.2.3.4.1. Cracks at welds. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4.2. Cracked or broken components. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

349

30.13.2.3.4.3. Bent or sprung components caused by mishandling, abuse, tire
explosion or rim wheel separation. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4.4. Pitting of components due to excessive corrosion. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4.5. Rust or other structural damage. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4.6. Inoperative air pressure warning light or gauge. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.4.7. Leaking, cracked, or deteriorating hoses. (T-0)
30.13.2.3.5. NDI techniques may be used to verify existence of cracks or other damage
to welds whenever a tire/wheel failure occurs inside a cage or rack, a weld is found to
be severely rusted or whenever a weld’s integrity is in doubt. Accomplish a one-time
NDI of all welds on locally manufactured cages racks.
30.13.2.3.6. Cages or racks removed from service and requiring structural repair, such
as component replacement or re-welding, will not be returned to service until certified,
by the manufacturer or a Registered Professional Engineer. (T-0) This certification will
ensure the cage or rack will withstand the maximum force transferred during a rim
wheel separation at 150 percent of the maximum tire specification pressure for the rim
wheel being serviced or as required by the applicable TO, whichever is greater. (T-0)
30.13.2.4. The supervisor will ensure a hose assembly consisting of the following
components is used for inflating rim wheels:
30.13.2.4.1. A clip-on chuck. (T-0)
30.13.2.4.2. A sufficient length of hose to allow the employee to stand outside the
trajectory. (T-0)
30.13.2.4.3. An in-line valve with a pressure gauge or an adjustable regulator. (T-0)
30.13.2.5. An approved tire inflator cage appropriately sized for tires being serviced will
be used for inflating multi-piece wheels. (T-0) A regulator gauge, shutoff valve, service
hose, and lock-on type chuck will be used with the cage to control the air when inflating.
(T-0) In addition, a bleed valve will be used to relieve pressure in the hose when the tire is
filled. (T-0) Refer to TO 36-1-191 for additional guidance.
30.13.2.6. A current OSHA 3421, along with OSHA charts entitled “Demounting and
Mounting Procedures for Truck/Bus Tires,” and “Multi-piece Rim Matching Chart,” will
be available and posted in the service area. (T-1)
30.13.2.7. A current rim manual and TO 36-1-191, containing instructions for the types of
wheels being serviced, will be available in the service area. (T-1)
30.13.2.8. The supervisor will ensure only tools recommended in the rim manual for the
type of wheel being serviced are used to service rim wheels. (T-0) Warning: Failure to
observe precautions outlined in this publication may result in faulty positioning of the tire
and/or rim parts, causing the assembly to burst with explosive force sufficient to cause
serious physical injury or death.
30.13.3. Wheel Component Acceptability.

350

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.13.3.1. Multi-piece wheel components will not be interchanged except as provided in
the charts or applicable rim manual. (T-0)
30.13.3.2. Multi-piece wheel components and single piece wheels will be inspected prior
to assembly. (T-0) Any wheel part bent out of shape, pitted from corrosion, broken, or
cracked will not be used and will be rendered unserviceable and discarded. (T-0) Replace
damaged or leaky valves. (T-0)
30.13.3.3. Rim flanges, rim gutters, rings, bead seating surfaces, and bead areas of tires
will be free of any dirt, surface rust, scale or loose, or flaked rubber build-up prior to
mounting and inflation. (T-0)
30.13.3.4. The size, including bead diameter and tire/wheel widths, and type of both the
tire and wheel will be checked for compatibility prior to assembly of the rim wheel. (T-0)
30.13.4. Safe Operating Procedure – Multi-piece Rim Wheels. The supervisor will establish a
safe operating procedure for servicing multi-piece rim wheels and will ensure employees are
instructed in and follow that procedure. (T-0) The procedure will include, at least, the
following elements:
30.13.4.1. Tires will be completely deflated by removing the valve core before a rim wheel
is removed from the axle. (T-1) If the axle and rim are secured by wedges and lug nuts,
loosen the lug nuts out to the end of the stud, but do not remove. Lightly tap on wedges to
break them free. When all wedges are broken free, lug nuts can be removed. Wedges can
become dangerous projectiles if these procedures are not followed. Employees will use
mechanical devices, such as dollies, to help them remove or mount large, heavy tires. (T-1)
30.13.4.2. A nonflammable rubber lubricant will be applied to bead and rim mating
surfaces during assembly of the rim wheel and inflation of the tire, unless the wheel or tire
manufacturer advises against its use. (T-0)
30.13.4.3. Tires mounted on multi-piece rims will only be inflated when constrained by a
restraining device or placed in a tire cage. (T-0) Tires on multi-piece rims mounted on
vehicles that are more than 20 percent under-inflated will not be re-inflated until inspected
by a qualified person and the tire certified safe to re-inflate. (T-0) Safeguards, such as
chains or remote-control inflation systems, will be used to encase the wheel and tire during
the re-inflation process. (T-0) Supervisors will ensure no personnel are in the trajectory
area during tire re-inflation. (T-0) Heat will not be applied to a multi-piece rim. (T-1)
30.13.5. Safe Operating Procedure – Single Piece Rim Wheels. The supervisor will establish
a safe operating procedure for servicing single piece rim wheels and will ensure employees are
instructed in and follow that procedure. (T-0) The procedure will include as a minimum the
following elements:
30.13.5.1. Tires will be completely deflated by removing the valve core before
demounting. (T-0) Mechanical devices, such as dollies, will be used to mount or remove
large, heavy tires. (T-0)
30.13.5.2. Mounting and demounting of tire will be performed only from the narrow ledge
side of wheel. (T-0) Avoid damage to tire beads while mounting tires on wheels. (T-0)
Tires will be mounted only on compatible wheels of matching bead diameter. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

351

30.13.5.3. A nonflammable rubber lubricant will be applied to bead and wheel mating
surfaces before assembly of the rim wheel and inflation of tire. (T-0)
30.13.5.4. If a bead expander is used to seat the beads, it will be removed before the valve
core is installed and as soon as the rim wheel becomes airtight (the tire bead slips onto the
bead seat). (T-0)
30.13.5.5. Tires may be inflated only when contained within a cage or rack, positioned
behind a barrier, or bolted on the vehicle with lug nuts fully tightened. (T-0) Tires will
never be serviced above the pressure stamped on the tire sidewall unless the manufacturer
recommends a higher pressure. (T-1)
30.13.5.6. When inflating a tire, employees will not place a rim wheel where it will rest
against or within one foot of any flat solid surface, as measured from the sidewall. (T-0)
30.13.5.7. Tires will not be inflated to more than their recommended operating pressure.
(T-1) The proper tire inflation pressure, tire size and load range can be found on the vehicle
information/data plates or stenciled on the door jamb/glove box or as specified in the TO
or owner’s manual. If this information is not available, TO 36-1-191 will be used to obtain
the recommended tire pressure. (T-1)
30.13.5.8. Employees will stay out of the trajectory when inflating a tire. (T-0)
30.13.5.9. If the tire beads are not fully seated by the time the tire is inflated to its
recommended pressure, the tire will be deflated, and rim wheel disassembled. (T-1) The
wheel and tire will be rechecked for compatibility, re-lubricated, repositioned and then reinflated IAW TOs and manufacturer’s requirements. (T-1)
30.13.5.10. No heat will be applied to a single-piece wheel. (T-0)
30.13.5.11. Cracked, broken, bent, or otherwise damaged wheels will not be reworked,
welded, brazed, or otherwise heated. (T-0)
30.13.5.12. High pressure air will not be used to inflate tires. (T-1)
30.13.5.13. Rims will be inspected and maintained IAW individual equipment
manufacturer’s rim manuals or TO 36-1-191. (T-1)
30.13.6. Demounting, Mounting, and Inflating All Types of Tires.
30.13.6.1. Personnel will:
30.13.6.1.1. Respect the potential power and explosive force of air under pressure.
Serious mishaps have resulted from lack of awareness of the explosive potential of
compressed air.
30.13.6.1.2. Make sure all tools are in good condition – not damaged, dented, or
deformed.
30.13.6.1.3. Remove valve core and exhaust all air from the tire (or tires, in the case
of a dual assembly), before demounting. (T-1) Probe the valve stem with a wire as a
final check to ensure the valve is not obstructed by foreign material. (T-1) Caution:
Do not stand in front of valve opening, as dirt particles may be blown into eyes.
30.13.6.1.4. Block vehicle so it cannot roll forward or backward after it is lifted. (T-1)

352

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.13.6.1.5. Place large hardwood blocks under the jack. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.6. After the load has been raised, it shall be cribbed, blocked, or otherwise
secured at once. (T-0)
30.13.6.1.7. Check rim diameter to ensure it matches rim diameter molded on tire.
(T-1)
30.13.6.1.8. Clean and inspect used rim parts. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.9. Use new tubes and new flaps in new tires. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.10. Inspect inside of tire for loose cords, cuts, penetrating objects, or other
damage. Tires that are damaged beyond simple repair will be removed from service.
(T-1) Remove dirt, debris, and liquids from inside of tire before tube is installed.
30.13.6.1.11. Lubricate with approved rubber lubricant. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.12. Use a clip-on chuck and extension hose with remote control valve and
pressure gauge, long enough to allow you to stand to one side – not in front of the
assembly – during inflation. (T-0)
30.13.6.1.13. Center tire properly on rim before inflating. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.14. Securely lock wheel down in safety cage or rack before attempting to
inflate tire to seat beads. (T-0)
30.13.6.1.15. Position the vehicle crane boom in the center of the hub prior to inflation
of off-the-road tires in field service work. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.16. Check for proper flange and lock ring seating. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.17. Adjust air pressure to manufacturer’s recommended cold operating
pressure after beads have been seated. (T-1)
30.13.6.1.18. Inspect valve cores for proper air retention. (T-1) Replace damaged or
leaky cores. (T-1)
30.13.6.2. Personnel will not:
30.13.6.2.1. Work on tire and rim assemblies until they review applicable safety
practices and procedures. (T-1)
30.13.6.2.2. Loosen lug nuts on dual-wheeled equipment with split or multi-piece rims
until all air is exhausted from both tires. (T-1) A broken or cracked rim part under
pressure may blow apart and seriously injure or kill if lugs are removed before air is
exhausted.
30.13.6.2.3. Apply heat or do repair work on an inflated tire, rim, or wheel assembly.
(T-1) Heat can increase tire air pressure sufficient to burst the tire or rim.
30.13.6.2.4. Re-inflate a tire that has been run flat or seriously under-inflated without
demounting that tire and checking tire and tube for damage. (T-1)
30.13.6.2.5. Mix rim parts of different manufacturers unless approved by those
manufacturers. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

353

30.13.6.2.6. Rework, weld, heat, or braze rim parts. (T-1) Always replace damaged
parts with same size, type and make.
30.13.6.2.7. Reuse tubes or flaps that have buckled or creased. (T-1)
30.13.6.2.8. Use a tube in a tire larger or smaller than that for which the tube was
designed. (T-1)
30.13.6.2.9. Inflate beyond recommended bead seating pressure. (T-1)
30.13.6.2.10. Stand over tire when inflating. (T-1)
30.14. Maintenance Operations.
30.14.1. Body Shops.
30.14.1.1. Wear protective gloves when exposed to jagged edges of mangled fenders,
decks and quarter panels. (T-1)
30.14.1.2. Wear full protective plastic face shield plus safety glasses or goggles when
cutting or grinding glass. (T-1) When handling large sheets of glass, gloves will be used
for a better grip and to keep hands from being cut. (T-1)
30.14.2. Welding in Vehicle Maintenance Shops.
30.14.2.1. General Precautions.
30.14.2.1.1. Inspect all equipment before use. (T-1) Equipment with worn, leaky, or
burned hoses or damaged cables and connections will not be used.
30.14.2.1.2. Never weld or cut gas tanks, oil barrels, or drums without purging and
inerting first. (T-1)
30.14.2.1.3. Weld behind flame resistant screens or in booths to protect other
employees from flying sparks and flash burns. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 20 for additional
guidance.
30.14.2.1.4. Always provide adequate ventilation. (T-0) Arrange work so air
movement pulls fumes away from the breathing zone. (T-0)
30.14.2.1.5. Wear clean, oil free, flame-resistant clothing while welding. (T-0) Wear
protective gloves and apron (hearing protection may be required for some welding
operations). (T-0)
30.14.2.1.6. Wear eye protection, as specified in Chapter 20. (T-1)
30.14.2.1.7. Provide a suitable fire extinguisher in the vicinity of the welding area.
(T-1) Mount fire extinguishers (if space permits) on portable welding carts used in
locations where adequate fire extinguishers are not available. (T-1)
30.14.2.1.8. Always mark hot work either with a sign or with chalk on the work itself.
(T-1)
30.14.2.1.9. Store welding equipment securely when not in use. (T-1)
30.14.2.1.10. Have welding tasks evaluated by installation BE to determine the need
for (and type of) respiratory protection. (T-1)

354

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.14.2.2. Gas Welding.
30.14.2.2.1. Store cylinders in areas that are isolated, protected, ventilated, dry and
removed from any heat source. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.2. Store oxygen cylinders and welding gas cylinders at least 20 feet apart or
separate them with a flame-resistant barrier at least 5 feet high when not connected for
use. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.3. Keep oxygen cylinders at least 35 feet away from oil, oil pits, and grease.
(T-1)
30.14.2.2.4. Keep cylinder valve caps in place when cylinders are not connected for
use. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.5. Shut cylinder valves off tightly when not in use. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.6. Do not use acetylene with tank pressure less than 15 psi.
30.14.2.2.7. Always leave the shutoff wrench on the valve stem. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.8. Secure all tanks with a chain or fire-resistant strap. (T-1)
30.14.2.2.9. Ensure cylinder contents are identified before use. (T-0)
30.14.2.3. Electric Arc Welding.
30.14.2.3.1. Carefully spread-out cables while performing work. (T-1) Avoid placing
cables in walkway areas to prevent tripping hazards. (T-1)
30.14.2.3.2. Join ground and electrode cables with connectors designed for that
purpose. (T-1)
30.14.2.3.3. Do not allow splices in the cable within 10 feet of the electrode holder.
(T-1)
30.14.2.3.4. To avoid electrical shock, check all connections, ground the work piece,
do not weld in wet locations, and do not use wet machines until they are dry and tested.
(T-1)
30.14.2.3.5. Store electrode holders in a safe location away from objects which
conduct electricity. (T-1)
30.14.3. Mobile Maintenance. Vehicles used routinely for mobile maintenance will include as
part of their equipment: baking soda for neutralizing acid spills, adequate fire extinguishers,
container of water and eye and/or face protection for use by individual performing vehicle
jump start procedures. (T-1) In cold climate regions, efforts will be made to prevent containers
of water from freezing.
30.14.4. Air Conditioning Maintenance. Gas and liquid coolants used in automotive air
conditioners will be handled with care, especially those stored under pressure. Wear eye and
face protection and gloves when servicing or purging these systems. (T-1)
30.14.5. Radiator Maintenance.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

355

30.14.5.1. Never open a pressurized radiator while the engine is hot and use appropriate
PPE when boiling out radiators. (T-1) The cleaning solutions contain caustic chemicals
that will burn if splashed on the skin or face. Flush the skin with water if splashed.
30.14.5.2. Wear protective gloves when handling a damaged radiator because it may have
jagged metal edges. (T-1)
30.14.6. Fuel Tank Repairs.
30.14.6.1. Remove vehicles with leaking fuel tanks from the shop immediately. (T-1)
Ground the siphon tank and pump out remaining fuel into a safety can. (T-1)
30.14.6.2. If the tank requires welding, cutting, or soldering, clean out all fuel and fuel
vapors. (T-1) The best way to do this is by steam cleaning. Steam the tank, both inside and
outside, for at least 10 minutes. Flush out the tank with hot water for five minutes. Then
dry the inside and outside thoroughly, using warm air. After cleaning, check for fuel odor
and, if needed, repeat the cleaning process. Test the tank for vapors prior to any welding.
30.14.7. Refueler Vehicle Maintenance Areas.
30.14.7.1. Open flames and spark-producing devices are prohibited within 50 feet of the
refueling maintenance shop when a refueling vehicle is undergoing maintenance and fuel
vapors are likely to be present. There will be no smoking within 50 feet of a refueler
maintenance building. (T-1) Danger signs will be posted in the immediate area and worded
as follows: NO OPEN FLAME OR IGNITION SOURCE BEYOND THIS POINT. (T-1)
Refer to Chapter 18 for additional guidance.
30.14.7.2. Static Grounding.
30.14.7.2.1. Aircraft refuelers will have a serviceable, static ground system. (T-1)
Refueling equipment in a refueler maintenance facility or undergoing maintenance will
be always connected to a serviceable ground, except when the vehicle is in motion.
(T-1) Refueling maintenance support equipment will be grounded. (T-1)
30.14.7.2.2. Storage bins will be grounded if located inside the maintenance bay. (T-1)
30.14.7.2.3. Ramp ground rods will have resistance values of 10,000 ohms or less.
(T-1) These static grounds do not require retesting unless damaged or if the system has
a deficiency. Any static ground which measures greater than 10,000 ohms will be
removed or replaced. (T-1) Static ground test data will be maintained by installation
CE. (T-1)
30.14.7.2.4. Fuel nozzle static grounding will be IAW TO 00-25-172. Only approved
75 amperes ground clips will be used IAW TO 00-25-172. (T-1)
30.14.7.2.5. Alligator clips will not be used to ground or bond aircraft and support
equipment. Only specified ground wire will be used in the shop or on refueling
equipment. (T-1) Ground cables will be inspected weekly for broken strands. (T-1)
Repaired or newly constructed grounding cable assemblies will be checked for
continuity. (T-1) User will check bonding devices on all aircraft refueling units and
associated equipment to ensure proper bonding prior to use. (T- 1)
30.14.7.3. The supporting Air Force weather organization, IAW the weather support
document, will advise the Fuels Management Flight of adverse or mission-limiting

356

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
weather, such as thunderstorms, lightning, strong winds, heavy rain, snow, or freezing
precipitation. (T-1) All maintenance on fuel systems of fuel servicing vehicles will cease
work conducted outside. The supporting Air Force weather organization will advise the
Fuels Service Center when the adverse weather advisory is terminated. (T-1) Refer to
Chapter 3 for additional information on weather.
30.14.7.4. Personnel will ground themselves prior to entering a refueler maintenance shop.
(T-1) (T-1) Personnel can ground themselves by touching their hand to the bare metal.
30.14.7.5. Certain areas in the refueler vehicle maintenance facility may become
hazardous when working on fuel systems, fuel leaks occur, or excessive vapors exist due
to venting. If the working environment has flammable vapors greater than 20 percent of
the lower explosive limit. Shut down the operation until the vapors are removed to maintain
a safe working environment. (T-1)
30.14.7.6. Whenever major maintenance is required to the main tank, all fuel and fuel
vapors will be cleaned out from the tank pumping system and filter separator and the tank
purged prior to any maintenance. (T-1) Cleaning procedures will comply with TO 36Y311-1, Removal of Rust and Sediment from Fuel and Oil Servicing Truck and Trailer Tanks
and Application of Coating, Interior, Fuel and Water Resistant. (T-1) Follow the
precautions outlined in the applicable TO of the equipment being serviced and permitrequired confined space requirements found in Chapter 23. (T-1)
30.14.7.7. When fuel filter elements are changed, old filters will be disposed of IAW
installation CE regulations. (T-1)
30.14.7.8. A minimum of two personnel, one qualified in the task to be performed and the
other a safety observer to provide assistance, will be available in the refueling maintenance
site whenever repairs are done on a vehicle fuel system. (T-1) Permit-required confined
space entries will be IAW Chapter 23 and hazardous energy control procedures will be
conducted IAW Chapter 21. (T-0) For minor maintenance after duty hours, the Vehicle
Flight Commander may waive, in writing, the two-person policy if all safety precautions
are taken and a qualified 5-skill level mechanic does the work. A vehicle operator for the
type of equipment being repaired may be the second person. Refer to AFI 24-302 for
additional guidance.
30.14.7.9. Personnel performing maintenance on fuel trucks will not wear shoes with
exposed nails or metal plates due to sparking risk.
30.14.7.10. All tools used in the refueling maintenance shop will be approved for such
use. (T-1)
30.14.7.11. Axle stands, rated at least 10 tons, will be used for axle support on refuelers.
(T-1)
30.14.7.12. Hydraulic jacks rated at least 10 tons will be used when lifting refueling
vehicles. (T-1)
30.14.8. Refueler Maintenance Buildings.
30.14.8.1. An empty refueling vehicle contains fuel vapors that could create a fire or
explode with contact by heat sources. Procedures will be developed prior to work in such
spaces to eliminate the hazard prior to work start. (T-1) Except under conditions listed

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

357

below, no other vehicular equipment will be serviced or repaired in refueler maintenance
shops when fuel servicing vehicles are present. (T-1)
30.14.8.1.1. Vehicle(s) containing only JP-5, JP-8, JP-10, or other high flashpoint fuels
may be serviced or repaired in refueler maintenance shops with fuel servicing vehicles
present.
30.14.8.1.2. Vehicle(s) containing only JP-4, AV-gas, Mo-gas or other low flashpoint
fuels may be serviced or repaired in refueler maintenance shops when no other fuel
servicing vehicles are present.
30.14.8.1.2.1. For remote/austere locations, commanders may authorize minor
maintenance (e.g., lubrication, oil and filter changes, headlight and tire changes) in
facilities not designed for refueler maintenance if the refueler vehicle is used to
transport.
30.14.8.1.2.2. Commanders will perform and document an appropriate risk
assessment prior to introducing other vehicles into a refueler maintenance facility
or performing maintenance on refuelers in other than a refueler maintenance
facility. (T-1) Risk assessments will be coordinated with F&ES Flight and the
occupational safety office. (T-1)
30.14.8.1.3. Because of the large number of fueling vehicles and carts normally
assigned, a DAF installation will have a separately sited maintenance and repair
facility, unless approved by AFCEC/COSM. (T-1) Maintenance and repair facilities
will be physically separated from other inhabited structures and any other source of
ignition by at least 50 feet. (T-1) Air National Guard units are recommended to separate
inhabited structures and any other source of ignition by at least 50 feet due to space
restrictions and fewer fueling vehicles and carts. Refer to AFI 24-302 and DAFMAN
32-1084 for additional guidance.
30.14.8.2. The refueling maintenance shop should be located near the refueling vehicle
parking area to minimize travel distance and help keep refuelers off installation streets.
30.14.8.3. The shop will be located so emergency services have immediate and direct
access on paved roads. (T-1)
30.14.8.4. Both external and internal earth grounds will be provided for the refuelers, and
a lightning protection system provided for the structure. (T-1)
30.14.8.5. Separate lockers, washing, and eating areas will be provided for employees.
(T-0) Adequate PPE for exposure to liquid fuels and vapors will be provided. (T-0) Refer
to AFI 24-302 and DAFMAN 32-1084 for additional information.
30.14.8.6. The amount of space required for refueler maintenance areas is determined by
the number of refueling vehicles assigned. Refer to DAFMAN 32-1084 for specific
requirements.
30.14.8.7. Floor drains will always be kept clear of contaminants which have a potential
for fire, safety, and health hazards. (T-1) Solvents or fuel will not be used to clean floor
drains. (T-1) Use PPE identified by the installation BE OH Assessment for use when
cleaning drains. (T-1)

358

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
30.14.9. Vehicle Maintenance Repair Pits.
30.14.9.1. Floor markings will be at least six feet from the edge of the pit in colors that
contrast with the surrounding area, or places a warning line at least six feet from the edge
of the pit, as well as stanchions that are capable of resisting: (T-0)
30.14.9.1.1. A force of at least 16 pounds applied horizontally against the stanchion at
a height of 30 inches without tipping over. (T-0)
30.14.9.1.2. Or places a combination of floor markings and warning lines at least six
feet from the edge of the pit. (T-0)
30.14.9.1.3. When two mor more pits in a common area are not more than 15 feet
apart, the employer may comply by placing contrasting floor markings at least 6 feet
from the pit edge around the entire area of the pits. (T-0)
30.14.9.1.4. Posts a readily visible caution signs that meet the requirements of
Chapter 17 that states, “Caution – Open Pit.” (T-0)
30.14.9.2. Toeboards may be omitted around vehicle repair, service, or assembly pits when
the supervisor can demonstrate that a toeboard would prevent access to a vehicle that is
over the pit.
30.14.9.3. All pits equipped with steps will have handrails and approved lighting IAW
NFPA 70. (T-1) Steps will be painted, coated, or made with an approved nonskid material
to prevent slippage. (T-1)
30.14.9.4. Adequate explosion-proof lighting will be installed in pits or portable
explosion-proof extension lights furnished. (T-1)
30.14.9.5. Pits will be built with drains equipped with oil or grease traps and will not be
connected to sanitary sewers. (T-1) Local exhaust will be evaluated by installation BE to
determine if it is necessary to remove vapors. (T-1) Exhaust fans used in pit ventilation
systems will be suitable for use with fuel vapors, e.g., have explosion-proof motors and
non-sparking blades IAW NFPA 70. (T-1)
30.14.9.6. Pits will be cleaned regularly with soap and water or an approved caustic
solution, but not flammable or combustible solvents to prevent fire, safety, and health
hazards. (T-1) Use PPE identified by the installation BE OH Assessment for use during pit
cleaning. (T-1)
30.14.9.7. To prevent accumulation of hazardous gases, the vehicle engine will not be
operated while a vehicle is positioned over a repair pit, unless required by manufacturer’s
instructions or TO. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

359
Chapter 31

COMPOSITE MATERIALS
31.1. Scope. Composite materials (e.g., fiberglass, carbon fiber, Kevlar) are widely used in DAF
operations. These materials can present potentially serious health and safety hazards to personnel
unless specific precautions are taken. Environmental, Safety and Occupational Health (ESOH)
personnel will evaluate any operations involving composite materials. (T-1) Paragraph 1.6., Job
Hazard Analysis, and DAFI 90-802 will be consulted when working with composite materials.
(T-1). For additional information, the OSHA Technical Manual, Section III, Polymer Matrix
Materials:
Advanced
Composites,
is
available
at
https://www.osha.gov/dts/osta/otm/otm_iii/otm_iii_1.html. Composite operations involving
confined spaces will follow guidance in Chapter 23.
31.2. Specific Responsibilities. Functional managers and supervisors will:
31.2.1. Ensure all personnel who work with or handle composite materials receive training
associated with required tasks. (T-0)
31.2.2. Ensure employees performing composite repair and fabrication tasks, or handling or
using hazardous materials, receive hazard communication training IAW AFI 90-821. (T-0)
31.2.3. Ensure SDSs are available for all hazardous materials used in composite work tasks.
(T-0)
31.2.4. Ensure employees handling and storing flammable or combustible chemicals are
trained in fire prevention and protection. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 6 for additional guidance.
31.2.5. Ensure appropriate spill and containment control procedures, and materials are readily
available in areas where composite materials are stored, dispensed, or used. (T-0)
31.2.6. Provide employees with PPE identified by the installation BE OH Assessment when
handling composite materials and chemicals. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 14 or additional
guidance.
31.2.7. Coordinate with installation CE to establish procedures for hazardous waste disposal.
(T-1)
31.3. General Requirements.
31.3.1. Many of the chemicals and materials used in composite fabrication and repairs pose
serious health or fire hazards and must be used and handled with care. Employees will refer to
the TO and SDS prior to starting any work when hazardous materials or chemicals are involved
or needed for the process. (T-1) Warning: Do not exceed manufacturer’s maximum batch
size or a violent exothermic reaction may result
31.3.2. Training. All personnel who work with composite materials will be trained on the
hazardous properties of chemicals and materials used. (T-0) Job safety training will include
PPE requirements, safe chemical handling procedures, and emergency treatment practices.
(T-1) This training will be documented IAW DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
31.3.3. Hazard Communication. Employees will receive hazard communication training IAW
AFI 90-821. (T-0)

360

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
31.3.4. Eyewash and Emergency Showers. Emergency shower facilities for composite repair
shops will be installed IAW Chapter 9. (T-1)
31.3.5. Exposure Monitoring. Supervisors will consult installation BE to ensure employees
who may be potentially exposed to airborne composite materials are monitored, and
appropriate respiratory protection requirements are established IAW AFI 48-137. (T-0)
31.3.6. Fire Protection. All facilities and operating areas will have fire protection IAW NFPA.
(T-1)
31.3.7. Ventilation Requirements. Ventilation for all composite repair and fabrication shops,
as well as locations where composites are installed and repaired (e.g., on-aircraft repairs) must
be based upon the recommendations provided in the unit’s OH Assessment conducted by
installation BE and installed IAW the manufacturer’s instructions. (T-1)
31.3.8. Handling Flammable and Combustible Liquids and Materials. Refer to Chapter 22
for additional information.
31.3.9. Storage Of Composite Materials. Composite materials, including resins and catalysts,
will be stored IAW the manufacturer’s instructions and SDSs. (T-1) If neither is available,
storage will be IAW locally developed guidance prepared in coordination with F&ES Flight,
occupational safety office, and installation CE. (T-1) Note: Composite materials may be
destroyed or damaged by improper storage conditions.
31.3.9.1. Storage Inside Buildings. When the inside storage is incidental and not the
primary purpose of the area, comply with Chapter 22, NFPA 30, the manufacturer’s
requirements. (T-1)
31.3.9.2. Outside Storage. To minimize the potential for environmental contamination,
installation CE will coordinate on the siting of all outside storage areas. (T-1) Refer to
Chapter 22 and NFPA 30 for additional guidance.
31.3.10. Electrical Requirements for Flammable Vapor Areas. Facility wiring will be
designed, installed and maintained IAW NFPA 70E,and UFC 3-600-01, and any specialized
criteria identified to maintain the safety of the operation. (T-0)
31.3.11. Powered and Non-powered Equipment and Tools Used Near Flammable and
Combustible Materials. Tools and equipment used in composite operations will be purchased,
installed, and maintained to ensure they do not create an ignition source to chemicals, chemical
byproducts, and finished products of the operation. (T-1)

31.4. Specific Applications.
31.4.1. Laboratories. All laboratories will have fire protection appropriate for their fire hazard
classification NFPA 45, Standard on Fire Protection for Laboratories Using Chemicals.
Develop chemical hygiene plan IAW AFI 90-821. (T-0)
31.4.2. Military Exchanges, Commissaries and Associate Retail Stores. Facilities offering unreacted composite materials (e.g., auto body fillers, fiberglass repair kits) will ensure the
materials are kept in their original containers and that all manufacturer precautions and use
information is provided to purchaser at time of sale. (T-1) Damaged or leaking packages will
be removed from sale and disposed of properly. (T-0) Contact installation CE for disposal
guidelines. In rooms or areas accessible to the public, storage will be limited to quantities

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

361

needed for display and normal merchandising purposes. (T-1) For storage of flammable and
combustible materials, refer to Chapter 22 and NFPA 30.
31.5. Hazardous Waste Disposal. Supervisors will coordinate with installation CE to establish
a hazardous waste material list for their unit and establish procedures for hazardous waste disposal
IAW AFMAN 32-7002. (T-1)
31.6. Special Mishap Considerations. Composite material mishaps require special handling.
Although burning composites may be extinguished, these materials may continue to smolder and
outgas toxic vapors. The F&ES Flight, installation BE and installation CE will establish
appropriate measures to handle mishaps involving composite materials. (T-1) Special firefighting
and debris handling requirements are identified in TO 00-105E-9, Aerospace Emergency Rescue
and Mishap Response Information (Emergency Services).
31.7. Use of Composite Materials in New System Acquisition, and System and Process
Modifications. New system acquisition or modification of existing systems and processes will be
carefully reviewed for fire protection, occupational safety, health, and environmental impact prior
to use by the applicable DAF Systems Program Office, Item Manager or Design/Process Engineer,
and the technical data developing organization. (T-1) When composite handling/repair/fabrication
processes are turned over to a contractor, the contracting officer will ensure associated DAF fire,
safety, health, and environmental data is provided to the contractor. (T-1)

362

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Chapter 32
PRECISION MEASUREMENT EQUIPMENT LABORATORY

32.1. Scope. This chapter addresses the hazards and safety requirements associated with PMEL
operations. Refer to DAFMAN 21-113, Air Force Metrology and Calibration Program
Management, for additional information and guidance on the Air Force’s Metrology and
Calibration program requirements. Note:
Due to the inherent differences between
Communication-Electronic maintenance and PMEL operations, guidance in Chapter 28 does not
apply to PMEL operations.
32.1.1. Housekeeping. A high standard of housekeeping is essential to safe operations in
laboratories. Dry floors are essential to physical safety. Liquid spills on floors will be
immediately cleaned and dried to minimize the possibility of slipping and falling. (T-0) In
addition, wet floors provide a conductive surface which may cause electrical shock, injury, or
death. All work and storage areas will be kept clean and free of unnecessary obstructions. (T-0)
Equipment and materials will be stored in a safe and orderly manner. (T-1) Refer to Chapter
2 for additional guidance on housekeeping.
32.1.2. Supervisor Responsibilities. Supervisors will:
32.1.2.1. Trained personnel on the location, selection and use of fire extinguishers, alarm
systems, main power switches, and evacuation procedures. (T-0)
32.1.2.2. Provide CPR training for employees required to perform work on energized high
voltage electric circuits. (T-0) Refer to Chapter 1 for additional guidance.
32.1.2.3. Receive proper ionizing and non-ionizing radiation safety training for personnel
performing maintenance on equipment from the IRSO. (T-1)
32.1.2.4. Ensure fire extinguishers are properly mounted, readily accessible, in a
