serviceable condition, and clearly visible IAW Chapter 6. (T-0)
32.1.2.5. Ensure all exits are clearly identified, posted with signs, doors unlocked, easily
accessible and clear paths of egress are provided IAW Chapter 6. (T-0)
32.1.2.6. Ensure all new chemicals, machines and processes are evaluated by the
occupational safety office, F&ES Flight, installation CE, and installation BE prior to
introduction into the work area. (T-0) Once approved, all personnel will be trained prior to
using the new chemicals, machines, or processes. (T-0) Document training for personnel.
(T-1)
32.1.2.7. Comply with housekeeping requirements IAW Chapter 2.
32.2. Hazards. Personnel engaged in PMEL operations are exposed to several hazards, including
exposure to electrical and electromagnetic energy, hazardous chemicals/materials, and flammable
and combustible liquids and gases. Particular attention must be given to protection against
exposure to toxic chemicals and ionizing radiation because the effects of any exposures are not
always immediately apparent. Potential health hazards can be managed by adhering to proper work
procedures, engineering controls and facility design, and appropriate PPE and clothing.
32.2.1. Electrical Hazards. All sources of electrical energy can be hazardous, personal injury
from electric shock and arcing may cause severe or fatal injuries. Safe electrical work practices,

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

363

safe equipment, and approved grounding procedures are essential to prevent electrical mishaps.
Personnel working on, with or near energized electrical circuits or equipment will adhere to
guidance in Chapters 8 and 21. (T-1)
32.2.2. Radiation Hazards. Hazardous radiation in PMEL operations may be classified as
either ionizing or non-ionizing radiation.
32.2.2.1. Ionizing Radiation. Radioactive materials found in calibration sources, detection
instrument check sources, and electron tubes produce ionizing radiation. Calibration
sources pose the greatest hazard because of the intensities involved and can become
hazardous when broken or touched directly. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.1096, Ionizing
Radiation, and AFMAN 48-148 for guidance on ionizing radiation hazards and DAFMAN
48-125 for guidance on use of ionizing radiation detection devices.
32.2.2.2. Non-ionizing Radiation. Refer to AFI 48-109 and 29 CFR § 1910.97,
Nonionizing Radiation, for guidance on management of hazards associated with nonionizing radiation. Employees with pacemakers and other electrically active implanted
medical devices must not be allowed to work in areas where the function of their device
may be adversely affected by exposure to known non-ionizing radiation or electromagnetic
fields. (T-0) All PMEL employees will be made aware of this requirement. (T-1) If there
are questions, refer the employee to their medical provider for assessment.
32.2.2.2.1. Laser Radiation. Laser energy presents a significant hazard to the eyes and
skin under direct or reflected viewing conditions. The use of optical viewing aids, e.g.,
binoculars, telescopes, can significantly increase the eye hazard from laser radiation,
particularly when used to view a point source of radiation. The radiant energy entering
the eye is increased by the magnifying power of the optical device. Refer to AFI 48139 for additional guidance.
32.2.2.2.2. Radio Frequency Radiation. Radio frequency radiation produces heat in
body tissues. Absorption by specific body organs depends upon the frequency and
intensity of the radio frequency radiation. It is possible for a person to absorb damaging
amounts of radio frequency radiation in deep tissue and organs with little or no
sensation of skin heating. Refer to AFI 48-109 for additional guidance.
32.2.3. Chemical Hazards. PMEL operations use and may expose personnel to hazardous
chemicals, which is defined as a chemical that poses a physical or health hazard. This definition
applies to most of the chemicals used in PMEL operations IAW DAFMAN 48-146.
32.2.4. Compressed Oil and Gas Hazards. Some testing and calibrating operations require use
of compressed oil at pressures of 30,000 psi. An oil leak above 4,000 psi produces a high
velocity stream of oil that may penetrate the skin and inject oil into body tissue. These highpressure injections can cause severe localized circulatory system damage and loss of the
injured extremity. Even low-pressure, high-volume systems pose a danger to personnel
because of the amount of energy stored in the system.
32.2.4.1. When a TO calls for a safety shield, the safety shield will be impact-rated and
3/16-inch polycarbonate material at a minimum. (T-0) The safety shield will either be
weighted or secured at the bottom to prevent tipping over when exposed to pressurized
materials. (T-0)

364

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
32.2.4.2. The safety shield will be placed between the technician and Item Under Test
(IUT) when pressure is applied to the IUT. (T-0)
32.2.4.3. If personnel are directly exposed to the rear of the IUT under pressure, a safety
shield will be placed behind the IUT to protect from potential rear facing ruptures. (T-0)
32.2.4.4. If a safety shield placement is not feasible due to the location of the IUT, then
face and eye protection will be used IAW Chapter 14. (T-0) Warning: Injected oil may
be life threatening and require immediate medical treatment. Items under pressure may
rupture with the potential to cause serious injury if over pressurized.
32.2.5. Fire Hazards. Flammable and combustible liquids will be handled, stored, and used
IAW Chapters 22 and 6. (T-0)
32.2.6. Laser Eye Protection. Appropriate laser eye protection will be worn when working
with laser equipment. Consult the Laser Safety Officer and the unit’s OH Assessment provided
by installation BE for the type of laser eye protection required. (T-1) Lasers will be used in a
controlled area and protection provided by required warning devices. (T-0) Refer to AFI 48139 for additional guidance.
32.2.7. Respiratory Protection. Inhalation of toxic levels of hazardous materials may cause
adverse health effects. These exposures may be short-term or long-term (chronic), or both.
Health effects from these exposures may vary from minor irritation and temporary illness to
permanent organ damage, cancer, and death. The proper use of approved respirators will
protect the wearer from toxic levels of airborne chemicals. (T-0) Refer to the unit’s OH
Assessment provided by installation BE for respiratory protection requirements IAW Chapter
14 and 29 CFR § 1910.134.

32.3. Ventilation Systems. PMEL cleaning and work areas must have appropriate ventilation.
(T-0) Ventilation at workstations will continue for a sufficient time after job completion to
minimize residual vapors and provide adequate makeup air. (T-0) Installation BE will identify
ventilation requirements, to include periodic testing IAW ACGIH’s Industrial Ventilation: A
Manual of Recommended Practice for Operations and Maintenance, DAFMAN 48-146, and FC
4-218-01F, Air Force Criteria for Precision Measurement Equipment Laboratory Design and
Construction. (T-1)
32.4. Electrical Safety for PMEL Operations. Refer to Chapters 8 and 21 for additional
guidance.
32.4.1. Laboratory Floors. The floor covering of calibration/repair areas will be as specified
in FC 4-218-01F. (T-0) Floor coverings that are damaged or do not meet specified
requirements, will be repaired or replaced. (T-0) Insulated mats or suitable substitutes are
required when personnel are exposed to energized high voltage circuits. (T-0) Use TO 00-25232, Control and Use of Insulating Matting for High-Voltage Application, and TO 00-25-234,
General Shop Practice Requirements for the Repair, Maintenance, and Test of Electrical
Equipment, for instructions on control, use, testing, inspection, and marking of insulating
matting in addition to any manufacturer guidance. (T-0) Insulating matting must be
permanently marked per 29 CFR § 1910.137. (T-0)
32.4.2. Equipment Grounding. Use the manufacturer’s instructions, 29 CFR §§ 1910.303,
General Requirements, 1910.304, Wiring Design and Protection, 1910.305, Wiring Methods,

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

365

Components, and Equipment for General Use and 1910.308, Special Systems, for guidance.
(T-0)
32.4.3. Facility Ground. The facility ground will be marked at its connection to the ground
buss bar. (T-1) The facility custodian will visually inspect the electrical and grounding systems
monthly for security and damage. (T-1) After repairs, the facility ground will be tested for
continuity and polarity by a qualified electrician. (T-1) Refer to AFMAN 32-1065 for
additional guidance.
32.4.4. Buss bars will be clean, free of corrosion and connections periodically checked for
tightness. (T-1)
32.4.5. Equipment Electrical Safety Devices.
32.4.5.1. Interlocks will not be disabled during maintenance or adjustments unless
prescribed by the manufacturer's instructions or applicable TOs. (T-1) During these
periods, a sign stating “Danger Interlocks Disabled” or other suitable commercially
available substitute will be placed on the equipment. (T-1) Interlocks will not be
permanently disabled or bypassed. (T-1) Refer to Chapters 17 and 21, and 29 CFR §
1910.306, Specific Purpose Equipment and Installations, for additional information on
hazardous energy control procedures and requirements.
32.4.5.2. Only qualified technicians will perform repair work on equipment with defective
interlocks. (T-1) Refer to 29 CFR §§ 1910.303, 1910.305 and 1910.306 for additional
guidance regarding interlocks.
32.4.5.3. When personnel are exposed to energized high voltage circuits, e.g., greater than
600 volts, safety observers trained in CPR will be present, unless the area supervisor
determines the risk of the operation does not warrant a safety observer. (T-0) Personnel
will follow guidance in UFC 3-560-01 when an arc flash hazard exists. (T-0)
32.4.6. Fuses and Circuit Breakers. Use NFPA 70 for circuit breakers/fuse box identification
requirements. (T-1)
32.4.6.1. Circuit breakers or fuses will be installed in all electrical circuits to interrupt the
current flow when it exceeds the current carrying capacity of the conductor and/or circuit.
(T-0) Do not bypass circuit breakers and fuses. (T-0) Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.306 for
additional guidance.
32.4.6.2. Equipment fuses will always be replaced by fuses of the size and type required
by the TO or manufacturer's instructions. (T-1) Use insulated fuse pullers to remove and
replace cartridge fuses. (T-1) Do not use wire, foil, solder, or other conductive materials as
substitutes for fuses. A lower amperage fuse of the same type may be used as a temporary
measure until the proper fuse is available. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.306 for additional
guidance.
32.4.7. Grounding Sticks. A grounding stick will be available for use at electronic
workstations where residual voltages may be encountered during maintenance on de-energized
equipment. (T-1)
32.4.8. Adjustments and Testing of Equipment. Electrical power will be disconnected before
technicians perform adjustments or repairs not involving alignment of mechanical/electrical
components. (T-0) Adjustments with power on will only be accomplished when required by

366

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
applicable TOs or manufacturer's instructions. (T-1) Technicians performing the
adjustment/alignment will use insulated tools to minimize the possibility of shock or damage
to equipment components. (T-1) Refer to Chapter 21 for proper hazardous energy control
procedures.
32.4.9. Electrical Safety Boards. Refer to Chapter 8 for required items included in an
emergency equipment kit or board.

32.5. Radioactive Material Safety.
32.5.1. Radioactive material will be handled in a manner that results in as low as reasonably
achievable (ALARA) exposures to personnel. (T-1) The IRSO and/or installation BE will
prescribe special procedures and precautionary measures as necessary. (T-1) In overseas areas,
disposal procedures will be coordinated with the IRSO and installation BE. (T-1) Refer to
AFMAN 40-201 for additional guidance.
32.5.2. Personnel will not handle or expose a radioactive source except as required in the
performance of duty. (T-1) Personnel using or exposed to radioisotopes will receive formal
training, followed by continuing OJT. (T-1) No one will be authorized to use, handle, or access
any area with an exposed or open ionizing radiation source without the employee and task
being evaluated by the RSO for possible entry into the US Air Force Dosimetry Program and
placed on the dosimetry program. (T-1) Refer to DAFMAN 48-125 for additional guidance.
32.5.3. In restricted areas where radioactive material is used or stored, appropriate placards
will be conspicuously posted at each entrance and around the exterior perimeter of each area
so at least one placard is clearly visible from any direction of approach. (T-0) Obstacles, such
as rope, barricades, etc., will be used to discourage unauthorized entry into controlled areas.
(T-1) Individual containers of radioactive material within the area will be appropriately
identified. (T-1)
32.5.4. Pregnant military personnel must report to PH, and civilian employees will report to
PH, who will, in coordination with installation BE and the Primary Care Manager, document
health risks and preventive actions. (T-1) For military personnel, an AF Form 469, Duty
Limiting Condition Report, will be completed and sent to the supervisor. (T-1)
32.5.5. Eating or drinking in any area in which a radiation ingestion hazard may exist is strictly
forbidden. (T-0) Personnel working with radioactive materials will wash their hands and face
before eating or drinking and upon leaving a contaminated area. (T-0)
32.5.6. Regardless of radiation intensity, radioactive items used in PMEL operations will not
be carried in pockets or clothing of personnel. (T-1)
32.5.7. In addition to placards, positive measures, such as locked or guarded gates or doors,
will be established to prevent unauthorized entry into high radiation areas IAW AFMAN 48148. (T-1)
32.5.8. When containers of radioactive materials are broken or damaged, the area will be
evacuated of all personnel and implement the emergency response plan IAW DAFI 10-2501,
Emergency Management Program. (T-1) Personnel essential to evaluating the condition of the
material, recovery of the radioisotope, or decontamination of that area will be authorized
access. (T-1) The PMEL supervisor and IRSO will be notified of the situation. (T-1) A survey
of the area will be conducted to determine the extent of the radiological hazard. (T-1) Personnel

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

367

who may have been exposed will be monitored to determine the extent of medical action
required. (T-1) The IRSO will direct the decontamination or recovery operation. (T-1) Broken
or damaged items will be properly packaged and disposed of. (T-1) Refer to AFMAN 40-201,
Radioactive Materials (RAM) Management, for additional disposal and handling instructions.
(T-1)
32.6. Equipment/Component Cleaning.
32.6.1. Vacuuming using a nonmetallic wand and suction nozzle is the preferred method for
cleaning electronic equipment. When using compressed air, five psi is adequate for equipment
cleaning; do not exceed 30 psi. (T-0) Refer to TO 00-25-234 for additional guidance.
32.6.2. Only approved and authorized solvents will be used with adequate ventilation. (T-0)
Consult installation BE if the characteristics of a solvent are in question and request a HRA if
not previously assessed during the unit’s OH Assessment. (T-1) Required PPE will be worn.
(T-0) Refer to Chapter 14 for additional PPE requirements when working around solvents.
32.6.3. Ultrasonic Cleaning. When using ultrasound-cleaning equipment, employees will
follow the manufacturer’s instructions for parts and equipment cleaning. (T-1) Refer to TO 0025-234 for additional guidance.
32.7. Tools and Equipment.
32.7.1. Insulated tools will be used when performing work on energized equipment. (T-1)
Placing rubber grips or tape on un-insulated tools does not make them acceptable insulated
tools. Wooden handle tools will not be used on energized equipment. (T-1)
32.7.2. Only wood or fiberglass ladders are acceptable for work on or near electrical
equipment. Refer to Chapter 7 for additional guidance on portable ladders.
32.7.3. Ground wrist straps will be disconnected when personnel are exposed to or working
on energized electrical circuits. (T-1)
32.7.4. Industrial equipment such as floor hoists, cranes, drill presses, grinders, and polishers
may pose hazards to technicians. Consult applicable maintenance, commercial data, Chapter
11, and TO 34-1-3 for proper operation and maintenance of such equipment.
32.7.5. Radar and Microwave Equipment. Radar and microwave equipment are sources of
radio frequency radiation. Personnel will be instructed on radio frequency radiation hazards
prior to working on this equipment and will not be exposed to radio frequency radiation levels
above permissible exposure limits. (T-0) Caution will be observed with any open waveguide
connected to an energized source of microwave radiation, as high-power densities can be found
at the waveguide opening. (T-1) A radio frequency radiation warning sign will be posted in
areas where access to power density levels exceed the permissible exposure limit. (T-1)
32.7.6. Hydraulic and Pneumatic Pressure Systems. TO 00-25-223, Integrated Pressure
Systems and Components (Portable and Installed), provides safe operating procedures for this
equipment and will be followed when using this equipment. (T-1)
32.7.7. Calibration/Repair of Fluid Analysis Equipment. Exposure to acids, electric current,
and petroleum distillates may occur when performing oil analysis operations and maintenance.
Employees will wear appropriate PPE when using this equipment and associated chemicals.
(T-1)

368

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
32.7.8. Calibration/Repair of Radiation Measuring Equipment. Personnel will follow AFI 48109, AFI 48-139, and AFMAN 40-201 when calibrating or repairing radiation test, measuring
and diagnostic equipment. (T-1)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

369
Chapter 33

PROCESS SAFETY MANAGEMENT (PSM) OF HIGHLY HAZARDOUS CHEMICALS
33.1. Scope. The DAF implements 29 CFR § 1910.119, Process Safety Management of Highly
Hazardous Chemicals, requirements to prevent or minimize consequences of catastrophic releases
of toxic, reactive, flammable or explosive chemicals. The regulations found on 29 CFR § 1910.119
will be complied with for program implementation and application. (T-0) PSM may also apply to
explosives and pyrotechnics. Refer to 29 CFR § 1910.119 and Mil-STD-882E for additional
guidance.
33.2. OSHA Regulation. 29 CFR § 1910.119, Appendix A, List of Highly Hazardous Chemicals,
Toxics and Reactives, is the mandatory appendix under PSM that contains a list of chemicals
known to be toxic, reactive, flammable, and/or explosive. These chemicals, whether used in a
mixture or a byproduct of a chemical process and/or reaction have the potential for a catastrophic
event at or above the Appendix threshold quantities. Note: 29 CFR § 1910.119, Appendix A will
be referred to as “Appendix A.”
33.3. Responsibilities.
33.3.1. Commanders and/or functional managers of organizations that own chemicals above
the Appendix A thresholds will:
33.3.1.1. Identify an individual to serve as the unit’s PSM Program Manager. (T-1) This
must be an employee with expertise in engineering and process operations that, alone or as
a team lead, has the specific experience and knowledge of the process being evaluated and
knowledgeable of the specific process hazard analysis (PHA) methodology used. (T-0) The
PSM Program Manager will lead the PSM Program Team in conducting the PHA,
developing operating procedures, development of training, and any other requirements
necessary to comply with 29 CFR § 1910.119. (T-1) IAW 29 CFR § 1910.119, the PSM
Program Manager will:
33.3.1.1.1. Identify the designated organizational representative(s) and PSM Program
Manager(s) to the Hazardous Materials Management Process (HMMP) Team in
writing. (T-1)
33.3.1.1.2. Maintain oversight of the organization’s program and ensure compliance.
(T-0)
33.3.1.2. Ensure the organization establishes, documents, and maintains a PSM Program
IAW 29 CFR § 1910.119. (T-0)
33.3.1.3. Support the organization in establishing and maintaining the PSM program.
(T-1)
33.3.1.4. Ensure the PSM Program Manager and organizational process team members,
prior to PSM operations, receive training IAW 29 CFR § 1910.119, initially and refresher
training every three years. (T-0)
33.3.1.5. Employee training will be documented with the name of employee, date of
training, and means used to verify the employee understands the training. (T-0)

370

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
33.3.1.6. Ensure an initial PHA is completed IAW 29 CFR § 1910.119(e). (T-0) At least
every five years or when changes occur, the PHA will be updated and certified for
accuracy. (T-0) These PHAs may be performed by internal staff members, contractors or
a combination of both, who meet 29 CFR § 1910.119(g), Training Requirements.
33.3.1.7. Maintain all documentation developed in support of implementing the PSM
program for program assessment by F&ES Flight, installation CE, installation BE, and
occupational safety or appropriate federal agencies. (T-1)
33.3.2. Tenant organizations will notify host occupational safety for assistance in contacting
the appropriate agencies to assist in determining if a PSM program is required. (T-1)
33.3.3. The Occupational Safety office will:
33.3.3.1. Assign safety personnel to assist process owners with coordinating PSM
procedures with appropriate agencies. (T-1)
33.3.3.2. Assign the OSM to maintain written certification of PSM compliance with 29
CFR § 1910.119(o)(1) for Higher Headquarter, OSHA or other regulatory inspection. (T-0)
33.3.3.3. Assess installation operations and advise MAJCOM/FLDCOM of the number
and type of operations that fall under the PSM program annually or when changes occur.
(T-1)
33.3.4. Installation BE will assist organizations with PSM programs to ensure HAZCOM
requirements are met. (T-1)
33.3.5. F&ES Flight will be involved with PSM program planning to ensure adequate
emergency response is planned into the process. (T-1)
33.3.6. Installation CE will be involved with PSM program planning to ensure adequate
compliance with Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) regulations. (T-0)
33.3.7. Installation Emergency Management will support the PSM Team to ensure emergency
actions from catastrophic events are incorporated into the installation Emergency Management
Plan 10-2. (T-1)
33.3.8. Other installation agencies will support, as required. (T-1)
33.3.9. PSM Program Team.
33.3.9.1. The PSM Program Team is a cross functional team (CFT) where representatives
from multiple installation organizations brought together to assist the affected organization
evaluate the process and identify hazards under evaluation. The team includes installation
BE and CE, F&ES Flight, occupational safety, employees with expertise in engineering
and process operations, employee(s) who have experience and knowledge specific to the
process being evaluated and the PSM Program Manager.
33.3.9.2. The PSM Program Team will meet to identify hazards in the chemical process
and facilities, identify controls applicable to the hazards, and coordinate emergency
response actions. (T-1)

33.4. Chemical Process and Storage Review. Certification of PSM Program compliance will
be conducted every three years or when new chemicals, increased quantities of chemicals, or
processes that contain chemicals listed in Appendix A are introduced to the installation. (T-0)

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

371

33.4.1. Occupational safety, in coordination with the HMMP team will conduct and certify an
assessment of the installation’s chemical quantities on-hand of those chemicals listed in
Appendix A and their location on the installation. (T-0)
33.4.2. When chemicals on the installation do not exceed the threshold quantities listed in
Appendix A, the occupational safety office will maintain written certification that Appendix
A threshold quantities were not exceeded and a PSM program is not required. (T-0)
33.4.3. If the chemicals on the installation do exceed the threshold quantities listed in
Appendix A:
33.4.3.1. The occupational safety office, in consultation with the HMMP Team, should
evaluate all locations containing the Appendix A chemicals for proximity to one another
for exceedance of the threshold amounts.
33.4.3.1.1. If there is no possible way for chemicals at the sites to interact with one
another or initiate unintentional release of material and the quantity on-hand does not
exceed the threshold quantity, then a PSM program may not be required. Ensure a copy
of OSHA-required PSM documentation listed in paragraph 33.4.2 is maintained in
the occupational safety office for higher headquarter or other regulatory agency review,
e.g., OSHA, EPA. (T-0)
33.4.3.1.2. Chemicals at sites that exceed threshold quantities in Appendix A or are in
close proximity to one another where chemicals could interact to exacerbate
unintentional release or pose hazards to personnel nearby, a PSM program is required.
(T-0) The occupational safety office will advise the installation commander and
affected organization commander of the exceedance and that a formal PSM program is
required. (T-0)
33.4.3.2. The organization with the exceedance has responsibility for establishing a PSM
program IAW 29 CFR § 1910.119. (T-0)
33.5. Training. It is very rare for installations to have an Appendix 1 exceedance that drives the
need for a PSM program. PSM training will be provided to all personnel who may be subject to
the requirement for an organizational PSM program. (T-1) Training will focus on making
employees knowledgeable of basic PSM requirements, principles, how to recognize process
hazards (e.g., chemical releases, fires, explosions), and know their role in preventing incidents.
(T-0)
33.5.1. Safety Office Personnel. Safety personnel will be familiar with 29 CFR § 1910.119
and this chapter. (T-0) Personnel need an awareness level of understanding of the PSM
elements; hazard awareness, to include chemical properties, reactivity, toxicity and physical
hazards; and emergency response, including evacuation procedures, spill containment and
communication protocols. Awareness training will be conducted upon initial assignment and
as needed, thereafter, to be able to identify program requirements. (T-2)
33.5.2. PSM Team Lead. When a PSM program is required, the PSM team lead is the
organizational focal point for managing the program whether the analysis is performed “inhouse” or contracted out. The PSM lead will be knowledgeable of 29 CFR § 1910.119
requirements and this chapter when fulfilling duties as described above. (T-0) When filling

372

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
this role, the PSM team leader will receive in-depth PSM training, which is commercially
available. (T-1) Training will cover the following items:
33.5.2.1. Understanding chemical hazards and risks. (T-1)
33.5.2.2. Operating procedures and safe work practices. (T-1)
33.5.2.3. Equipment integrity and reliability. (T-1)
33.5.2.4. Management of change. (T-1)
33.5.3. Process Employees (Operations, Maintenance and Engineering). Each employee
involved in operating a process will be trained in an overview of the process and in the
operating procedures as specified in 29 CFR § 1910.119(f). (T-0) The training will include
emphasis on the specific safety and health hazards, emergency operations including shutdown,
and safe work practices applicable to the employee’s job tasks. (T-0)
33.5.4. HMMP Team. HMMP team personnel will be familiar with 29 CFR § 1910.119 and
this chapter. (T-0) They need functional knowledge of report generation within the EESOHMIS system and an awareness level of understanding of the PSM elements.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
Table 33.1. Process Safety Management (PSM) Decision Flow Chart.

Chapter 34
ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING SAFETY

34.1. Scope.
34.1.1. This chapter covers the safety requirements when using additive manufacturing
equipment. Additive manufacturing (often called 3D printing) is used to make prototype parts
and production parts quickly or in place of parts manufactured using conventional means. Each
type of part requires different materials and additive manufacturing processes to meet
specifications for the intended purpose it will be used.
34.1.2. Additive manufacturing has many safety and health hazards that must be addressed,
such as airborne nano particles, chemical vapors, skin irritants, burns, mechanical motion, and
lasers (not an all-inclusive list). Refer to International Organization of Standardization (ISO)
17296-2, Additive Manufacturing – General Principles – Part 2 Overview of Process
Categories and Feedstock, for additional information.
34.2. Responsibilities.
34.2.1. Commanders. Commanders must ensure that installation BE and occupational safety
are notified when additive manufacturing equipment will be purchased to ensure proper
controls are put in place. (T-1)
34.2.2. Supervisors. Supervisors will train employees IAW paragraph 34.6. (T-1)
34.3. Types of Additive Manufacturing. There are seven commonly used types of additive
manufacturing. This list is not all-inclusive.
34.3.1. Material Extrusion. Material extrusion uses plastic or ceramic filaments or pastes that
are heated and layered to make a part. Parts are heated or a chemical reaction is used to bind
the parts together. Secondary processing requires removal of support structures. Note: This is
a form of additive manufacturing, which is well-known for being used significantly in hobby
3D printing.
34.3.2. Powder Bed Infusion. Powder bed infusion uses metal, ceramic, or plastic powder
heated with a laser or electron beam to melt the powder in selective locations to build a part.
The process may also use binders and fillers to build a part. Secondary processing may include
removal of loose powder or any support material. Additionally, a variety of operations are
conducted to improve the finishing, accuracy of the part and material properties.
34.3.3. Vat Photopolymerization. Vat photopolymerization uses a liquid resin and an
ultraviolet laser to cure the resin selectively to make a part. This process has secondary
processing to finish curing the material, remove support material, and clean the part using
chemicals.
34.3.4. Material Jetting. Material jetting selectively places build material and uses chemical
reaction bonding or solidified melted material activated by a radiation light source to build a
part. Common materials used are liquid polymers or wax with or without fillers. Secondary
processing may include support material removal and further light exposure to finalize curing
of the part.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

375

34.3.5. Binder Jetting. Binder jetting uses powder, powder blends, or particulate materials,
such as metal, ceramic, plastic, or sand with a liquid adhesive to create a chemical reaction to
make the parts. Secondary processing would include removal of loose powder and adding a
suitable liquid material to further complete the part.
34.3.6. Sheet Lamination. Sheet lamination uses sheets of paper, metal foil, polymers, or
composites of metal or ceramic powder held together with a binder to layer on top of each
other to make a part. Each layer is bound to the next using thermal or chemical reaction bonding
or ultrasound. Secondary processing includes removal of waste material, and possibly
sintering, infiltration, heat treatment, sanding, or machining to improve the finish.
34.3.7. Directed Energy Deposition. Directed energy deposition uses heat to melt materials as
they are deposited to fuse into a part. Materials used are a filament or paste of plastics and
ceramics. Secondary processing consists of removal of support structures.
34.4. Categories of Part Processing. There are two categories of processes when making parts:
34.4.1. Single-step Process. This is completed when the part is finished printing and no
additional processing is required to complete the part. Essentially, the part is removed from the
machine and used as is.
34.4.2. Multi-step Process. The part requires multiple operations to achieve the final result.
Other processes include use of chemicals to clean the part, increase stability of the part,
improve the part finish, etc.
34.5. Hazards.
34.5.1. Additive manufacturing processes have numerous hazards that can differ depending
on the process and materials in use. Supervisors must evaluate how to address these hazards
from receipt and storage of materials used for parts, pre-printing cleaning and set up, the
manufacturing process itself, clearing malfunctions, and the hazards from post processing and
equipment cleaning. (T-1) SDSs for materials used in this process will be maintained and
referred to when evaluating controls to protect personnel. (T-1) Prior to beginning an additive
manufacturing process, contact installation BE for a HRA and occupational safety for
evaluation. (T-1) Common hazards include (this is not an all-inclusive list):
34.5.1.1. Inhalation. Inhalation hazards include nanoparticles, fumes and volatile organic
compounds (VOCs) from the heating of materials and those materials in powdered form.
Exposure to these hazards may require ventilation or respiratory protection when working
around processes.
34.5.1.2. Skin exposure. Resins, powders, solvents, binders, ultraviolet lights, and lasers
can have significant effects on exposed skin. Wear appropriate hand protection to prevent
exposure when working with an additive manufacturing process. (T-1)
34.5.1.3. Laser exposure. Lasers are used by some processes to adhere material together.
Ensure proper guarding is in place and wear appropriate eye protection as identified by the
installation BE. (T-1)
34.5.1.4. Burns. These processes heat materials to temperatures over 150o F where
touching these heated parts will cause burns to hands. Employees will wait to conduct
maintenance on equipment until the machine has cooled down or use approved hand
protection. (T-1)

376

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026
34.5.1.5. Chemical Exposure. Processes use a variety of chemicals as part of machine
maintenance, the process itself, or to finish the part. Many of these chemicals are
combustible or flammable, refer to Chapter 22 for additional guidance. Refer to the SDSs
on how to properly store, handle, and use the chemicals. (T-1) Contact installation BE to
assist with appropriate controls, such as ventilation and PPE, including by not limited to
eye protection, respiratory protection, and chemical gloves.
34.5.1.6. Moving parts. These machines work in pre-programmed paths and can catch
fingers, hair, and loose clothing that comes into contact with moving parts and injuring
employees. Ensure machines are strategically placed to minimize inadvertent contact with
moving parts and maintain manufacturer guarding when provided. (T-1)
34.5.1.7. Electrical Exposure. Equipment that is damaged, malfunctioned, or dismantled
for maintenance or servicing will be deenergized to protect against electrical shock. (T-1)
Refer to Chapter 21 for hazardous energy control requirements.
34.5.1.8. Noise Hazards. Noise hazards can be created with the operation of the
equipment. Some individual machines or multiple machines in one location may reach a
level of hazardous noise and must be evaluated by installation BE to determine whether
hearing protection will be needed. (T-0) Refer to AFI 48-127 for additional information.
34.5.2. Contamination of materials and chemicals used in additive manufacturing processes
can create a secondary risk to family members at home when clothing is not changed or
properly decontaminated prior to leaving the workplace. Seek guidance from the installation
BE whether this may be a concern for the processes used in the work center. (T-2)
34.5.3. Evaluate set up to protect against fire hazards, consult F&ES Flight to ensure
appropriate controls.

34.6. Exposure Controls. Each hazard must be evaluated and a control put in place to protect
employees. (T-1) Contact the installation BE and occupational safety office for assistance and
follow the manufacturer’s documentation. The following are possible controls to implement when
using additive manufacturing equipment (this is not an all-inclusive list):
34.6.1. Placement. Placing the equipment in a dedicated room not constantly occupied by
employees ensures that they are not continually exposed to the hazards of the equipment like
fumes, nanoparticles, noise, etc.
34.6.2. Ventilation. Providing adequate ventilation and exhaust to the machines minimizes
employee exposure to fumes and particles, reducing the need for PPE.
34.6.3. Guarding. Placing the machines in well-designed enclosures aids in ventilating fumes
out of the room and further reduces employee exposure. Some machines come with their own
designed enclosure that are typically not sealed when closed and do not reduce exposure.
Contact installation BE to assist with determining best method. Enclosures also assist with
preventing employee contact with heated or moving parts.
34.6.4. PPE. PPE needed for the machines used in the work center are based on the materials
used during operation and chemicals used for cleaning and post processing of parts. Contact
installation BE for assistance on identifying PPE to use. Common PPE would include N95
respirator, chemical resistant gloves, and eye protection to protect against splashes.

DAFMAN91-203 24 FEBRUARY 2026

377

34.6.5. Work Practices. Work practices that will help in protecting employees could be
requiring machine shutdown and cooldown before removing parts or conducting service or
maintenance, use of hazardous energy control when servicing or maintaining equipment
(reference Chapter 21), and/or storage requirements for materials used in the printing process
and the post processing of parts. Many chemicals used in cleaning and post processing are
flammable.
34.7. Training. Employees will be trained prior to working with additive manufacturing
equipment. (T-1) Training will include:
34.7.1. Operation of Equipment. (T-1) Employees will be trained in inspection, operation, and
servicing of equipment. (T-1)
34.7.2. HAZCOM. (T-1) Employees will be trained on the HAZCOM requirements when
dealing with chemicals used in the printing and finishing process. (T-1)
34.7.3. PPE. Employees will be trained on the PPE to be used in the printing and finishing
process IAW Chapter 14. (T-1)
34.7.4. Training will be documented as described in DAFI 91-202. (T-1)
34.7.5. Supervisors will follow machine operator requirements as listed in TO 34A-1-1,
Additive Manufacturing Qualification of Technicians, Machines and Facilities.
34.7.6. Supervisors will contact the AFRSO/AMPO Additive Manufacturing Data Exchange
at the following websites: AdditiveManufacturingDataExchange@groups.af.mil or
website: https://usaf.dps.mil/teams/AFRSOAdditiveManufacturingDataExchange, for
required qualification training for additive manufacturing.

OTIS C. JONES
Brigadier General, USAF
Chief of Safety
